> 2. **Forgetting the kinematic requirement.** Many students state "4 legs = static stability" without deriving that the minimum requirement is **3 legs grounded + 1 leg in swing**, hence the bound $n \geq 4$.
> 3. **Mixing up gaits.** "Trot" is diagonal-pair synchrony; "Pace" is lateral-pair synchrony. Conflating them is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers.** Stride length must be in meters, time in seconds, velocity in m/s. KTU evaluators deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing unit.
> 5. **Not labeling diagrams.** Footfall diagrams MUST include the time axis (left-to-right) and leg labels (FL, FR, ML, MR, RL, RR).
> 6. **Citing incorrect robot names.** It is **BigDog** (not "Big Dog" or "Bigdog"), **Spot** (not "Boston Dynamics' Spot"), and **Raibert Hopper** (not "Rai-bert").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module-1 — Legged Locomotion Examples:**

- ⭐ **1 leg** → Raibert Hopper → **Dynamic balance** mandatory (no static stability possible).
- ⭐ **2 legs** → ASIMO, Atlas → **ZMP-based control**; statically marginal.
- ⭐ **4 legs** → BigDog, Spot, MiniCheetah → **Trot/Pace/Bound/Gallop**; statically stable with 3 grounded legs.
- ⭐ **6 legs** → RHex, Genghis, ATHLETE → **Alternating tripod gait** ensures static stability.
- ⭐ **8 legs** → Spider-inspired → Highest redundancy, slowest gait.
- ⭐ **Minimum legs for static walking = 4** (since one leg must swing, three must stay grounded).
- ⭐ **Duty factor $\beta = T_{stance} / T_{cycle}$** — higher $\beta$ = more ground contact = slower but more stable.
- ⭐ **Support polygon** is the convex hull of grounded feet; CoM must lie inside for static stability.
- ⭐ **Velocity relation:** $v_{avg} = L_s / T_c = \beta \cdot v_{push}$.
- ⭐ **Froude number** $Fr = v^2 / (g \cdot L)$ compares gaits across species/robots.
- ⭐ **Cost of Transport (CoT):** legged robots excel on rough terrain; wheeled robots on smooth terrain.
- ⭐ **Hexapods** are the "sweet spot" for **research and educational platforms** due to easy static stability.
- ⭐ **Quadrupeds** are the "sweet spot" for **industrial and military deployment** due to dynamic versatility.
- ⭐ Always pair **examples with their defining features** (e.g., ASIMO + ZMP, RHex + single-DoF legs, Spot + electric quadruped).

> **End of Notes — Module 1: Examples of Legged Robot Locomotion**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aerial Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aerial Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Aerial Robot** is an autonomously operating, untethered, airborne mechatronic platform capable of sustained, controlled, and intelligent flight within a three-dimensional operational volume without an onboard human pilot. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework (PEMET756 – Mobile Robotics), Aerial Robots are formally classified as a sub-branch of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) that integrate real-time **perception**, **cognition**, **planning**, and **actuation** subsystems to execute mission objectives such as surveillance, mapping, payload delivery, and swarm coordination.

Formally, the aerial robot can be abstracted as a **rigid-body dynamical system with 6 Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)** — three translational coordinates (x, y, z) and three rotational coordinates (φ, θ, ψ) — operating under gravity and aerodynamic forces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Aerial Robots in Module 1 must be understood under three lenses — (1) Mechanical Configuration, (2) Flight Kinematics & Dynamics, and (3) Control & Autonomy Architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Aerodynamic Metrics:**
> - Air density at sea level: **ρ ≈ 1.225 kg/m³**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **g ≈ 9.81 m/s²**
> - Standard reference frame: **NED (North-East-Down) or ENU (East-North-Up)**
> - Hover thrust per motor (quadrotor): **T_i = m·g / 4** for symmetric loading

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **hovering hummingbird** carrying a tiny backpack. The bird flaps its wings to generate lift, tilts its body to move forward or sideways, and uses its tail to steer — all while constantly adjusting based on wind, obstacles, and destination. An aerial robot is the engineered analog of this hummingbird:

- **Wings/Propellers** = Lift-generating rotors (the "muscles")
- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** = The inner ear of the bird (balance sensing)
- **GPS + Vision Camera** = The bird's eyes and memory of landmarks
- **Flight Controller (e.g., Pixhawk, Raspberry Pi + PX4)** = The bird's brain
- **Battery / Power Distribution Board** = The bird's heart pumping energy

In short: **an aerial robot is a flying computer with sensors and rotors, capable of "thinking" while airborne.**

## 1.3 Classification of Aerial Robots

| Class | Lift Principle | Hover Capable | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed-Wing UAV** | Aerodynamic wing (forward airspeed) | ❌ No | Long-range mapping, agriculture |
| **Single-Rotor Helicopter** | Main + tail rotor | ✅ Yes | Heavy payload delivery |
| **Multi-Rotor (Quad/Hexa/Octo)** | Multiple fixed-pitch rotors | ✅ Yes | Inspection, cinematography, research |
| **Hybrid VTOL** | Tilting rotors + wings | ✅ Yes | Long endurance + vertical take-off |
| **Flapping-Wing (Ornithopter)** | Biomimetic wing flapping | ⚠️ Limited | Research, stealth micro-aircraft |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently expect students to differentiate **rotary-wing vs fixed-wing** in 3-mark Part A questions. Remember: only rotary-wing and VTOL platforms can *hover stationary* in air.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 6-DOF Rigid Body Model of an Aerial Robot

An aerial robot in 3D space is mathematically represented as a **rigid body** with **6 Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)**, divided into:

1. **Translational DOF (3):** Surge (along x), Sway (along y), Heave (along z)
2. **Rotational DOF (3):** Roll (φ about x), Pitch (θ about y), Yaw (ψ about z)

### State Vector Representation

The complete state of the aerial robot at any time *t* is described by:

- **Position in inertial frame:** p = [x, y, z]ᵀ ∈ ℝ³
- **Orientation (Euler angles):** Θ = [φ, θ, ψ]ᵀ ∈ ℝ³
- **Linear velocity (body frame):** v = [u, v, w]ᵀ ∈ ℝ³
- **Angular velocity (body frame):** ω = [p, q, r]ᵀ ∈ ℝ³

### Why 6-DOF Matters

- A **quadrotor** has **4 actuators (rotors)** but **6 DOF** → the system is **under-actuated**: it cannot independently command all 6 axes. Yaw, for instance, is controlled indirectly by *differential motor torque*.
- A **hexacopter** has 6 actuators → **fully-actuated**, offering redundancy for fault tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Under-actuation is a high-yield KTU concept.** If an exam asks "Why can't a quadrotor move sideways directly?" — the answer is: *Because all 4 rotors produce thrust only along the body z-axis; lateral motion is achieved indirectly by tilting the platform.*

## 2.2 Coordinate Frames and Rotation Matrices

To map body-frame velocities to inertial-frame positions, we use the **ZYX Euler rotation matrix**:

The orientation of the body frame B with respect to the inertial frame I is given by the rotation matrix R(Θ) ∈ SO(3), constructed from three successive elementary rotations:

- **Roll (φ)** about body x-axis
- **Pitch (θ)** about body y-axis
- **Yaw (ψ)** about body z-axis

The order matters: rotations are **not commutative**. The standard aerospace convention applies yaw → pitch → roll (intrinsic Tait-Bryan ZYX).

## 2.3 Forces and Moments Acting on an Aerial Robot

For a quadrotor in steady, symmetric flight:

| Force / Moment | Symbol | Source | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thrust (Lift)** | T | Rotor angular speed ω² | Body z-axis (upward) |
| **Weight** | W = m·g | Gravity | Inertial z-axis (downward) |
| **Drag** | D | Aerodynamic resistance | Opposite to velocity |
| **Roll Moment** | τ_φ | Differential motor torque | About body x-axis |
| **Pitch Moment** | τ_θ | Differential motor torque | About body y-axis |
| **Yaw Moment** | τ_ψ | Reactive torque from rotors | About body z-axis |

### Hover Condition (Most Important KTU Concept)

For a quadrotor with *n* identical rotors, the **hover condition** requires that total thrust exactly cancels gravity:

$$T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i = m \cdot g$$

For a symmetric quadrotor (*n = 4*):

$$T_i = \frac{m \cdot g}{4}$$

This is the foundational equation every aerial robot must satisfy to maintain stationary flight.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU PEMET756 examinations. Memorize units alongside each expression.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hover thrust total | $T_{\text{total}} = m \cdot g$ | N | Mass × gravity |
| 2 | Per-rotor hover thrust (quad) | $T_i = m \cdot g / 4$ | N | Symmetric loading |
| 3 | Lift (aerodynamic, fixed-wing) | $L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2 S C_L$ | N | Wing area S, lift coeff. C_L |
| 4 | Drag (aerodynamic, fixed-wing) | $D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2 S C_D$ | N | Drag coefficient C_D |
| 5 | Thrust from rotor | $T = C_T \rho A (\omega R)^2$ | N | R = rotor radius, ω = ang. speed |
| 6 | Induced velocity (hover, momentum theory) | $v_i = \sqrt{T / (2 \rho A)}$ | m/s | A = rotor disk area |
| 7 | Hover power (ideal) | $P = T \cdot v_i = T^{3/2} / \sqrt{2 \rho A}$ | W | Cubic root relation |
| 8 | Drag-to-thrust ratio | $D/T = \tan(\alpha)$ | dimensionless | α = angle of attack |
| 9 | Euler rate → body rate | $\omega = W(\Theta) \cdot \dot{\Theta}$ | rad/s | W = transformation matrix |
| 10 | Inertial velocity | $\dot{p} = R(\Theta) \cdot v$ | m/s | v in body frame |
| 11 | Motor torque (Newton's 3rd) | $\tau_M = C_M \rho A R (\omega R)^2$ | N·m | Reaction torque on frame |
| 12 | Yaw torque (quad) | $\tau_\psi = \tau_{M,1} - \tau_{M,2} + \tau_{M,3} - \tau_{M,4}$ | N·m | Diagonal motor pairs cancel |
| 13 | Battery energy | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | J | V = voltage, I = current |
| 14 | Flight endurance | $t_{\text{end}} = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}}$ | s | Capacity / average current |
| 15 | Reynolds number (rotor blade) | $Re = \frac{\rho \cdot c \cdot v}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | μ = dynamic viscosity |

> [!WARNING]
> **Important:** In exam answers, always state the **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming steady, level, symmetric flight and incompressible airflow"). Marks are awarded for assumption statements in KTU 2024 Scheme.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Aerial robots are transforming industries:

- **Precision Agriculture:** Multispectral UAVs scan crop health over hectares in minutes.
- **Disaster Response:** Thermal drones locate survivors in collapsed buildings.
- **Logistics:** Amazon Prime Air, medical blood delivery in Rwanda.
- **Infrastructure Inspection:** Power-line, bridge, and wind-turbine blade inspection.
- **Defense & Swarm Robotics:** Coordinated UAV swarms for reconnaissance and jamming.
- **Mapping & Surveying:** LiDAR-equipped drones generate cm-resolution 3D maps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice between **fixed-wing** and **multi-rotor** is governed by the **endurance vs hover capability** trade-off. Fixed-wings offer 1–24 hour endurance but cannot hover; multi-rotors offer precise hover but only 20–40 minutes of flight time.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hover Power Using Momentum (Actuator-Disk) Theory

The Actuator-Disk Theory models a rotor as an ideal disk that imparts momentum to a uniform, incompressible airflow. We will derive the **ideal power required to hover**.

### Step 1: Set Up the Control Volume

Consider a control volume bounded by:
- An inflow plane far above the rotor at velocity v∞
- The actuator disk of area A = π R²
- An outflow plane far below the rotor at velocity v∞ + 2 v_i

The induced velocity **v_i** is the additional velocity imparted by the rotor.

### Step 2: Apply Conservation of Mass

For an incompressible, steady flow, the mass flow rate through the disk is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v_i$$

### Step 3: Apply Conservation of Momentum

The thrust force equals the rate of change of momentum of the air:

$$T = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta v = \rho \cdot A \cdot v_i \cdot (2 v_i) = 2 \rho A v_i^2$$

### Step 4: Solve for Induced Velocity

Rearranging the thrust equation:

$$v_i^2 = \frac{T}{2 \rho A}$$

$$v_i = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}}$$

### Step 5: Compute Power

Power is the rate of work done against the airflow. The force T acts over the induced velocity v_i:

$$P = T \cdot v_i$$

Substituting:

$$P = T \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{hover,ideal}} = \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}}$$

### Step 6: Interpretation

- Power scales with **T^(3/2)** → doubling thrust increases power by **2.83×**, not 2×.
- Larger disk area **A** → **less** power for the same thrust (this is why big rotors are efficient).
- This is why **heavy-lift quadrotors** use **large-diameter propellers**, not high RPM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** The T^(3/2) law is one of the most celebrated results in rotorcraft engineering. It explains why helicopters hit a "power wall" — every additional kilogram of payload requires a disproportionate increase in power.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ZYX Euler Rotation Matrix for Aerial Robot Orientation

The rotation matrix R(Θ) maps a vector expressed in the body frame {B} to the inertial frame {I}.

### Step 1: Define Elementary Rotations

Rotation about z-axis by yaw angle ψ:

$$R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Rotation about y-axis by pitch angle θ:

$$R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

Rotation about x-axis by roll angle φ:

$$R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Combine in ZYX Order

The complete body-to-inertial rotation matrix is:

$$R(\Theta) = R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)$$

Performing the multiplication step-by-step:

$$\begin{aligned}
R(\Theta) &=
\begin{bmatrix} c\psi & -s\psi & 0 \\ s\psi & c\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} c\theta & 0 & s\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -s\theta & 0 & c\theta \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & c\phi & -s\phi \\ 0 & s\phi & c\phi \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix}
c\theta c\psi & s\phi s\theta c\psi - c\phi s\psi & c\phi s\theta c\psi + s\phi s\psi \\
c\theta s\psi & s\phi s\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi & c\phi s\theta s\psi - s\phi c\psi \\
-s\theta & s\phi c\theta & c\phi c\theta
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Where **c** denotes cosine and **s** denotes sine.

### Step 3: Apply to Thrust Vector

The total thrust acts along body z-axis: T_body = [0, 0, T]ᵀ.

Its expression in the inertial frame is:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_I &= R(\Theta) \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ T \end{bmatrix} \\
&= T \cdot \begin{bmatrix} c\phi s\theta c\psi + s\phi s\psi \\ c\phi s\theta s\psi - s\phi c\psi \\ c\phi c\theta \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Equilibrium Force Balance (Small Angle)

For small roll and pitch angles (sφ ≈ φ, sθ ≈ θ), the inertial-frame thrust simplifies to:

$$T_I \approx T \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \theta c\psi + \phi s\psi \\ \theta s\psi - \phi c\psi \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This shows that **small tilts of the quadrotor generate lateral accelerations** — the fundamental mechanism for translational motion despite under-actuation.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Show that a quadrotor achieves lateral motion by tilting."* The derivation above is the expected model answer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Quadrotor Newton–Euler Equations of Motion

### Translational Dynamics

Applying Newton's Second Law in the inertial frame:

$$\begin{aligned}
m \ddot{x} &= T(\cos\phi \sin\theta \cos\psi + \sin\phi \sin\psi) - D_x \\
m \ddot{y} &= T(\cos\phi \sin\theta \sin\psi - \sin\phi \cos\psi) - D_y \\
m \ddot{z} &= T \cos\phi \cos\theta - m g - D_z
\end{aligned}$$

Where D_x, D_y, D_z are aerodynamic drag forces along each axis.

### Rotational Dynamics

Applying Euler's rotational equation in the body frame:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} \dot{p} + (I_{zz} - I_{yy}) q r &= \tau_\phi \\
I_{yy} \dot{q} + (I_{xx} - I_{zz}) p r &= \tau_\theta \\
I_{zz} \dot{r} + (I_{yy} - I_{xx}) p q &= \tau_\psi
\end{aligned}$$

Where I_xx, I_yy, I_zz are principal moments of inertia and (p, q, r) are body-frame angular rates.

### Control Allocation (Quadrotor)

The four motor thrusts T₁, T₂, T₃, T₄ are mapped to total thrust and moments as:

$$\begin{bmatrix} T \\ \tau_\phi \\ \tau_\theta \\ \tau_\psi \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
0 & -d & 0 & d \\
d & 0 & -d & 0 \\
-c & c & -c & c
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} T_1 \\ T_2 \\ T_3 \\ T_4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:
- **d** = arm length (distance from CoM to rotor)
- **c** = thrust-to-torque coupling coefficient (typically ≈ 0.01 m for small rotors)

This **control allocation matrix** is the bridge between *high-level commands* (T, τ_φ, τ_θ, τ_ψ) and *low-level motor speeds*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Control Architecture Comparison

| Control Method | Description | Pros | Cons | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PID** | Proportional-Integral-Derivative on each axis | Simple, robust, low compute | Tuning tedious; poor for coupled nonlinear systems | ✅ Most common in lab exams |
| **LQR** | Linear Quadratic Regulator, optimal state feedback | Optimal gains; handles MIMO | Requires linearized model | ✅ Theory question favorite |
| **Backstepping** | Recursive Lyapunov-based design | Handles nonlinearities directly | Complex mathematics | ✅ Advanced CO question |
| **MPC** | Model Predictive Control with horizon | Constraint handling; preview-based | High computational cost | ✅ Industry trend |
| **Geometric Control on SE(3)** | Lie-group based | Global validity, no singularities | Very advanced | ⚠️ Research-level |

> [!TIP]
> For 14-mark questions, **PID + tuning diagram** or **LQR derivation via Riccati equation** are the two highest-yield choices for KTU PEMET756 Module 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Aerial Robot (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Ground Control Station] -->|Telemetry Link 2.4 GHz| B1[Onboard Flight Computer]
    A1 -->|Mission Upload| B1
    B1 --> C1[State Estimator EKF]
    B1 --> C2[Path Planner A-Star RRT]
    B1 --> C3[Controller PID LQR]
    C1 --> D1[IMU 6 Axis Accelerometer Gyro]
    C1 --> D2[GPS Receiver U Blox]
    C1 --> D3[Barometer BMP280]
    C1 --> D4[Optical Flow Sensor]
    C1 --> D5[Magnetometer HMC5883]
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> E1[Electronic Speed Controllers ESC]
    E1 --> F1[Brushless Motor M1 Front Right]
    E1 --> F2[Brushless Motor M2 Rear Right]
    E1 --> F3[Brushless Motor M3 Rear Left]
    E1 --> F4[Brushless Motor M4 Front Left]
    F1 --> G1[Propeller 10 inch CW]
    F2 --> G2[Propeller 10 inch CCW]
    F3 --> G3[Propeller 10 inch CW]
    F4 --> G4[Propeller 10 inch CCW]
    B1 --> H1[Power Module BEC]
    H1 --> I1[LiPo Battery 4S 14.8 V]
```

## 4.2 Quadrotor Motor Configuration and Rotation Sense

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Motor 1 FR CW Rotation] -->|Positive Roll Moment| ROLL[Roll Axis X]
    M2[Motor 2 RR CCW Rotation] -->|Negative Roll Moment| ROLL
    M3[Motor 3 RL CW Rotation] -->|Positive Pitch Moment| PITCH[Pitch Axis Y]
    M4[Motor 4 FL CCW Rotation] -->|Negative Pitch Moment| PITCH
    M1 -->|Reactive Torque Cancel| YAW[Yaw Axis Z]
    M2 -->|Reactive Torque Add| YAW
    M3 -->|Reactive Torque Cancel| YAW
    M4 -->|Reactive Torque Add| YAW
```

## 4.3 Aerial Robot Control Loop (Closed-Loop Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REF[Reference Trajectory Xd Yd Zd] --> COMP[Error Computation]
    COMP -->|Position Error| P[Proportional Integral Derivative Controller]
    P -->|Desired Attitude| ATT[Attitude Controller]
    ATT -->|Motor Commands| ESC[Electronic Speed Controller]
    ESC --> MOT[Brushless Motors]
    MOT --> ROT[Rotors Generate Thrust]
    ROT -->|Force Torque| DYN[Quadrotor Dynamics 6 DOF]
    DYN -->|State Measurement| SENS[Sensors IMU GPS]
    SENS --> EST[State Estimator Extended Kalman Filter]
    EST -->|Estimated Position Velocity| COMP
```

## 4.4 Classification Taxonomy of Aerial Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AR[Aerial Robots] --> A1A[Fixed Wing UAV]
    AR --> A1B[Rotary Wing UAV]
    AR --> A1C[Hybrid VTOL]
    AR --> A1D[Flapping Wing Biomimetic]
    A1B --> A1B1[Single Rotor Helicopter]
    A1B --> A1B2[Multi Rotor]
    A1B2 --> A1B2A[Quadrotor 4 Rotors]
    A1B2 --> A1B2B[Hexacopter 6 Rotors]
    A1B2 --> A1B2C[Octocopter 8 Rotors]

    A1C --> A1C2[Tilt Wing]
    A1C --> A1C3[Convertible Tailsitter]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid diagrams follow the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10** safeguards: every node ID is alphanumeric (A1, B1, F1, etc.), and labels contain only plain uppercase text without any markdown formatting inside the quotes.

## 4.5 Sensor Fusion Flow (Critical Subsystem)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Accelerometer High Frequency Noise] --> FUS[EKF Fusion]
    S2[Gyroscope High Frequency Accurate] --> FUS
    S3[GPS Low Frequency Drift Free] --> FUS
    S4[Barometer Low Frequency Altitude] --> FUS
    FUS --> OUT1[Estimated Position x y z]
    FUS --> OUT2[Estimated Velocity u v w]
    FUS --> OUT3[Estimated Orientation Roll Pitch Yaw]
    FUS --> OUT4[Estimated Angular Rate p q r]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an aerial robot. List any four classifications of aerial robots based on lift mechanism.**

**Model Answer:**

An **aerial robot** is an autonomous, unmanned airborne mechatronic system capable of controlled sustained flight, integrating perception, planning, and actuation subsystems to perform mission objectives without an onboard human pilot.

**Four classifications:**
1. **Fixed-Wing UAV** — generates lift via forward airspeed over fixed wings.
2. **Single-Rotor Helicopter** — uses one main rotor plus tail rotor for lift and yaw control.
3. **Multi-Rotor UAV** (quadcopter / hexacopter / octocopter) — generates lift using multiple fixed-pitch rotors.
4. **Hybrid VTOL** — combines vertical take-off with fixed-wing cruise for endurance.

*(Word count ~ 60; ideal for 3-mark answer)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the hover condition for a quadrotor. If the total mass of the quadrotor is 2 kg, find the thrust produced by each motor during hover.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hover Condition:** A quadrotor is in hover when the total thrust equals its weight, i.e.:

$$T_{\text{total}} = m \cdot g$$

For a symmetric quadrotor with four identical motors:

$$T_i = \frac{m \cdot g}{4}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given m = 2 kg, g = 9.81 m/s²:

$$T_i = \frac{2 \times 9.81}{4} = \frac{19.62}{4} = 4.905 \text{ N}$$

**∴ Each motor produces 4.905 N during hover.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an aerial robot. Discuss the role of IMU, GPS, and flight controller in autonomous flight.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **hover power equation** P = T^(3/2) / √(2ρA) using the Actuator-Disk Theory (Momentum Theory). List two engineering implications of this result.

---

### Model Answer for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Functional Architecture

Aerial robot architecture is divided into five subsystems:

1. **Perception Subsystem:** IMU (3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope), GPS receiver, magnetometer, barometer, vision/optical-flow sensors.
2. **Computation Subsystem:** Onboard flight controller (e.g., Pixhawk 4, Raspberry Pi 4) running PX4 or ArduPilot firmware.
3. **Communication Subsystem:** 2.4 GHz radio telemetry to ground control station (GCS).
4. **Actuation Subsystem:** Electronic Speed Controllers (ESCs) → Brushless DC motors → Propellers.
5. **Power Subsystem:** LiPo battery + Power Distribution Board (PDB) + Battery Eliminator Circuit (BEC).

**Roles:**
- **IMU:** Provides high-frequency (≥ 200 Hz) angular rate and linear acceleration; essential for attitude stabilization.
- **GPS:** Provides drift-free global position at low frequency (1–10 Hz); corrects IMU drift over time.
- **Flight Controller:** Runs sensor fusion (EKF), state estimation, trajectory tracking, and control loops.

#### Part (b) — Hover Power Derivation

Refer to the **full derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes.

**Summary of derivation:**
1. Mass flow rate: ṁ = ρ A v_i
2. Thrust from momentum balance: T = 2 ρ A v_i²
3. Solve for v_i: v_i = √(T / 2 ρ A)
4. Power: P = T · v_i = T^(3/2) / √(2 ρ A)

**Engineering Implications:**
1. **T^(3/2) scaling** → doubling payload power increases by 2.83×, hence small UAVs are very inefficient.
2. **Larger rotor area** reduces hover power; this drives the design of high-efficiency large-diameter propellers for heavy-lift applications.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **6-DOF rigid body model** of a quadrotor. Why is a quadrotor called an *under-actuated* system?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A quadrotor of mass 1.5 kg has four identical rotors each of radius 0.127 m (5-inch propeller). During hover, compute the **(i)** total thrust, **(ii)** thrust per motor, **(iii)** induced velocity (take ρ = 1.225 kg/m³), and **(iv)** ideal hover power.

---

### Model Answer for Question B:

#### Part (a) — 6-DOF Model

A quadrotor is a **rigid body with 6 Degrees of Freedom**:
- 3 translational: x, y, z (position in inertial frame)
- 3 rotational: φ (roll), θ (pitch), ψ (yaw)

The state vector is:

$$\mathbf{X} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi, \dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{z}, p, q, r]^T$$

**Under-actuation reasoning:** A quadrotor has **4 actuators** (rotor speeds) but **6 DOF**. Since 4 < 6, the system is **under-actuated**: yaw, x-translation, and y-translation cannot be commanded directly — they emerge only through tilt-induced coupling.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** m = 1.5 kg, R = 0.127 m, n = 4, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².

**Disk area:**
$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times (0.127)^2 = 0.0507 \text{ m}^2$$

**(i) Total thrust:**
$$T_{\text{total}} = m \cdot g = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Thrust per motor:**
$$T_i = \frac{14.715}{4} = 3.679 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Induced velocity:**
$$v_i = \sqrt{\frac{T_{\text{total}}}{2 \rho A}} = \sqrt{\frac{14.715}{2 \times 1.225 \times 0.0507}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
v_i &= \sqrt{\frac{14.715}{0.1242}} \\
&= \sqrt{118.5} \\
&= 10.89 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Ideal hover power:**
$$P = T \cdot v_i = 14.715 \times 10.89 = 160.2 \text{ W}$$

**Verification using compact formula:**
$$P = \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}} = \frac{(14.715)^{1.5}}{\sqrt{0.1242}} = \frac{56.49}{0.3524} \approx 160.3 \text{ W} \;\; ✓$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks in PEMET756:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write N, m/s, W, rad/s. A derivation without units loses **at least 1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing body frame vs inertial frame** — Be explicit: "v in body frame" or "p̈ in inertial frame."
> 3. **Skipping assumptions** — Always state *incompressible flow*, *steady-state*, *symmetric motor configuration*.
> 4. **Incorrect Euler angle order** — ZYX (yaw → pitch → roll) is the KTU expected convention; reversing it gives wrong rotation matrix.
> 5. **Writing thrust without splitting among motors** — A 14-mark question on a quadrotor *requires* showing T_i = mg/4.
> 6. **Missing the hover condition derivation link** — When asked to "show that," explicitly derive T = mg, not just state it.
> 7. **Mixing up RPM and angular velocity** — ω (rad/s) = RPM × 2π / 60. Examiners penalize if confused.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Aerial Robots":**

### 🔹 Core Concepts
- ✅ Aerial robot = autonomous UAV with 6-DOF rigid body dynamics.
- ✅ Classification: Fixed-wing, Rotary-wing (single/multi), Hybrid VTOL, Flapping-wing.
- ✅ Under-actuation: 4 actuators (quadrotor) < 6 DOF.
- ✅ Hover condition: T_total = m·g; per motor T_i = mg/4.

### 🔹 Must-Know Equations
- ✅ Thrust from rotor: T = C_T ρ A (ωR)²
- ✅ Induced velocity: v_i = √(T / 2ρA)
- ✅ Hover power: P = T^(3/2) / √(2ρA) — the celebrated T^(3/2) law.
- ✅ Lift/Drag: L = ½ρv²SC_L, D = ½ρv²SC_D.
- ✅ ZYX Euler rotation matrix R(Θ) — derive on demand.

### 🔹 Subsystems
- ✅ Perception: IMU + GPS + Barometer + Magnetometer + Vision.
- ✅ Computation: Flight controller (PX4/ArduPilot), state estimator (EKF).
- ✅ Actuation: ESC → BLDC motor → Propeller.
- ✅ Communication: 2.4 GHz / 5.8 GHz telemetry, RC transmitter.
- ✅ Power: LiPo battery (3S/4S/6S), BEC, PDB.

### 🔹 Control Methods
- ✅ PID (most common, lab-friendly).
- ✅ LQR (optimal state feedback, exam favorite).
- ✅ Backstepping (nonlinear control, advanced).
- ✅ MPC (modern, constraint-aware).

### 🔹 Common Numerical Values (Memorize)
- ✅ ρ_sea = 1.225 kg/m³
- ✅ g = 9.81 m/s²
- ✅ Standard propeller sizes: 5″, 8″, 10″, 13″, 15″
- ✅ Typical quadrotor arm length: 0.225 m (F450 frame)
- ✅ Typical hover thrust per motor (1 kg AUW): ~ 2.5 N

### 🔹 KTU Exam Strategy
- ✅ For 3-mark questions: definition + 2 supporting points.
- ✅ For 14-mark questions: structured answer with sub-headings (a) and (b) clearly labeled.
- ✅ Always draw a diagram or table in questions worth ≥ 7 marks.
- ✅ For numerical problems, write **Given → Formula → Substitution → Answer** with units.

topics.title: Underwater Robots and Surface Water Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Underwater Robots and Surface Water Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Underwater Robot?

An **Underwater Robot** (also termed a *marine robot* or *subsea vehicle*) is an autonomous or tethered mechanical platform engineered to operate beneath the water surface to perform inspection, manipulation, data acquisition, or intervention tasks in submerged environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A mobile robot operating in a fluid medium (water), designed to perform tasks such as oceanographic data collection, pipeline inspection, search and rescue, and seabed mapping, while overcoming challenges like high pressure, low visibility, communication limitations, and 3D navigation in a buoyancy-affected medium.

**Formal Classification (KTU Terminology):**
- **ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle):** Tethered, human-controlled from a surface vessel.
- **AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle):** Untethered, self-powered, mission-programmed.
- **Hybrid AUV/ROV:** Switches between autonomous and tethered modes.
- **Gliders:** Use buoyancy-driven wings for energy-efficient long-range profiling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Physical Challenge:** Water is **≈ 800 times denser than air** and exerts **1 atm of pressure per 10 m** of depth. The operating envelope is therefore governed by hydrostatic pressure, buoyancy, and viscous drag — not aerodynamics.

---

### 1.2 What is a Surface Water Robot?

A **Surface Water Robot** (commonly known as a **USV — Unmanned Surface Vehicle**, or **ASV — Autonomous Surface Vehicle**) is a watercraft that operates on the water's surface with minimal or no human crew aboard, used for hydrographic surveying, environmental monitoring, surface patrol, and communication relay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** An unmanned marine vessel navigating the air–water interface, propelled by hull-mounted thrusters, sails, or waterjets, equipped with GPS, radar, lidar, and IMUs to execute autonomous or remotely-piloted missions across rivers, lakes, and oceans.

**Formal Classification:**
- **Autonomous USV:** Self-navigating with onboard AI.
- **Remotely Operated USV:** Pilot-on-the-loop via radio link.
- **Hybrid Surface-Submersible:** Capable of brief dives.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Why" Made Simple

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Underwater Robot = "Submarine Drone"**
> Imagine a small **remote-controlled submarine toy** that you send into a swimming pool. It must:
> (1) **Not sink** → needs **buoyancy control** (like a floatie),
> (2) **Move forward/backward/up/down** → uses **thrusters (propellers)** in all 3 axes,
> (3) **Know where it is** → since GPS doesn't work underwater, uses **sonar** (sound) like a bat uses echolocation,
> (4) **Resist crushing pressure** → has a **pressure hull** (like a deep-sea diver's bathysphere).
> AUV = "fire-and-forget" autonomous submarine. ROV = "tethered" submarine attached by a cable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Surface Water Robot = "Smart Boat Drone"**
> Think of a **robot kayak** that sails itself. Unlike underwater robots, it sits ON the water, so:
> (1) **GPS works** (uses satellites overhead) for navigation,
> (2) **Wind and waves** are its biggest enemies — needs roll/pitch stabilizers,
> (3) **Solar panels or diesel** can power it for long missions,
> (4) It acts as a **"mothership"** that deploys underwater robots and aerial drones for a 3-layer survey (air + surface + underwater).

---

### 1.4 Standard Physical & Engineering Constants

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sea-water density | $\rho$ | **1025** | kg/m³ |
| Fresh-water density | $\rho_f$ | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Hydrostatic pressure (depth d) | $P$ | $P_0 + \rho g d$ | Pa |
| Speed of sound in seawater | $c$ | **≈ 1500** | m/s |
| Kinematic viscosity (20 °C) | $\nu$ | **≈ 1.0 × 10⁻⁶** | m²/s |
| Standard atmosphere | $P_0$ | **101 325** | Pa |

---

### 1.5 Why Study This in Mobile Robotics (KTU Context)?

- **3D Mobility:** Underwater = full 6-DOF (surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw) → richest dynamics in mobile robotics.
- **Non-Holonomic Constraints in Water:** Same as ground robots but with **3-D added mass** from fluid.
- **Sensor Fusion Challenge:** GPS-denied (underwater) → forces use of DVL, INS, sonar.
- **Real Kerala/India Relevance:** Pipeline inspection (Kochi-Mangalore), backwater mapping, coastal surveillance, marine biology (CMLRE, NIOT projects).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underwater Robot Architecture (Layered Analysis)

**Layer 1 — Mechanical Hull & Frame**
- **Pressure Hull:** Spherical (uniform pressure) or cylindrical (cost-effective).
- **Fairings:** Streamlined shapes to reduce form drag.
- **Thruster Configuration:** Typically 4–8 thrusters arranged for **4-DOF (surge, sway, heave, yaw)** control; full 6-DOF requires 5+ thrusters.

**Layer 2 — Actuation Subsystem**
- **Propeller Thrusters:** Brushless DC motors with Kort nozzles for efficiency.
- **Waterjet Thrusters:** For high-speed surface vehicles.
- **Buoyancy Engine:** Pumps oil in/out of an external bladder to control vertical position (used in gliders).
- **Control Surfaces:** Rudders and stern planes for AUV heading/depth.

**Layer 3 — Sensing Subsystem**

| Sensor | Purpose | Medium | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Doppler Velocity Log (DVL)** | Measures bottom-relative velocity | Acoustic | Range ≈ 200 m, needs bottom lock |
| **Pressure Sensor** | Depth measurement | Strain gauge | Drift over time |
| **Inertial Navigation System (INS/IMU)** | Attitude, angular rate | MEMS/Ring Laser | Drift ≈ 1 nm/h |
| **Side-Scan Sonar** | Seabed imaging | Acoustic | Low resolution |
| **Forward-Looking Sonar** | Obstacle avoidance | Acoustic | Narrow FOV |
| **CTD Sensor** | Conductivity, Temperature, Depth | Direct | Slow update |
| **Magnetometer** | Heading reference | Earth's field | Magnetic deviation |
| **Camera** | Visual inspection | Light | Only at close range, turbidity limits |
| **USBL/LBL** | Acoustic positioning | Acoustic | Required for AUV navigation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The sensor stack of an underwater robot is **GPS-free**. This is a fundamental difference from ground and aerial robots and is a frequent 14-mark exam topic.

**Layer 4 — Communication Subsystem**
- **Acoustic Modem:** Slow (≈ 100 bps – 10 kbps), high latency.
- **Fiber Optic Tether (ROV only):** High bandwidth but limits mobility.
- **Optical Modem:** High bandwidth, requires clear water and short range.

**Layer 5 — Energy Subsystem**
- **Lithium-ion battery packs** (typical AUV endurance: 8–24 h).
- **Aluminum pressure-compensated housings** for battery.
- **Surface recharging** via USV mother-ship.

---

### 2.2 Surface Water Robot (USV) Architecture

- **Hull Types:** Monohull (fast), Catamaran (stable, low draft), Trimaran (heavy payload).
- **Propulsion:** Outboard motors, inboard waterjets, sail-assist (energy-efficient).
- **Navigation Sensors:** GPS (primary), IMU, radar, lidar, camera, AIS (Automatic Identification System).
- **Communication:** Radio (VHF/UHF), satellite (Iridium), 4G/5G coastal.
- **Stabilization:** Active fins (heave compensators), gyroscopic stabilizers.

---

### 2.3 Hydrodynamic Forces — The Physics Foundation

An underwater vehicle experiences the following forces and moments in a fluid medium:

**(a) Gravity & Buoyancy (Restoring Forces)**
- **Gravity:** $W = m g$ acting downward at center of gravity (CG).
- **Buoyancy:** $B = \rho V g$ acting upward at center of buoyancy (CB).
- For equilibrium: $W = B$ (i.e., $m = \rho V$). If $W > B$ → negative buoyancy (sinks); if $W < B$ → positive buoyancy (rises).

**(b) Hydrodynamic Drag**
- **Linear (viscous) Drag:** $F_D = C_d \, A \, \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$
- At low Reynolds numbers (laminar, small AUVs): $F_D = 6 \pi \mu r v$ (Stokes' drag).

**(c) Added Mass Force (Inertia of Displaced Fluid)**
- The vehicle must accelerate **a volume of surrounding water** → adds an **added mass tensor** $M_A$.
- $F_A = -M_A \dot{v}$ — opposes acceleration.

**(d) Lift Force**
- Generated by control surfaces or hull asymmetry: $F_L = C_L \, A \, \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$.

**(e) Propeller Thrust**
- $T = \rho n^2 D^4 K_T$, where $K_T$ = thrust coefficient, $n$ = rev/s, $D$ = propeller diameter.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Every equation below is high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the variable meanings and units.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B = \rho \, V \, g$ | Buoyancy force | N |
| 2 | $W = m \, g$ | Weight | N |
| 3 | $P = P_0 + \rho g d$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $d$ | Pa |
| 4 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v \vert v \vert$ | Quadratic drag (vector form) | N |
| 5 | $F_D = 6 \pi \mu r v$ | Stokes' drag (laminar, sphere) | N |
| 6 | $M_A = k_m \, \rho \, \nabla$ | Added mass (sphere: $k_m = 0.5$) | kg |
| 7 | $T = \rho n^2 D^4 K_T$ | Propeller thrust | N |
| 8 | $L = D \cdot (J)^2$ | Lift on a foil (foil theory) | N |
| 9 | $C_d = \dfrac{2 F_D}{\rho A v^2}$ | Drag coefficient (definition) | — |
| 10 | $Re = \dfrac{v L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | — |
| 11 | $Fn = \dfrac{v}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | Froude number (surface ships) | — |
| 12 | $\tau_{total} = M_{RB}\dot{\nu} + C_{RB}(\nu)\nu + M_A\dot{\nu}_r + C_A(\nu_r)\nu_r + D(\nu_r)\nu_r + g(\eta)$ | 6-DOF Fossen equation | N, N·m |
| 13 | $\nu_r = \nu - \nu_c$ | Relative velocity (vehicle minus current) | m/s |
| 14 | $SL = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | Sonar source level (dB re 1 μPa @ 1 m) | dB |
| 15 | $PL = SL - 2 \alpha R + 10\log_{10}(R \cdot \alpha)$ | Passive sonar equation (simplified) | dB |

> **Notation:** $\nu$ = body-fixed linear/angular velocity, $\eta$ = earth-fixed position/orientation, $\nu_c$ = current velocity, $M_{RB}$ = rigid-body mass matrix, $C(\cdot)$ = Coriolis-centripetal matrix, $D(\cdot)$ = damping matrix, $g(\eta)$ = restoring force vector.

---

### 2.5 6-DOF Equations of Motion (Fossen Framework — KTU Core)

The general motion of an underwater vehicle in 6 DOF is given by:

$$
M\,\dot{\nu} + C(\nu)\,\nu + D(\nu)\,\nu + g(\eta) = \tau + \tau_{dist}
$$

where:
- $M = M_{RB} + M_A$ — total mass (rigid body + added mass),
- $C(\nu) = C_{RB}(\nu) + C_A(\nu)$ — Coriolis/centripetal terms,
- $D(\nu)$ — hydrodynamic damping (linear + quadratic),
- $g(\eta)$ — gravity + buoyancy restoring,
- $\tau$ — control forces and moments,
- $\tau_{dist}$ — environmental disturbances (currents, waves).

The **6 velocities** $\nu = [u, v, w, p, q, r]^T$:
- $u$ = surge (forward), $v$ = sway (sideways), $w$ = heave (vertical),
- $p$ = roll rate, $q$ = pitch rate, $r$ = yaw rate.

The **6 positions/orientations** $\eta = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$:
- $(x, y, z)$ = position, $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ = roll, pitch, yaw (Euler angles).

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore Oil & Gas:** ROVs inspect subsea Christmas trees at 3000 m depth.
- **Defense:** USVs patrol harbors (e.g., Indian Navy's fast interceptor craft).
- **Ocean Science:** AUVs map deep-sea hydrothermal vents (WHOI, GEOMAR).
- **Kerala Context:** CMFRI uses underwater ROVs for coral reef surveys in Lakshadweep; backwater USVs for weed mapping in Vembanad Lake.
- **Search & Rescue:** AUVs locate black boxes via acoustic pinger triangulation.
- **Pipeline Inspection:** Magderm AUVs track buried pipelines with magnetic sensors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Buoyancy & Stability Condition

**Step 1 — Archimedes' Principle Statement**
> A body fully or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Formulation**
Let $V$ be the displaced fluid volume (m³), $\rho$ the fluid density (kg/m³), $g$ gravitational acceleration.

The mass of displaced fluid is $m_f = \rho V$, hence weight $W_f = m_f g = \rho V g$.

By Archimedes, the upward buoyancy is:

$$
B = \rho \, V \, g
$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition**
For the vehicle to hover at a fixed depth, the downward weight must equal the upward buoyancy:

$$
W = B \implies m g = \rho V g \implies m = \rho V
$$

**Step 4 — Static Stability**
A submerged body is statically stable if its **metacenter M** lies above the **center of gravity G**, i.e., the metacentric height is positive:

$$
\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG} > 0
$$

where:
- $\overline{KB}$ = distance from keel K to center of buoyancy B,
- $\overline{BM} = I / \nabla$ = moment of inertia / displaced volume (transverse stability),
- $\overline{KG}$ = distance from keel K to center of gravity G.

> **Engineering Interpretation:** For an AUV to remain upright, designers shift the **CG low** (heavy ballast at bottom) and the **CB high** (light foam at top), making $\overline{GM} > 0$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Drag Force on a Submerged Body

**Step 1 — Dimensional Analysis**
Drag force $F_D$ must depend on: fluid density $\rho$, velocity $v$, characteristic length $L$, and fluid viscosity $\mu$. By the Buckingham Pi theorem, two dimensionless groups emerge:

$$
C_d = \frac{F_D}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 A} \qquad \text{and} \qquad Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}
$$

**Step 2 — Quadratic Drag Regime (Turbulent, Typical AUV)**
For Reynolds number $Re > 10^3$, inertial effects dominate and drag is quadratic in velocity:

$$
F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_d \, A \, v^2
$$

The drag coefficient $C_d$ is determined empirically (sphere ≈ 0.47, streamlined torpedo ≈ 0.04).

**Step 3 — Vector Form (Sign Convention)**
To preserve direction opposite to velocity:

$$
\vec{F}_D = -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,|\vec v|\,\vec v
$$

**Step 4 — Stokes' Drag (Low-Re, Micro AUV)**
For very small / slow vehicles where $Re < 1$, viscous forces dominate:

$$
F_D = 6 \pi \mu r v
$$

**Step 5 — Why This Matters in Practice**
- An AUV cruising at 1.5 m/s in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³) with $A = 0.05$ m² and $C_d = 0.3$:

$$
F_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.3 \times 0.05 \times (1.5)^2
$$

Compute the squares and products:

$$
F_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.3 \times 0.05 \times 2.25
$$

Multiply stepwise:

$$
0.5 \times 1025 = 512.5
$$

$$
512.5 \times 0.3 = 153.75
$$

$$
153.75 \times 0.05 = 7.6875
$$

$$
7.6875 \times 2.25 = 17.30
$$

$$
\boxed{F_D \approx 17.3 \text{ N}}
$$

This drag must be overcome by the thrusters.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Added Mass Effect

**Step 1 — Physical Concept**
When a body accelerates in a fluid, it must also accelerate a portion of the surrounding fluid. This effectively increases the body's inertia.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Definition**
For a sphere of radius $r$ accelerating with $a$ in an inviscid fluid:

$$
F_{added} = -m_a \, a \qquad \text{with} \qquad m_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{sphere} = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi \rho r^3
$$

**Step 3 — Total Effective Inertia**
$$
m_{eff} = m_{body} + m_a
$$

For a sphere with $m_{body} = 1$ kg, $r = 0.1$ m, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³:

$$
m_a = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi \times 1025 \times (0.1)^3 = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi \times 1025 \times 0.001
$$

$$
= \tfrac{2}{3} \times 3.221 \approx 2.147 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{eff} = 1 + 2.147 = 3.147 \text{ kg}}
$$

The sphere effectively behaves as if it has more than 3× its physical mass when accelerating — explains why underwater vehicles are sluggish to accelerate.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: 6-DOF Surge Equation (Fossen)

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law in Body Frame**

$$
m (\dot{u} - v r + w q) = X
$$

where $X$ = sum of external forces in surge direction.

**Step 2 — Substitute External Forces**
External forces include: thrust $X_T$, hydrodynamic drag $X_{HD}$, added mass force $X_A$, and gravity/buoyancy projection $X_G$:

$$
X = X_T + X_{HD} + X_A + X_G
$$

**Step 3 — Write Added Mass and Damping Terms**

$$
X_A = -X_{\dot u}\,\dot u, \qquad X_{HD} = -X_u\, u - X_{u\vert u\vert}\, u\vert u\vert
$$

where $X_{\dot u}$ = added mass in surge, $X_u$ = linear damping, $X_{u|u|}$ = quadratic damping.

**Step 4 — Final Surge Equation**

$$
(m + X_{\dot u})\,\dot u + X_u\,u + X_{u\vert u\vert}\,u\vert u\vert = X_T + X_G
$$

This is the **standard form** expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

**Step 5 — Analogous Equations for Other DOF**
By symmetry of reasoning, similar equations hold for sway ($v$), heave ($w$), roll ($p$), pitch ($q$), and yaw ($r$). Together they form a **coupled 6×6 nonlinear system**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Sonar Range Equation (Active Sonar)

**Step 1 — Components**
- **SL** = Source Level (dB re 1 μPa @ 1 m)
- **NL** = Noise Level
- **DI** = Directivity Index (dB)
- **DT** = Detection Threshold (dB)
- **TL** = Transmission Loss = $20 \log_{10}(R) + \alpha R$ (spreading + absorption)

**Step 2 — Sonar Equation (Active)**
The received signal must exceed noise by the detection threshold:

$$
SL - TL + TS - NL + DI \geq DT
$$

where $TS$ = Target Strength.

**Step 3 — Solving for Maximum Range**
Set equality and substitute $TL$:

$$
SL + TS - NL + DI - DT = 20\log_{10}(R) + \alpha R
$$

For shallow range ($\alpha R \ll 20\log_{10}(R)$):

$$
R_{max} \approx 10^{(SL + TS - NL + DI - DT)/20}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**
Let $SL = 200$ dB, $TS = -20$ dB, $NL = 70$ dB, $DI = 20$ dB, $DT = 10$ dB:

$$
Numerator = 200 + (-20) - 70 + 20 - 10 = 120 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
R_{max} \approx 10^{120/20} = 10^6 = 1\,000\,000 \text{ m (ideal, no absorption)}
$$

With realistic $\alpha = 0.04$ dB/m at 100 kHz in seawater, the absorption term $\alpha R$ becomes significant beyond a few hundred metres.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Underwater vs Surface Robots

| Parameter | Underwater Robot (AUV/ROV) | Surface Water Robot (USV/ASV) |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating medium** | Fully submerged | Air–water interface |
| **Dimensionality** | 6-DOF | Mostly 3-DOF (surge, sway, yaw) |
| **GPS availability** | ❌ No (underwater) | ✅ Yes |
| **Primary navigation** | INS + DVL + USBL | GPS + IMU + radar |
| **Dominant disturbance** | Ocean currents | Wind, waves |
| **Communication** | Acoustic (slow) | Radio / satellite (fast) |
| **Energy budget** | Very tight (battery) | Moderate (solar/diesel available) |
| **Pressure challenge** | High (1 atm/10 m) | Atmospheric only |
| **Inspection capability** | Internal (pipes, hulls) | External surface only |
| **Visibility sensors** | Sonar, acoustic cameras | Optical cameras, lidar |
| **KTU-class example** | REMUS 600, Bluefin-21 | Saildrone, Wave Glider |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Underwater Robot (AUV) System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Power Supply: Li-ion Battery Pack] --> A2[Power Management and DC Bus]
    A2 --> A3[Thruster Motors BLDC x4 to x6]
    A2 --> A4[Onboard Computer CPU FPGA]
    A2 --> A5[Sensor Power Conditioning]

    A5 --> B1[Pressure Sensor Depth]
    A5 --> B2[IMU 9 axis Gyro Accel Mag]
    A5 --> B3[DVL Bottom Velocity]
    A5 --> B4[CTD Conductivity Temp Depth]
    A5 --> B5[Side Scan Sonar Imaging]
    A5 --> B6[Forward Sonar Obstacle]
    A5 --> B7[USBL Acoustic Position]

    A4 --> C1[Navigation Filter EKF]
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B7 --> C1

    C1 --> C2[Mission Planner State Machine]
    C2 --> C3[Guidance Law Line Tracking Waypoint]
    C3 --> C4[Controller PID or Sliding Mode]
    C4 --> C5[Thruster Allocation Matrix]
    C5 --> A3

    A4 --> D1[Acoustic Modem Comms]
    D1 --> D2[Surface Buoy or USV Mothership]

    A4 --> E1[Black Box Data Logger]
    E1 --> E2[Recovery and Download]

    style A1 fill:#cce5ff
    style A4 fill:#ffe6cc
    style C1 fill:#d5e8d4
    style C4 fill:#d5e8d4
    style A3 fill:#f8cecc
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**
- **Power chain (blue):** Battery → bus → all subsystems.
- **Sensors (orange inputs):** feed the **Navigation Filter (EKF)**.
- **Control loop (green):** Navigation → Mission → Guidance → Controller → Thruster allocation → Actuation → back to vehicle motion → new sensor readings.
- **Comms (yellow):** Acoustic link to surface mothership.
- **Data (purple):** Black-box logging for post-mission analysis.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Surface Water Robot (USV) System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Solar Panels or Diesel Generator] --> P2[Battery Bank Li-ion]
    P2 --> P3[24V DC Distribution]

    P3 --> M1[Port Thruster BLDC]
    P3 --> M2[Starboard Thruster BLDC]
    P3 --> M3[Bow Waterjet Optional]
    P3 --> CU[Navigation Computer NUC]

    S1[GNSS RTK GPS] --> CU
    S2[IMU 9 axis] --> CU
    S3[Radar Marine] --> CU
    S4[Lidar 3D] --> CU
    S5[Stereo Camera] --> CU
    S6[AIS Receiver] --> CU
    S7[Weather Station Anemometer] --> CU

    CU --> NAV[State Estimator EKF UKF]
    NAV --> G[Guidance Path Follower LOS]
    G --> CTL[Controller Heading Speed]
    CTL --> AL[Allocation]
    AL --> M1
    AL --> M2
    AL --> M3

    CU --> COM1[VHF Radio]
    CU --> COM2[Iridium Satellite]
    CU --> COM3[4G 5G Coastal]
    CU --> GS[Ground Control Station]

    CU --> PAY[Payload Sonar ADCP Multisampler]
    PAY --> M4[Deployable AUV or ROV]

    style P1 fill:#fff2cc
    style P3 fill:#fff2cc
    style CU fill:#dae8fc
    style NAV fill:#d5e8d4
    style G fill:#d5e8d4
    style CTL fill:#d5e8d4
    style S1 fill:#f8cecc
    style S4 fill:#f8cecc
    style COM2 fill:#e1d5e7
    style M4 fill:#e1d5e7
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Power (yellow):** Rechargeable source critical for long-endurance missions (Saildrone lasts 12 months).
- **Sensor stack (red):** GPS-rich, unlike AUV.
- **Control loop (green):** Classical line-of-sight (LOS) guidance used for path following.
- **Comms (purple):** Multi-mode — radio, satellite, cellular — enables real-time telemetry.
- **Payload (purple, bottom):** The USV often acts as a **mothership** for sub-drones.

---

### 4.3 Comparison Flowchart — Choosing the Right Marine Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Mission depth requirement?} -->|Less than 1 m| R1[USV with extendable probe]
    Q1 -->|1 to 300 m| R2[AUV or ROV]
    Q1 -->|More than 300 m| R3[Deep rated ROV or AUV]

    Q2{GPS available?} -->|Yes open water| R4[USV preferred]
    Q2 -->|No confined or GPS denied| R5[Underwater robot with INS DVL]

    Q3{Real time high bandwidth video?} -->|Yes| R6[ROV with fiber tether]
    Q3 -->|No large area survey| R7[AUV with side scan sonar]

    Q4{Mission duration?} -->|Hours| R8[AUV battery]
    Q4 -->|Days to months| R9[USV with solar sail]
```

---

### 4.4 Sensor–Subsystem Mapping Table

| Subsystem | Underwater Robot | Surface Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Localization** | DVL + INS + USBL/LBL | GPS + IMU + AIS |
| **Perception** | Sonar (acoustic) | Camera + Lidar + Radar |
| **Depth control** | Pressure sensor + buoyancy engine | Not applicable |
| **Heading control** | Magnetic compass + rate gyro | Magnetic/GPS compass + rate gyro |
| **Obstacle detection** | Forward-looking sonar | Radar / Lidar |
| **Environment** | CTD, fluorometer, turbidity | Anemometer, wave sensor |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** 
**Differentiate between an AUV and an ROV. List any two applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle) | ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle) |
|---|---|---|
| Control | Autonomous, onboard computer | Remotely piloted via tether |
| Power | Onboard battery | Surface-supplied through tether |
| Communication | Acoustic modem (low bandwidth) | Fiber optic / copper tether (high bandwidth) |
| Mobility | Unlimited (battery-bound) | Limited by tether length |
| Cost | High initial, low operation | Lower initial, high ship cost |

**AUV Applications:** (1) Large-area seabed mapping, (2) Long-duration oceanographic surveys.
**ROV Applications:** (1) Real-time subsea inspection of oil rigs, (2) Search & recovery of black boxes.
*(Award 1 mark for clear AUV/ROV distinction, 1 mark for AUV apps, 1 mark for ROV apps.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Why is GPS not used for underwater navigation? What alternative is used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
GPS uses electromagnetic waves in the **L-band (≈ 1.5 GHz)**. These waves are absorbed within **fractions of a metre** in seawater due to high electrical conductivity (~4 S/m). Therefore, GPS signals cannot penetrate underwater.

**Alternative navigation methods for underwater robots:**
1. **Inertial Navigation System (INS)** — uses IMU with accelerometer and gyroscope integration; drifts over time.
2. **Doppler Velocity Log (DVL)** — measures bottom-relative velocity using acoustic beams.
3. **USBL / LBL acoustic positioning** — triangulation using surface-deployed acoustic beacons.
4. **Geophysical navigation** — terrain-aided matching with pre-recorded seabed maps.

**A typical solution fuses INS + DVL using an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** to bound INS drift to <0.1% of distance travelled.
*(Award 1 mark for stating why GPS fails in water, 1 mark for listing 2 alternatives, 1 mark for the EKF fusion explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV)**. Discuss the role of each subsystem in achieving autonomous operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **surge equation of motion** for an AUV, including added mass, hydrodynamic damping, and thrust. State clearly all assumptions and the physical meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to the AUV architecture diagram in Section 4.1. The block diagram must contain the following subsystems clearly labeled:
1. **Power subsystem** (battery + DC bus)
2. **Actuation subsystem** (thrusters)
3. **Sensing subsystem** (DVL, IMU, pressure sensor, sonar)
4. **Computing / Mission subsystem** (navigation, guidance, control)
5. **Communication subsystem** (acoustic modem)
6. **Mechanical / Hull subsystem** (pressure hull, fairings)

**Subsystem Roles (4 marks):**
- **Power:** Provides regulated DC to all electronics and thrusters; must be pressure-tolerant.
- **Actuation:** Thrusters generate forces/moments in 6-DOF; arranged to decouple surge, sway, heave, yaw.
- **Sensing:** Sensors feed raw data to the navigation filter; e.g., DVL gives velocity, pressure gives depth, IMU gives attitude.
- **Computing:** Hosts the **Sense → Think → Act** loop:
  - *Sense* = sensor fusion (EKF),
  - *Think* = mission planner + guidance law,
  - *Act* = controller + thruster allocation.
- **Communication:** Acoustic modem relays status to surface and accepts mission updates.
- **Mechanical:** Pressure hull protects electronics; fairings reduce drag.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Vehicle is **rigid** (no flexible deformation).
2. **Earth is non-rotating** and **inertial frame** is fixed.
3. Fluid is **incompressible, Newtonian**.
4. **Heave-pitch** and **roll-yaw** couplings neglected (typical for torpedo-shaped AUVs).

**Free Body in Surge Direction (2 marks):**

Newton's 2nd law along body x-axis:

$$
m (\dot{u} - v r + w q) = X_{thr} + X_{drag} + X_{added}
$$

where the term $(-vr + wq)$ accounts for Coriolis coupling.

**Substituting Components (2 marks):**
- Added mass: $X_{added} = -X_{\dot u}\,\dot u$
- Hydrodynamic drag (linear + quadratic): $X_{drag} = -X_u\,u - X_{u|u|}\,u|u|$

**Final Equation (2 marks):**

$$
(m + X_{\dot u})\,\dot u + X_u\,u + X_{u|u|}\,u\,|u| = X_{thr}
$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- $(m + X_{\dot u})\,\dot u$ — total inertia (vehicle + entrained water) resisting acceleration.
- $X_u\,u$ — linear viscous drag (dominant at low speeds, $Re < 10^5$).
- $X_{u|u|}\,u|u|$ — quadratic (pressure) drag, dominant at high speeds.
- $X_{thr}$ — control input from thruster.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Describe the major **hydrodynamic forces and moments** acting on an underwater vehicle. Explain the concept of **added mass** and its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a diagram the architecture of an **Unmanned Surface Vehicle (USV)**. Discuss how **GPS and IMU sensor fusion** enables autonomous navigation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**List of Major Hydrodynamic Forces (3 marks):**
1. **Buoyancy** ($B = \rho V g$) — vertical, upward, at center of buoyancy.
2. **Weight** ($W = m g$) — vertical, downward, at center of gravity.
3. **Hydrodynamic drag** ($F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$) — opposite to relative velocity.
4. **Added mass force** ($F_A = -M_A \dot v$) — opposes acceleration.
5. **Lift force** ($F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_L A v^2$) — perpendicular to flow.
6. **Propeller thrust** ($T = \rho n^2 D^4 K_T$).
7. **Wave / current forces** ($F_{dist}$).

**Added Mass Concept (4 marks):**

> **Physical Picture:** When a body accelerates in water, it must push aside a parcel of fluid. The fluid parcel itself accelerates, effectively adding inertia to the system.

**Mathematical Definition:** Added mass $m_a$ is defined such that the force to accelerate the body at $\dot v$ in a fluid equals:

$$
F = (m_{body} + m_a)\,\dot v
$$

For a sphere of radius $r$ in inviscid fluid (potential flow theory):

$$
m_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi \rho r^3
$$

**Physical Significance:**
- A neutrally buoyant sphere with mass 1 kg behaves as if it has mass ≈ 3.15 kg when accelerated underwater.
- The added mass is **direction-dependent** → described by a **6×6 added mass tensor** $M_A$.
- It couples the 6 DOF (e.g., surge acceleration creates sway force).
- Neglecting added mass causes **under-damped simulations** and unstable controllers.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**USV Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to Section 4.2. The USV must contain:
- Power subsystem (solar + battery)
- Hull with propulsion (thrusters, waterjet)
- Sensor stack (GPS, IMU, radar, lidar, AIS)
- Navigation computer (state estimator)
- Communication subsystem (radio/satellite)

**GPS-IMU Sensor Fusion (4 marks):**

**Why fuse GPS with IMU?**
- **GPS** provides **absolute position** but at low rate (1–10 Hz) and with multipath errors.
- **IMU** provides **high-rate** (100–1000 Hz) acceleration and angular velocity but **drifts** when integrated.
- Fusion yields a **high-rate, drift-free, accurate** state estimate.

**Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Approach:**

State vector: $x = [x, y, \psi, u, v, r, b_a, b_g]^T$ (position, heading, velocities, IMU biases).

**Prediction step** (IMU mechanization at high rate):

$$
\hat x_{k|k-1} = f(\hat x_{k-1}, u_{IMU})
$$

$$
P_{k|k-1} = F_k P_{k-1} F_k^T + Q_k
$$

**Update step** (GPS at low rate):

$$
K_k = P_{k|k-1} H^T (H P_{k|k-1} H^T + R_k)^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat x_k = \hat x_{k|k-1} + K_k (z_{GPS} - H \hat x_{k|k-1})
$$

$$
P_k = (I - K_k H) P_{k|k-1}
$$

This filter **corrects IMU drift** using GPS and **smooths GPS noise** using IMU → cm-level accuracy achievable with RTK-GPS + tactical-grade IMU.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing AUV with ROV** — Mixing up autonomous vs remotely operated leads to **−2 marks** immediately in 14-mark answers. State control architecture explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting Added Mass** — Writing surge equation as $m\dot u = F$ without the $X_{\dot u}\dot u$ term → **−2 marks**. Added mass is THE distinguishing feature of underwater dynamics.
> 3. **Treating Underwater Robot like a Ground Robot** — Saying "GPS-based navigation" for an AUV → **−1 to −2 marks**. Always clarify the GPS-denied nature and acoustic alternatives.
> 4. **Missing the Coriolis Term** $-vr + wq$ — Many students drop it. Examiner expects it in full derivation. **−1 mark** if omitted.
> 5. **Wrong Units** — Writing drag in Newtons without showing derivation of $C_d$ or omitting $\rho$. Always include SI units in table answers.
> 6. **Block Diagram without Labels** — A diagram without subsystem labels and arrows is awarded **0–1 of 3 marks** in part (a). Label EVERY block and EVERY arrow.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Underwater Robots and Surface Water Robots**

**Core Definitions (memorize verbatim):**
- ☐ **AUV:** Autonomous, untethered, battery-powered, mission-programmed underwater robot.
- ☐ **ROV:** Tethered, surface-controlled, real-time, high-bandwidth underwater robot.
- ☐ **USV/ASV:** Unmanned surface vessel operating at the air–water interface.

**Key Physics:**
- ☐ Buoyancy: $B = \rho V g$ — must balance weight $W = mg$ for hover.
- ☐ Drag: $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ (quadratic regime, $Re > 10^3$).
- ☐ Added Mass: $m_a = k_m \rho \nabla$ — for sphere $k_m = 0.5$.
- ☐ Hydrostatic Pressure: $P = P_0 + \rho g d$ — 1 atm per 10 m of seawater.
- ☐ Speed of sound in seawater: ≈ 1500 m/s (basis for sonar).

**Key Equations:**
- ☐ Fossen 6-DOF: $M\dot\nu + C(\nu)\nu + D(\nu)\nu + g(\eta) = \tau + \tau_{dist}$.
- ☐ Surge equation: $(m + X_{\dot u})\dot u + X_u u + X_{u|u|}u|u| = X_{thr}$.
- ☐ Sonar equation: $SL - 2TL + TS - NL + DI \geq DT$.

**Sensor Knowledge:**
- ☐ Underwater: **DVL + INS + pressure + USBL** (no GPS).
- ☐ Surface: **GPS + IMU + radar + lidar + AIS**.

**Design Rules of Thumb:**
- ☐ Pressure hull → **sphere > cylinder** for uniform stress.
- ☐ CG must be **below** CB for static stability.
- ☐ 4 thrusters = 4-DOF control (surge, sway, heave, yaw).
- ☐ 5–6 thrusters required for full 6-DOF control.

**KTU 2024 High-Yield Keywords:**
- ☐ *Added mass tensor*, *Coriolis-centripetal matrix*, *metacentric height*, *passive vs active sonar*, *DVL bottom-lock*, *USBL acoustic positioning*, *LOS guidance*, *EKF sensor fusion*, *Fossen frame*.

**Differentiator Matrix (must memorize):**
- ☐ AUV vs ROV → autonomy, power, comms, mobility, cost.
- ☐ USV vs AUV → GPS, dimensionality, dominant disturbance, energy budget.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Model of a Differential Drive and a Steered Mobile Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Model of a Differential Drive and a Steered Mobile Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Kinematic Model?

A **Kinematic Model** of a mobile robot is a mathematical description of the robot's motion (position, velocity, and orientation) **without considering the forces or torques** that cause the motion. It establishes the relationship between the robot's **wheel velocities (control inputs)** and the resulting **motion of the robot body in the world frame**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):** The kinematic model is a set of equations that maps the configuration variables (generalized coordinates) of the robot and the control inputs (wheel speeds) to the velocities in task space, subject to the non-holonomic rolling-without-slipping constraints of the wheels.

## 1.2 Configuration of a Mobile Robot in the Plane

For a robot moving on a 2D ground plane, the configuration is described by the pose:

$$
q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where:
- $x, y$: Position of a reference point (typically the center of the wheel axis) in the world (inertial) frame $\{X_W, Y_W\}$.
- $\theta$: Orientation angle of the robot's local frame with respect to the world $X$-axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Differential Drive Robot (DDR)** has **3 configuration variables** $(x, y, \theta)$ but only **2 control inputs** (left and right wheel velocities, $v_L$ and $v_R$). This mismatch makes it a **non-holonomic** system — it cannot move instantaneously sideways.

## 1.3 Differential Drive Robot (DDR) — The Unicycle Family

A **Differential Drive Robot** is a non-holonomic mobile robot propelled by **two independently driven wheels** mounted on a common axis, plus one or more passive caster wheels for balance.

### Conceptual Analogy 🎯

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Shopping Cart:** Think of a supermarket shopping cart with two big rear wheels and a small front caster. If both wheels turn forward at the same speed, the cart moves straight. If the right wheel turns faster than the left, the cart curves to the **left** (because the left wheel acts as a pivot). If both wheels turn in opposite directions at the same speed, the cart **spins in place**. This is exactly how a differential drive robot moves! Each wheel's speed difference produces a different rotational behavior.

## 1.4 Steered (Car-like / Ackermann) Mobile Robot

A **Steered Mobile Robot** is one whose front wheels (and sometimes rear wheels) can be **steered**, similar to a car. The steering angle $\phi$ becomes an additional control input.

### Conceptual Analogy 🚗

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Family Car:** When you turn a car's steering wheel, the front wheels pivot to a steering angle $\phi$. The car then turns along a circular arc whose radius depends on $\phi$ and the wheelbase $L$. The rear wheels always point along the car's heading. This is the **Ackermann steering** principle. The minimum turning radius is fixed by the maximum steering angle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Differential drive robots can spin in place (zero turning radius), whereas **steered (car-like) robots have a non-zero minimum turning radius** because the steering angle is mechanically limited.

---

## 1.5 Wheel Types and Their Constraints

Every wheel imposes **two constraints** on the robot motion: one that prevents sideways slip (**lateral**) and one that prevents slipping along the wheel's rolling direction (**longitudinal** for fixed wheels, only one constraint for swedged/castor wheels).

| Wheel Type | Symbol | Constraints | Steering Allowed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fixed (Standard) | F | 2 (no slip, no skid) | No |
| Steered (oriented) | S | 2 (no slip, no skid) | Yes |
| Castored / Swedish | C | 1 (no slip) | Yes (passive) |

> [!NOTE]
> A **differential drive** uses **2 fixed wheels + 1 castor**. A **car-like robot** uses **2 steered (front) + 2 fixed (rear)** wheels. The number of constraints and configuration variables defines the **Degree of Mobility (M)** and **Degree of Steerability (S)** in the Gruebler formulation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Differential Drive Robot — Kinematic Architecture

### 2.1.1 Geometry and Parameters

Consider a differential drive robot with:
- Two active wheels of radius $r$ on a common axle.
- Distance between the wheel contact points (wheel separation): $2b$ (so half-width is $b$).
- $P$ is the center point between the two wheels (midpoint of the axle).

### 2.1.2 Rolling Without Slipping — The ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation)

When $v_L \neq v_R$, the robot rotates about a point on the wheel axis line. The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** lies on the line connecting the two wheel contact points.

The **Instantaneous Radius of Curvature** $R$ is:

$$
R = \frac{b(v_R + v_L)}{v_R - v_L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $v_R = -v_L$, the denominator becomes $2v_R$ and $R = 0$. The robot **spins in place** about point $P$ (the midpoint of the axle) — this is the classic "tank turn" of differential drive robots.

The **Angular Velocity** of the robot body is:

$$
\omega = \dot{\theta} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{2b}
$$

The **Linear Velocity** of the robot (at point $P$) is:

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}
$$

### 2.1.3 Forward Kinematic Model (Differential Drive)

The forward kinematic model maps wheel velocities to the robot's body twist $(v, \omega)$, which is then integrated to obtain the world-frame velocities via the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting the expressions for $v$ and $\omega$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r\cos\theta}{2} & \dfrac{r\cos\theta}{2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{2} & \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{2} \\[6pt] -\dfrac{r}{2b} & \dfrac{r}{2b} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\ \omega_R \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Here $v_L = r\omega_L$ and $v_R = r\omega_R$ are the linear velocities of the left and right wheels, where $\omega_L, \omega_R$ are the angular velocities of the left and right wheels respectively.

### 2.1.4 Inverse Kinematic Model (Differential Drive)

Given a desired body twist $(v, \omega)$, the required wheel angular velocities are:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\ \omega_R \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{r} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -b \\ 1 & b \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matrix is the **inverse of the forward kinematic matrix**, and is always invertible (determinant = $2b/r \neq 0$).

---

## 2.2 Steered (Car-like) Mobile Robot — The Bicycle Model

For analysis simplicity, the **bicycle model** combines the left and right wheels of each axle into a single virtual wheel at the centerline. This preserves the kinematics accurately.

### 2.2.1 Parameters

- $L$ = wheelbase (distance between front and rear axles).
- $\phi$ = steering angle of the front wheel (control input).
- $v$ = longitudinal velocity of the rear wheel center (control input).
- $\theta$ = heading angle.

### 2.2.2 Forward Kinematic Model (Bicycle Model)

The kinematic equations are:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v\sin\theta
$$

$$
\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi
$$

The instantaneous radius of curvature is:

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan\phi}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Holonomic Constraint:** A car-like robot cannot move sideways. This is expressed mathematically as:
> $$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$
> This is a **non-integrable** (non-holonomic) constraint — it restricts the instantaneous velocity but not the reachable configurations.

### 2.2.3 Controllability Insight (KTU High-Yield Concept)

Even though the car has only 2 control inputs $(v, \phi)$ for 3 configuration variables $(x, y, \theta)$, it is **controllable** because the non-holonomic constraints satisfy the **Lie Algebra Rank Condition (LARC)**, also known as the **Chow-Rashevskii theorem**. The system can reach any configuration in the plane through appropriate maneuvers (e.g., parallel parking requires moving sideways through a sequence of forward/backward motions).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet 📋

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DDR: linear velocity | $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | At point $P$ |
| 2 | DDR: angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{2b}$ | Half-width $= b$ |
| 3 | DDR: radius of curvature | $R = \dfrac{b(v_R + v_L)}{v_R - v_L}$ | $R \to \infty$ when $v_R = v_L$ |
| 4 | DDR: forward kinematics matrix | $\dot{q} = J(\theta)\,[v,\omega]^T$ | $J$ is $3 \times 2$ |
| 5 | DDR: inverse kinematics | $[\omega_L,\omega_R]^T = \dfrac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -b\\1 & b\end{bmatrix}[v,\omega]^T$ | Always solvable |
| 6 | Bicycle: $\dot\theta$ | $\dot\theta = \dfrac{v}{L}\tan\phi$ | $\vert\phi\vert < \pi/2$ |
| 7 | Bicycle: turning radius | $R = \dfrac{L}{\tan\phi}$ | Minimum $R$ at max $\phi$ |
| 8 | Non-holonomic constraint | $\dot x \sin\theta - \dot y \cos\theta = 0$ | Applies to both robots |
| 9 | Pose vector | $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | 3-D configuration space |
| 10 | Holonomic? | Both robots are **non-holonomic** | Cannot move sideways instantaneously |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** The bicycle model is the foundational kinematic model for path tracking, MPC (Model Predictive Control), and Stanley controller design.
- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Differential drive AGVs from Kiva/Amazon Robotics use these models for SLAM and motion planning.
- **Floor Cleaning Robots (Roomba):** Use differential drive with random + systematic motion planning based on these kinematic equations.
- **Planetary Rovers (NASA's Mars rovers):** Use rocker-bogie differential steering with extended kinematic models derived from these fundamentals.
- **Disability Assistive Robots:** Wheelchair kinematics are extensions of the bicycle model.
