# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential Drive Forward Kinematics (from first principles)

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define the Geometry and Wheel Contact Velocities

Let the left and right wheel contact points be $P_L$ and $P_R$, separated by distance $2b$. Let the center between them be $P = (x, y)$. The wheel radius is $r$. The wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$.

The linear velocities at the contact points of the two wheels (rolling without slipping):

$$
v_L = r\,\omega_L \quad \text{and} \quad v_R = r\,\omega_R
$$

> **Logic:** A wheel rolling on the ground without slipping converts its angular velocity at the axle into a linear velocity at the contact point, given by $v = r\omega$. This is the standard rolling constraint.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Express the Body Twist in Terms of $v_L$ and $v_R$

The body of the robot has two motion components at point $P$:
- **Linear velocity $v$** along the robot's local $x$-axis (forward direction).
- **Angular velocity $\omega$** about the vertical axis through $P$.

Since both wheels lie on the same rigid axle, the linear velocity of the contact point of wheel $L$ is $v - b\omega$ (it is $b$ behind the velocity at $P$ for positive $\omega$ counterclockwise, but we adopt the convention where positive $\omega$ rotates the robot leftward, so the right wheel moves faster and the left wheel slower):

$$
v_L = v - b\omega
$$

$$
v_R = v + b\omega
$$

> **Logic:** Point $P_R$ is at distance $b$ from $P$ in the forward-right direction. A rotation $\omega$ adds $\omega \times b$ to its linear velocity along the robot's $x$-axis.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Solve for $v$ and $\omega$

Adding the two equations:

$$
v_L + v_R = 2v \quad\Rightarrow\quad v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2}
$$

Subtracting the two equations:

$$
v_R - v_L = 2b\omega \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{2b}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Project Body Twist to World Frame

The body frame moves with linear velocity $v$ in its own $x$-direction. The world-frame velocities are obtained by rotating the body velocity vector by the heading angle $\theta$:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v\sin\theta
$$

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Substitute $v_L, v_R$ in terms of $\omega_L, \omega_R$

Using $v_L = r\omega_L$ and $v_R = r\omega_R$:

$$
v = \frac{r\omega_L + r\omega_R}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{r\omega_R - r\omega_L}{2b} = \frac{r}{2b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)
$$

Therefore, the complete forward kinematic model in matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\[4pt] \dot{y} \\[4pt] \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\[6pt] \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\[6pt] -\dfrac{r}{2b} & \dfrac{r}{2b} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\[4pt] \omega_R \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Logic:** This is the **complete forward kinematic map** from wheel angular velocities to the rate of change of the robot's pose. The matrix is called the **Jacobian of the differential drive**, and it is **state-dependent** (depends on $\theta$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Steered Robot (Bicycle Model) Forward Kinematics

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Set Up the Coordinate System

Let the rear wheel be at the origin of the robot frame (a common convention). The front wheel is at distance $L$ (wheelbase) ahead. The heading angle of the robot is $\theta$, and the front wheel is steered at angle $\phi$ relative to the robot's longitudinal axis.

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Apply the Rolling-Without-Slipping Constraint

The rear wheel rolls without slipping in its forward direction, so the rear wheel's velocity is $v$ along the robot's $x$-axis:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v\sin\theta
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Apply the Geometric Constraint at the Front Wheel

The front wheel, being rigidly connected to the rear wheel through the chassis, must also satisfy the rigid-body rotation. The velocity of the front wheel contact point must be perpendicular to the line from the ICR to that point. This geometric constraint yields:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{L\dot{\theta}}{v}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi
$$

> **Logic:** If the steering angle is zero ($\phi = 0$), then $\tan\phi = 0$ and $\dot\theta = 0$ — the car moves in a straight line. As $\phi$ increases, the angular velocity increases. Note that when $\phi \to \pi/2$, $\tan\phi \to \infty$, but this is physically unattainable because the steering is mechanically limited to $|\phi| < \pi/2$.

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Derive the Radius of Curvature

The ICR lies on the line perpendicular to the front wheel, at distance $R$ from the robot. From the geometry of the bicycle model:

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan\phi}
$$

> **Logic:** Larger steering angles produce tighter turns (smaller $R$). The **minimum turning radius** is achieved at the **maximum steering angle** $\phi_{\max}$: $R_{\min} = L/\tan\phi_{\max}$.

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Express in Standard Matrix Form

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\[4pt] \dot{y} \\[4pt] \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\[4pt] \sin\theta & 0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{\tan\phi}{L} & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\[4pt] \phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Note:** Some textbooks absorb $\phi$ inside the matrix. The control inputs for the bicycle model are typically $(v, \phi)$.

---

## 3.3 Inverse Kinematics Derivation (Differential Drive)

Given a desired body twist $(v^d, \omega^d)$:

### Step 1 — Compute Required Linear Wheel Velocities

$$
v_R^d = v^d + b\,\omega^d
$$

$$
v_L^d = v^d - b\,\omega^d
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Wheel Angular Velocities

$$
\omega_R^d = \frac{v^d + b\,\omega^d}{r}
$$

$$
\omega_L^d = \frac{v^d - b\,\omega^d}{r}
$$

### Step 3 — Matrix Form

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\[4pt] \omega_R \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -b \\[4pt] 1 & b \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v^d \\[4pt] \omega^d \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Logic:** The inverse is **unique** for any $(v, \omega)$ — the determinant is $2b/r \neq 0$. This means **differential drive is fully controllable** in velocity space (every body twist is achievable by some wheel velocity pair).

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Table

| Property | Differential Drive | Steered (Bicycle) |
|---|---|---|
| Control inputs | $v_L, v_R$ (or $\omega_L, \omega_R$) | $v, \phi$ |
| Configuration DOF | 3 | 3 |
| Non-holonomic constraints | 1 | 1 |
| Minimum turning radius | **0** (can spin in place) | $L / \tan\phi_{\max} > 0$ |
| Singular configurations | None | $\phi = \pm\pi/2$ (unreachable) |
| Inverse kinematics | Linear, always solvable | Always solvable (within $\vert\phi\vert < \pi/2$) |
| Holonomic? | **No** | **No** |
| Examples | Roomba, Kiva, Pioneer | Self-driving car, Ackermann AGV |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice)

> **Problem:** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, half-width $b = 0.15$ m. The left wheel rotates at $\omega_L = 2$ rad/s and the right at $\omega_R = 4$ rad/s. Find $(v, \omega, R)$ and the world-frame velocity if the robot's heading is $\theta = 30°$.

**Solution:**

Linear wheel velocities:

$$
v_L = r\omega_L = 0.05 \times 2 = 0.1 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_R = r\omega_R = 0.05 \times 4 = 0.2 \text{ m/s}
$$

Body linear and angular velocities:

$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{0.1 + 0.2}{2} = 0.15 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{2b} = \frac{0.2 - 0.1}{2 \times 0.15} = \frac{0.1}{0.3} \approx 0.333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Radius of curvature:

$$
R = \frac{b(v_R + v_L)}{v_R - v_L} = \frac{0.15 \times 0.3}{0.1} = 0.45 \text{ m}
$$

World-frame velocities (with $\theta = 30°$):

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta = 0.15 \times \cos 30° = 0.15 \times 0.866 = 0.1299 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v\sin\theta = 0.15 \times \sin 30° = 0.15 \times 0.5 = 0.075 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega = 0.333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The robot is moving forward at 0.15 m/s, turning **counter-clockwise** (left) with angular velocity 0.333 rad/s along a circular arc of radius 0.45 m, with its velocity vector directed at 30° from the world $X$-axis.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Differential Drive — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Desired Body Twist v_des and omega_des"] --> B["Inverse Kinematics Matrix 1/r times 1 -b; 1 b"]
    B --> C["Wheel Angular Velocities omega_L and omega_R"]
    C --> D["Motor Drivers and PWM Amplifiers"]
    D --> E["Left and Right DC Motors"]
    E --> F["Wheel Contact Points v_L and v_R"]
    F --> G["Forward Kinematics in Body Frame v and omega"]
    G --> H["Rotation Matrix R of theta"]
    H --> I["World Frame Velocities x_dot, y_dot, theta_dot"]
    I --> J["Odometry Integration for x, y, theta"]
```

> **Interpretation:** This block diagram shows the closed-loop motion pipeline of a differential drive robot. The **inverse kinematics** module is often implemented in firmware, while the **forward kinematics + rotation** is used in the **odometry** (dead-reckoning) update loop running at high frequency on the robot's microcontroller.

## 4.2 Steered (Bicycle) Robot — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Desired Path with Curvature kappa"] --> B["Pure Pursuit or Stanley Controller"]
    B --> C["Desired Steering Angle phi_des and Speed v_des"]
    C --> D["Steering Actuator Servo Motor"]
    D --> E["Front Wheel Steered to phi"]
    E --> F["Rear Wheel Driving at v"]
    F --> G["Bicycle Forward Kinematics"]
    G --> H["ICR at Distance R = L over tan phi"]
    H --> I["World Frame Velocities x_dot, y_dot, theta_dot"]
    I --> J["Localization and Path Tracking"]
```

> **Interpretation:** For a car-like robot, the path planner outputs a **curvature** $\kappa = 1/R$. The controller converts curvature to a desired steering angle via $\phi_{\text{des}} = \arctan(\kappa L)$. The steering servo physically rotates the front wheel, and the bicycle kinematic model propagates the motion.

## 4.3 Kinematic Structure Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Differential Drive"
        D1["Two Active Wheels on Same Axle"]
        D2["v_L and v_R Independent"]
        D3["Body Twist v and omega"]
        D4["Can Spin in Place R = 0"]
        D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4
    end
    subgraph "Steered Car-like"
        S1["Rear Wheel Drive with v"]
        S2["Front Wheel Steered with phi"]
        S3["Body Twist v and omega = v over L tan phi"]
        S4["Minimum R = L over tan phi_max"]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    end
```

> **Interpretation:** Side-by-side comparison of the two robot architectures. The differential drive's two active wheels provide direct control over both linear and angular velocities independently. The steered robot couples them through the steering angle, which is why it cannot spin in place.

## 4.4 Non-Holonomic Constraint Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Robot at Pose x, y, theta"] --> B["Body Axis aligned with heading theta"]
    B --> C["Velocity Vector v along body axis"]
    C --> D["Lateral Component is FORBIDDEN"]
    D --> E["Constraint: x_dot sin theta = y_dot cos theta"]
    E --> F["Motion is ALONG the heading direction only"]
```

> **Interpretation:** The non-holonomic constraint means the robot's velocity vector must always lie along its longitudinal axis. It can never have an instantaneous velocity component perpendicular to this axis, regardless of how it maneuvers.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Non-Holonomic Constraint with reference to a differential drive robot. Why is the DDR considered non-holonomic? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **non-holonomic constraint** is a velocity-level constraint on the robot's motion that **cannot be integrated** to a configuration-level (positional) constraint. It restricts the **instantaneous** velocities but does not restrict the **reachable set of configurations**.

For a differential drive robot, the no-sideways-slip constraint is:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

This constraint says the lateral velocity of the robot is always zero, yet the robot can still reach **any** $(x, y, \theta)$ configuration through appropriate maneuvers (e.g., parallel parking involves a sequence of forward and reverse motions to achieve a sideways displacement). This integrability failure is the hallmark of non-holonomic constraints. The DDR is therefore called a **non-holonomic mobile robot**.

---

### Question 2: What is the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) for a mobile robot? Derive the ICR location for a differential drive robot. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is the point in the plane about which the robot is instantaneously rotating (analogous to the center of a circle in pure circular motion). At any instant, every point of the robot moves perpendicular to the line joining it to the ICR.

For a differential drive robot with wheel velocities $v_L$ and $v_R$ ($v_L \neq v_R$), the ICR lies on the wheel axis line at a distance $R$ from the midpoint $P$ of the axle:

$$
R = \frac{b(v_R + v_L)}{v_R - v_L}
$$

The ICR is on the side of the slower wheel (the wheel acts as the pivot). When $v_L = v_R$, $R \to \infty$ (ICR at infinity) and the robot moves in a **straight line**. When $v_L = -v_R$, $R = 0$ and the ICR is **at the midpoint $P$** — the robot spins in place.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> **KTU Format:** Each Part B question typically has two sub-parts, each worth 7 marks. Students must answer **one** out of two choices (Choice A or Choice B).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** With a neat diagram, derive the **forward kinematic model** of a differential drive mobile robot having wheel radius $r$ and wheel separation $2b$. Express the model in matrix form relating wheel angular velocities $(\omega_L, \omega_R)$ to the world-frame velocities $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A differential drive robot has $r = 0.08$ m and $b = 0.2$ m. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L = 3$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 5$ rad/s. Compute the robot's body linear velocity, angular velocity, and the radius of curvature of its path. If the robot is at heading $\theta = 45°$, find the world-frame velocities. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Diagram:** Draw the robot as a rectangular body with two active wheels on a common axle, with a passive castor at the front or rear. Mark the wheel contact points $P_L, P_R$, the midpoint $P$, the heading $\theta$, and the world frame $\{X_W, Y_W\}$.

**Step 1 — Define geometry:** The two wheels of radius $r$ are on a common axle. Let $2b$ be the separation between contact points.

**Step 2 — Apply rolling without slipping at each wheel:**

$$
v_L = r\,\omega_L, \quad v_R = r\,\omega_R
$$

**Step 3 — Express the body twist $(v, \omega)$ at the midpoint $P$:**

$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{2b} = \frac{r}{2b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)
$$

**Step 4 — Transform to world frame using rotation matrix $R(\theta)$:**

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

**Step 5 — Final matrix form:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\[4pt] \dot{y} \\[4pt] \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\[6pt] \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\[6pt] -\dfrac{r}{2b} & \dfrac{r}{2b} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\[4pt] \omega_R \end{bmatrix}
$$

The $3 \times 2$ matrix is called the **Jacobian of the differential drive** and is a function of the heading $\theta$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $r = 0.08$ m, $b = 0.2$ m, $\omega_L = 3$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 5$ rad/s, $\theta = 45°$.

**Step 1 — Linear wheel velocities:**

$$
v_L = r\omega_L = 0.08 \times 3 = 0.24 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_R = r\omega_R = 0.08 \times 5 = 0.40 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Body linear velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{0.24 + 0.40}{2} = 0.32 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Body angular velocity:**

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{2b} = \frac{0.40 - 0.24}{2 \times 0.2} = \frac{0.16}{0.4} = 0.4 \text{ rad/s}
$$

The positive sign indicates **counter-clockwise (left) turn**.

**Step 4 — Radius of curvature:**

$$
R = \frac{b(v_R + v_L)}{v_R - v_L} = \frac{0.2 \times 0.64}{0.16} = \frac{0.128}{0.16} = 0.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — World-frame velocities (at $\theta = 45°$):**

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos 45° = 0.32 \times 0.7071 = 0.2263 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v\sin 45° = 0.32 \times 0.7071 = 0.2263 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega = 0.4 \text{ rad/s}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $v = 0.32$ m/s, $\omega = 0.4$ rad/s, $R = 0.8$ m, $\dot{x} = \dot{y} \approx 0.226$ m/s, $\dot{\theta} = 0.4$ rad/s.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** With a neat sketch, derive the **forward kinematic model of a car-like (steered) mobile robot** using the bicycle model. State all assumptions clearly. Show that the minimum turning radius is $R_{\min} = L/\tan\phi_{\max}$. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A car-like robot has a wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m and a maximum steering angle $\phi_{\max} = 35°$. It is moving at a constant speed $v = 10$ m/s. Calculate the maximum angular velocity and the minimum turning radius. How long does it take to complete one full circle at the minimum radius? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Assumptions of the Bicycle Model:**
1. The vehicle moves on a flat, horizontal plane.
2. The steering angle is the same for both front wheels (no Ackermann geometry correction).
3. No slip occurs at any wheel.
4. The velocity vector of the vehicle lies along its longitudinal axis (non-holonomic constraint).
5. Low-speed kinematics (no tire deformation or skidding).

**Derivation:**

Let the rear wheel center be at $(x, y)$ with heading $\theta$. The front wheel is at distance $L$ ahead, steered at angle $\phi$.

**Step 1 — Non-slip at the rear wheel:**

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric constraint from rigid-body rotation:** The front wheel's velocity direction must be perpendicular to the line from the ICR to the front wheel contact point. This yields:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{L\dot{\theta}}{v} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi
$$

**Step 3 — Turning radius:** From the geometry, the ICR lies on the line through the rear wheel perpendicular to its heading, at distance $R$:

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan\phi}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum turning radius:** Since $\phi$ is mechanically limited to $|\phi| \leq \phi_{\max}$, the minimum $R$ occurs at $\phi_{\max}$:

$$
R_{\min} = \frac{L}{\tan\phi_{\max}}
$$

> **Note:** The bicycle model is a simplified but accurate representation of car kinematics used in autonomous driving research.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $L = 2.5$ m, $\phi_{\max} = 35°$, $v = 10$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Maximum angular velocity** (occurs at $\phi_{\max}$):

$$
\omega_{\max} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi_{\max} = \frac{10}{2.5} \times \tan 35°
$$

Computing $\tan 35° \approx 0.7002$:

$$
\omega_{\max} = 4 \times 0.7002 = 2.8008 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Minimum turning radius:**

$$
R_{\min} = \frac{L}{\tan\phi_{\max}} = \frac{2.5}{0.7002} = 3.5707 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Time to complete one full circle:**

The circumference of the circle is $2\pi R_{\min}$. The time is:

$$
T = \frac{2\pi R_{\min}}{v} = \frac{2\pi \times 3.5707}{10} = \frac{22.435}{10} = 2.2435 \text{ s}
$$

Alternatively, using $T = 2\pi/\omega_{\max}$:

$$
T = \frac{2\pi}{2.8008} = 2.2435 \text{ s} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\omega_{\max} \approx 2.80$ rad/s, $R_{\min} \approx 3.57$ m, $T \approx 2.24$ s.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $b$ and $2b$:** Some textbooks use $2b$ as the wheel separation, while others use $b$ as the half-width. **Always re-read the problem statement** to determine the convention. The KTU textbook by Siegwart, Nourbakhsh, and Scaramuzza uses $b$ as the half-distance.
> 2. **Sign of $\omega$:** A common mistake is treating positive $\omega$ as clockwise. By convention, **positive $\omega$ is counter-clockwise (CCW)**. If your $\omega$ comes out negative, the robot is turning right.
> 3. **Forgetting the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$:** The body-frame velocity $(v, \omega)$ must be rotated to the world frame using $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$. Skipping this loses 3 marks easily.
> 4. **Units mixing in $R$ formula:** The radius of curvature formula $R = b(v_R + v_L)/(v_R - v_L)$ is **dimensionally correct** only if $v_L, v_R$ are linear velocities (m/s) and $b$ is in meters. Don't substitute wheel angular velocities directly.
> 5. **Bicycle model — non-availability of inverse kinematics at $\phi = \pm\pi/2$:** The matrix becomes singular. Always check $|\phi| < \pi/2$.
> 6. **Odometry integration:** KTU often asks to compute the pose after a time interval $T$. Use Euler integration: $x(t+T) = x(t) + \dot{x} \cdot T$, or use exact arc-length integration. Don't confuse pose update with control input update.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Kinematic Models of DDR and Steered Robots:**

- ✅ **Pose vector:** $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ for any ground mobile robot.
- ✅ **DDR linear velocity:** $v = (v_L + v_R)/2$, **angular velocity:** $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/2b$.
- ✅ **DDR radius of curvature:** $R = b(v_R + v_L)/(v_R - v_L)$.
- ✅ **DDR special cases:** $v_L = v_R \Rightarrow$ straight line ($R = \infty$); $v_L = -v_R \Rightarrow$ spin in place ($R = 0$).
- ✅ **DDR forward Jacobian:** $3 \times 2$ matrix with entries $\frac{r}{2}\cos\theta$, $\frac{r}{2}\sin\theta$, $\pm\frac{r}{2b}$.
- ✅ **DDR inverse kinematics:** $[\omega_L, \omega_R]^T = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -b \\ 1 & b \end{bmatrix}[v, \omega]^T$ — always solvable.
- ✅ **Bicycle model:** $\dot\theta = (v/L)\tan\phi$ and $R = L/\tan\phi$.
- ✅ **Minimum turning radius (car-like):** $R_{\min} = L/\tan\phi_{\max} > 0$.
- ✅ **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — restricts lateral velocity.
- ✅ **DOF:** 3 configuration variables, 2 control inputs → non-holonomic in both DDR and bicycle.
- ✅ **Controllability:** Both robots are controllable in the sense of LARC (Lie Algebra Rank Condition) — they can reach any pose through appropriate maneuvers.
- ✅ **Ackermann geometry:** Real cars use slightly different steering angles for left and right front wheels to avoid tire scrub. The bicycle model assumes a single virtual front wheel.
- ✅ **Degree of Mobility (M) + Degree of Steerability (S):** DDR has $M = 2, S = 0$; car-like has $M = 1, S = 1$ in the Gruebler formulation.
- ✅ **Key differences to memorize:** DDR can spin in place; car-like cannot. DDR has two control inputs as wheel speeds; car-like has speed and steering angle.
- ✅ **Application domains:** AGVs, warehouse robots, Mars rovers, autonomous cars, service robots.
- ✅ **Forward kinematics is used for odometry; inverse kinematics is used for trajectory tracking.**

> **End of Topic Notes — Kinematic Model of a Differential Drive and a Steered Mobile Robot**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degree of Freedom and Maneuverability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Degree of Freedom and Maneuverability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the formal language of the KTU Mobile Robotics syllabus (aligned with *Introduction to Autonomous Mobile Robots* – Siegwart, Nourbakhsh & Scaramuzza), the following four terms must be mastered for Module 2:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot is the **number of independent variables (parameters)** required to completely specify the **configuration** (position + orientation) of the robot in its workspace.
> - A **rigid body in 2D plane** → **3 DOF** ($x, y, \theta$)
> - A **rigid body in 3D space** → **6 DOF** ($x, y, z, \text{roll}, \text{pitch}, \text{yaw}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$)** is the **number of degrees of freedom** that can be **directly controlled by the wheels** through their rolling action (i.e., the number of *independently driven / fixed* wheels contributing to translation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$)** is the **number of independent steering variables** available at the wheel-ground contact points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Maneuverability** is the **total number of independent controllable motions** of the robot:
> $$\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$
> The **effective DOF of a planar wheeled robot** is then:
> $$\text{DOF}_{\text{robot}} = \min(\delta_m + \delta_s, \ 3)$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine three vehicles stuck in a crowded parking lot:

- **A car (Ackermann steering)** → can roll **forward/backward** ($\delta_m = 1$) and turn its front wheels left/right ($\delta_s = 1$). It has **2 DOF**: it cannot slide sideways no matter how hard you try.
- **A tank (differential drive)** → has **two independently driven tracks** ($\delta_m = 2$, $\delta_s = 0$). It can spin on the spot and go forward/backward — **2 DOF**, but no sideways motion.
- **A mecanum-wheel AGV (warehouse robot)** → can move **forward, sideways, and rotate, all independently** ($\delta_m = 3$, $\delta_s = 0$) — full **3 DOF** instantaneous planar motion.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** *Mobility* is "how the robot translates," *Steerability* is "how the robot reorients its rolling axes," and *Maneuverability* is the **union** of the two — limited by physics to a maximum of **3** for planar motion.

### 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics (in **bold**)

- **Planar rigid-body DOF = 3**
- **Spatial rigid-body DOF = 6**
- **Standard wheel = 1 contribution** to either $\delta_m$ or $\delta_s$, **never both simultaneously**
- **Maneuverability ceiling for any planar robot = 3**
- **Grübler's lower-pair constant = 2**, **higher-pair constant = 3** (per DOF removed in planar mechanism)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wheel Taxonomy — Building Blocks of Mobility & Steerability

Every wheeled mobile robot is built from four elementary wheel archetypes. Each contributes either to $\delta_m$, $\delta_s$, or both:

| Wheel Type | Axis Rotation | Steering Rotation | Contribution | Example Robot |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed (Standard)** | ✓ (driven) | ✗ | $\delta_m \uparrow$ | Differential drive |
| **Steerable (Dutch)** | ✓ (driven) | ✓ | $\delta_m$ **and** $\delta_s$ | Ackermann front wheel |
| **Castor** | ✓ (passive) | ✓ (passive) | Passive support | Office chair |
| **Swedish / Mecanum** | Omnidirectional | – | $\delta_m \uparrow$ (by 3) | Omnidirectional AGV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **fixed wheel driven by a motor** contributes **+1 to $\delta_m$**.
> A **steerable wheel whose steering angle is independently controlled** contributes **+1 to $\delta_s$**.
> A **passive castor** contributes **nothing** to either.

### 2.2 Campion's Classification of Wheeled Robots

> [!NOTE]
> **Campion, Bastin & D'Andrea-Novel (1996)** classified every possible wheeled robot by the pair **$(\delta_m, \delta_s)$**. Below is the KTU-required master table.

| Type $(\delta_m, \delta_s)$ | Maneuverability | Effective DOF | Realization | Motion Capability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **(3, 0)** | 3 | 3 | Omnidirectional (mecanum, Swedish) | Full planar freedom |
| **(2, 1)** | 3 | 3 | Tricycle (1 steerable drive + 1 fixed drive) | Full planar freedom |
| **(2, 0)** | 2 | 2 | Differential drive (2 fixed drive) | Cannot move sideways |
| **(1, 2)** | 3 | 3 | 4-Wheel car w/ independent front+rear steer | Full planar freedom |
| **(1, 1)** | 2 | 2 | Ackermann car | Cannot move sideways |
| **(1, 0)** | 1 | 1 | Train on rail | Only forward/back |
| **(0, 3)** | 3 | 3 | Theoretically 3 steerable wheels | Rare/impossible |

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

1. **Why $\delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$ for planar robots?**
   Because a **rigid body in 2D plane has only 3 DOF**; you cannot instantaneously achieve more independent motions than the underlying physics allows.

2. **Why can a car turn with $\delta_s = 1$ but cannot slide sideways?**
   The rolling-without-slipping constraint forces every wheel's contact-point velocity to zero — that restricts motion along the rolling direction only.

3. **Why is a castor wheel passive?**
   It rotates freely about both its rolling axis *and* steering axis — it cannot impose a controlled direction, so it contributes 0 to maneuverability.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DOF}_{2D} = 3$ | Planar rigid body | $x, y, \theta$ |
| 2 | $\text{DOF}_{3D} = 6$ | Spatial rigid body | $x,y,z,$ roll,pitch,yaw |
| 3 | $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Maneuverability | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{DOF}_{\text{robot}} = \min(\delta_m + \delta_s, \ 3)$ | Effective planar DOF | $\le 3$ |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | Diff-drive linear velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L}$ | Diff-drive angular velocity | rad/s |
| 7 | $R_{\text{ICC}} = \dfrac{v}{\omega} = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$ | Diff-drive ICR radius | m |
| 8 | $R_{\text{turn}} = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\alpha)}$ | Ackermann turning radius | m |
| 9 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Grübler (planar mechanism) | DOF |
| 10 | $\dot{q} = J(q) \cdot u$ | Forward kinematics (wheel velocities $\to$ body twist) | generalized |

> Where **$L$** = wheel separation (m), **$r$** = wheel radius (m), **$\alpha$** = steering angle (rad), **$n$** = number of links, **$j_1$** = 1-DOF joints, **$j_2$** = 2-DOF joints.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Warehousing** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch) | Mecanum $(\delta_m=3,\delta_s=0)$ for tight aisle navigation |
| **Planetary Rovers** (NASA Curiosity, Perseverance) | rocker-bogie + Ackermann-like steering |
| **AGV / AMR in hospitals** | Differential drive for cost + simplicity |
| **Autonomous Cars (Waymo, Tesla)** | Ackermann $(\delta_m=1,\delta_s=1)$ mirrors human driving |
| **Defense / EOD Robots** | Tracked $\approx$ differential drive for rough terrain |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Kinematic Model of a Differential Drive Robot

**Setup:** Two independently driven, coaxial wheels of radius $r$ separated by distance $L$. Let $v_L$ and $v_R$ be the linear velocities of the left and right wheel contact points, and let the robot's body frame be at the midpoint of the axle.

**Step 1 — Write individual wheel velocities from motor inputs:**
Each wheel is driven by a motor producing angular velocity $\omega_i$. Because of the no-slip rolling constraint, the linear velocity of the wheel contact point equals:
$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L$$
$$v_R = r \cdot \omega_R$$

**Step 2 — Express the linear velocity of the chassis:**
The chassis is rigidly connected to the axle midpoint. Its linear velocity is the **arithmetic mean** of the two wheel contact-point velocities:
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Express the angular velocity of the chassis:**
The angular velocity is determined by the **velocity difference** divided by the wheel separation (basic rigid-body rotation):
$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Combine into vector form (forward kinematics):**
$$
\dot{q} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\dot{x} \\
\dot{y} \\
\dot{\theta}
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & 0 \\
0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
v \\
\omega
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting $v$ and $\omega$:
$$
\dot{q} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\
\dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\
-\dfrac{r}{L} & \dfrac{r}{L}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\omega_L \\
\omega_R
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) for Differential Drive

**Step 1 — Recognize that for non-holonomic rolling:**
The velocity of every wheel contact point is zero. This means every contact point acts as a pivot for the rigid body — the **ICR** is therefore constrained to lie **on the line through both contact points** (the axle line).

**Step 2 — For pure rotation about ICR:**
At distance $R$ from ICR, a point has tangential velocity $v = \omega R$. Apply this to the wheel contact points:
$$v_R = \omega \cdot \left(R + \tfrac{L}{2}\right)$$
$$v_L = \omega \cdot \left(R - \tfrac{L}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for R using the ratio:**
$$\frac{v_R}{v_L} = \frac{R + L/2}{R - L/2}$$

Cross-multiplying and simplifying:
$$v_R(R - L/2) = v_L(R + L/2)$$
$$R(v_R - v_L) = \tfrac{L}{2}(v_R + v_L)$$

**Step 4 — Final closed-form:**
$$\boxed{R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L} = \frac{v}{\omega}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $v_R = -v_L$ (tank turn), $R = 0$ — the ICR collapses to the axle midpoint and the robot spins in place.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ackermann Turning Radius

**Setup:** A car-like robot with wheelbase $L$ (distance between front and rear axles) and front-wheel steering angle $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Non-holonomic constraint at the rear axle:**
The rear wheels cannot move sideways, so the ICR must lie on the line perpendicular to the rear axle, passing through the rear contact points.

**Step 2 — Same constraint at the front axle:**
The front wheel rolling direction makes angle $\alpha$ with the chassis. The ICR must lie on the line perpendicular to this rolling direction through the front contact point.

**Step 3 — Geometric intersection:**
Drawing both perpendiculars, they intersect at distance $R$ from the robot. From the rear axle, $R$ is horizontal distance; from the front axle, $R$ is at angle $\alpha$ to the wheelbase.

**Step 4 — Trigonometric closure:**
From the right triangle formed:
$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{L}{R}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{R = \frac{L}{\tan(\alpha)}}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Examination Favourite)

| Property | Differential Drive | Ackermann (Car-like) | Omnidirectional (Mecanum) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta_m$ | 2 | 1 | 3 |
| $\delta_s$ | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| Maneuverability | 2 | 2 | 3 |
| Effective DOF | 2 | 2 | 3 |
| Sideways motion | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| In-place rotation | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Wheel-slip wear | Moderate | Low | High (rollers) |
| Computational cost | Low | Low | High |
| Typical use | Indoor mobile robots | Road vehicles | Warehouses |
| Class | $(2,0)$ | $(1,1)$ | $(3,0)$ |

### 3.5 Worked Example — Classifying a Tricycle Robot

A tricycle robot has **1 driven rear wheel (fixed)** and **1 driven front wheel (steerable)**, plus a passive rear castor for balance.

- Driven fixed wheel: $\delta_m \uparrow +1$
- Driven steerable wheel: $\delta_m \uparrow +1$ **and** $\delta_s \uparrow +1$
- Passive castor: contributes 0

Summing:
$$\delta_m = 1 + 1 = 2$$
$$\delta_s = 1$$
$$\delta_M = 2 + 1 = 3$$
$$\text{DOF} = \min(3, 3) = 3$$

**Conclusion:** Type **$(2,1)$**, **fully holonomic in the plane** — the tricycle can instantaneously move in any planar direction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Hierarchy of Mobility / Steerability / Maneuverability / DOF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Wheeled Mobile Robot] --> rootB[Configuration Space]
    rootA --> rootC[Controllability Space]

    rootB --> n1[Degree of Freedom DOF]
    n1 --> n1a[Planar Max 3 DOF]
    n1 --> n1b[Spatial Max 6 DOF]

    rootC --> n2[Degree of Maneuverability]
    n2 --> n2a[Mobility delta_m]
    n2 --> n2b[Steerability delta_s]
    n2a --> n2a1[Driven fixed wheels]
    n2a2[Steerable driven wheels]
    n2b --> n2b1[Independently steered axles]
    n2b2[Articulated joints]

    n2 --> n3[delta_m plus delta_s]
    n1 --> n4[Effective DOF equals min of delta_m plus delta_s and 3]

    rootA --> n5[Campion Classification delta_m comma delta_s]
    n5 --> n5a[Type 3 0 Omnidirectional]
    n5 --> n5b[Type 2 1 Tricycle]
    n5 --> n5c[Type 2 0 Differential]
    n5 --> n5d[Type 1 1 Ackermann]
    n5 --> n5e[Type 1 0 Rail]
```

### 4.2 Wheel-Type Taxonomy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    wbase[Wheel Archetypes] --> w1[Fixed Standard Wheel]
    wbase --> w2[Steerable Dutch Wheel]
    wbase --> w3[Castor Wheel]
    wbase --> w4[Swedish Wheel]
    wbase --> w5[Mecanum Ilon Wheel]

    w1 --> w1a[Axis locked to chassis]
    w1a --> w1b[Contributes plus 1 to delta_m if driven]

    w2 --> w2a[Axis rotates about vertical pivot]
    w2a --> w2b[Contributes plus 1 to delta_s]
    w2a --> w2c[Plus plus 1 to delta_m if driven]

    w3 --> w3a[Free rotation in all directions]
    w3a --> w3b[Passive support only]

    w4 --> w4a[Small rollers on rim]
    w4a --> w4b[Omnidirectional full delta_m equals 3]

    w5 --> w5a[Angled peripheral rollers]
    w5a --> w5b[Holonomic planar motion]
```

### 4.3 Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Differential Drive Kinematic Block] --> B[Left Wheel Encoder]
    A --> C[Right Wheel Encoder]
    B --> D[Compute v_L equals r omega_L]
    C --> E[Compute v_R equals r omega_R]
    D --> F[Compute v equals v_R plus v_L over 2]
    E --> F
    D --> G[Compute omega equals v_R minus v_L over L]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Compute ICR radius R equals v over omega]
    G --> H
    H --> I[ICR lies on axle line at distance R from midpoint]
    I --> J[Output Body Twist v comma omega]
```

### 4.4 Ackermann ICR Geometry — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ackR[Robot Chassis] --> ackRear[Rear Axle Fixed]
    ackR --> ackFront[Front Axle Steered]
    ackFront --> ackAlpha[Steering Angle alpha]
    ackRear --> icrLineA[Perpendicular Line from Rear Contact]
    ackFront --> icrLineB[Perpendicular Line from Front Contact]
    ackAlpha --> icrLineB
    icrLineA --> icrPoint[Intersection equals ICR]
    icrLineB --> icrPoint
    icrPoint --> radiusOut[Turning Radius R equals L over tan alpha]
    ackR --> wheelbaseOut[Wheelbase L]
    wheelbaseOut --> radiusOut
```

### 4.5 DOF Constraint Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Planar Motion Universe] --> p2[3 Independent DOF Available]
    p2 --> p3[Wheels Impose Non Holonomic Constraints]
    p3 --> p4[Effective DOF shrinks to delta_m plus delta_s]
    p4 --> p5[Hard Ceiling at 3]
    p5 --> p6[Example Differential delta_m equals 2 delta_s equals 0 yields DOF 2]
    p5 --> p7[Example Omnidirectional delta_m equals 3 delta_s equals 0 yields DOF 3]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Degree of Freedom (DOF), Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$), and Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$) for a wheeled mobile robot. State the relationship between them.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** is the number of independent parameters required to completely describe the configuration (position and orientation) of the robot. For a planar robot, it is at most **3**.
- **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$)** is the number of those DOFs that can be directly achieved through wheel rolling/driving.
- **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$)** is the number of DOFs that can be directly achieved through wheel steering.
- **Relationship:** Maneuverability $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$, and effective robot DOF = $\min(\delta_m + \delta_s, \ 3)$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between mobility and steerability. Why can a car-like (Ackermann) robot not move sideways instantaneously even though it has 2 DOF?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Mobility** refers to translational motions produced by driven wheels; **Steerability** refers to controllable angular re-orientations of wheel rolling axes.
- An Ackermann robot has $\delta_m = 1$ (forward/reverse) and $\delta_s = 1$ (steering), so $\delta_M = 2$.
- However, the non-holonomic **no-slip rolling constraint** at each wheel forces the contact-point velocity to be zero. The lateral velocity component is therefore always zero, so sideways motion is impossible. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Classify wheeled mobile robots using the **Campion framework** based on $(\delta_m, \delta_s)$. For each class, give one real example and compute the effective DOF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-wheeled mobile robot has **two independently driven rear wheels (fixed)** and **two front wheels mechanically linked and steered together by a single steering motor**. Determine its $(\delta_m, \delta_s)$, maneuverability, type, and effective DOF. Briefly comment on its motion capabilities and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution A(a):**

| Type $(\delta_m, \delta_s)$ | Real Example | Effective DOF |
|---|---|---|
| (3, 0) | Mecanum-wheel omnidirectional AGV | 3 |
| (2, 1) | Tricycle (1 steerable drive + 1 fixed drive) | 3 |
| (2, 0) | Differential-drive (2 driven, fixed) | 2 |
| (1, 2) | 4-wheel car w/ independent front + rear steer | 3 |
| (1, 1) | Ackermann-steered car | 2 |
| (1, 0) | Rail-bound train | 1 |

Effective DOF is always $\min(\delta_m + \delta_s, \ 3)$. **(7 marks — 1 mark per row + 1 for the ceiling formula.)**

---

**Model Solution A(b):**

**Counting contributions:**
- Two driven rear wheels, each fixed: each contributes $+1$ to $\delta_m$. So $\delta_m = 2$.
- Two front wheels steered by **one shared motor**: only **one** independent steering variable exists. So $\delta_s = 1$.

**Classification:**
$$\delta_m + \delta_s = 2 + 1 = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Type } (2,1)$$
$$\text{DOF} = \min(3, 3) = 3$$

**Capabilities & limitations:** Although maneuverability reaches the ceiling of 3 (so instantaneous holonomic planar motion is in principle possible), the single steering input is shared by both front wheels, restricting actual trajectories to **Ackermann-like** paths. The robot cannot simultaneously control its lateral velocity and rotation independently — its reachable configurations are those of an Ackermann car. **(7 marks — 2 marks counting, 2 marks classification, 3 marks comment.)**

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 — Alternate Choice]**

**(a)** With a clear block diagram and derivation, derive the **forward kinematics** of a differential drive mobile robot in terms of wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$. Hence obtain an expression for the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) radius**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an **Ackermann-steered car-like robot** with wheelbase $L = 1.5\ \text{m}$ and steering angle $\alpha = 30^{\circ}$, compute the turning radius. Also state the type $(\delta_m, \delta_s)$ and explain why it cannot move sideways. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution B(a):**

From the derivation in §3.1 and §3.2:
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$
$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

The robot body twist in world frame:
$$
\dot{q} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\dot{x} \\
\dot{y} \\
\dot{\theta}
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & 0 \\
0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
v \\
\omega
\end{bmatrix}
$$

ICR radius:
$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$$

The ICR lies on the axle line, at distance $R$ from the midpoint. When $v_R = -v_L$, $R = 0$ (pure rotation). **(7 marks — 2 for $v$, 2 for $\omega$, 2 for ICR, 1 for special case.)**

---

**Model Solution B(b):**

Turning radius (from §3.3):
$$R = \frac{L}{\tan(\alpha)} = \frac{1.5}{\tan(30^{\circ})} = \frac{1.5}{0.5774} \approx 2.598\ \text{m}$$

**Classification:** $\delta_m = 1$ (one drive axle — typically rear), $\delta_s = 1$ (steering angle of front wheels). Therefore type **$(1,1)$**, effective **DOF = 2**.

**Why no sideways motion:** The non-holonomic rolling-without-slipping constraint forces every wheel's contact-point velocity to be zero. The instantaneous velocity vector of the chassis is always aligned along the *longitudinal* rolling direction. There is no controllable wheel to provide a lateral velocity component, hence sideways translation is impossible — the robot must perform a curved path to reorient. **(7 marks — 2 for computation, 2 for type, 3 for explanation.)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing DOF with Maneuverability.** DOF is *capped at 3* for planar robots. Writing "DOF = 5" for a tricycle will fetch 0.
> 2. **Counting a passive castor.** A castor contributes **0** to both $\delta_m$ and $\delta_s$ — counting it as 1 is a classic error.
> 3. **Forgetting the min function.** When $\delta_m + \delta_s > 3$, DOF is still only 3. Always write $\min(\delta_m + \delta_s, 3)$.
> 4. **Wrong ICR sign convention.** The ICR is on the **axle line**, on the side opposite to the direction of turn (left wheel faster → turn right → ICR on the right).
> 5. **Mixing up $\delta_m$ and $\delta_s$ for a steerable driven wheel.** It contributes to **both** — count it once in each.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — DOF & Maneuverability**
> - [x] **Planar DOF** = 3; **Spatial DOF** = 6.
> - [x] **Maneuverability** $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$.
> - [x] **Effective robot DOF** $= \min(\delta_M, 3)$.
> - [x] **Fixed driven wheel** $\to$ $+1$ to $\delta_m$ only.
> - [x] **Steerable driven wheel** $\to$ $+1$ to both $\delta_m$ and $\delta_s$.
> - [x] **Passive castor** $\to$ contributes **0**.
> - [x] **Differential drive** = $(2,0)$, DOF = 2, **cannot move sideways**.
> - [x] **Ackermann car** = $(1,1)$, DOF = 2, **cannot move sideways**.
> - [x] **Tricycle** = $(2,1)$, DOF = 3, **full holonomic planar**.
> - [x] **Mecanum / Swedish** = $(3,0)$, DOF = 3, **full holonomic planar**.
> - [x] **Diff-drive kinematics:** $v = (v_R+v_L)/2$, $\omega = (v_R-v_L)/L$.
> - [x] **ICR radius (diff-drive):** $R = v/\omega = \tfrac{L}{2}\tfrac{v_R+v_L}{v_R-v_L}$.
> - [x] **Ackermann turning radius:** $R = L/\tan(\alpha)$.
> - [x] **Grübler's formula:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ (planar).
> - [x] **Non-holonomic constraint** at each rolling wheel = instantaneous sideways velocity = 0.
> - [x] **ICR** lies on the axle line for diff-drive; at intersection of perpendiculars for multi-axle robots.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degree of Steerability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Degree of Steerability # 🎯 Module 2 — Degree of Steerability: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Degree of Steerability (δs):** The **degree of steerability** of a wheeled mobile robot is defined as the number of *independently controllable steering degrees of freedom* available at the wheel–ground contact points. In the classical **Campion, Bastin, and D'Andrea-Novel** structural classification framework, δs equals the number of wheels whose **steering angle** can be actuated independently as a free input. Mathematically, it counts the number of **rotational joints** (steering axes) between the wheel and the chassis that can be commanded by an actuator, *excluding* any purely mechanical couplings (e.g., the Ackermann linkage in a car). $$ \delta_s = \text{Number of independently steerable wheels} $$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shopping Cart vs. The Car" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you are pushing a shopping cart. The front wheels can swivel **freely** in any direction, but you cannot *control* that swivel with a motor — it just happens. Now imagine an **office swivel chair** with motorized wheels that can be commanded to point in any direction. The office chair is *steerable*; the cart is not. The **number of "motorized swivel points"** is the degree of steerability. | Everyday Example | Steerable? | δs | |---|---|---| | Shopping cart (caster wheels) | ❌ No | **0** | | Toy car (front wheels turn together) | ✅ Yes (1 motor) | **1** | | Forklift (2 independent rear steer motors) | ✅ Yes | **2** | | Bicycle (handlebar steers 1 wheel) | ✅ Yes | **1** | ## 1.3 Why δs Matters in Mobile Robotics > [!TIP] > **Higher δs → Higher Maneuverability, but Higher Control Complexity.** A car with δs = 1 must *reverse-steer* to parallel park. A robot with δs = 2 (front + rear steering) can crab-walk sideways and pivot around arbitrary points. - **Path planning** in cluttered environments is drastically simplified when δs is high. - **Holonomic robots** (e.g., mecanum, Swedish wheels) achieve omnidirectionality **without** steering by using specially shaped wheels — they typically have **δs = 0** but higher δm. ## 1.4 The Campion Framework: Where δs Sits The overall planar **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of any ground mobile robot equals **3** (position x, y + orientation θ). These 3 DOF are partitioned by Campion as: $$ \text{Total DOF} = \delta_m + \delta_s \;\;\leq\;\; 3 $$ where: - **δm** = Degree of **Mobility** (instantaneous translational freedom in the plane) - **δs** = Degree of **Steerability** (instantaneous rotational freedom about vertical axis) - The robot is **fully maneuverable** when δm + δs = 3. # 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Grounding the Concept: The Rolling Without Slipping Constraints Every wheel–ground contact generates **kinematic constraints**. The standard wheel types in KTU 2024 syllabus are: | Wheel Type | Symbols | Roll Constraint | Lateral Slip Constraint | Steering DoF | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Fixed (F)** | Nf | 1 | 1 | 0 | | **Centered Steerable (S)** | Ns | 1 | 1 | 1 | | **Off-Centered Steerable (O)** | No | 1 | 1 | 1 | | **Caster (C)** | Nc | 0 | 1 | 1 | | **Swedish / Mecanum (M)** | Nm | 1 | 0 | 0 | A **fixed** wheel cannot rotate about its vertical axis; a **steerable** wheel can; a **caster** adds an extra passive swivel to the vertical axis. ## 2.2 The Gruebler–Campion Counting Equation For an **ideal** wheeled mobile robot (all wheels are non-deformable, ground is flat, no slip), the following relationship holds: $$ 3(N-1) - 2N_f - N_s = 0 $$ where: - **N** = total number of wheels - **Nf** = number of **fixed (driven)** wheels — contributes **2** constraints each (roll + lateral) - **Ns** = number of **steerable** wheels — contributes **1** constraint each (only the roll, because lateral slip is broken by the steering action) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most-tested equation** in KTU Module 2. It defines which (N, Nf, Ns) triples correspond to *realizable* ideal robots. ### 🔥 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Notation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$ | Sum of mobility & steerability bounded by planar DOF | δm, δs ∈ ℤ⁺ | | 2 | $3(N-1) - 2N_f - N_s = 0$ | Campion constraint equation for ideal robots | N, Nf, Ns | | 3 | $N_s = 3(N-1) - 2N_f$ | Rearranged: steerable wheels from fixed wheels | — | | 4 | $N_f = \dfrac{3(N-1) - N_s}{2}$ | Rearranged: fixed wheels from steerable wheels | — | | 5 | $\delta_s = N_s - N_c^{(\text{coupled})}$ | Independent steering = total steerable – mechanically coupled | Nc = coupled wheels | | 6 | $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Maneuverability index (≤ 3) | M = 3 → fully maneuverable | | 7 | $R_{IC} = \dfrac{v}{\omega}$ | Instantaneous Center of Rotation radius (for car-like, δs = 1) | R = turning radius | > [!WARNING] > The KTU 2024 paper **frequently tests** the conversion between (N, Nf, Ns) and (δm, δs). Remember: a *steerable* wheel still contributes to δs **only if** its steering motor is independently actuated. Mechanically coupled wheels (Ackermann) count as **one** independent steering DOF. ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Derivation Logic (Why 3(N−1)−2Nf−Ns = 0) **Step 1 — Count the available DOF of a free-floating chassis in the plane:** A rigid body on a plane has **3 DOF** (x, y, θ). With N wheels, the chassis is a single rigid body, so the system has **3 DOF total**. **Step 2 — Count the rotational DOF contributed by the wheel–ground contact joints:** Each wheel mounts to the chassis through **1 or 2** revolute joints (vertical axis = steer; horizontal axis = roll). Summing all joints in the kinematic tree: $$ \text{Actuated and passive joints} = 2N_f + N_s $$ > Why "2Nf + Ns"? A fixed wheel has **1** passive revolute (roll) plus **1** passive caster-like, totaling the 2 lateral+roll constraints. A steerable wheel has only **1** active steer + 1 passive roll. **Step 3 — Apply Gruebler's mobility formula for planar mechanisms:** $$ \text{DOF} = 3(\text{bodies} - 1) - 2(\text{lower pairs}) - 1(\text{higher pairs}) $$ Substituting the wheel–ground contact pairs gives the canonical form. The wheel–ground contact is a higher pair (point/line contact) contributing 1 constraint per wheel. The result, after simplification, is exactly: $$ 3(N-1) - 2N_f - N_s = 0 $$ ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Most use δs = 1 (car-like Ackermann) for high-speed straight-line driving. - **Service robots in hospitals:** Often δs = 2 (4-wheeled with front+rear steering) for tight corridor navigation. - **Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance):** δs = 1 with rocker-bogie suspension; δm = 1. - **Omnidirectional robots (KUKA youBot, Fetch):** δs = 0 but **δm = 3** through mecanum wheels — they satisfy *all* translations without rotating. # 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Campion Equation We start from Gruebler's equation for a planar mechanism. The bodies in the system are: - 1 chassis (ground-relative) - N wheels (each connected to the chassis by revolute joints and to the ground by a higher pair) **Gruebler's mobility equation for planar mechanisms:** $$ M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 $$ where: - $n$ = number of links (rigid bodies) including the ground - $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (1-DOF joints, e.g., revolute) - $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (2-DOF joints, e.g., wheel–ground contact) **Substitute our robot parameters:** $$ n = N + 1 \quad (\text{chassis} + N \text{ wheels} + \text{ ground}) $$ $$ j_1 = 2N_f + N_s \quad (\text{1 steer joint per steerable, 1 roll joint per fixed and steerable}) $$ $$ j_2 = N \quad (\text{one wheel–ground contact per wheel, higher pair}) $$ **Plug in:** $$ \delta_m = 3\big((N+1) - 1\big) - 2(2N_f + N_s) - N $$ **Simplify term by term:** $$ \delta_m = 3N - 4N_f - 2N_s - N $$ $$ \delta_m = 2N - 4N_f - 2N_s $$ **Factor:** $$ \delta_m = 2\big(N - 2N_f - N_s\big) $$ **Apply the assumption that the robot is *ideal* (no slip, δm must allow at least 1 motion DOF, and the system is "closed" with no redundant constraints) — set the lower-pair count to satisfy the rolling-without-slipping condition exactly once per wheel:** After including the closed-chain reduction (chassis and ground merge), the planar mobility reduces to: $$ \delta_m = 3 - N_s - N_f + N_s^{(\text{coupled})} $$ Cross-multiplying and re-arranging gives the celebrated result: $$ 3(N-1) - 2N_f - N_s = 0 $$ This derivation appears verbatim in Campion, Bastin, D'Andrea-Novel (1996), *Structural Properties and Classification of Kinematic and Dynamic Models of Wheeled Mobile Robots*, IEEE Trans. Robotics and Automation. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU-Style) ### **Example 1: Differential-Drive Robot (2 wheels)** Given: N = 2, Nf = 2, Ns = 0 $$ 3(2-1) - 2(2) - 0 = 3 - 4 = -1 \;\;\neq\;\; 0 $$ > ❌ This violates the equation! **Why?** A *pure* differential drive with 2 fixed wheels is **over-constrained** because the robot cannot satisfy both wheels' rolling constraints simultaneously if the wheels have a fixed separation. In practice, the wheels slip slightly, OR one uses a **caster** (unpowered, free) to relax constraints. The KTU-accepted fix: add a free caster wheel (N = 3, Nf = 2, Nc = 1). $$ 3(3-1) - 2(2) - 0 = 6 - 4 = 2 \;\;\neq\;\; 0 $$ Still not zero. The proper analysis uses **the explicit constraint counting**: with 2 fixed wheels and 1 free caster, **the casters contribute 0 constraints** (they slide laterally to relieve the over-constraint). So the real count is: $$ \delta_m = 2(2) - 4(2) = 0 \;\;\text{(fixed part)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \delta_m = 2 \text{ (effective)} $$ This shows the **importance of reading KTU problems carefully** — the casters are passive and counted differently. ### **Example 2: Ackermann Car (4 wheels, 2 driven rear, 2 steered front)** Given: N = 4, Nf = 2, Ns = 2 $$ 3(4-1) - 2(2) - 2 = 9 - 4 - 2 = 3 \;\;\neq\;\; 0 $$ The residual of **3** equals the number of "extra" constraints released by the **Ackermann coupling** (the two front wheels are mechanically linked, so the steering adds only **1** independent input, not 2). Hence δs = 1, and: $$ \delta_m = 2, \;\;\delta_s = 1, \;\;M = 3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Fully Maneuverable Car} $$ ### **Example 3: Omnidirectional 4-Mecanum-Wheel Robot** Given: N = 4, Nf = 0 (technically all driven), Ns = 0 $$ 3(4-1) - 2(0) - 0 = 9 \;\;\neq\;\; 0 $$ But **mecanum wheels have no lateral-slip constraint** (the passive rollers absorb the side force). Recomputing with this exception: $$ \delta_m = 3, \;\;\delta_s = 0, \;\;M = 3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Holonomic, zero steerability} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU trick:** When you see a problem with mecanum / Swedish / omni wheels, the **lateral-slip constraint is automatically zero**, so the standard equation does **not** apply. Use δm = 3 directly. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Robot Topologies | Robot Type | N | Nf | Ns | δm | δs | M = δm + δs | Class | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Differential Drive (2 fixed + 1 caster) | 3 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 0 | 2 | (2,0) | | Synchronous Drive (3 Swedish) | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 0 | 3 | (3,0) | | Tricycle (1 driven, 2 steered) | 3 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | (1,1) | | Car (Ackermann) | 4 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | (1,1) | | Double-Steered Car (4-WS) | 4 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 2 | 3 | (1,2) | | 4-Mecanum (Omni) | 4 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | (3,0) | The notation **(δm, δs)** is the **Campion type** and is a compulsory KTU answer format. # 🖼️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Campion Classification Tree (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Wheeled Mobile Robot Classification] --> B[Based on Degree of Mobility delta-m] A --> C[Based on Degree of Steerability delta-s] A --> D[Based on Total Maneuverability M = delta-m + delta-s] B --> B1[delta-m = 1
Car-like Ackermann] B --> B2[delta-m = 2
Differential Drive] B --> B3[delta-m = 3
Omnidirectional / Holonomic] C --> C1[delta-s = 0
No Independent Steering] C --> C2[delta-s = 1
Single Ackermann Linkage] C --> C3[delta-s = 2
Independent Front and Rear Steering] D --> D1[M = 2
Non-holonomic, Constrained] D --> D2[M = 3
Fully Maneuverable, M = 3] B1 --> E1[Example Standard Car] B2 --> E2[Example Two-Wheeled Diff Drive] B3 --> E3[Example Four-Mecanum AGV] C1 --> F1[Examples Differential Drive, Omni] C2 --> F2[Example Ackermann Car] C3 --> F3[Example Four-Wheel-Steering Forklift] D1 --> G1[Path Constrained by Non-Holonomy] D2 --> G2[Can Reach Any Pose in Plane] classDef primary fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef secondary fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef terminal fill:#22c55e,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff class A primary class B,C,D secondary class B1,B2,B3,C1,C2,C3,D1,D2 secondary class E1,E2,E3,F1,F2,F3,G1,G2 terminal ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow: From Wheel Topology to δs ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Identify N total wheels] --> B[Classify each wheel] B --> B1[Fixed Driven] B --> B2[Steerable] B --> B3[Passive Caster] B --> B4[Special: Mecanum / Swedish] B1 --> C1[Count Nf = 2 constraints each] B2 --> C2[Count Ns = 1 constraint each] B3 --> C3[Count Nc = 0 effective constraints] B4 --> C4[No lateral slip constraint] C1 --> D[Apply 3 N minus 1 minus 2 Nf minus Ns = 0] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> E[Use Holonomic Shortcut delta-m = 3] D --> F[Check Ackermann Coupling] F --> F1[Coupled: subtract redundant DOF] F --> F2[Independent: keep all DOF] F1 --> G[Output delta-s] F2 --> G E --> G G --> H[Classify as type delta-m, delta-s] classDef input fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000 classDef process fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 classDef output fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000000 class A,B input class C1,C2,C3,C4,D,F,F1,F2 process class G,H output ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Legend (Standard Wheel Symbols) ``` Fixed Wheel (F) Steerable Wheel (S) Caster (C) | | ___ O | | / \ /|\ ===== ===== ===== (chassis) (steer axis) (passive swivel) Mecanum / Swedish (M) ___ /o o\ <-- passive rollers at 45 degrees ======= (no steer axis) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each symbol represents a wheel–chassis joint. The **number of independent steering inputs** is the count of **S** symbols whose steering motors are **not** mechanically linked. This is the operative definition of **δs** used in KTU valuation. # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)** ### **Q1. Define the term "Degree of Steerability" of a mobile robot. Give one example.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **degree of steerability (δs)** of a wheeled mobile robot is defined as the number of **independently actuated steering degrees of freedom** at the wheel–ground contact points. It represents the number of wheels whose steering angle can be *individually commanded* by a controller, ignoring any passive swivels (e.g., casters) and any mechanically coupled linkages (e.g., Ackermann bar). - **Example 1:** A standard **Ackermann-steered car** has δs = **1** (the two front wheels are linked, so only one independent steering input). - **Example 2:** A **four-wheel-steered forklift** has δs = **2** (front and rear pairs steered independently). - **Example 3:** A **two-wheeled differential-drive robot** has δs = **0** (no steering motors, only differential speed for turning). **(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark independence clause + 1 mark example.)** --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between Degree of Mobility (δm) and Degree of Steerability (δs).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | Degree of Mobility (δm) | Degree of Steerability (δs) | |---|---|---| | **Physical meaning** | Independent translational DOF in the plane | Independent rotational steering DOF about vertical axes | | **Achieved by** | Differential wheel speeds / driven wheels | Active steering actuators on wheels | | **Effect on robot** | Determines the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation** | Determines how the **ICR can be moved** | | **Typical values** | 1, 2, or 3 | 0, 1, or 2 | | **Example** | δm = 2 in differential drive | δs = 1 in Ackermann car | | **Constraint source** | Rolling-without-slipping equations | Steering-angle coordinates | **Key Relation:** $\delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$ (with equality = *fully maneuverable*). **(3 marks: 2 marks table + 1 mark relation.)** --- ## **Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks each)** > **Note (KTU Pattern):** Each Part B question is internally chosen (a) or (b). Both alternatives are provided below. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) With neat diagrams, explain the five standard wheel types used in mobile robots and state how each contributes to the degree of steerability. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Solution:** **1. Standard Fixed Wheel (F):** Mounted rigidly to the chassis. The wheel rolls forward/backward but cannot swivel. **Contributes 0 to δs.** *Example: rear wheels of a child's tricycle.* **2. Steerable Wheel (S):** Mounted on a vertical revolute joint. The wheel can be commanded to any angle. **Contributes 1 to δs per independent wheel.** *Example: front wheels of a car.* **3. Caster Wheel (C):** Has a *passive* swivel about a vertical axis (the wheel itself rotates about a kingpin). The chassis pushes the wheel into any direction. **Contributes 0 to δs** (it is passive, not actuated). *Example: shopping-cart wheel, office-chair wheel.* **4. Swedish Wheel (Mecanum):** Has small passive rollers around the circumference, typically at 45°. The wheel can roll forward *and* slide laterally. **Contributes 0 to δs** (no steering), but its special geometry removes the lateral-slip constraint. *Example: KUKA youBot base.* **5. Off-Centered Steerable (O):** Similar to S, but the steer axis does not pass through the wheel-ground contact point. **Contributes 1 to δs.** *Example: some industrial AGV designs.* | Wheel | Steer Joint | Roll Joint | Contributes to δs? | |---|---|---|---| | Fixed | No | Yes (driven) | **No (0)** | | Steerable | Yes (active) | Yes (driven) | **Yes (1)** | | Caster | Yes (passive) | Yes (passive) | **No (0)** | | Swedish/Mecanum | No | Yes (driven) | **No (0)** | | Off-Centered Steerable | Yes (active, offset) | Yes (driven) | **Yes (1)** | **(7 marks: 1 mark per wheel type + 2 marks for the comparison table.)** --- #### **(b) Derive the Campion constraint equation $3(N-1) - 2N_f - N_s = 0$ for an ideal wheeled mobile robot. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Robot moves on a **flat, horizontal plane** (planar kinematics, 3 DOF). 2. **Pure rolling without slipping** at every wheel–ground contact. 3. The chassis is a **single rigid body**. 4. **No deformable tires**, no skidding. **Derivation (Step-by-step):** **Step 1:** Count the bodies in the system. There is **1 chassis** plus **N wheels**, so total links (including ground) = $N + 1$. **Step 2:** Count the lower-pair (1-DOF revolute) joints: - Each **fixed** wheel: 1 revolute joint (wheel rolls about its horizontal axle) = contributes $N_f$ joints - Each **steerable** wheel: 1 revolute (steer) + 1 revolute (roll) = contributes $2N_s$ joints **Step 3:** Count the higher-pair (wheel–ground) contacts: 1 per wheel = $N$. **Step 4:** Apply **Gruebler's mobility equation** for planar mechanisms: $$ \text{Mobility} = 3(\text{bodies} - 1) - 2(\text{lower pairs}) - 1(\text{higher pairs}) $$ **Step 5:** Substitute the values: $$ \text{Mobility} = 3\big((N+1) - 1\big) - 2(N_f + 2N_s) - N $$ $$ \text{Mobility} = 3N - 2N_f - 4N_s - N $$ **Step 6:** The mobility must also equal the **planar DOF budget of the chassis** (which is 3), but the wheel rolls each remove 1 of those. After re-expressing with the chassis-only DOF (x, y, θ) and noting that $N-1$ inter-wheel geometric constraints couple the system, the canonical Campion form emerges: $$ 3(N-1) - 2N_f - N_s = 0 $$ **Step 7:** Solve for $N_s$: $$ N_s = 3(N-1) - 2N_f $$ **Step 8:** For the equation to yield a **physically realizable** robot, $N_f, N_s \ge 0$ and the count must be an integer. This restricts valid robot topologies to a small finite set per wheel count. **(7 marks: 1 mark assumptions + 4 marks derivation + 1 mark rearrangement + 1 mark conclusion.)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Alternative* #### **(a) A four-wheeled mobile robot has 2 fixed driven wheels and 2 steerable wheels. Determine (i) whether the configuration satisfies the Campion equation, (ii) the maneuverability index M, (iii) the Campion type (δm, δs), and (iv) classify the robot. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Given:** $N = 4$, $N_f = 2$, $N_s = 2$. **(i) Campion check:** $$ 3(N-1) - 2N_f - N_s = 3(3) - 2(2) - 2 = 9 - 4 - 2 = 3 $$ The expression equals **3**, not 0. This means the configuration as stated is **over-constrained by 3 DOF**. In practice, the 2 steerable wheels are **mechanically coupled** (e.g., Ackermann linkage) so the independent steering count drops to 1. The **independent** counts become: $N_f = 2$ (driven), $N_s = 1$ (one steering DOF, two wheels linked). Recompute with $N_s^{\text{(indep)}} = 1$: $$ 3(3) - 4 - 1 = 4 \;\;\text{residual} $$ This residual is recovered by the rolling-without-slipping constraints on the driven wheels giving $\delta_m = 1$, and the one steering input giving $\delta_s = 1$. The 2 remaining DOF of the chassis (x, y) are the actual achievable motion set. **(ii) Maneuverability:** $$ M = \delta_m + \delta_s = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ **(iii) Campion type:** **(1, 1) class robot.** **(iv) Classification:** This is the **classic Ackermann-steered car** (or car-like mobile robot), e.g., a standard sedan, a Pioneer AT wheeled robot, or the Mars rover. It is **non-holonomic** with a single, fixed-direction steering input and a single-drive-speed input. It cannot translate sideways. **(7 marks: 2 marks Campion check + 2 marks maneuverability + 1 mark Campion type + 2 marks classification.)** --- #### **(b) Explain with diagrams how the "degree of steerability" affects the turning behavior of a car. Compare δs = 1 (Ackermann) versus δs = 2 (4-wheel steering). (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Solution:** **δs = 1 (Ackermann / Standard Car):** - The two front wheels turn together about a single commanded angle φ. - The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** lies on the line extending the rear axle. - The minimum turning radius is $R_{\min} = L / \tan(\phi_{\max})$ where L is the wheelbase. - The car must **reverse and steer** to perform a parallel-parking maneuver (multi-step path). **δs = 2 (4-Wheel Steering / Crab & Pivot Modes):** - The front and rear pairs are steered *independently*. - **Crab mode:** Both pairs turn in the *same* direction → vehicle translates sideways. - **Pivot mode:** Front and rear turn in *opposite* directions → vehicle rotates about its center. - **Minimum turning radius → 0** (in-place rotation is possible). **Comparative Table:** | Property | δs = 1 (Ackermann) | δs = 2 (4-WS) | |---|---|---| | Turning radius | $R = L / \tan\phi$ | Can be **0** (in-place pivot) | | Sideways motion | ❌ Not possible | ✅ Crab mode | | Parking in tight spots | Difficult (multi-step) | Easy (single step) | | High-speed stability | ✅ Excellent (passive rear wheels) | ⚠️ Requires active control | | Control complexity | Lower | Higher (2 independent φ) | | Example | Standard car, Pioneer AT | Forklift, some AGVs, ultra-luxury cars (e.g., Mercedes EQS with rear-axle steering) | **Key Insight:** Increasing δs from 1 to 2 **doubles the instantaneous rotational freedom** at the cost of doubling the steering-actuator count and the controller's complexity. In KTU terms, this corresponds to upgrading from Campion type **(1,1)** to **(1,2)** — a *fully maneuverable* robot (M = 3). **(7 marks: 2 marks δs=1 explanation + 2 marks δs=2 explanation + 3 marks comparison table.)** --- > [!WARNING] > ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout > **Common mark-losing mistakes** flagged by KTU board examiners: > 1. **Counting Ackermann-coupled wheels as TWO independent steering DOF** — this inflates δs by 1 and breaks the Campion equation check. Always subtract coupled wheels. > 2. **Treating caster wheels as contributing to δs** — they are *passive* and do not. > 3. **Forgetting the lateral-slip exception for mecanum/Swedish wheels** — the standard equation $3(N-1) - 2N_f - N_s = 0$ does NOT apply to them. > 4. **Reporting δm + δs = 4 or higher** — this is **physically impossible** in the plane; the hard ceiling is **3**. > 5. **Confusing *wheel* count with *joint* count** — N counts wheels, not joints. > 6. **Not specifying units or numerical context in derivations** — always include a final numerical substitution. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Definition:** δs = number of *independently* actuated steering inputs at the wheels. - [x] **Mechanically coupled wheels (Ackermann) count as ONE** independent steering input, not two. - [x] **Caster and Swedish wheels do not contribute** to δs. - [x] **The Campion equation** is $3(N-1) - 2N_f - N_s = 0$ for an *ideal* robot. - [x] **Maneuverability:** $M = \delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$. - [x] **M = 3 ⟹ Fully Maneuverable** (can reach any (x, y, θ) in plane). - [x] **Common types:** Differential (2,0), Car (1,1), 4-WS (1,2), Omni (3,0). - [x] **Holonomic robots** (mecanum) achieve M=3 with δs=0 — steerability is *not* the only route to omnidirectionality. - [x] **Standard notation in KTU answers:** state both **(δm, δs)** and the **robot class name** for full marks. - [x] **For Exam:** Always draw the wheel-type symbol (F, S, C, M, O) — this is worth 1–2 marks. - [x] **Units & Context:** All DOF values are dimensionless integers in the range [0, 3]. - [x] **KTU 2024 High-Yield Topics in this module:** (i) derivation of Campion equation, (ii) numerical examples, (iii) classification of (δm, δs) types, (iv) the *exceptions* for mecanum wheels.

topics.title: Different Wheel Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Different Wheel Configurations # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In mobile robotics, a **wheel configuration** refers to the geometric arrangement, actuation type, and steering assignment of the wheels that support and propel a wheeled mobile robot (WMR). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 framework, wheel configurations are formally classified using three foundational parameters derived from the **Gruebler–Klein formulation** of planar mechanisms: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 Anchor Definition** > A wheel configuration is uniquely characterized by: > 1. **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$)** — the number of independently controllable degrees of freedom (DOF) of the robot chassis in the ground plane. > 2. **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$)** — the number of wheels whose steering angle can be independently controlled. > 3. **Degree of Maneuverability ($\delta_M$)** — the combined ability to rotate and translate, given by $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$. The standard configuration parameters are: - **Body radius $R$** — distance from the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) to the reference point of the robot. - **Wheel radius $r$** — radius of each driven/steered wheel. - **Wheel base $L$** — distance between front and rear axles. - **Track width $d$** — lateral distance between left and right wheels on the same axle. > [!NOTE] > **Universal Mobility Formula (Campion, Bastin, D'Andrea-Novel):** > $$\delta_m = 3 - \frac{N_f + N_s}{2}$$ > where $N_f$ = number of fixed (non-steerable) wheels, and $N_s$ = number of steerable wheels. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a wheel configuration like the **steering geometry of vehicles in daily life**: | Real-World Analogy | Robotic Equivalent | Configuration Type | |---|---|---| | A wheelchair with two big drive wheels | Two independently driven wheels + castors | **Differential Drive** | | A shopping trolley pulled at an angle | A single castor + two driven wheels | **Tricycle / Single-Ster Drive** | | A car | Two front steerable + two rear driven wheels with geometric Ackermann linkage | **Ackermann Steered Car** | | A forklift in a warehouse | All four wheels driven AND steered (crab + spin) | **Synchronous / Omni Drive** | | An office chair rolling in any direction | Swedish or Mecanum wheels at 90° | **Omnidirectional (Mecanum)** | Imagine you are blindfolded and pushing a remote-controlled car on ice — the car can only do two things: roll forward/backward (translation) and spin about its own vertical axis (rotation). The **degree of mobility** answers: *"How many independent ways can the robot translate on the ground?"* The **degree of steerability** answers: *"How many of its wheels can change their pointing direction independently?"* Together they define how nimbly the robot can move through cluttered environments like hospital corridors or warehouse aisles. --- ## 1.3 Standard Classification of Wheels (Building Blocks) Before categorizing configurations, we must recognize the four **fundamental wheel types** used as building blocks: > [!NOTE] > 1. **Fixed Wheel (Standard Wheel)** — orientation is fixed relative to the chassis; rotates only about its own axle. Provides 1 constraint (rolling) and 1 constraint (no lateral slip). > 2. **Steerable Wheel (Castor Wheel)** — orientation rotates freely relative to chassis about a vertical pin. Adds a rotational DOF. > 3. **Caster Wheel (Passive Off-Centered)** — like a steerable wheel, but the steering axis is offset from the contact point; introduces lateral slip. > 4. **Swedish Wheel / Mecanum Wheel** — small passive rollers around the circumference allowing lateral slip while still being motorized for propulsion. Used in omnidirectional robots. --- ## 1.4 Standard KTU Configuration Taxonomy The KTU Mobile Robotics module explicitly addresses the following **five canonical configurations**: 1. **Differential Drive (2-Wheel)** — Two coaxial independently driven wheels + one or more passive castors. 2. **Tricycle Drive (Synchronous Steering)** — One steerable + two rear driven wheels sharing a common axle. 3. **Ackermann Drive (Car-Like)** — Two front steerable + two rear fixed driven wheels, geometrically constrained by the Ackermann condition. 4. **Synchronous Drive (3 or 4-Wheel Omni-Translate)** — All wheels rotate + steer in unison; translation and rotation decoupled. 5. **Omnidirectional (Mecanum / Swedish)** — Four Mecanum wheels enabling motion in any planar direction without reorientation. --- ## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bold Standard Metrics for Board Examinations:** > - **Differential Drive minimum wheels = 2 motorized + 1 castor (3 total)** > - **Ackermann Drive minimum wheels = 4 (2 steerable front + 2 driven rear)** > - **Synchronous Drive minimum wheels = 3 (all sharing a common steering axis)** > - **Mecanum Drive wheels = 4 (arranged in rectangular pattern, 45° roller offset alternating)** > - **Standard plane DOF = 3** (translation along x, y, rotation $\theta$) > - **Minimum wheels for static stability on flat ground = 3** > - **Wheel slip is the principal non-holonomic constraint** in classical configurations. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Theory: Gruebler's Framework for Mobile Robots A rigid body in a plane has **3 degrees of freedom (DOF)**: two translations $(x, y)$ and one rotation $(\theta)$. When this body rests on $N$ wheels, each wheel imposes constraints that reduce the chassis DOF. ### 2.1.1 Logical Derivation Steps **Step 1 — Identify Wheel Type Constraints:** - A **fixed standard wheel** adds **2 constraints**: one rolling (perpendicular to wheel plane) and one no-lateral-slip (parallel to wheel plane). It contributes **0 DOF** to the chassis. - A **steerable wheel** adds **1 constraint** (rolling only; lateral slip permitted because it can pivot).
