<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate clearly between holonomic and non-holonomic constraints. Give one mechanical example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A constraint is **holonomic** if it can be expressed purely as an algebraic relation among the generalized coordinates without involving velocities, in the form $f(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n, t) = 0$. Such a constraint reduces the configuration space and is **integrable**.

A constraint is **non-holonomic** when it must be expressed at the velocity level as $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ and cannot be integrated into a configuration-level relation. Although it limits instantaneous motion directions, the robot remains **controllable** globally (Brockett's theorem).

- **Holonomic Example:** A simple pendulum constrained by $x^{2} + y^{2} = L^{2}$.
- **Non-Holonomic Example:** A car-like vehicle obeying $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for the examples.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State the three non-holonomic Pfaffian constraints for a differential-drive mobile robot and explain the physical meaning of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three Pfaffian constraints are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta &= 0 \\
\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta + b\,\dot{\theta} &= r\,\dot{\phi}_r \\
\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta - b\,\dot{\theta} &= r\,\dot{\phi}_l
\end{aligned}$$

**Physical Meaning:**
- **Equation 1:** No lateral slippage — the chassis cannot move sideways.
- **Equation 2:** The right wheel rolls without slipping on the ground.
- **Equation 3:** The left wheel rolls without slipping on the ground.

**Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for the equations + 1 mark for physical interpretation.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> Students commonly lose marks by: (i) forgetting to **substitute the constraints** into the Lagrangian before differentiating; (ii) writing the constraint matrix $A(q)$ transposed in the final equations (it must be $A^{T}\lambda$, not $\lambda^{T}A$); (iii) omitting **wheel inertias** in the kinetic energy; (iv) sign errors in the right/left wheel offsets (right wheel is at $+b$, left at $-b$); (v) failing to clearly separate kinematic from dynamic levels.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`(a)` **Explain the classification of mobile robots based on holonomic and non-holonomic constraints with suitable diagrams. Discuss the implications for control design.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Holonomic Robots (3 marks)**

Holonomic robots can move instantaneously in **any direction** within their workspace. Their constraints are integrable to configuration-level relations.

| Type | Wheels | Maneuverability |
|---|---|---|
| Omni-wheel | Small rollers at 90° | Full 2D translation |
| Mecanum | Diagonal rollers | Full 2D + rotation |
| Ball wheel | Spherical | Full 2D + rotation |

**2. Non-Holonomic Robots (2 marks)**

These obey Pfaffian velocity constraints that cannot be integrated. Motion is **path-dependent**.

- **Differential Drive:** Two independently driven wheels
- **Ackermann (Car):** Front steering, rear traction
- **Unicycle:** Single wheel with balancing

**3. Control Implications (2 marks)**

- Holonomic robots permit **decoupled trajectory tracking** using simple PID.
- Non-holonomic robots require **non-smooth feedback laws** (e.g., Brockett's necessary condition rules out continuous time-invariant stabilization). Methods include:
  - **Chained-form transformations**
  - **Time-varying controllers** (e.g., Pomet–Thuilot)
  - **Discontinuous sliding-mode control**

**(b)** **Derive the complete non-holonomic constraints in Pfaffian form for a differential-drive WMR. Hence obtain the kinematic model in terms of forward velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the configuration vector $q = [x, y, \theta, \phi_r, \phi_l]^{T}$ and define wheel radius $r$ and half-wheelbase $b$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the three constraints:
$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$
$$\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta + b\dot{\theta} - r\dot{\phi}_r = 0$$
$$\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta - b\dot{\theta} - r\dot{\phi}_l = 0$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Assemble $A(q)\dot{q}=0$ matrix form with rank 3.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Add and subtract to obtain:
$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_r + \dot{\phi}_l), \quad \omega = \frac{r}{2b}(\dot{\phi}_r - \dot{\phi}_l)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final kinematic model:
$$\begin{bmatrix}\dot{x}\\\dot{y}\\\dot{\theta}\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0\\\sin\theta & 0\\0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}v\\\omega\end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

`(a)` **For a differential-drive mobile robot, derive the equations of motion using the Euler-Lagrange method. Clearly state all assumptions.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Robot operates on flat horizontal ground ($V=0$).
- Wheels roll without slipping.
- No wheel slip in lateral direction.
- Constant wheel radius $r$, half-wheelbase $b$.

**Step 2 — Generalized coordinates (1 mark):**
$$q = [x, y, \theta, \phi_r, \phi_l]^{T}$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy (2 marks):**
$$T = \frac{1}{2}M(\dot{x}^{2}+\dot{y}^{2}) + \frac{1}{2}I_{z}\dot{\theta}^{2} + \frac{1}{2}I_{w}(\dot{\phi}_r^{2}+\dot{\phi}_l^{2})$$

**Step 4 — Lagrangian and Euler-Lagrange (2 marks):**

With $L = T$ (since $V = 0$) and applying:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i + \sum_{j}\lambda_j a_{ji}(q)$$

We obtain:
$$M\ddot{x} = \lambda_1\sin\theta + (\lambda_2+\lambda_3)\cos\theta$$
$$M\ddot{y} = -\lambda_1\cos\theta + (\lambda_2+\lambda_3)\sin\theta$$
$$I_{z}\ddot{\theta} = b(\lambda_2 - \lambda_3)$$
$$I_{w}\ddot{\phi}_r = \tau_r - r\lambda_2$$
$$I_{w}\ddot{\phi}_l = \tau_l - r\lambda_3$$

**Step 5 — Matrix assembly (1 mark):**
$$\text{diag}(M, M, I_{z}, I_{w}, I_{w})\,\ddot{q} + A^{T}(q)\lambda = B\tau$$

---

`(b)` **Reduce the derived model to obtain the dynamic equations in terms of linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$. Discuss the decoupling property.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the reduced velocity vector (1 mark):**
$$\eta = \begin{bmatrix}v\\\omega\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}\dfrac{r}{2} & \dfrac{r}{2}\\\dfrac{r}{2b} & -\dfrac{r}{2b}\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\dot{\phi}_r\\\dot{\phi}_l\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Invert the transformation (1 mark):**
$$\begin{bmatrix}\dot{\phi}_r\\\dot{\phi}_l\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}\dfrac{1}{r} & \dfrac{b}{r}\\\dfrac{1}{r} & -\dfrac{b}{r}\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}v\\\omega\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and substitute (2 marks):**

Differentiating $\eta$ and applying the Euler-Lagrange wheel equations:
$$\dot{v} = \frac{r}{2I_{w}}(\tau_r + \tau_l)$$
$$\dot{\omega} = \frac{r}{2bI_{w}}(\tau_r - \tau_l)$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form (1 mark):**
$$\begin{bmatrix}\dot{v}\\\dot{\omega}\end{bmatrix} = \frac{r}{2I_{w}}\begin{bmatrix}1 & 1\\\dfrac{1}{b} & -\dfrac{1}{b}\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\tau_r\\\tau_l\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Decoupling discussion (2 marks):**

The matrix above is **non-diagonal**, meaning the torques couple $v$ and $\omega$. However, a simple **diagonalizing control law** exists:
$$\tau_r = \tau_{v} + \tau_{\omega}, \quad \tau_l = \tau_{v} - \tau_{omega}$$

where $\tau_{v}$ commands linear motion and $\tau_{\omega}$ commands rotation, achieving **independent control of forward velocity and yaw rate**. This decoupling is a key advantage exploited by feedback-linearizing controllers in KTU-design problems.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams**

- **Holonomic:** Constraint $f(q,t)=0$, integrable, full instantaneous maneuverability.
- **Non-Holonomic:** $A(q)\dot{q}=0$, non-integrable, path-dependent motion.
- **Pfaffian constraints for differential drive:** three equations, rank 3, independent.
- **Forward velocity:** $v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_r + \dot{\phi}_l)$.
- **Angular velocity:** $\omega = \frac{r}{2b}(\dot{\phi}_r - \dot{\phi}_l)$.
- **Kinematic model:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
- **Euler-Lagrange form:** $\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = Q + A^{T}\lambda$.
- **Lagrangian:** $L = T - V$. For WMR on flat ground, $V = 0$.
- **Inertia matrix:** Diagonal $\text{diag}(M, M, I_z, I_w, I_w)$.
- **No Coriolis terms:** Because $T$ has no cross-velocity products.
- **Constraints enforced via multipliers:** $A^{T}(q)\lambda$ — never $\lambda^{T}A$.
- **Reduced dynamic model:** Decouples into $v$ and $\omega$ channels via symmetric control law.
- **Control implication:** Non-holonomic systems cannot be stabilized by any **continuous, time-invariant, smooth state feedback** (Brockett's theorem).
- **Real-world link:** Ackermann cars, AGVs, differential-drive warehouse bots, planetary rovers.
- **Examiner's favorite:** Always asks to derive Pfaffian constraints + Euler-Lagrange equations + reduced form. Practice the full chain at least three times before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential-Drive WMR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differential-Drive WMR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Differential-Drive Wheeled Mobile Robot (DD-WMR):**
> A *non-holonomic* wheeled mobile robot whose locomotion is generated by **two independently actuated wheels** mounted on a common longitudinal axis, with the differential angular velocity of these wheels producing both translational and rotational motion of the robot chassis. The configuration is typically stabilized by one or more passive castor or omni-wheels.

The state of the robot is described by its **posture** (or pose):

$$
q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $(x, y)$ is the position of a reference point on the robot (typically the midpoint between the two drive wheels) in the global frame, and $\theta$ is the orientation angle of the robot's longitudinal axis with respect to the global X-axis.

### 1.2 Physical Architecture (Typical)

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Left drive wheel | Independently actuated, encoder-mounted |
| Right drive wheel | Independently actuated, encoder-mounted |
| Castor / omni wheel | Passive balance support |
| DC / Servo motor pair | Provides actuation torque |
| Wheel encoder | Measures wheel angular velocity |
| Wheel radius $r$ | Physical constant |
| Wheelbase $L$ (or $2d$) | Distance between drive-wheel contact points |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The differential-drive mechanism is a *kinematically simple yet dynamically rich* system. It is the most frequently tested mobile platform in KTU examinations under Module-2, especially for kinematics, non-holonomy, and trajectory tracking derivations.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Tank Steering Model:**
> Imagine a *toy tank* with two rubber tracks. To make the tank move straight forward, both tracks must rotate at identical speeds. To make the tank turn right, the right track slows down (or reverses) while the left track continues forward — the tank *pivots* about a point between the tracks. A differential-drive WMR behaves **identically**: the wheel that "lags" acts like the inner track of a turning tank.
>
> The *bicycle-rider intuition* also helps: when two wheels turn at different rates, the entire chassis must rotate about some external pivot (the **Instantaneous Centre of Curvature, ICC**). That pivot lies on the perpendicular bisector of the wheel axle.

### 1.4 Why "Differential"?

The robot's motion is governed by the **difference** between the two wheel angular velocities:

$$
\Delta \omega = \omega_R - \omega_L
$$

- $\Delta \omega = 0 \Rightarrow$ straight-line translation
- $\Delta \omega > 0 \Rightarrow$ left turn (right wheel faster)
- $\Delta \omega < 0 \Rightarrow$ right turn (left wheel faster)

This is precisely why it is called **differential** — the *difference* of the two wheel velocities encodes the **turning behaviour**.

### 1.5 Classification Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Within the family of WMRs, the differential-drive robot belongs to the category of **fixed-wheel synchronous-drive** platforms with **two degrees of actuation freedom** and **three degrees of posture freedom**, but only **two controllable degrees of freedom** — making it *under-actuated* and *non-holonomic*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Non-Holonomic Constraint

A wheel satisfying **pure rolling without slipping** imposes one rolling constraint and one lateral (no-slip) constraint. For a standard fixed wheel, the no-slip condition restricts the lateral velocity of the contact point to zero. For the entire differential-drive WMR (with both wheels aligned along the same axis), the resulting constraint at the reference point is:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

This is a **first-order non-integrable** (Pfaffian) constraint — meaning the robot can achieve **any** pose $(x, y, \theta)$ via a sequence of feasible motions, even though at *every instant* its velocity must satisfy the above equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** *Holonomic* = constraint is integrable (a closed-loop geometric restriction). *Non-holonomic* = constraint is non-integrable but limits only velocity, not reachable configuration space. **Differential-drive WMR is non-holonomic** — a frequently asked 3-mark question.

### 2.2 Forward Kinematics — From Wheel Velocities to Robot Velocities

Let:
- $v_L$ = linear velocity of the left wheel contact point
- $v_R$ = linear velocity of the right wheel contact point
- $r$ = wheel radius
- $\omega_L, \omega_R$ = angular velocities of left and right wheels
- $L$ = wheelbase (distance between the two drive wheels)

Then the **linear velocity** $v$ and **angular velocity** $\omega$ of the robot's reference point are:

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulae?**
> - *Linear velocity* is simply the average — both wheels push the chassis forward, so averaging gives the centre velocity.
> - *Angular velocity* comes from the rigid-body rotation: the difference in wheel speeds creates a couple, and the angular velocity of a rigid body rotating about its ICC is the velocity at the contact point divided by the radius from ICC. Since the ICC lies at perpendicular distance $L/2$ from each wheel, $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$.

### 2.3 Inverse Kinematics — From Robot Velocities to Wheel Velocities

The inverse mapping is obtained by inverting the forward relations:

$$
v_R = v + \frac{L}{2}\omega \quad ; \quad v_L = v - \frac{L}{2}\omega
$$

In matrix form (a **2×2 Jacobian**):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v_R \\ v_L \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \dfrac{L}{2} \\[2mm] 1 & -\dfrac{L}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.4 Instantaneous Centre of Curvature (ICC)

The robot, at any instant, rotates about a fixed point on the line perpendicular to the axle passing through the reference point. This point is the **ICC**. Its coordinates and radius $R$ are:

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}
$$

$$
x_{ICC} = x - R\sin\theta \quad ; \quad y_{ICC} = y + R\cos\theta
$$

**Special cases:**
- If $v_R = v_L$ → $R \to \infty$ → straight-line motion (ICC at infinity)
- If $v_R = -v_L$ → $R = 0$ → pure in-place rotation about the robot's reference point

### 2.5 Posture Kinematics (Global Frame)

Once $v$ and $\omega$ are known, the time evolution of the posture is:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v\sin\theta
$$

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

In vector-matrix form:

$$
\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD KTU FORMULA TABLE — Memorize All Entries.**

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Formula / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $q$ | Robot posture vector | $q = [x,\ y,\ \theta]^T$ |
| $v$ | Robot linear velocity | $v = \tfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ |
| $\omega$ | Robot angular velocity | $\omega = \tfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ |
| $v_R$ | Right wheel linear velocity | $v_R = v + \tfrac{L}{2}\omega$ |
| $v_L$ | Left wheel linear velocity | $v_L = v - \tfrac{L}{2}\omega$ |
| $R$ | Turning radius (ICC) | $R = \tfrac{L}{2}\cdot\tfrac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$ |
| $\dot{x}$ | X-velocity in world frame | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ |
| $\dot{y}$ | Y-velocity in world frame | $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ |
| $\dot{\theta}$ | Angular velocity in world frame | $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ |
| Constraint | Non-holonomic (no slip) | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ |
| $J$ | Inverse-kinematics Jacobian | $\begin{bmatrix}1 & L/2\\ 1 & -L/2\end{bmatrix}$ |
| Pose update (Euler) | Discrete posture step | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega\Delta t$ |
| | | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\cos(\theta_k)\Delta t$ |
| | | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v\sin(\theta_k)\Delta t$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This model is the *backbone* of every warehouse AGV (Amazon Kiva robots), autonomous floor cleaners (iRobot Roomba), and most academic research mobile platforms (Pioneer P3-DX, TurtleBot 2). It is also the canonical plant used in KTU lab simulations of trajectory-tracking controllers (PID, sliding-mode, MPC).

### 2.7 Degrees of Freedom vs. Controllability

- **Configuration space dimension** = 3 (x, y, θ)
- **Number of actuators** = 2 (two motors)
- **Controllable DoF** = 2 (v and ω)
- **Non-holonomic DoF** = 1 (lateral motion forbidden at every instant)
- **Brockett condition** → smooth time-invariant state-feedback stabilization is **impossible**; one must use either *time-varying*, *discontinuous*, or *non-smooth* control laws.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "the robot has 3 DoF" (true) with "3 control inputs" (false). Always state that the *controllable* degrees of freedom are **2**, hence the system is under-actuated.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Forward Kinematic Model

We begin from rigid-body kinematics of two coaxial wheels.

**Step 1:** Each wheel is in pure rolling contact with the ground. The linear velocity of each wheel contact point equals the wheel's angular velocity times its radius:

$$
v_L = r\omega_L \quad ; \quad v_R = r\omega_R
$$

**Step 2:** Because the robot is a single rigid body, the velocity of the midpoint (reference point $P$) along the robot's longitudinal axis is the average of the two wheel velocities (since $P$ is equidistant from both contact points):

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}
$$

Substituting the expressions from Step 1:

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** The angular velocity of the rigid body about its ICC is the same everywhere. Using the right wheel (at distance $L/2$ from the midpoint):

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_{midpoint\text{ longitudinal}}}{L/2}
$$

However, more directly: the relative tangential velocity of the right wheel with respect to the left wheel is $(v_R - v_L)$, and this acts across the wheelbase $L$. Therefore:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

**Step 4:** Combining Steps 2 and 3, the forward-kinematic mapping is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r}{2} & \dfrac{r}{2} \\[2mm] \dfrac{r}{L} & -\dfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}
$$

> This is the **kinematic Jacobian** $J_{FK}$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Inverse Kinematic Model

Inverting the 2×2 matrix from Step 4:

**Step 1:** Compute the determinant of $J_{FK}$:

$$
\det(J_{FK}) = \left(\frac{r}{2}\right)\left(-\frac{r}{L}\right) - \left(\frac{r}{2}\right)\left(\frac{r}{L}\right) = -\frac{r^2}{L}
$$

Since $r \neq 0$ and $L \neq 0$, the determinant is non-zero → the Jacobian is **invertible** for all admissible robot geometries.

**Step 2:** Apply the standard 2×2 inverse formula:

$$
J_{FK}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(J_{FK})} \begin{bmatrix} -r/L & -r/2 \\ -r/L & r/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply through:

$$
J_{FK}^{-1} = -\frac{L}{r^2}\begin{bmatrix} -r/L & -r/2 \\ -r/L & r/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Expanding each entry:

- Top-left: $-\tfrac{L}{r^2}\cdot(-\tfrac{r}{L}) = \tfrac{1}{r}$
- Top-right: $-\tfrac{L}{r^2}\cdot(-\tfrac{r}{2}) = \tfrac{L}{2r}$
- Bottom-left: $-\tfrac{L}{r^2}\cdot(-\tfrac{r}{L}) = \tfrac{1}{r}$
- Bottom-right: $-\tfrac{L}{r^2}\cdot(\tfrac{r}{2}) = -\tfrac{L}{2r}$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \dfrac{L}{2} \\[2mm] 1 & -\dfrac{L}{2} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying the wheel angular velocity by $r$ recovers the linear wheel velocities:

$$
v_R = v + \frac{L}{2}\omega \quad ; \quad v_L = v - \frac{L}{2}\omega
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Non-Holonomic Constraint

**Step 1:** Consider the velocity of the reference point $P$ in the global frame. Decompose it into components along the robot's longitudinal axis $\hat{x}_r$ and lateral axis $\hat{y}_r$:

$$
\vec{v}_P = \dot{x}\hat{X} + \dot{y}\hat{Y} = v\hat{x}_r
$$

where $\hat{x}_r = (\cos\theta,\ \sin\theta)$ and $\hat{y}_r = (-\sin\theta,\ \cos\theta)$ in the global frame.

**Step 2:** The lateral velocity component must be zero because the wheels cannot skid sideways (pure rolling):

$$
\vec{v}_P \cdot \hat{y}_r = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$
(\dot{x}\hat{X} + \dot{y}\hat{Y})\cdot(-\sin\theta,\ \cos\theta) = 0
$$

$$
-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0\;}
$$

> This single scalar constraint reduces the robot's instantaneous 3-dimensional velocity vector $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$ to a 2-dimensional manifold of feasible velocities.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A differential-drive WMR has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.30$ m. The right wheel rotates at $\omega_R = 10$ rad/s and the left wheel at $\omega_L = 6$ rad/s. Determine: (i) the linear velocity $v$ of the robot, (ii) the angular velocity $\omega$, (iii) the radius of curvature $R$ of its path, and (iv) the change in posture after $\Delta t = 2$ s, assuming initial pose $(0, 0, 0)$.

**Solution:**

**Step (i):** Linear velocity of each wheel:

$$
v_R = r\omega_R = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_L = r\omega_L = 0.05 \times 6 = 0.3 \text{ m/s}
$$

Robot linear velocity:

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.5 + 0.3}{2} = \frac{0.8}{2} = 0.4 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step (ii):** Robot angular velocity:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.5 - 0.3}{0.30} = \frac{0.2}{0.30} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step (iii):** Radius of curvature:

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.4}{2/3} = 0.4 \times \frac{3}{2} = 0.6 \text{ m}
$$

Alternative check using the direct formula:

$$
R = \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L} = \frac{0.30}{2}\cdot\frac{0.8}{0.2} = 0.15 \times 4 = 0.6 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step (iv):** Posture update at $\Delta t = 2$ s, initial pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$:

$$
\theta(2) = \theta_0 + \omega\Delta t = 0 + \tfrac{2}{3}\times 2 = \tfrac{4}{3} \approx 1.333 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
x(2) = x_0 + \int_0^{2} v\cos(\omega t)\,dt = \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(\omega t)\Big|_0^2 = 0.6\sin(4/3) \approx 0.6 \times 0.9746 \approx 0.5848 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y(2) = y_0 + \int_0^{2} v\sin(\omega t)\,dt = \frac{v}{\omega}[1 - \cos(\omega t)] = 0.6[1 - \cos(4/3)] \approx 0.6 \times 0.7766 \approx 0.4660 \text{ m}
$$

Final posture: $\;q(2) \approx (0.5848,\ 0.4660,\ 1.333)$.

### 3.5 Derivation of the Euler-Integrated Posture Update (Discrete Form)

In digital implementations, $\omega$ and $v$ are held constant over a sampling period $\Delta t$. Using forward Euler integration:

$$
\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t
$$

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos(\theta_k)\,\Delta t
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin(\theta_k)\,\Delta t
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Euler?** The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mentions numerical integration of the kinematic model for simulation. Euler integration is the simplest, but at high speeds or large $\Delta t$ it drifts. For 14-mark KTU questions requiring higher accuracy, mention *exact integration over a circular arc* using the ICC method.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mechanical Architecture of a Differential-Drive WMR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    chassis["ROBOT CHASSIS"]
    motorL["LEFT DC MOTOR"]
    motorR["RIGHT DC MOTOR"]
    encL["LEFT ENCODER"]
    encR["RIGHT ENCODER"]
    wheelL["LEFT DRIVE WHEEL radius r"]
    wheelR["RIGHT DRIVE WHEEL radius r"]
    castorF["FRONT CASTOR PASSIVE"]
    castorB["REAR CASTOR PASSIVE"]
    mcu["MICROCONTROLLER / SBC"]
    pwr["BATTERY PACK"]

    pwr --> mcu
    mcu -->|PWM signal| motorL
    mcu -->|PWM signal| motorR
    motorL -->|shaft| wheelL
    motorR -->|shaft| wheelR
    motorL -.->|tach feedback| encL
    motorR -.->|tach feedback| encR
    encL -->|pulse train| mcu
    encR -->|pulse train| mcu
    wheelL --- chassis
    wheelR --- chassis
    castorF --- chassis
    castorB --- chassis
```

### 4.2 Geometric Diagram — ICC and Posture Frame

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GLOBAL["GLOBAL FRAME X-Y"]
        ICC["ICC Instantaneous Centre of Curvature"]
        P["P REFERENCE POINT x,y,theta"]
        WL["LEFT WHEEL CONTACT vL"]
        WR["RIGHT WHEEL CONTACT vR"]
    end

    P -->|perpendicular bisector of axle| ICC
    WL --- P
    P --- WR
    WR -->|arc path| ICC
    WL -->|arc path| ICC
```

> **Geometric description (read alongside the Mermaid block):**
> - The two drive-wheel contact points $L$ and $R$ lie on a common axle of length $L$.
> - $P$ is the midpoint, at perpendicular distance $L/2$ from each contact point.
> - The ICC lies on the perpendicular bisector of the axle at distance $R$ from $P$.
> - Both wheels and the reference point $P$ sweep *concentric arcs* about the ICC at the same angular velocity $\omega$.

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Kinematic Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DESIRED POSTURE xd yd thetad"] --> B["TRAJECTORY GENERATOR"]
    B --> C["FEEDFORWARD vd omegad"]
    C --> D["INVERSE KINEMATICS J inverse"]
    D --> E["WHEEL VELOCITIES vR vL"]
    E --> F["DIFFERENTIAL DRIVE PLANT"]
    F --> G["ACTUAL POSTURE x y theta"]
    G --> H["ENCODER FEEDBACK"]
    H --> I["ODOMETRY ESTIMATOR"]
    I --> J["FEEDBACK CONTROLLER e.g. PID"]
    J --> C
```

### 4.4 Sign Convention Diagram — Turning Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CASE1["CASE A STRAIGHT LINE"]
        A1["vR EQUALS vL"]
        A2["R approaches INFINITY"]
        A3["PURE TRANSLATION"]
    end
    subgraph CASE2["CASE B LEFT TURN"]
        B1["vR GREATER THAN vL"]
        B2["R positive finite"]
        B3["ICC to the LEFT of robot"]
    end
    subgraph CASE3["CASE C RIGHT TURN"]
        C1["vL GREATER THAN vR"]
        C2["R positive finite"]
        C3["ICC to the RIGHT of robot"]
    end
    subgraph CASE4["CASE D IN PLACE ROTATION"]
        D1["vR EQUALS MINUS vL"]
        D2["R equals ZERO"]
        D3["PIVOT ABOUT P"]
    end
```

### 4.5 Coordinate Transformation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WF["WORLD FRAME X Y"]
        XP["x horizontal"]
        YP["y vertical"]
    end
    subgraph RF["ROBOT FRAME Xr Yr"]
        XR["Xr along forward axis"]
        YR["Yr along lateral axis"]
    end
    WF -->|rotation by theta| RF
    RF -->|inverse rotation by minus theta| WF
```

> **Read-along description:** The transformation matrix from world to robot frame is the 2D rotation:

$$
R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

> The body-frame velocity $(v, 0)$ transforms to world frame via $(v\cos\theta, v\sin\theta)$, matching the $\dot{x},\dot{y}$ equations derived earlier.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module-2]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a differential-drive wheeled mobile robot. State the non-holonomic constraint equation.*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

A differential-drive WMR uses **two independently driven wheels** mounted on a common axis. The linear velocity $v$ of the robot is the average of the two wheel velocities, and the angular velocity $\omega$ is proportional to their difference.

> The differential-drive principle:

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}
$$

The non-holonomic (no-slip) constraint equation is:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

*(Sketch: label the chassis, two drive wheels, caster, wheelbase $L$, wheel radius $r$, and indicate $v_R, v_L, v, \omega, \theta$. Marks awarded: 1 for sketch, 1 for principle, 1 for constraint equation.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module-2]**
*Define the Instantaneous Centre of Curvature (ICC) for a differential-drive mobile robot. Show that when $v_R = -v_L$, the robot rotates about its own reference point.*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

The ICC is the point about which the entire robot instantaneously rotates. The turning radius is:

$$
R = \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}
$$

If $v_R = -v_L$, then $v_R + v_L = 0$, hence $R = 0$. This means the ICC coincides with the reference point $P$, so the robot undergoes **pure rotation in place** with no translation. The angular velocity is:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{2v_R}{L}
$$

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module-2]**

(a) Derive the forward and inverse kinematic equations of a differential-drive mobile robot. Clearly define all symbols. **(7 marks)**

(b) A differential-drive WMR has $r = 0.04$ m and $L = 0.25$ m. The robot must move with $v = 0.5$ m/s and $\omega = 1.2$ rad/s. Determine (i) the required wheel angular velocities, (ii) the turning radius, and (iii) verify the non-holonomic constraint if the robot is currently at $(1, 2, \pi/4)$ m. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Forward and Inverse Kinematics:**

*Forward Kinematics derivation:* (write as in Section 3.1 above)

Step 1: $v_L = r\omega_L$, $v_R = r\omega_R$.

Step 2: Linear velocity of the midpoint:

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

Step 3: Angular velocity from the differential:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

Step 4: Combine in matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r/2 & r/2 \\ r/L & -r/L \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Inverse Kinematics derivation:* (write as in Section 3.2 above)

Step 1: Compute determinant: $\det = -r^2/L$.

Step 2: Invert the 2×2 matrix.

Step 3: Final result:

$$
\omega_R = \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{L}{2}\omega\right), \qquad \omega_L = \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{L}{2}\omega\right)
$$

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Wheel angular velocities:**

$$
\omega_R = \frac{1}{0.04}\left(0.5 + \frac{0.25}{2}\times 1.2\right) = \frac{0.5 + 0.15}{0.04} = \frac{0.65}{0.04} = 16.25 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\omega_L = \frac{1}{0.04}\left(0.5 - 0.15\right) = \frac{0.35}{0.04} = 8.75 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(ii) Turning radius:**

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.5}{1.2} = 0.4167 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Verification of non-holonomic constraint at $(1, 2, \pi/4)$:**

Compute $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta = 0.5 \times \cos(\pi/4) = 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v\sin\theta = 0.5 \times \sin(\pi/4) = 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536 \text{ m/s}
$$

Substitute into the constraint equation:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0.3536\times 0.7071 - 0.3536\times 0.7071 = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

The non-holonomic constraint is satisfied (as expected for any pure-rolling motion).

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary, Module-2]**

(a) With a clear schematic, define the posture of a differential-drive WMR. Derive the differential-drive kinematic model relating wheel velocities to the time-rate of change of posture. **(7 marks)**

(b) A differential-drive robot starts at the origin with orientation $\theta = 0$. It moves with $v = 0.3$ m/s and $\omega = 0.5$ rad/s for 4 seconds. Using exact integration over the circular arc, determine the final posture of the robot. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Posture and Kinematic Model:**

*Posture definition:* The posture (or pose) is the triplet $(x, y, \theta)$, where:
- $(x, y)$ = position of the reference point $P$ in the world frame.
- $\theta$ = orientation of the robot's forward axis with respect to the world X-axis.

*Derivation of the kinematic model:*

Step 1: Express robot velocity in the world frame. The linear velocity $v$ is along the robot's longitudinal axis, whose unit vector is $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. Hence:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v\sin\theta
$$

Step 2: The angular velocity in the world frame equals the body-frame angular velocity:

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

Step 3: Substitute $v$ and $\omega$ from the wheel kinematics:

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

Step 4: Combine to obtain:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\[2mm] \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\[2mm] \dfrac{r}{L} & -\dfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(b) Exact Arc Integration:**

Given: $v = 0.3$ m/s, $\omega = 0.5$ rad/s, $t = 4$ s, initial pose $(0, 0, 0)$.

Step 1: Turning radius:

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.3}{0.5} = 0.6 \text{ m}
$$

Step 2: Total angle swept:

$$
\Delta\theta = \omega t = 0.5 \times 4 = 2 \text{ rad} \approx 114.59^\circ
$$

Step 3: Final orientation:

$$
\theta_f = \theta_0 + \Delta\theta = 0 + 2 = 2 \text{ rad}
$$

Step 4: ICC coordinates (since ICC is on the perpendicular to the initial forward direction, i.e., the Y-axis, at distance $R$):

$$
x_{ICC} = x_0 - R\sin\theta_0 = 0 - 0.6\sin 0 = 0
$$

$$
y_{ICC} = y_0 + R\cos\theta_0 = 0 + 0.6\cos 0 = 0.6 \text{ m}
$$

Step 5: Final position (rotation of vector from ICC to $P$ by angle $\Delta\theta$):

The vector from ICC to initial $P$ is $(0 - 0, 0 - 0.6) = (0, -0.6)$. After rotating by $\Delta\theta = 2$ rad counter-clockwise:

$$
\Delta x = 0\cdot\cos 2 - (-0.6)\sin 2 = 0.6\sin 2 \approx 0.6 \times 0.9093 = 0.5456
$$

$$
\Delta y = 0\cdot\sin 2 + (-0.6)\cos 2 = -0.6\cos 2 \approx -0.6 \times (-0.4161) = 0.2497
$$

Final position:

$$
x_f = x_{ICC} + \Delta x = 0 + 0.5456 \approx 0.546 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_f = y_{ICC} + \Delta y = 0.6 + 0.2497 \approx 0.850 \text{ m}
$$

**Final posture:** $\;q(4) \approx (0.546\ \text{m},\ 0.850\ \text{m},\ 2.000\ \text{rad})$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Differential-Drive WMR**
> 1. **Sign confusion in $\omega$:** A *positive* $\omega$ usually corresponds to a *counter-clockwise* (left) turn. Many students swap the sign of $\omega_R - \omega_L$ between forward and inverse kinematics. **Always derive both directions from the same starting assumption.**
> 2. **Forgetting the non-holonomic constraint:** Examiners *specifically* test whether students can state and apply $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. A 14-mark answer missing this loses **at least 2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing $r$ and $R$:** Lowercase $r$ = wheel radius. Uppercase $R$ = turning radius (ICC). These are *entirely different quantities*. Markers will deduct for symbol misuse.
> 4. **Treating the discrete Euler update as exact:** The Euler approximation is *not* exact; for a circular arc, the exact arc-length integration must be used for full marks. If the question says "exact" or "without approximation," use $\sin/\cos$ integrals.
> 5. **Omitting units:** Always quote $\omega$ in **rad/s**, $v$ in **m/s**, and $R$ in **m**. A unitless answer is incomplete.
> 6. **Forgetting the wheelbase definition:** State that $L$ is the *distance between the two drive-wheel contact points*, **not** the distance between motor shafts or chassis edges. Markers often deduct for ambiguous definitions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential-Drive WMR — Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - [x] **Architecture:** Two coaxial drive wheels + passive castor(s); wheel radius $r$, wheelbase $L$.
> - [x] **Forward kinematics:** $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$ and $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$.
> - [x] **Inverse kinematics:** $\omega_R = (v + L\omega/2)/r$ and $\omega_L = (v - L\omega/2)/r$.
> - [x] **Posture vector:** $q = (x, y, \theta)$; three configuration DoF but only two control inputs.
> - [x] **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ (no lateral slip).
> - [x] **Turning radius:** $R = v/\omega = (L/2)(v_R + v_L)/(v_R - v_L)$.
> - [x] **Special case:** $v_R = -v_L \Rightarrow R = 0 \Rightarrow$ in-place pivot.
> - [x] **Special case:** $v_R = v_L \Rightarrow R = \infty \Rightarrow$ straight-line motion.
> - [x] **Posture dynamics:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
> - [x] **Under-actuation:** Cannot instantaneously move sideways — Brockett condition fails for smooth feedback stabilization.
> - [x] **Discrete integration:** Euler update available, but exact arc integration gives $\Delta\theta = \omega\Delta t$, $\Delta x = R[\sin(\theta_0 + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta_0]$, $\Delta y = R[-\cos(\theta_0 + \omega\Delta t) + \cos\theta_0]$.
> - [x] **Sensor coupling:** Wheel encoders provide $\omega_L, \omega_R$; combined with $r$ and $L$, they yield $v, \omega$ for odometry.
> - [x] **Applications:** Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva), TurtleBot, Pioneer P3-DX, iRobot Roomba, autonomous forklifts, research testbeds for trajectory tracking control.
> - [x] **Pitfall awareness:** Distinguish $r$ vs $R$, watch sign conventions, always state non-holonomic constraint explicitly, and never confuse configuration DoF with control DoF.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamics of WMR with Slip
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamics of WMR with Slip

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **2024 Scheme** syllabus for PEMET756 (Mobile Robotics), a *Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR)* is treated, under ideal conditions, by the **non-holonomic pure rolling constraint** — the velocity of the wheel contact point relative to the ground is identically zero. The topic **"Dynamics of WMR with Slip"** relaxes this assumption: it models the WMR when the wheel-ground contact exhibits a *relative tangential motion*, i.e., the wheel is either:

- **Longitudinally slipping** (the wheel rotates faster or slower than the linear advance of the wheel center demands — *driving slip* or *braking slip*), and/or
- **Laterally slipping** (the wheel velocity vector is not aligned with the wheel plane — *side slip / cornering slip*).

The resulting model is a **second-order dynamic system** (Newton–Euler or Lagrangian) augmented by **slip-induced generalized forces** at each wheel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> *"Dynamic modeling of WMR with slip — slip ratio, slip angle, traction forces, and effect on controllability and stability."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Icy Parking Lot"

Imagine driving a **car on a wet, icy parking lot**. You turn the steering wheel, you press the accelerator, but the car:
- Skids forward more than the wheel rotation suggests (*longitudinal slip*),
- Drifts sideways through the turn instead of carving a clean arc (*lateral slip*).

The driver's input is correct, the wheel spins correctly, but the **road is not gripping the tire** as theory assumes. The robot (or car) is then said to operate in a **slip regime**. The "ideal" kinematic model fails — you must add a **dynamic layer** that captures the *tire-road interaction force*.

A WMR on a smooth lab floor with brand-new rubber tires behaves *almost* like the ideal model. The same WMR on a dusty warehouse floor, on a wet ramp, or under aggressive acceleration behaves like the icy parking lot. That gap between **kinematics and reality** is exactly what this topic closes.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|--------|----------|---------------------|
| $m$ | Robot mass | **kg** |
| $I_z$ | Yaw moment of inertia | **kg·m²** |
| $I_w$ | Wheel moment of inertia | **kg·m²** |
| $r$ | Wheel radius | **m** |
| $b$ | Half wheelbase | **m** |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $\mu$ | Tire-road friction coefficient | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard *slip ratio* and *slip angle* are **dimensionless** quantities bounded roughly by $\vert\sigma\vert \leq 1$ and $\vert\alpha\vert \leq 10^\circ$–$15^\circ$ for stable cornering on dry asphalt.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Slip Exists — The Physical Picture

Every wheel-ground contact transmits forces through a **contact patch**. The patch cannot produce an infinite force. When the **demanded tangential force** exceeds $\mu N$ (where $N$ is the normal load), the patch yields — and slip begins. Three failure modes are commonly modeled:

1. **Longitudinal slip** — wheel rotating faster (acceleration slip) or slower (braking slip) than the no-slip condition demands.
2. **Lateral / side slip** — wheel plane not aligned with the instantaneous velocity vector (cornering).
3. **Combined slip** — both occur simultaneously (the realistic case under hard cornering while braking).

### 2.2 Slip Ratio (Longitudinal)

For a single driven wheel of radius $r$ spinning at angular velocity $\omega$, with linear velocity $v$ of the wheel center:

$$
\sigma \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{r\omega - v}{r\omega}, & \text{if } v < r\omega \quad (\text{acceleration / driving slip}) \\[8pt]
\dfrac{r\omega - v}{v}, & \text{if } v > r\omega \quad (\text{braking slip}) \\[8pt]
0, & \text{if } v = r\omega \quad (\text{ideal rolling})
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.3 Slip Angle (Lateral)

If the wheel heading makes an angle with the actual velocity vector at the contact patch:

$$
\alpha \;=\; \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{v_y}{v_x}\right)
$$

For small angles: $\alpha \approx v_y / v_x$.

### 2.4 Traction Force Model (Pacejka-style, KTU-relevant form)

The longitudinal traction force at one wheel:

$$
F_{x,\text{tract}} \;=\; \mu N \,\dfrac{C_\sigma \,\sigma}{1 + C_\sigma \,\vert\sigma\vert} \quad \text{(Burckhardt simplified)}
$$

where $C_\sigma$ is a longitudinal stiffness constant (typically 10–25 for dry asphalt).

The lateral (cornering) force:

$$
F_{y,\text{tract}} \;=\; \mu N \,C_\alpha \,\alpha \quad \text{(linear region, small } \alpha\text{)}
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma = (r\omega - v)/\max(v, r\omega)$ | Longitudinal slip ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\alpha = \arctan(v_y / v_x)$ | Slip angle | rad |
| 3 | $F_{x,t} = \mu N\, C_\sigma \sigma / (1 + C_\sigma \vert\sigma\vert)$ | Longitudinal traction | N |
| 4 | $F_{y,t} = \mu N\, C_\alpha \alpha$ | Lateral traction (linear) | N |
| 5 | $m\ddot{x} = \sum F_{x,i}\cos\phi - \sum F_{y,i}\sin\phi$ | Longitudinal Newton | N |
| 6 | $m\ddot{y} = \sum F_{x,i}\sin\phi + \sum F_{y,i}\cos\phi$ | Lateral Newton | N |
| 7 | $I_z\ddot{\phi} = \sum M_{z,i}$ | Yaw moment balance | N·m |
| 8 | $I_w \dot{\omega}_i = \tau_i - r\, F_{x,t,i}$ | Wheel rotational dynamics | N·m |
| 9 | $\vert F_{x,t} \vert \leq \mu N$ | Friction circle bound | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Students confuse $v$ in the slip ratio with the *robot longitudinal velocity* versus the *wheel-center velocity*. For a single-wheel model they coincide; for an articulated robot with steering, use the velocity of the wheel contact patch resolved along the wheel plane.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous cars (Tesla, Waymo):** slip-aware controllers prevent skidding during emergency braking.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva, Fetch):** slip detection on smooth concrete avoids localization drift from wheel odometry.
- **Planetary rovers (NASA Perseverance, Curiosity):** slip is the dominant cause of path-tracking error on Martian regolith; **visual odometry** is fused precisely because wheel-encoder odometry fails under slip.
- **Agri-robotics:** tractors on muddy fields operate almost permanently in a slip regime.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Reference Frames and Variables

Define the **inertial frame** $\{X, Y\}$ and the **body frame** $\{x_b, y_b\}$ attached to the robot center of mass (CoM). Let:

- $(x, y)$ — position of CoM in inertial frame,
- $\phi$ — yaw angle of the body frame w.r.t. inertial $X$-axis,
- $v$ — magnitude of CoM velocity in body frame,
- $\dot{\phi}$ — yaw rate,
- $\omega_i$ — angular velocity of the $i$-th wheel ($i = 1, 2$ for differential-drive).

The no-slip kinematic model would give:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\phi, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\phi, \quad \dot{\phi} = \omega
$$

With slip, these become **inequalities** that must be augmented with forces.

### 3.2 Derivation of Dynamic Equations with Slip (Differential-Drive WMR)

We consider a **differential-drive WMR** of mass $m$, yaw inertia $I_z$, with two wheels of radius $r$ at half-track $b$. Each wheel is driven by torque $\tau_i$.

**Step 1 — Write the wheel-center velocities.**

The left wheel center is at $(-b, 0)$ in body frame, the right at $(+b, 0)$. The velocity of wheel-$i$ center, in body frame, is:

$$
v_{i,x} = v, \qquad v_{i,y} = (-1)^{i}\, b\,\dot{\phi} \quad (i = 1 \Rightarrow \text{right},\; i = 2 \Rightarrow \text{left})
$$

**Step 2 — Form the slip ratio for each wheel.**

Let $\omega_1$ = right wheel, $\omega_2$ = left wheel. The longitudinal velocity at each wheel contact patch is $v_{i,x}$, so:

$$
\sigma_i \;=\; \dfrac{r\omega_i - v_{i,x}}{\max\!\left(v_{i,x},\, r\omega_i\right)} \;=\; \dfrac{r\omega_i - v}{\max(v,\, r\omega_i)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the longitudinal traction force at each wheel.**

$$
F_{x,i} \;=\; \mu N_i \,\dfrac{C_\sigma \sigma_i}{1 + C_\sigma \vert\sigma_i\vert}
$$

For symmetric loading $N_i = mg/2$, so:

$$
F_{x,i} \;=\; \mu\,\dfrac{mg}{2}\,\dfrac{C_\sigma \sigma_i}{1 + C_\sigma \vert\sigma_i\vert}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's second law in body frame.**

Let $F_{x,t} = F_{x,1} + F_{x,2}$ be the *total* longitudinal traction force along $x_b$, and assume the lateral traction force $F_{y,t}$ from side-slip is small but present (we retain it for completeness):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m\dot{v} \;-\; m\,v\,\dot{\phi}^{2}\!\big/\,? \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;& \text{(resolved to inertial)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Working directly in the inertial frame (cleaner for a board exam), the position dynamics are:

$$
\ddot{x} \;=\; \frac{1}{m}\Bigl(F_{x,t}\cos\phi - F_{y,t}\sin\phi\Bigr)
$$

$$
\ddot{y} \;=\; \frac{1}{m}\Bigl(F_{x,t}\sin\phi + F_{y,t}\cos\phi\Bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Yaw moment balance.**

The differential tractive force produces a yaw moment about the CoM. With track $2b$:

$$
I_z\ddot{\phi} \;=\; b\bigl(F_{x,2} - F_{x,1}\bigr) \;+\; M_{\text{slip}}
$$

where $M_{\text{slip}}$ captures the additional yaw moment from lateral slip at each wheel:

$$
M_{\text{slip}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{2} F_{y,i}\,(\pm b)
$$

**Step 6 — Wheel rotational dynamics.**

The torque $\tau_i$ at each wheel must overcome inertial reaction and overcome the longitudinal traction:

$$
I_w\,\dot{\omega}_i \;=\; \tau_i \;-\; r\,F_{x,i}
$$

**Step 7 — Combine into the standard state-space form.**

Choosing the state vector $\mathbf{q} = [x,\; y,\; \phi,\; v,\; \dot{\phi},\; \omega_1,\; \omega_2]^{\mathsf{T}}$, the dynamic model with slip is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v\cos\phi \\[4pt]
\dot{y} &= v\sin\phi \\[4pt]
\dot{\phi} &= \dot{\phi} \\[4pt]
\dot{v} &= \frac{F_{x,t}}{m} \\[4pt]
\ddot{\phi} &= \frac{b\bigl(F_{x,2} - F_{x,1}\bigr) + M_{\text{slip}}}{I_z} \\[4pt]
\dot{\omega}_i &= \frac{\tau_i - r F_{x,i}}{I_w}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **non-holonomic constraint** $\dot{x}\sin\phi - \dot{y}\cos\phi = 0$ of the ideal model is now replaced by a **bounded inequality** $\vert F_{x,t}\vert \leq \mu m g$ — the friction-circle bound. This is the key formal change introduced by slip.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style)

**Problem (KTU-pattern):** A differential-drive WMR with $m = 50$ kg, $I_z = 5$ kg·m², $b = 0.3$ m, $r = 0.15$ m, $I_w = 0.05$ kg·m² travels at $v = 1$ m/s with $\omega_1 = 8$ rad/s and $\omega_2 = 6$ rad/s. Assume $\mu = 0.8$, $C_\sigma = 15$, $g = 9.81$. Compute the slip ratios and longitudinal traction forces at each wheel.

**Solution:**

Step (a) — slip ratios:

$$
r\omega_1 = 0.15 \times 8 = 1.20 \text{ m/s}, \quad r\omega_2 = 0.15 \times 6 = 0.90 \text{ m/s}
$$

Both are *greater than* $v = 1$ m/s $\Rightarrow$ driving-slip case:

$$
\sigma_1 = \frac{r\omega_1 - v}{r\omega_1} = \frac{1.20 - 1.00}{1.20} = 0.1667
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = \frac{r\omega_2 - v}{r\omega_2} = \frac{0.90 - 1.00}{0.90} = -0.1111
$$

Step (b) — longitudinal traction forces:

$$
F_{x,1} = 0.8 \times \frac{50 \times 9.81}{2} \times \frac{15 \times 0.1667}{1 + 15 \times 0.1667}
$$

$$
F_{x,1} = 196.2 \times \frac{2.5}{1 + 2.5} = 196.2 \times 0.7143 = 140.1 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{x,2} = 196.2 \times \frac{15 \times (-0.1111)}{1 + 15 \times 0.1111} = 196.2 \times \frac{-1.6667}{2.6667} = -122.7 \text{ N}
$$

Step (c) — net yaw moment (slip-induced asymmetry):

$$
M_z = b(F_{x,2} - F_{x,1}) = 0.3 \times (-122.7 - 140.1) = -78.84 \text{ N·m}
$$

The robot will yaw **clockwise** at rate $\ddot{\phi} = -78.84 / 5 = -15.77$ rad/s² even though the driver commands straight motion — slip has created an *uncommanded yaw moment*, a classic failure mode in real WMRs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Slip-Dynamics Feedback Loop

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the complete causal chain from driver command → traction force → vehicle motion, with the slip-perturbation branch explicitly highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Command: Torque tau_i and Steering Angle delta] --> B[Wheel Rotational Dynamics I_w d omega dt]
    B --> C[Wheel Angular Velocity omega_i]
    C --> D[Slip Ratio Computation sigma_i = r omega - v divided by max]
    D --> E[Traction Force Model F_x = mu N times Burckhardt function of sigma]
    E --> F[Newton Euler Body Forces and Yaw Moment]
    F --> G[Robot Acceleration a and Yaw Rate d phi dt]
    G --> H[Integrator Kinematics: x dot y dot phi dot]
    H --> I[Robot Pose x y phi]
    I --> J[Wheel Center Velocity v computed from pose rate]
    J --> D
    E --> K[Friction Circle Bound F_x squared plus F_y squared less than mu N squared]
    K --> F
    I --> L[Path Tracking Error]
    L --> M[Feedback Controller Adjusts tau_i]
    M --> A
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

For readers who need a **higher-level functional map** (especially useful when studying for the 14-mark derivation question):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Layer: Desired Path] --> B[Controller: PID or Sliding Mode]
    B --> C[Actuator Dynamics: Motor plus Gearbox]
    C --> D[Wheel Slip Subsystem: sigma and alpha computation]
    D --> E[Tire Road Interaction: F_x and F_y generation]
    E --> F[Robot Body Dynamics: Newton Euler]
    F --> G[Output: Actual Pose and Velocity]
    G --> H[Sensor Layer: Encoders IMU GPS Camera]
    H --> I[State Estimator: Kalman or EKF]
    I --> B
    G --> J[Slip Observer: Compares v odometry to v visual]
    J --> K[Slip Compensation Block]
    K --> B
```

### 4.3 Causal Summary Table

| Block | Function | Slip Relevance |
|-------|----------|----------------|
| Driver Command | Generates $\tau_i, \delta$ | Inputs are reference values |
| Wheel Rotational | $I_w \dot{\omega} = \tau - r F_x$ | Slip reduces effective torque |
| Slip Ratio | $\sigma = (r\omega - v)/(\cdot)$ | Encodes the *mismatch* |
| Traction Model | Burckhardt / Pacejka | **Non-linear slip-to-force map** |
| Newton-Euler | $m\mathbf{a} = \sum \mathbf{F}$ | Forces contain slip terms |
| Friction Circle | $\vert F_x \vert^2 + \vert F_y \vert^2 \leq (\mu N)^2$ | **Saturation boundary** |
| Observer | Compares predicted vs measured pose | Detects slip *post-hoc* |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 2]**
*Define longitudinal slip ratio $\sigma$ and lateral slip angle $\alpha$ for a single wheel of a WMR. Under what condition does each become zero?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Longitudinal slip ratio $\sigma$** — dimensionless quantity defined as the relative difference between the wheel-peripheral speed $r\omega$ and the wheel-center linear velocity $v$:
  $\sigma = (r\omega - v)/\max(v, r\omega)$.

- **Lateral slip angle $\alpha$** — angle between the wheel-heading direction (wheel plane) and the actual velocity vector at the contact patch:
  $\alpha = \arctan(v_y / v_x)$.

- **Zero-slip conditions:**
  $\sigma = 0 \Leftrightarrow r\omega = v$ (pure rolling, no skid).
  $\alpha = 0 \Leftrightarrow$ velocity vector aligned with wheel plane (no cornering slip).

*(Award 1 mark each for definition + zero condition of σ, and 1 mark for α.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Sample, Module 2]**
*State the friction-circle bound for a single wheel and explain its physical meaning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The friction-circle bound states that the *combined* longitudinal and lateral traction forces at a single wheel cannot exceed the maximum friction force:

$$
F_{x}^{2} + F_{y}^{2} \;\leq\; (\mu N)^{2}
$$

where $N$ is the normal load on the wheel and $\mu$ the friction coefficient.

**Physical meaning:** The tire-road contact patch has a finite *friction budget*. Whether you spend it on braking/acceleration ($F_x$) or cornering ($F_y$), the *vector sum* is bounded. Exceed it and the wheel either locks (braking) or breaks away (cornering).

*(1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for explaining $\mu N$, 1 mark for the budget interpretation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Module 2 — KTU Past Pattern)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the **dynamic equations of motion of a differential-drive WMR** considering both longitudinal and lateral slip at each wheel. Clearly define all symbols and state the slip-ratio and slip-angle relations you use. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive WMR has $m = 40$ kg, $I_z = 4$ kg·m², wheel radius $r = 0.1$ m, half-track $b = 0.25$ m, $\mu = 0.7$, $C_\sigma = 12$, $g = 9.81$ m/s². The right wheel is driven at $\omega_1 = 12$ rad/s and the left at $\omega_2 = 9$ rad/s while the robot CoM velocity is $v = 1.0$ m/s. Compute the slip ratios at both wheels, the longitudinal traction forces, and the resulting yaw moment. Comment on the robot's path. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Award marks as follows:

- 1 mark — Frame definition and choice of state variables.
- 2 marks — Wheel-center velocities $v_{i,x}, v_{i,y}$ in body frame.
- 1 mark — Slip-ratio definition and Burckhardt traction force.
- 2 marks — Newton-Euler equations for $m\ddot{x}, m\ddot{y}, I_z\ddot{\phi}$ in inertial frame.
- 1 mark — Final state-space form and friction-circle bound $\vert F_x \vert^2 + \vert F_y \vert^2 \leq (\mu N)^2$.

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.2, Steps 1–7** of these notes.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Step (i) — peripheral speeds:
$r\omega_1 = 0.1 \times 12 = 1.20$ m/s; $r\omega_2 = 0.1 \times 9 = 0.90$ m/s. Both $> v = 1$ m/s ⇒ driving slip.

Step (ii) — slip ratios:
$\sigma_1 = (1.20 - 1.00)/1.20 = 0.1667$
$\sigma_2 = (0.90 - 1.00)/0.90 = -0.1111$

Step (iii) — Normal load: $N_i = mg/2 = 40 \times 9.81 / 2 = 196.2$ N.

Step (iv) — Traction forces:

$$
F_{x,1} = 0.7 \times 196.2 \times \frac{12 \times 0.1667}{1 + 12 \times 0.1667} = 137.3 \times \frac{2.0}{3.0} = 91.55 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{x,2} = 0.7 \times 196.2 \times \frac{12 \times (-0.1111)}{1 + 12 \times 0.1111} = 137.3 \times \frac{-1.333}{2.333} = -78.49 \text{ N}
$$

Step (v) — Yaw moment:
$M_z = b(F_{x,2} - F_{x,1}) = 0.25 \times (-78.49 - 91.55) = -42.51$ N·m.

Step (vi) — Yaw acceleration:
$\ddot{\phi} = -42.51 / 4 = -10.63$ rad/s².

**Comment:** The right wheel produces forward thrust (+91.55 N) while the left produces a *reverse* traction (−78.49 N) — both wheels slip in the same rotational sense (driving), but the asymmetric slip creates a net **clockwise yaw moment**. The robot will spin rather than drive straight, illustrating how slip destroys the *no-slip straight-line invariant* of the ideal differential-drive model.

*(Award marks: 1 each for steps (i)–(iv), 1 for (v), 1 for (vi), 1 for comment.)*

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Module 2 — Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Sample, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Burckhardt traction model** for a single wheel. Show how it captures the saturation behaviour at high slip and how it reduces to a linear relation for small slip. State the friction-circle bound and explain why it matters for slip-aware control of WMRs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car-like WMR with rear-wheel drive has $m = 1200$ kg, $I_z = 2500$ kg·m², wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m, rear-axle to CoM $l_r = 1.4$ m, front-axle to CoM $l_f = 1.1$ m, $C_\alpha = 80{,}000$ N/rad per axle, $v = 20$ m/s, front steering angle $\delta = 0.05$ rad. Assuming small slip angles, compute the front and rear slip angles and the resulting lateral tire forces. Is the vehicle *understeering* or *oversteering*? Use the *understeer/oversteer gradient* $K = \frac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Burckhardt model + friction circle (7 marks):**

- 2 marks — Burckhardt formula derivation:
  $F_x = \mu N \dfrac{C_\sigma \sigma}{1 + C_\sigma \vert\sigma\vert}$.
  At $\sigma \to 0$: Taylor expansion gives $F_x \approx \mu N C_\sigma \sigma$ — *linear* region (1 mark for the linear limit, 1 mark for the high-slip saturation $F_x \to \mu N$).

- 1 mark — Saturation explanation: as $\vert\sigma\vert \to \infty$, $F_x \to \mu N$ (lock or spin saturation).

- 2 marks — Friction circle: $\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} \leq \mu N$, graphical interpretation as a 2D budget.

- 2 marks — Why it matters for control: anti-lock braking (ABS), electronic stability program (ESP), and traction control all operate *on* the friction circle; without it, slip-aware control is impossible. (Practical KTU link.)

**(b) Bicycle-model slip calculation (7 marks):**

Step (i) — Static normal loads:
$W_f = mg\,l_r/L = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 1.4/2.5 = 6591$ N.
$W_r = mg\,l_f/L = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 1.1/2.5 = 5181$ N.

Step (ii) — Slip angles (bicycle model, small angle):

$$
\alpha_f = \delta - \frac{l_f \dot{\phi}}{v}, \qquad \alpha_r = -\frac{l_r \dot{\phi}}{v}
$$

But we are not given $\dot{\phi}$ — we use the *steady-state* bicycle solution where the slip angles are approximately:

$$
\alpha_f \approx \delta - \dfrac{l_f}{L}\,\delta = \delta\,\dfrac{l_r}{L} = 0.05 \times \dfrac{1.4}{2.5} = 0.028 \text{ rad}
$$

(More precisely, at steady-state: $\dot{\phi} \approx v \delta / L$, so $l_f \dot{\phi}/v = l_f \delta / L$.)

Step (iii) — Lateral tire forces:

$$
F_{y,f} = -C_{\alpha f}\,\alpha_f = -80{,}000 \times 0.028 = -2240 \text{ N (per axle, front)}
$$

$$
F_{y,r} = -C_{\alpha r}\,\alpha_r = -C_{\alpha r}\left(-\dfrac{l_r \dot{\phi}}{v}\right) = +C_{\alpha r}\,\dfrac{l_r \delta}{L} = +80{,}000 \times 0.0224 = +1792 \text{ N}
$$

(Here we used $C_{\alpha f} = C_{\alpha r} = 80{,}000$ N/rad — single given value for both axles.)

Step (iv) — Understeer/oversteer gradient:

$$
K = \frac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}} = \frac{6591}{80{,}000} - \frac{5181}{80{,}000} = \frac{1410}{80{,}000} = 0.01763 \text{ rad/(m/s²)}
$$

Since $K > 0$, the vehicle is **understeering** — the front axle saturates first. The driver must *increase* steering angle to maintain a higher lateral acceleration, characteristic of a passenger-car WMR.

*(Award marks: 1 for step (i), 2 for step (ii), 2 for step (iii), 2 for step (iv).)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing $v$ in the slip ratio:** Use the *wheel-center* velocity, not the CoM velocity, when wheel positions differ from CoM.
> 2. **Forgetting the friction-circle bound:** Many solutions derive the dynamics but forget to state the saturation inequality. A 1- to 2-mark cut is routine.
> 3. **Sign errors in yaw moment:** $M_z = b(F_{x,2} - F_{x,1})$ vs. $b(F_{x,1} - F_{x,2})$ — the order of the indices matters. Mark which wheel is "1" and "2" on your diagram.
> 4. **Mixing inertial vs body frames:** Newton-Euler is cleanest in the *inertial* frame for translation; the *body* frame for moments. Don't mix in one equation.
> 5. **Treating $\mu$ as a constant:** In reality $\mu = \mu(v, \sigma, \text{road})$. For board purposes, assume constant — but state the assumption explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Slip ratio** $\sigma = (r\omega - v)/\max(v, r\omega)$ — dimensionless, sign carries direction of slip.
- ✅ **Slip angle** $\alpha = \arctan(v_y / v_x)$ — small in normal driving, blows up at the friction limit.
- ✅ **Burckhardt / Pacejka traction model:** non-linear force-vs-slip; **linear** for $\vert\sigma\vert \ll 1/C_\sigma$, **saturates** to $\mu N$ at high slip.
- ✅ **Friction circle:** $\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} \leq \mu N$ — the master inequality of slip-aware control.
- ✅ **Newton-Euler equations** for a differential-drive WMR *with slip* — see Section 3.2, Steps 4–6.
- ✅ **State-space form** with $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \phi, v, \dot\phi, \omega_1, \omega_2]^{\mathsf{T}}$ — slip replaces the kinematic constraint $\dot x \sin\phi - \dot y \cos\phi = 0$ with the inequality bound.
- ✅ **Slip-induced uncommanded yaw moment** — differential traction asymmetry generates rotation even under "straight" command.
- ✅ **Wheel rotational dynamics:** $I_w \dot\omega_i = \tau_i - r F_{x,i}$ — the *load torque* on the motor is the slip-traction force.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** warehouse AMRs (wheel-odometry drift), planetary rovers (Perseverance visual-odometry fusion), autonomous cars (ABS/ESP), agricultural tractors (muddy fields).
- ✅ **Board-exam formula mastery:** Slip ratio, slip angle, Burckhardt, friction circle, Newton-Euler, yaw moment — all six are independent 3-mark potential items.
- ✅ **Friction coefficient $\mu$** is *not* constant — board assumption: constant; research note: depends on road, speed, slip.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

A **Car-Like Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR)** is a non-holonomic ground vehicle whose locomotion mechanism mimics a conventional automobile: it possesses a **fixed rear axle (driving wheels)** and a **steerable front axle (steering wheels)**. The **Dynamic Model** of such a robot is a set of differential equations derived from **Newton-Euler mechanics** that describe the time evolution of the robot's generalized coordinates — typically its position $(X, Y)$, heading angle $\theta$, longitudinal velocity $v$, and steering angle $\phi$ — by explicitly accounting for **inertial properties (mass $m$, yaw moment of inertia $I_z$)**, **tire-road interaction forces**, and **internal steering actuation dynamics**.

Mathematically, the dynamic model extends the geometric (kinematic) description by introducing the **force/moment balance equations**, transforming the purely geometric rolling constraints into a dynamic state-space representation suitable for **nonlinear control design** (e.g., backstepping, sliding mode, MPC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Car-like WMR dynamic modeling bridges the gap between *idealized motion planning* (kinematics) and *real-world implementation* (where mass, friction, and actuator limits dominate). This topic is essential for designing trajectory-tracking and stabilization controllers for autonomous vehicles.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Driving a Real Car in a Physics Simulator"**
>
> Imagine you are playing a realistic driving video game (like *Forza* or *Assetto Corsa*). In the simplest "arcade" mode, the car turns the instant you turn the steering wheel — there is **no weight, no tire slip, no inertia**. This is the **kinematic model** of a car-like WMR: the response is *geometrically instantaneous*.
>
> Now switch to the **"simulation" mode** with full physics enabled. When you sharply turn the wheel, the car **leans**, the **rear end drifts**, and you must wait for the **steering rack to physically rotate** before the wheels point in a new direction. The car has **mass = 1500 kg**, **rotational inertia**, and the **tires deform** at the contact patch. This richer, physically faithful description is the **dynamic model**.
>
> In short:
> - **Kinematic model** = "Where the car *should* be going" (geometric idealization).
> - **Dynamic model** = "Where the car *actually* is, given its mass, inertia, and the friction grip of the tires" (Newtonian reality).

## Key Physical Quantities (Standardized Symbols)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $m$ | Total mass of the vehicle | **kg** |
| $I_z$ | Yaw moment of inertia (about vertical axis) | **kg·m²** |
| $l_f$ | Distance from CG to front axle | **m** |
| $l_r$ | Distance from CG to rear axle | **m** |
| $L = l_f + l_r$ | Wheelbase | **m** |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration ($\approx 9.81$) | **m/s²** |
| $\mu$ | Tire-road friction coefficient (dimensionless) | — |
| $C_\alpha$ | Tire cornering stiffness (front/rear) | **N/rad** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Holonomic Constraint (Car-Like):** The vehicle **cannot translate laterally** relative to its body frame. This is mathematically expressed as:
> $$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$
> This single scalar constraint renders the system **under-actuated at the kinematic level** but **controllable** due to the Brockett necessary condition being satisfied only when *dynamic* inputs (forces/torques) are considered.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Dynamic Model? (The "Why" Behind the Equations)

A purely **kinematic** model assumes the commanded velocity $v$ and steering angle $\phi$ are achieved **instantaneously**. In real hardware:

1. **The motor produces torque, not velocity** — there is a lag between command and execution.
2. **The tires slip** under high lateral acceleration — they cannot generate infinite cornering force.
3. **The vehicle has inertia** — it cannot change heading direction abruptly.
4. **The steering actuator is a second-order system** — the steering rate $\dot{\phi}$ is itself a control input, not the angle $\phi$ directly.

The **dynamic model** captures all of these phenomena through the **Newton-Euler equations** applied to a rigid body on a plane.

## 2.2 System Decomposition (Step-by-Step Logic)

The derivation proceeds in five logical stages:

- **Step 1 — Choose the Generalized Coordinates:** The state vector is chosen as $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta, v, \phi]^T$, where $(x,y,\theta)$ is the **pose** in the global frame, $v$ is the longitudinal velocity, and $\phi$ is the steering angle.
- **Step 2 — Express the Non-Holonomic Constraint:** The rear wheels do not slip sideways, giving the constraint equation. This **eliminates one degree of freedom**, reducing the independent velocity inputs to two: $v$ and $\phi$ (or equivalently, $v$ and $\dot{\phi}$ in dynamic formulation).
- **Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law in the Body Frame:** The vehicle is treated as a **single rigid body** of mass $m$ and yaw inertia $I_z$. Forces from the tires (longitudinal $F_x$ and lateral $F_y$) act at the front and rear axle contact patches.
- **Step 4 — Resolve Forces onto the Body $x_b$ and $y_b$ Axes:** Because the front wheels are steered by angle $\phi$, the front tire force must be **rotated** by $\phi$ before being added to the body-frame balance.
- **Step 5 — Combine Kinematic and Dynamic Equations:** The full state-space dynamic model is obtained by chaining the **kinematic pose update** $\dot{\mathbf{p}} = f(\mathbf{p}, v, \phi)$ with the **dynamic force/steering update** $\dot{v} = g(\cdot)$ and $\ddot{\phi} = h(\cdot)$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles every equation a student is expected to recall for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. The vertical bar notation $\vert$ is rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ | Body $x$-axis velocity projected to global $X$ | Pose update |
| 2 | $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ | Body $y$-axis velocity projected to global $Y$ | Pose update |
| 3 | $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{v}{L}\tan\phi$ | Yaw rate from bicycle model | Heading update |
| 4 | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Non-holonomic rolling constraint | Eliminating $y$-DOF |
| 5 | $m(\dot{v} - v\dot{\theta}) = F_{xr} + F_{xf}\cos\phi - F_{yf}\sin\phi$ | Body-frame longitudinal force balance | Longitudinal dynamics |
| 6 | $m(v\dot{\theta}) = F_{yr} + F_{xf}\sin\phi + F_{yf}\cos\phi$ | Body-frame lateral force balance | Lateral dynamics |
| 7 | $I_z\ddot{\theta} = l_f(F_{xf}\sin\phi + F_{yf}\cos\phi) - l_r F_{yr}$ | Yaw moment balance | Rotational dynamics |
| 8 | $F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f}(\delta_f - \theta_{vf})$ | Linear tire model (front) | Tire force computation |
| 9 | $F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r}(-\theta_{vr})$ | Linear tire model (rear) | Tire force computation |
| 10 | $\delta_{in} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R - t/2}\right)$ | Ackermann inner wheel angle | Steering geometry |
| 11 | $\delta_{out} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R + t/2}\right)$ | Ackermann outer wheel angle | Steering geometry |
| 12 | $J_\phi \ddot{\phi} + b_\phi \dot{\phi} = \tau_\phi$ | Steering actuator dynamics (2nd order) | Steering lag modeling |
| 13 | $a_{lat,max} = \mu g$ | Maximum sustainable lateral acceleration | Stability envelope |
| 14 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\phi_{max})}$ | Minimum turning radius | Maneuverability bound |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Highway Driving:** The dynamic model is used in **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** frameworks (e.g., Apollo, Autoware) to predict the vehicle's future trajectory over a horizon, accounting for tire saturation.
- **Drift Control & Racing:** When the tire forces saturate (nonlinear regime), the linear model breaks down and a **friction-circle** or **Pacejka magic formula** tire model is employed — the dynamic model is the gateway to these advanced formulations.
- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Even at low speeds, the steering motor has finite bandwidth. A dynamic model that includes $J_\phi$ and $b_\phi$ prevents **control windup** during sharp turns.
- **Trailer-Truck Backing Control:** The dynamic model is essential when a car tows trailers — kinematic models fail catastrophically for **rearward jackknife prevention**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Car-Like WMR Dynamic Model (Full Newton-Euler)

We adopt the **bicycle-model abstraction**: the two front wheels are merged into a single steered wheel at the front axle, and similarly for the rear. Let $(x_c, y_c)$ be the **center of gravity (CG)** position in the global frame, and $\theta$ the heading angle measured from the global $X$-axis.

### Step 1: Define the State Vector

The state vector for the dynamic model is:
$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_c \\ y_c \\ \theta \\ v \\ \phi \end{bmatrix}$$

The two control inputs are the **driving force** $F_d$ (applied to the rear axle) and the **steering torque** $\tau_\phi$.

### Step 2: Apply the Non-Holonomic Rolling Constraint

Since the rear wheels are aligned with the body $x$-axis and roll without slipping, the **velocity of the rear axle contact point** must be zero in the body $y$-direction:
$$\dot{y}_c \cos\theta - \dot{x}_c \sin\theta = 0$$

This is the standard non-holonomic constraint.

### Step 3: Kinematic Relations (Pose Update)

The longitudinal velocity $v$ of the CG is the projection of $(\dot{x}_c, \dot{y}_c)$ onto the body $x$-axis. Combined with the constraint:
$$\dot{x}_c = v\cos\theta$$
$$\dot{y}_c = v\sin\theta$$

The yaw rate $\dot{\theta}$ follows from the **Ackermann bicycle geometry**: a wheelbase $L = l_f + l_r$ and steering angle $\phi$ produce an instantaneous turn of radius $R = L / \tan\phi$:
$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi$$

### Step 4: Force Balance in the Body Frame (Longitudinal)

The CG sees a longitudinal acceleration $\dot{v} - v\dot{\theta}$ (Coriolis-corrected) equal to the sum of longitudinal tire forces. The front wheel force $F_{xf}$ must be **projected** onto the body $x$-axis by multiplying by $\cos\phi$:
$$m(\dot{v} - v\dot{\theta}) = F_{xr} + F_{xf}\cos\phi - F_{yf}\sin\phi$$

**Logic:** The negative $F_{yf}\sin\phi$ term arises because the front tire's lateral force has a small longitudinal component pointing **backward** when the wheel is steered.

### Step 5: Force Balance in the Body Frame (Lateral)

The lateral equation captures centripetal acceleration $v\dot{\theta}$:
$$m v\dot{\theta} = F_{yr} + F_{xf}\sin\phi + F_{yf}\cos\phi$$

### Step 6: Yaw Moment Balance

Taking moments about the CG, the front axle is at distance $l_f$ ahead and the rear at $l_r$ behind:
$$I_z\ddot{\theta} = l_f(F_{xf}\sin\phi + F_{yf}\cos\phi) - l_r F_{yr}$$

**Logic:** Front forces contribute a positive (nose-right) moment about the CG; rear lateral force $F_{yr}$ produces a nose-left moment (subtracted).

### Step 7: Linear Tire (Cornering) Force Model

For small slip angles, tire force is proportional to slip angle with stiffness $C_\alpha$:
$$F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f}(\delta_f - \theta_{vf}) \approx C_{\alpha f}\phi \quad \text{(for small slip)}$$
$$F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r}(-\theta_{vr})$$

### Step 8: Steering Actuator Dynamics

The steering column is modeled as a **second-order rotational system** with inertia $J_\phi$, damping $b_\phi$, and applied torque $\tau_\phi$:
$$J_\phi \ddot{\phi} + b_\phi \dot{\phi} = \tau_\phi$$

### Step 9: Assemble the Complete State-Space Model

Combining all the above, the **complete nonlinear dynamic model** of the car-like WMR is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_c &= v\cos\theta \\
\dot{y}_c &= v\sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \dfrac{v}{L}\tan\phi \\
\dot{v} &= \dfrac{1}{m}\left(F_{xr} + F_{xf}\cos\phi - F_{yf}\sin\phi\right) + v\dot{\theta} \\
\ddot{\phi} &= \dfrac{1}{J_\phi}\left(\tau_\phi - b_\phi\dot{\phi}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the linear tire model and assuming the driving force is applied at the rear wheel ($F_{xr} = F_d$, $F_{xf} = 0$ for rear-wheel drive):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{v} &= \dfrac{F_d}{m} - \dfrac{C_{\alpha f}\phi\sin\phi}{m} - \dfrac{C_{\alpha r}}{m}\!\left(\dfrac{v}{L}\tan\phi\right)\!\cos\theta \cdot (\text{tertiary terms}) \\
&\approx \dfrac{F_d}{m} \quad \text{(first-order simplification)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The full coupled form is usually retained for **nonlinear controller design**.

### Step 3.2: Derivation of the Ackermann Steering Geometry

The Ackermann condition ensures that **all four wheels** rotate about a common instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) of radius $R$ from the rear axle.

For a vehicle with track width $t$ (distance between left and right wheels on the same axle):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\delta_{in} &= \frac{L}{R - t/2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta_{in} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L}{R - t/2}\right) \\
\tan\delta_{out} &= \frac{L}{R + t/2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta_{out} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L}{R + t/2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Ackermann error** (deviation from the ideal geometry) is:
$$\epsilon_A = \delta_{in} - \delta_{out}$$

In a perfectly designed steering linkage, $\epsilon_A = 0$. Real automotive linkages (and WMR steering systems) aim to minimize this error to reduce tire scrub and wear.

### Step 3.3: Minimum Turning Radius Derivation

From $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$, the instantaneous radius of curvature is:
$$R = \frac{v}{\dot{\theta}} = \frac{L}{\tan\phi}$$

When the steering angle reaches its mechanical limit $\phi = \phi_{max}$:
$$R_{min} = \frac{L}{\tan\phi_{max}}$$

For example, a car with $L = 2.5$ m and $\phi_{max} = 35°$ has $R_{min} \approx 3.57$ m.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Students often confuse the **kinematic** and **dynamic** turning radius. The kinematic $R = L/\tan\phi$ is the *geometric* radius assuming no tire slip. The dynamic radius (when the tire saturates) is **larger** and given by $R_{dyn} = v^2 / (a_{lat,max}) = v^2 / (\mu g)$.
