Heading is mathematically defined as the rotation angle of the robot's local coordinate frame $\{R\}$ about the **Z-axis (yaw)** of the global frame $\{W\}$. Formally:

$$\psi = \arctan\left(\frac{\dot{y}_{robot}}{\dot{x}_{robot}}\right)$$

Where $\psi$ is measured in **radians** (or **degrees**) and the orientation is expressed as part of the pose vector:

$$\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

Here, $\theta \equiv \psi$ represents the planar heading. The KTU syllabus categorizes heading sensors into three principal families:

| Sensor Class | Reference Frame | Sensor Type |
|--------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Compass (Magnetometer)** | Earth's Magnetic Field | Exteroceptive |
| **Gyroscope** | Inertial Frame | Proprioceptive |
| **Inclinometer / Accelerometer** | Gravity Vector | Proprioceptive |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Blindfolded Walk":**
> Imagine a person walking blindfolded in a room. To know which way they are facing, they have two natural "sensors":
> 1. **A built-in sense of rotation (Gyroscope)** — they remember turning left or right by a certain amount, like a spinning top that resists tilting.
> 2. **A pull toward a known landmark (Compass)** — they can feel the pull of Earth's magnetic field toward the North, just like sailors using a magnetic compass at sea.
>
> A *gyroscope* drifts over time (the person forgets exactly how much they turned), while a *compass* can be disturbed by metal objects (disturbances). Modern mobile robots use **both together (sensor fusion)** — the same way a sailor uses both a spinning gyrocompass and a magnetic compass to navigate accurately across the ocean.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- Earth's magnetic flux density: **B ≈ 50 µT (microtesla)** at the surface, varying between **25 µT – 65 µT** depending on geographic location.
- Horizontal component of Earth's field (used for 2D heading): **B_H ≈ 20 µT** at mid-latitudes.
- Magnetic declination: angle between true North and magnetic North, must be compensated.
- Standard heading update rate in mobile robots: **10 Hz – 100 Hz** for terrestrial applications.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examinations, students must remember that a heading sensor output is **always relative** (to magnetic North or to its own initial orientation). Converting it to a **global heading** requires calibration for *magnetic declination* and *hard-iron / soft-iron distortions* caused by the robot's own motors and chassis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Heading Sensors (Detailed)

Mobile robots require heading information under three operational conditions:
1. **Static heading** (robot is stationary but needs to know orientation).
2. **Dynamic heading** (robot is moving, requires real-time updates).
3. **Long-term accuracy** (no drift over hours of operation).

Each sensor family addresses these differently:

#### 2.1.1 Magnetic Compass (Magnetometer)
- **Operating Principle:** Measures the components of the Earth's magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$ along three orthogonal axes $(B_x, B_y, B_z)$ using one of three transduction mechanisms:
  - **Hall Effect Sensor** — semiconductor sensing of voltage induced by $\vec{B} \times \vec{v}$.
  - **Magnetoresistive (AMR / GMR / TMR)** — change in electrical resistance proportional to field strength.
  - **Fluxgate** — uses a ferromagnetic core driven into saturation; sensitive to **nT-level** fields.
- **2D Heading Equation** (assuming the sensor is held level):
$$\psi_{mag} = \arctan 2(B_y, B_x) = \text{atan2}(B_y, B_x)$$
- The `atan2` function (used in KTU problems) returns values in **$(-\pi, \pi]$**.

#### 2.1.2 Gyroscope
- **Operating Principle:** Measures angular velocity $\omega$ (in **rad/s**) about its sensitive axis based on the **Coriolis Effect** (MEMS gyros) or **Sagnac Effect** (optical gyros).
- **Heading Update via Integration:**
$$\psi(t) = \psi(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \omega_z(\tau)\, d\tau$$
- In discrete time (sampled at interval $\Delta t$):
$$\psi_k = \psi_{k-1} + \omega_z \cdot \Delta t$$
- **Drift Problem:** MEMS gyros drift at rates of **1°/min to 10°/min** without compensation. This is the **single most important KTU exam point** about gyroscopes.

#### 2.1.3 Inclinometer / Accelerometer
- **Operating Principle:** A 3-axis accelerometer at rest measures the gravity vector $\vec{g}$ instead of true linear acceleration. By decomposing $\vec{g}$ into its components, the **roll (φ)** and **pitch (θ)** can be determined.
- **Tilt Equations:**
$$\phi = \arctan 2(A_y, A_z) \quad ; \quad \theta = \arctan 2\left(-A_x, \sqrt{A_y^2 + A_z^2}\right)$$
- These are then used to **tilt-compensate** the magnetometer.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all the equations** students must memorize for the KTU Mobile Robotics ESE on heading sensors. Variables are defined below the table.

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\psi = \text{atan2}(B_y, B_x)$ | 2D heading from magnetometer (level sensor) | rad |
| 2 | $\psi_k = \psi_{k-1} + \omega_z \cdot \Delta t$ | Gyroscope heading integration (Euler) | rad |
| 3 | $\phi = \text{atan2}(A_y, A_z)$ | Roll angle from accelerometer | rad |
| 4 | $\theta = \text{atan2}\left(-A_x, \sqrt{A_y^2+A_z^2}\right)$ | Pitch angle from accelerometer | rad |
| 5 | $B_x^c = B_x\cos\theta + B_y\sin\phi\sin\theta + B_z\cos\phi\sin\theta$ | Tilt-compensated X-axis field | µT |
| 6 | $B_y^c = B_y\cos\phi - B_z\sin\phi$ | Tilt-compensated Y-axis field | µT |
| 7 | $\psi = \text{atan2}(-B_y^c, B_x^c)$ | Tilt-compensated heading | rad |
| 8 | $\psi_{fused} = \alpha \cdot \psi_{gyro} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \psi_{mag}$ | Complementary filter output | rad |
| 9 | $\alpha = \frac{\tau}{\tau + \Delta t}$ | Complementary filter weight | dimensionless |
| 10 | $e_k = \psi_{mag,k} - \psi_{gyro,k}$ | Innovation / error in fusion | rad |

**Variable Definitions:**
- $A_x, A_y, A_z$ — accelerometer readings on each axis (in **g** or **m/s²**).
- $B_x, B_y, B_z$ — magnetometer raw readings (in **µT** or **Gauss**, 1 G = 100 µT).
- $B_x^c, B_y^c$ — magnetometer readings after tilt compensation.
- $\omega_z$ — gyroscope angular velocity about the vertical axis.
- $\alpha$ — complementary filter weight, typically **0.95 – 0.98**.
- $\tau$ — time constant of the filter, in **seconds**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Heading sensors are the **silent workhorses** behind every autonomous system. Specific applications:
- **Autonomous Ground Vehicles (AGVs):** Warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva) use MEMS IMUs to dead-reckon between fiducial corrections.
- **Aerial Drones (UAVs):** Flight controllers like Pixhawk fuse gyros (short-term) with magnetometers (long-term) — losing the magnetometer causes "toilet-bowl effect" drift in GPS-denied flight.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Use fiber-optic gyroscopes (FOGs) for heading because magnetic fields are distorted by water and metal hulls.
- **Agricultural Robotics:** Tilt-compensated compasses on uneven terrain keep row-following accurate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heading from a 2-Axis Magnetometer (Level Case)

**Problem Setup:** A mobile robot carries a 2-axis magnetometer in a horizontal plane. The sensor outputs $(B_x, B_y)$ relative to its body frame. Derive the heading angle $\psi$ with respect to magnetic North.

**Step 1 — Represent the Earth's field in the body frame.**

Assume the Earth's magnetic field vector in the **global frame** $\{W\}$ lies entirely along the $X_W$ axis (magnetic North). Its magnitude is the horizontal field strength $B_H$.

$$\vec{B}_W = \begin{bmatrix} B_H \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 2D rotation matrix about the Z-axis.**

The body frame $\{R\}$ is rotated by heading $\psi$ about $Z_W$. The 2D rotation matrix is:

$$R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & \sin\psi & 0 \\ -\sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Transform the field into the body frame.**

$$\vec{B}_R = R_z(\psi)\, \vec{B}_W = \begin{bmatrix} B_H\cos\psi \\ -B_H\sin\psi \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Equate to the measured components.**

Since the sensor outputs are $(B_x, B_y)$:

$$B_x = B_H\cos\psi \quad ; \quad B_y = -B_H\sin\psi$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\psi$ using `atan2`.**

Taking the ratio eliminates $B_H$:

$$\frac{B_y}{B_x} = \frac{-B_H\sin\psi}{B_H\cos\psi} = -\tan\psi$$

Therefore:

$$\psi = \text{atan2}(-B_y, B_x)$$

> This is the canonical KTU result. The **negative sign on $B_y$** is critical — losing it costs 2 marks in board valuation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tilt Compensation of a 3-Axis Magnetometer

**Problem Setup:** The robot is on a slope, with roll $\phi$ and pitch $\theta$ measured by the accelerometer. Derive the tilt-corrected heading.

**Step 1 — Rotate the field from the body frame into the global frame.**

The body frame is related to the global frame by:

$$R_{R \to W} = R_z(-\psi)\, R_y(-\theta)\, R_x(-\phi)$$

We wish to undo the roll and pitch (but not yaw) to obtain a "level" magnetic field. Apply $R_y(\theta)\, R_x(\phi)$ to the raw measurements:

$$\begin{bmatrix} B_x^c \\ B_y^c \\ B_z^c \end{bmatrix} = R_y(\theta)\, R_x(\phi) \begin{bmatrix} B_x \\ B_y \\ B_z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Write out the rotation matrices.**

$$R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product $R_y(\theta)\, R_x(\phi)$ row-by-row.**

Multiplying these two $3\times 3$ matrices:

$$\begin{aligned} R_y R_x &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta\sin\phi & \sin\theta\cos\phi \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta\sin\phi & \cos\theta\cos\phi \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Extract the first two rows (the parts that matter for heading).**

The first row gives $B_x^c$:

$$\begin{aligned} B_x^c &= \cos\theta \cdot B_x + \sin\theta\sin\phi \cdot B_y + \sin\theta\cos\phi \cdot B_z \end{aligned}$$

The second row gives $B_y^c$:

$$\begin{aligned} B_y^c &= \cos\phi \cdot B_y - \sin\phi \cdot B_z \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the corrected heading.**

$$\begin{aligned} \psi &= \text{atan2}\bigl(-B_y^c,\; B_x^c\bigr) \end{aligned}$$

This is the formula implemented in nearly every drone flight controller (e.g., ArduPilot, PX4) and is a **guaranteed 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Complementary Filter (Sensor Fusion)

**Problem Setup:** A gyroscope drifts with bias $b_\omega$, and a magnetometer is noisy. Derive a fusion filter that produces a stable heading $\psi_f$.

**Step 1 — Define the gyro-predicted heading with bias.**

$$\begin{aligned} \psi_{gyro,k} &= \psi_{k-1} + (\omega_{z,k} - b_\omega)\Delta t \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Define the magnetometer heading (the "absolute" reference).**

$$\begin{aligned} \psi_{mag,k} &= \text{atan2}(-B_y, B_x) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Combine using a first-order complementary filter.**

The filter's transfer function combines a **high-pass path** (gyro) and a **low-pass path** (magnetometer):

$$\begin{aligned} \psi_{fused,k} &= \alpha \cdot \bigl(\psi_{fused,k-1} + \omega_{z,k}\Delta t\bigr) + (1-\alpha) \cdot \psi_{mag,k} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the error $e_k = \psi_{mag,k} - \psi_{gyro,k}$.**

$$\begin{aligned} \psi_{fused,k} &= \psi_{fused,k-1} + \omega_{z,k}\Delta t + (1-\alpha)\, e_k \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Z-domain analysis (advanced, for 14-mark questions).**

Taking the Z-transform and solving for the transfer function from magnetometer to output:

$$\begin{aligned} H_{mag}(z) &= \frac{(1-\alpha)}{1 - \alpha z^{-1}} \end{aligned}$$

This is a **first-order IIR low-pass filter** with cutoff determined by $\alpha$. The complementary transfer function from the gyro is a high-pass filter — by design, they sum to **unity**, ensuring no heading is "lost."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Heading Sensor System Architecture

The following diagram captures the full signal flow from raw sensor inputs to a fused heading output, including all calibration and compensation stages. This is a **block-level functional architecture flow** — the recommended approach when physical schematics are too complex to render directly in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Earth Magnetic Field B_Earth] --> M1[3-Axis Magnetometer]
    B[Robot Angular Velocity omega_z] --> G1[MEMS Gyroscope Z-axis]
    C[Gravity Vector g] --> ACC[3-Axis Accelerometer]
    
    M1 --> RAW[Raw Field Vector Bx By Bz]
    G1 --> INT[Discrete Integration psi_gyro]
    ACC --> TILT[Tilt Angles phi theta]
    
    TILT --> COMP[Tilt Compensation R_y * R_x]
    RAW --> COMP
    COMP --> HEAD[Compensated Heading atan2 -Byc Bxc]
    
    HEAD --> FUS[Fusion Stage Complementary Filter]
    INT --> FUS
    FUS --> OUT[Stable Heading psi_fused]
    
    OUT --> CTRL[Robot Motion Controller]
    OUT --> NAV[Navigation Stack]
    
    RAW --> CAL[Hard-Soft Iron Calibration]
    CAL --> M1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree: Types of Heading Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HS[Heading Sensors for Mobile Robots] --> P[Proprioceptive]
    HS --> E[Exteroceptive]
    
    P --> G[Gyroscopes]
    P --> I[Inclinometers / Accelerometers]
    P --> ENC[Wheel Encoders - Odometry]
    
    E --> C[Magnetic Compasses]
    E --> GPS[GPS - Course Over Ground]
    E --> VIS[Vision-based - Sun, Stars, Markers]
    
    G --> G1[MEMS Gyro]
    G --> G2[Ring Laser Gyro RLG]
    G --> G3[Fiber Optic Gyro FOG]
    G --> G4[Mechanical Gyro - Historical]
    
    C --> C1[Hall Effect]
    C --> C2[Magnetoresistive AMR GMR TMR]
    C --> C3[Fluxgate]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Error Sources and Compensation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EM[Earth Magnetic Field] -->|Distorted by| HI[Hard Iron Distortion - Permanent Magnets]
    EM -->|Distorted by| SI[Soft Iron Distortion - Ferromagnetic Materials]
    EM -->|Affected by| DEC[Magnetic Declination]
    EM -->|Affected by| TILT2[Platform Tilt]
    
    HI --> ELL[Ellipsoid Fitting Calibration]
    SI --> ELL
    ELL --> COR[Corrected Field Vector]
    
    DEC --> ADD[Add Declination Offset]
    COR --> ADD
    TILT2 --> TC[Tilt Compensation]
    ADD --> TC
    TC --> FH[Final Corrected Heading]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the heading sensor block diagram" in the exam, students should reproduce a **simplified version** of Section 4.1 (4–5 blocks) showing: *Sensor → Compensation → Fusion → Output*. The detailed error-correction flow (Section 4.3) earns bonus marks if included in a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*What is a heading sensor? List any two heading sensors used in mobile robotics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **heading sensor** is a device that measures the orientation (heading angle $\psi$) of a mobile robot with respect to a reference direction, typically magnetic North. Two common heading sensors are:
1. **Magnetic Compass (Magnetometer)** — measures the Earth's magnetic field to determine absolute heading.
2. **Gyroscope** — measures angular velocity, which is integrated to obtain relative heading.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two sensors — total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*State the principle of operation of a MEMS gyroscope.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) gyroscope** operates on the **Coriolis Effect**. When a proof mass is driven to oscillate at a known velocity $v$ along one axis, and the gyroscope rotates with angular velocity $\omega$ about a perpendicular axis, a **Coriolis force** $F_c = 2m(\vec{v} \times \vec{\omega})$ is induced on a sensing axis. The displacement caused by this force is capacitively measured, yielding a voltage proportional to $\omega$. *(3 marks — full credit if the term "Coriolis Effect" is mentioned and the cross-product relationship is given.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**(a)** Explain the working of a 3-axis magnetometer as a heading sensor. Derive the heading equation for a level sensor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is gyroscope drift? Explain with a numerical example how integrating angular velocity leads to cumulative heading error. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of 3-Axis Magnetometer (7 marks):**

A 3-axis magnetometer (typically a Hall-effect or magnetoresistive sensor) measures the components of the Earth's magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$ along three orthogonal axes of the robot's body frame.

1. The Earth's field has a horizontal component $B_H$ pointing toward magnetic North. The sensor measures $(B_x, B_y, B_z)$.
2. For a sensor held **level** (no roll or pitch), the field lies entirely in the $X-Y$ plane:
$$B_z = 0, \quad B_x = B_H\cos\psi, \quad B_y = -B_H\sin\psi$$
3. The heading is recovered using the two-argument arctangent:
$$\psi = \text{atan2}(-B_y, B_x)$$
4. The `atan2` function returns values in $(-\pi, \pi]$ and correctly handles all four quadrants — unlike `atan`, which fails for $\psi > 90°$.
5. The output gives the **magnetic heading**; to get **true heading**, the local magnetic declination $\delta$ must be added:
$$\psi_{true} = \psi_{mag} + \delta$$

*(1 mark each for points 1–5, 2 marks for the final equation. Total 7 marks.)*

**(b) Gyroscope Drift (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** A gyroscope measures angular velocity $\omega_z$, not absolute heading. To obtain heading, the angular velocity must be **integrated over time**:
$$\psi(t) = \int_0^t \omega_z(\tau)\, d\tau$$
2. **Source of Drift (2 marks):** Every gyroscope has a **bias** $b_\omega$ (a non-zero output even when stationary) and **random noise**. In a MEMS gyroscope, $b_\omega$ can drift due to temperature changes and aging.
3. **Numerical Example (3 marks):** Suppose a gyroscope has a constant bias of $b_\omega = 0.1°/\text{s}$. After **$t = 60$ seconds** of integration:
$$\text{Error} = b_\omega \times t = 0.1°/\text{s} \times 60\,\text{s} = 6°$$
After **1 hour (3600 s):**
$$\text{Error} = 0.1°/\text{s} \times 3600\,\text{s} = 360° \;(\text{full circle!})$$
4. This shows that **pure inertial heading** is unusable for long-duration missions without external correction (magnetometer, GPS, or visual landmarks). This is why **sensor fusion** is essential in mobile robotics.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the tilt compensation technique used to obtain correct heading from a magnetometer mounted on a mobile robot traversing uneven terrain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the complementary filter equation used to fuse gyroscope and magnetometer data for stable heading estimation. What is the role of the weight $\alpha$? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Tilt Compensation (7 marks):**

1. **Problem Statement (1 mark):** When the robot is on a slope, the magnetometer's $Z$-axis tilts away from vertical. A naïve `atan2(-By, Bx)` produces a wrong heading because it assumes the sensor is level.
2. **Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Accelerometer → Roll(φ), Pitch(θ) → Rotation Matrix R_y(θ)·R_x(φ)
                                                ↓
Raw Magnetometer (Bx, By, Bz) ────────────→ Corrected Field (Bxc, Byc)
                                                ↓
                                  Heading = atan2(-Byc, Bxc)
```

3. **Key Equations (3 marks):**
$$B_x^c = B_x\cos\theta + B_y\sin\phi\sin\theta + B_z\cos\phi\sin\theta$$
$$B_y^c = B_y\cos\phi - B_z\sin\phi$$
$$\psi = \text{atan2}(-B_y^c, B_x^c)$$

The accelerometer provides $\phi = \text{atan2}(A_y, A_z)$ and $\theta = \text{atan2}(-A_x, \sqrt{A_y^2 + A_z^2})$, which are substituted into the above.

---

**(b) Complementary Filter Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Starting Point (1 mark):** Gyro gives short-term accuracy but drifts; magnetometer gives long-term accuracy but is noisy. We want the best of both.
2. **Filter Equation (4 marks):**
$$\psi_{fused,k} = \alpha\,\psi_{gyro,k} + (1-\alpha)\,\psi_{mag,k}$$
where $\psi_{gyro,k} = \psi_{fused,k-1} + \omega_{z,k}\Delta t$.
3. **Role of $\alpha$ (2 marks):** $\alpha$ is the **weighting factor** (typically **0.95–0.98**). It determines the filter's time constant $\tau = \alpha \Delta t / (1 - \alpha)$. A higher $\alpha$ trusts the gyroscope more (good for short, fast maneuvers); a lower $\alpha$ trusts the magnetometer more (good for correcting long-term drift).
4. **Practical Tuning (1 mark — bonus):** In KTU exams, mention that $\alpha$ is tuned empirically based on the gyroscope's drift rate and the magnetometer's noise floor.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students marks in KTU board valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $\psi = \text{atan2}(-B_y, B_x)$ — costs **2 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Confusing the order of `atan2` arguments** — always **Y first, X second**: `atan2(Y, X)`, not `atan2(X, Y)`.
> 3. **Forgetting magnetic declination** when converting magnetic heading to true heading.
> 4. **Assuming $B_z = 0$ without stating "for a level sensor"** — examiners deduct marks if the assumption is not explicit.
> 5. **Writing "gyroscope measures heading"** — wrong. Gyroscope measures **angular velocity**, not heading. Heading is obtained by **integration**.
> 6. **Skipping the units in the formula sheet** — KTU valuation key explicitly penalizes missing units in derivations.
> 7. **Confusing roll ($\phi$) and pitch ($\theta$) axes** in the tilt compensation derivation. The **rotation order is critical** and must be stated (yaw → pitch → roll, or equivalent).

---

### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Heading sensor** = device that measures the robot's **orientation angle $\psi$** about the vertical (Z) axis.
- ✅ The **three principal types** are: **Magnetometer (compass), Gyroscope, and Accelerometer/Inclinometer**. None is perfect alone — fusion is mandatory.
- ✅ **Magnetometer equation (level case):** $\psi = \text{atan2}(-B_y, B_x)$. Always use `atan2`, never `atan`.
- ✅ **Gyroscope equation:** $\psi_k = \psi_{k-1} + \omega_z \Delta t$ — heading comes from **integration**, hence the **drift problem**.
- ✅ **Gyro drift** accumulates linearly with time: $\text{Error} = b_\omega \cdot t$. A bias of even $0.1°/\text{s}$ yields $6°$ error in 1 minute and a **full $360°$ in 1 hour**.
- ✅ **Tilt compensation** requires accelerometer-derived roll $\phi$ and pitch $\theta$, then rotates the magnetic field into a level virtual plane.
- ✅ **Complementary filter** combines high-frequency gyro accuracy with low-frequency magnetometer stability: $\psi_{fused} = \alpha \psi_{gyro} + (1-\alpha) \psi_{mag}$, with $\alpha \approx 0.95$.
- ✅ **Magnetic declination** is the angle between magnetic and true North — must be added for true heading: $\psi_{true} = \psi_{mag} + \delta$.
- ✅ **Hard-iron distortion** (additive constant offset from permanent magnets) and **soft-iron distortion** (multiplicative scaling from ferrous materials) must be calibrated out using **ellipsoid fitting** or similar techniques.
- ✅ **Standard unit for magnetic field:** Tesla (T). Earth's field is **~50 µT** at the surface.
- ✅ **KTU exam hot topics:** Tilt compensation derivation, complementary filter derivation, comparison of gyroscope vs. magnetometer error characteristics.
- ✅ **In a 14-mark KTU question**, always include: (i) the **rotational matrix**, (ii) the **direction of rotation**, (iii) the **final `atan2` equation with proper sign**, and (iv) the **assumptions** (e.g., level sensor, calibrated magnetometer).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accelerometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Accelerometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
An **accelerometer** is an electromechanical inertial sensor that measures **proper acceleration** — the physical acceleration experienced by the sensor relative to free-fall. In the context of mobile robotics, it is the cornerstone of the Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) and provides the second time-derivative of the robot's position with respect to an inertial reference frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Proper Acceleration vs. Coordinate Acceleration:**
> - **Coordinate (free-fall) acceleration** = $\vec{a}_{coord} = \ddot{\vec{r}}$ in an inertial frame.
> - **Proper acceleration** = the acceleration relative to free-fall, which is what the sensor actually reports: $\vec{a}_{proper} = \vec{a}_{coord} - \vec{g}$, where $\vec{g}$ is the local gravitational vector.
> - A sensor at rest on Earth reads $+\vec{g}$ (≈ **9.80665 m/s²**), not zero.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Imagine a small steel ball glued to the end of a weak spring inside a transparent jar. When the jar sits still on a table, the spring compresses slightly because the table pushes the jar upward against the ball's inertia. Now imagine shaking the jar horizontally — the ball, due to its inertia, appears to "lag behind" and stretches the spring sideways. By measuring how much the spring stretches, you can deduce how hard (and in what direction) the jar is being accelerated. That is essentially how a **proof-mass + spring** accelerometer works.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does an accelerometer "feel" gravity?**
> Because gravity and any other acceleration are **indistinguishable** by a local mechanical experiment (Einstein's Equivalence Principle). The proof mass responds to the **net non-gravitational force** (the contact force from the suspension), which is exactly Newton's second law relative to free-fall.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Metrics
- Standard gravity: **$g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$**
- Typical MEMS range: $\pm 2g$ to $\pm 200g$
- Typical bandwidth: **DC to 500 Hz (consumer MEMS)**; up to 10 kHz for tactical-grade sensors.
- Units: $\text{m/s}^2$ (SI), or multiples of $g$ (e.g., $1g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Generalized Mass–Spring–Damper
The most generic accelerometer model is a single-degree-of-freedom proof mass $m$ suspended by a spring (stiffness $k$) and a damper (viscous coefficient $b$). Let $x$ be the displacement of the proof mass relative to the sensor housing, and $y$ be the displacement of the housing itself.

Newton's second law applied to the proof mass yields:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{y}$$

Rearranging into standard control form (with input $\ddot{y}$ and output $x$):

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{b}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = -\ddot{y}$$

The right-hand side $\ddot{y}$ is the **housing acceleration** — i.e., the acceleration we ultimately want to measure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two natural frequencies of interest:**
> - **Undamped natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$
> - **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{km}}$

### 2.2 Measurement Model (Stochastic Form)
A realistic accelerometer output is corrupted by several error sources:

$$\tilde{a} = (1 + s)\,a + b_a + n(t)$$

where:
- $\tilde{a}$ = measured (raw) acceleration
- $a$ = true proper acceleration
- $s$ = scale-factor (gain) error
- $b_a$ = bias offset (constant or slowly varying, units of $\text{m/s}^2$)
- $n(t)$ = zero-mean white noise (units of $\text{m/s}^2/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Quantity | Symbolic Form | Typical Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|--------------------|---------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Newton's law for proof mass | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{y}$ | N, m, kg, s | Core dynamic model |
| 2 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Mechanical resonance of the sensor |
| 3 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = b / (2\sqrt{km})$ | dimensionless | Critically damped when $\zeta = 1$ |
| 4 | Transfer function $X(s)/\ddot{Y}(s)$ | $\dfrac{-1}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}$ | $\text{s}^2/\text{m}$ | Maps input accel to proof-mass displacement |
| 5 | Output voltage (capacitive) | $V_{out} = \alpha \cdot x + V_{ref}$ | Volts | Converts displacement to electrical signal |
| 6 | Stochastic measurement model | $\tilde{a} = (1+s)a + b_a + n(t)$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Full error budget |
| 7 | Random walk coefficient | $VRW = \sigma_n \sqrt{T_s}$ | $\text{m/s}/\sqrt{\text{hr}}$ | Used in INS error propagation |
| 8 | Proper accel vs coordinate | $\vec{a}_{proper} = \vec{a}_{coord} - \vec{g}$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Sensor reading interpretation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Any pipe symbol `|x|` in KTU answer scripts should be written as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to prevent markdown table-breaking.

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Mobile Robotics
- **Dead-reckoning** of mobile robots: integrating $\int a \, dt \Rightarrow$ velocity; double-integration $\Rightarrow$ position (subject to drift).
- **Tilt estimation**: at rest, $\tilde{a} = -\vec{g}$ provides roll/pitch angles.
- **Vibration analysis & shock detection** in legged/wheeled platforms.
- **Sensor fusion** in EKF/UKF with gyros and GPS for full INS/GPS navigation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Accelerometer Transfer Function

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram.**
A proof mass $m$ is suspended inside the sensor housing by a linear spring (constant $k$) and a viscous damper (constant $b$). Define:
- $y(t)$ = absolute displacement of the housing (the robot's body).
- $x(t)$ = displacement of the proof mass *relative to the housing*.
- $z(t) = x(t) + y(t)$ = absolute displacement of the proof mass.

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law to the proof mass in an inertial frame.**
The forces acting on the mass are the spring force ($-kz$) and the damping force ($-b\dot{z}$). No external field force is needed for the inertial reference:

$$m\ddot{z} = -kz - b\dot{z}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $z = x + y$ and expand derivatives.**

$$m(\ddot{x} + \ddot{y}) = -k(x + y) - b(\dot{x} + \dot{y})$$

**Step 4 — Group terms with $x$ on the left side.**

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{y} - b\dot{y} - ky$$

**Step 5 — Recognition:** in nearly all MEMS designs, the damper and spring are anchored to the housing; their *absolute* motion is small compared to the proof-mass relative motion, so $b\dot{y} \ll b\dot{x}$ and $ky \ll kx$. We drop the cross-terms on the right:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx \approx -m\ddot{y}$$

**Step 6 — Take the Laplace transform** (zero initial conditions):

$$ms^2 X(s) + bs X(s) + kX(s) = -ms^2 Y(s)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the transfer function** $H(s) = X(s)/\ddot{Y}(s)$, where $\ddot{Y}(s) = s^2 Y(s)$:

$$H(s) = \frac{X(s)}{\ddot{Y}(s)} = \frac{-m}{ms^2 + bs + k} = \frac{-1}{s^2 + \frac{b}{m}s + \frac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 8 — Express in standard second-order form** using $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$:

$$\boxed{\;H(s) = \frac{-1}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}\;}$$

with
$\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $\zeta = b/(2\sqrt{km})$.

**Step 9 — Frequency response insight.**
For $|\omega| \ll \omega_n$ (operating well below resonance), the magnitude response is approximately flat: $\vert H(j\omega) \vert \approx 1/\omega_n^2$. Thus the proof-mass displacement is **linearly proportional to the housing acceleration** — the very property we exploit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating regime rule:** Always design the sensor so that the highest acceleration frequency of interest (e.g., robot body vibration, often 10–200 Hz) is at least 3–5× **below** $\omega_n$, ensuring the response is flat and in-phase.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Stochastic Error Model

**Step 1 — Ideal (true) output.** An ideal sensor maps true acceleration $a$ to a clean signal $a$ exactly.

**Step 2 — Apply scale-factor error.** A real sensor has a multiplicative gain error $s$ (e.g., 1–2% drift):

$$a' = (1 + s)\,a$$

**Step 3 — Add a deterministic bias.** A constant offset $b_a$ (units of $\text{m/s}^2$) shifts the reading:

$$a'' = (1 + s)\,a + b_a$$

**Step 4 — Add stochastic noise.** Random electronic and Brownian noise $n(t)$:

$$\tilde{a}(t) = (1 + s)\,a(t) + b_a + n(t)$$

**Step 5 — Variance propagation.** If the noise is zero-mean with variance $\sigma_n^2$ and sampled at period $T_s$:

$$\sigma_a^2 = \sigma_n^2 / T_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ARW} = \sigma_n \sqrt{T_s} \;\;[\text{m/s}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> Double-integration of noise gives the position error standard deviation:
> $\sigma_{pos} \propto \sigma_n \, t^{3/2}$, which is why inertial-only navigation drifts unboundedly without aiding.

### 3.3 Derivation of Tilt from a Stationary Accelerometer
At rest, $\vec{a}_{coord} = \vec{0}$, so the proper acceleration reduces to $-\vec{g}$. For a 2-axis tilt (roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$):

$$a_x = -g\sin\theta, \quad a_y = g\sin\phi\cos\theta, \quad a_z = -g\cos\phi\cos\theta$$

Solving for the angles:

$$\theta = \arcsin(-a_x / g), \quad \phi = \arcsin(a_y / (g\cos\theta))$$

This is a **board-favourite derivation** for KTU 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a MEMS Capacitive Accelerometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input Proper Acceleration a] --> B1{Seismic Proof Mass m}
    B1 --> C1[Spring k and Damper b Suspension]
    C1 --> D1[Relative Displacement x t]
    D1 --> E1[Capacitive Differential Pickoff]
    E1 --> F1[Charge to Voltage C V Converter]
    F1 --> G1[Demodulation and Low Pass Filter]
    G1 --> H1[ADC Digitisation]
    H1 --> I1[Digital Temperature Compensation]
    I1 --> J1[Calibrated Digital Output a measured]
    J1 --> K1[Host Microcontroller or EKF]
    K1 --> L1[Mobile Robot Pose Estimate]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **seismic proof mass** is the inertial element responding to acceleration.
- The **spring-damper** converts acceleration into mechanical displacement.
- The **capacitive pickoff** converts that mechanical motion into an electrical signal (change in gap $\Rightarrow$ change in capacitance).
- The **C/V converter** plus **demodulator** extracts a baseband voltage.
- The **ADC** + **temperature compensation** yield a calibrated digital reading.
- The host (typically an EKF running on the robot's main processor) fuses this with gyro and GPS data.

### 4.2 Taxonomy of Accelerometer Technologies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Accelerometer Technologies] --> B0[Piezoelectric]
    A0 --> C0[Piezoresistive]
    A0 --> D0[Capacitive MEMS]
    A0 --> E0[Tunneling]
    A0 --> F0[Optical Fiber Bragg Grating]
    B0 --> B1[High frequency dynamic only no DC response]
    C0 --> C1[DC capable rugged large footprint]
    D0 --> D1[DC capable low power small MEMS dominant in mobile robots]
    E0 --> E1[Ultra high sensitivity research grade]
    F0 --> F1[Immune to EMI used in harsh environments]
```

### 4.3 Signal Conditioning Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Raw Capacitance Change Delta C] --> Pre[Charge Amplifier Front End]
    Pre --> Rect[Synchronous Demodulator]
    Rect --> LPF[Anti Alias Low Pass Filter]
    LPF --> ADC2[Sigma Delta ADC]
    ADC2 --> DSP[Digital Compensation Bias and SF]
    DSP --> Out[Calibrated Acceleration Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU question: *"Why is a synchronous demodulator used in a MEMS accelerometer front end?"* — because the carrier excitation (typically 100 kHz–1 MHz) shifts the signal away from the 1/f noise corner, and the demodulator retrieves the baseband acceleration while rejecting low-frequency drift.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define proper acceleration. Why does a stationary accelerometer on Earth read a non-zero value, and what is the magnitude of that reading?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Proper acceleration is the physical acceleration of a sensor relative to free-fall, defined as $\vec{a}_p = \vec{a}_{coord} - \vec{g}$. A stationary accelerometer at Earth's surface experiences an upward normal reaction from the table; in free-fall that reaction would be zero, so the "extra" non-gravitational acceleration is exactly equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to gravity. Therefore the reading is $9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$ along the vertical axis (1 mark). The cause is the contact force between the sensor housing and the supporting surface, which the proof mass responds to via the suspension (1 mark). The numerical value of standard gravity is $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$ (1 mark).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List three error sources in a MEMS accelerometer. State the units of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Bias error $b_a$** — a constant offset; units: $\text{m/s}^2$ (1 mark).
2. **Scale-factor error $s$** — a multiplicative gain error; units: dimensionless / ppm (1 mark).
3. **Random noise $n(t)$** — zero-mean stochastic disturbance; units: $\text{m/s}^2/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ or equivalent ARW form $\text{m/s}/\sqrt{\text{hr}}$ (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-3 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a) Derive the transfer function of a generic single-axis mass-spring-damper accelerometer from Newton's second law and obtain the expressions for natural frequency and damping ratio. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. Define coordinates: housing displacement $y(t)$, relative displacement $x(t)$, absolute proof-mass displacement $z = x + y$. (1 mark)
2. Apply Newton's second law in inertial frame:
   $m\ddot{z} = -kz - b\dot{z}$ (1 mark)
3. Substitute $z = x + y$ and group terms:
   $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{y} - b\dot{y} - ky$ (1 mark)
4. For MEMS-scale designs, cross-terms on the right are negligible:
   $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx \approx -m\ddot{y}$ (1 mark)
5. Take Laplace transform (zero ICs):
   $(ms^2 + bs + k)X(s) = -ms^2 Y(s)$ (1 mark)
6. Solve and normalize by $m$ to obtain:
   $H(s) = \dfrac{X(s)}{\ddot{Y}(s)} = \dfrac{-1}{s^2 + \dfrac{b}{m}s + \dfrac{k}{m}}$ (1 mark)
7. Read off: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $\zeta = b/(2\sqrt{km})$ (1 mark).

**(b) With reference to a mobile robot application, explain how a tri-axial accelerometer can be used to estimate roll and pitch when the robot is stationary. Derive the relevant equations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. State assumption: robot is stationary, so coordinate acceleration $\vec{a}_{coord} = \vec{0}$, hence the measured proper acceleration is $\vec{a}_p = -\vec{g}$. (1 mark)
2. Express gravity vector in body frame (rotated by roll $\phi$ and pitch $\theta$):
   $\vec{g}_{body} = R_x(\phi)\,R_y(\theta)\,(0, 0, -g)^T$. (1 mark)
3. Write component equations:
   $a_x = g\sin\theta$, $a_y = -g\sin\phi\cos\theta$, $a_z = -g\cos\phi\cos\theta$ (1 mark)
   *(Note: sign convention depends on axis orientation; equivalent forms accepted.)*
4. Invert for angles:
   $\theta = \arcsin(a_x / g)$ (1 mark)
5. $\phi = \arcsin\!\big(-a_y / (g\cos\theta)\big)$ (1 mark)
6. Discuss limitation: only **roll and pitch** can be obtained; **yaw** is unobservable from gravity alone because gravity has no horizontal component to resolve heading. (1 mark)
7. Mobile-robot use case: correcting IMU tilt before fusing with GPS for outdoor navigation; safety tilt alarms for biped/wheeled balance. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) With neat block diagrams, describe the construction and working of a MEMS capacitive accelerometer. List two advantages over piezoelectric accelerometers. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. State the core physical principle: a proof mass suspended by compliant beams deflects under acceleration; the deflection is sensed capacitively. (1 mark)
2. Construction details: silicon proof mass, folded-beam suspension, interdigitated comb-finger structures forming differential capacitors. (1 mark)
3. Working: under acceleration, the proof mass moves by $\Delta x$, changing the gap of one capacitor by $+\Delta x$ and the other by $-\Delta x$, producing a differential output $\Delta C / C \propto \Delta x$. (2 marks)
4. Signal chain: capacitive pickoff → charge amplifier → synchronous demodulator → LPF → ADC → digital compensation. (1 mark)
5. Advantages over piezoelectric: (i) measures **DC** acceleration, while piezo sensors cannot; (ii) lower power and smaller size — suitable for mobile-robot onboard use. (1 mark)
6. Additional note on noise performance and integration with IMU. (1 mark)

**(b) Develop the full stochastic measurement model of a tri-axial accelerometer. Show how its bias and noise contribute to position error after double integration. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. Write the per-axis measurement model:
   $\tilde{a}_i = (1 + s_i)\,a_i + b_{a,i} + n_i(t)$, for $i \in \{x, y, z\}$. (1 mark)
2. Combine into vector form: $\tilde{\vec{a}} = (I + S)\,\vec{a} + \vec{b}_a + \vec{n}(t)$, where $S$ is the diagonal scale-factor error matrix. (1 mark)
3. Integrate once to obtain velocity error:
   $\vec{v}(t) = \vec{v}(0) + \int_0^t \vec{b}_a\,d\tau + \int_0^t \vec{n}(\tau)\,d\tau = \vec{v}(0) + \vec{b}_a t + \vec{n}_v(t)$ (1 mark)
4. Bias contribution grows **linearly** with time: $b_a \cdot t$. (1 mark)
5. Integrate a second time to obtain position error:
   $\vec{p}(t) = \vec{p}(0) + \int_0^t \vec{v}(\tau)\,d\tau$. Plug in $\vec{v}$ from step 3. (1 mark)
6. Bias contribution: $\vec{b}_a t^2 / 2$ — **quadratic** growth in position. (1 mark)
7. Random-noise contribution (variance): $\sigma_p^2(t) \propto \sigma_n^2 \, t^3$ — **cubic** growth. Conclude: inertial-only navigation must be aided (e.g., GPS, wheel odometry) to bound drift. (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing pitfalls in accelerometry questions:**
> 1. **Confusing proper and coordinate acceleration** — this costs 1–2 marks in nearly every derivational question. Always clarify which one your equation refers to.
> 2. **Dropping the cross-terms ($b\dot{y}$, $ky$) without justification** — examiners expect a one-line remark (e.g., "negligible in typical MEMS designs") before simplification.
> 3. **Forgetting that the transfer function gives displacement, not acceleration** — the final answer must be expressed as $X(s)/\ddot{Y}(s)$, not $X(s)/Y(s)$.
> 4. **Units inconsistency** — write $\text{m/s}^2$ for accel, $\text{rad/s}$ for natural frequency, dimensionless for damping ratio. Mixing units loses ½ to 1 mark.
> 5. **Claiming yaw can be measured from gravity** — it cannot; this is a classic trap.
> 6. **Ignoring gravity when integrating acceleration to get position** — must first remove $\vec{g}$ from the reading, or restrict to short time windows where it's treated as a known bias.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Proper acceleration** = acceleration relative to free-fall; equals $\vec{a}_{coord} - \vec{g}$. A stationary sensor reads $g$, not zero.
- **Generic model** = mass-spring-damper with transfer function $H(s) = -1/(s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2)$.
- **Natural frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$; **damping ratio** $\zeta = b/(2\sqrt{km})$.
- **MEMS capacitive** sensors are dominant in mobile robotics due to DC response, small size, and low power.
- **Stochastic error model**: $\tilde{a} = (1+s)a + b_a + n(t)$ — bias, scale factor, noise.
- **Position drift** from bias grows as $t^2$; from noise as $t^{3/2}$ in std-dev (or $t^3$ in variance). This is why inertial-only nav drifts.
- **Tilt estimation** (roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$) is straightforward from a stationary 3-axis accelerometer; **yaw** is unobservable.
- **Operating regime rule** of thumb: stay well below $\omega_n$ (typically 3–5× margin) to ensure linear, flat frequency response.
- **Equivalence Principle** is the deep reason an accelerometer cannot distinguish gravity from horizontal acceleration by a local mechanical test.
- In KTU answers, always **state assumptions, justify simplifications, and quote units.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IMUs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IMUs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** is an electronic sensor module that measures and reports a body's **specific force** (linear acceleration, including gravity), **angular velocity**, and optionally the **magnetic field strength** along its three mutually orthogonal axes (x, y, z). It forms the cornerstone of the inertial navigation stack in any mobile robotic platform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Examiner Key):**
> An IMU is a 6-DOF (Degrees of Freedom) or 9-DOF sensor cluster comprising:
> - **3-axis Accelerometer** → measures linear acceleration (m/s²)
> - **3-axis Gyroscope** → measures angular velocity (rad/s)
> - **3-axis Magnetometer** → measures magnetic field (μT) [optional, for 9-DOF]

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robot's Inner Ear"

Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped into the middle of an unknown room. How would you know which way you are facing, how fast you are turning, or whether you are tilting forward?
- The **accelerometer** is like the fluid in your **inner ear (vestibular system)** — it senses which way "down" is (gravity) and pushes back when you start moving.
- The **gyroscope** is like spinning on a chair with your eyes closed — you feel the rotation in your gut even though you cannot see it.
- The **magnetometer** is like a built-in compass needle — it always points to magnetic north, giving you an absolute heading reference.

Combined, these three give the robot a complete sense of **self-motion** without needing to look at the outside world.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **Earth's magnetic field:** ≈ **25 – 65 μT** (varies by latitude)
> - **Sample rate of commercial IMUs:** **100 Hz – 1 kHz**
> - **Coordinate frame standard:** **Right-hand rule**, ENU (East-North-Up) or NED (North-East-Down)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Breakdown of the Three Sub-Sensors

#### 2.1.1 Accelerometer
- **Working Principle:** Based on a **proof mass** suspended by flexures. When the body accelerates, the mass displaces; the displacement is proportional to the applied force via Newton's Second Law $F = ma$.
- **What it actually measures:** Specific force, not pure acceleration.
- **Specific Force Equation:**

$$a_{measured} = a_{true} - g$$

Where $a_{true}$ is the true kinematic acceleration and $g$ is the gravity vector. When the robot is stationary, the accelerometer reads exactly **1 g** along the upward axis.

- **Use case in robotics:** Tilt estimation (pitch and roll), vibration analysis, step detection in pedestrian dead reckoning.

#### 2.1.2 Gyroscope
- **Working Principle:** Based on the **Coriolis Effect**. A vibrating structure experiences a Coriolis force when the body rotates; this force is measured to infer angular velocity.
- **Output:** Angular velocity $\omega$ in rad/s about each axis.
- **Critical Weakness:** Must be **integrated** to obtain orientation, which causes **drift** over time.

#### 2.1.3 Magnetometer
- **Working Principle:** Hall-effect or magneto-resistive sensor that detects Earth's magnetic field.
- **Output:** Magnetic flux density in μT or Gauss.
- **Use case:** Provides an absolute heading (yaw) reference, compensating for gyroscope drift.

### 2.2 Why Sensor Fusion is Mandatory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No single IMU sub-sensor is sufficient.** Each has a fatal flaw:
> - Accelerometer → **noisy** at high frequency, cannot measure yaw.
> - Gyroscope → **drifts** over long durations.
> - Magnetometer → **disturbed** by nearby ferrous objects and electronics.
>
> **Fusion** combines them to get the best of all worlds.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific force | $f = a - g$ | $f$ = measured, $a$ = true accel, $g$ = gravity | m/s² |
| Orientation update (gyro integration) | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$ | $\theta$ = angle, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $\Delta t$ = sample period | rad, rad/s, s |
| Roll from accelerometer | $\phi = \arctan\left(\dfrac{a_y}{a_z}\right)$ | $\phi$ = roll angle | rad |
| Pitch from accelerometer | $\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{-a_x}{\sqrt{a_y^2 + a_z^2}}\right)$ | $\theta$ = pitch angle | rad |
| Yaw from magnetometer | $\psi = \arctan2\left(\dfrac{m_y}{m_x}\right)$ | $\psi$ = yaw, $m$ = magnetic field | rad |
| Complementary filter | $\hat{x} = \alpha \cdot x_{gyro} + (1-\alpha) \cdot x_{accel}$ | $\alpha$ = weight (typically 0.95 – 0.98) | dimensionless |
| Rotation matrix (Z-axis) | $R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\psi$ = yaw | rad |
| Quaternion update | $q_{k+1} = q_k + \dfrac{1}{2}\,\Omega(\omega_k)\,q_k \cdot \Delta t$ | $q$ = quaternion, $\Omega$ = skew-symmetric matrix of $\omega$ | rad/s |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drones / Quadcopters:** IMU is the primary sensor for stabilization at **1 kHz** loop rates; GPS and vision are too slow.
- **Autonomous Cars:** IMUs bridge GPS outages in tunnels and provide short-term trajectory predictions.
- **Smartphones:** Screen rotation, step counting, AR/VR tracking.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA):** IMUs combined with star trackers give inertial attitude during solar eclipses when cameras fail.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Orientation from Gyroscope Integration

**Starting Premise:** The gyroscope outputs angular velocity $\omega(t)$ in the body frame. To get the orientation angle, we integrate over time.

**Step 1 — Write the kinematic differential equation.**

$$\frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} = \omega(t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply separation of variables and integrate over one sample period $[t_k, t_{k+1}]$.**

$$\int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt}\, dt = \int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} \omega(t)\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the left side using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.**

$$\theta(t_{k+1}) - \theta(t_k) = \int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} \omega(t)\, dt$$

**Step 4 — Approximate the integral using the assumption that $\omega$ is constant over a short interval $\Delta t = t_{k+1} - t_k$ (zero-order hold).**

$$\int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} \omega(t)\, dt \approx \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the discrete-time update equation.**

$$\boxed{\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exams:** This is why gyroscope **drift** occurs. Even a tiny constant bias $\beta$ in the gyroscope integrates into a linearly growing error: $\theta_{error}(t) = \beta \cdot t$. After 1 hour at a bias of just 0.001 rad/s, the error becomes **3.6 radians (≈ 206°)** — completely unusable.

### 3.2 Derivation: Tilt Angles from a Stationary Accelerometer

**Starting Premise:** When the robot is stationary, the accelerometer reads only the reaction to gravity.

**Step 1 — Write the gravity vector in the world frame (NED convention).**

$$g^{world} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -g \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Rotate this vector into the body frame using the rotation matrix $R_{wb}$.**

$$a^{body} = R_{wb} \cdot g^{world}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the rotation for small roll $\phi$ and pitch $\theta$ (Euler XYZ convention).**

$$a^{body} = \begin{bmatrix} -g\sin\theta \\ g\sin\phi\cos\theta \\ -g\cos\phi\cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for roll $\phi$.** Divide the second component by the third component to eliminate $g$.

$$\tan\phi = \frac{a_y}{a_z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\phi = \arctan\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for pitch $\theta$.** Divide the first component by the magnitude of the horizontal plane projection.

$$\tan\theta = \frac{-a_x}{\sqrt{a_y^2 + a_z^2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{-a_x}{\sqrt{a_y^2 + a_z^2}}\right)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Complementary Filter for Sensor Fusion

**Premise:** We want to fuse a high-frequency but drifting gyroscope with a low-frequency but stable accelerometer.

**Step 1 — Define the fused estimate $\hat{\theta}_k$.**

$$\hat{\theta}_k = \alpha \cdot \hat{\theta}_{gyro,k} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \hat{\theta}_{accel,k}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the gyro integration from Section 3.1.**

$$\hat{\theta}_{gyro,k} = \hat{\theta}_{k-1} + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and rewrite the fusion equation.**

$$\hat{\theta}_k = \alpha \left(\hat{\theta}_{k-1} + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t\right) + (1-\alpha)\,\theta_{accel,k}$$

**Step 4 — Expand and group terms.**

$$\hat{\theta}_k = \hat{\theta}_{k-1} + \alpha \cdot \omega_k \cdot \Delta t + (1-\alpha)\left(\theta_{accel,k} - \hat{\theta}_{k-1}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Interpret each term physically.**
- $\hat{\theta}_{k-1}$ → **prediction** (from prior estimate)
- $\alpha \cdot \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$ → **gyro contribution** (high-frequency)
- $(1-\alpha)\left(\theta_{accel,k} - \hat{\theta}_{k-1}\right)$ → **correction** (low-frequency, removes drift)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical KTU value:** $\alpha \in [0.95, 0.98]$ for a balance between responsiveness and noise rejection. Lower $\alpha$ trusts the accelerometer more (smoother but laggy); higher $\alpha$ trusts the gyro more (responsive but drifting).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IMU Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Physical Motion Input] --> B[IMU Sensor Cluster]
    B --> C1[3-Axis MEMS Accelerometer]
    B --> C2[3-Axis MEMS Gyroscope]
    B --> C3[3-Axis Magnetometer Optional]
    C1 --> D1[Raw Linear Acceleration Vector]
    C2 --> D2[Raw Angular Velocity Vector]
    C3 --> D3[Raw Magnetic Field Vector]
    D1 --> E[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Calibration and Bias Removal]
    F --> G[Sensor Fusion Engine]
    G --> H1[Orientation Quaternion]
    G --> H2[Linear Acceleration Body Frame]
    G --> H3[Heading Yaw]
    H1 --> I[Robot Navigation Stack]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Motor Controller and Path Planner]
```

### 4.2 Sensor Fusion Flow (Complementary Filter)

```mermaid
graph LR
    GyroIn[Gyroscope Input omega] --> Integ[Discrete Integrator]
    AccelIn[Accelerometer Input a] --> TiltCalc[Tilt Angle Calculation]
    MagIn[Magnetometer Input m] --> YawCalc[Yaw Angle Calculation]
    Integ --> AddNode{Summing Node alpha weighting}
    TiltCalc --> AddNode
    AddNode --> OutEst[Final Orientation Estimate theta hat]
    YawCalc --> OutYaw[Yaw Estimate psi hat]
    OutEst --> OutFinal[Robot Pose Update]
    OutYaw --> OutFinal
```

### 4.3 Coordinate Frame Transformation Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    W[World Frame ENU] -->|Rotation R wb| B[Body Frame]
    W -->|Rotation R wn| N[Navigation Frame]
    N -->|Rotation R nb| B
    B -->|Accelerometer Reading| Sensor[IMU Raw Output]
    B -->|Gyroscope Reading| Sensor
    Sensor -->|Inverse Rotation R bw| W
    Sensor -->|Inverse Rotation R bn| N
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU). List its major sub-components and state the physical quantity measured by each.

**Model Answer:**
An IMU is an electronic sensor module used in mobile robotics to measure a body's specific force, angular rate, and optionally the surrounding magnetic field.
- **Accelerometer:** measures linear acceleration (m/s²) along 3 axes.
- **Gyroscope:** measures angular velocity (rad/s) about 3 axes.
- **Magnetometer:** measures magnetic flux density (μT) along 3 axes (used as a digital compass).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Why does an integrated gyroscope reading drift over time? How is this drift compensated in a 9-DOF IMU?

**Model Answer:**
Drift occurs because orientation is obtained by integrating angular velocity: $\theta(t) = \int \omega(t)\,dt$. A constant bias $\beta$ in the gyroscope produces a linearly growing error $\beta \cdot t$. In a 9-DOF IMU, this drift is compensated using the accelerometer (for roll and pitch) and the magnetometer (for yaw) through sensor fusion techniques such as the complementary filter or Kalman filter.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Derive the discrete-time update equation for orientation using gyroscope integration. Show all steps clearly. State the assumption made about the angular velocity within one sample period. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gyroscope reports a constant bias of $0.02^\circ/\text{s}$. Compute the orientation error after (i) 1 minute and (ii) 1 hour. Comment on the practical implications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Gyroscope Integration Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Kinematic relation:**
$$\frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} = \omega(t)$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over sample interval $[t_k, t_{k+1}]$:**
$$\theta_{k+1} - \theta_k = \int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} \omega(t)\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Zero-order hold assumption:** $\omega(t) \approx \omega_k$ for $t \in [t_k, t_{k+1}]$.

**Step 4 — Closed-form:**
$$\boxed{\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t}$$

**(b)** Drift Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert bias to rad/s:** $\beta = 0.02 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 3.49 \times 10^{-4}$ rad/s.

**Step 2 — Error formula:** $\theta_{error}(t) = \beta \cdot t$.

**Step 3 — At $t = 60$ s:**
$$\theta_{error} = 3.49 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 60 = 0.0209 \text{ rad} \approx 1.2^\circ$$

**Step 4 — At $t = 3600$ s:**
$$\theta_{error} = 3.49 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 3600 = 1.256 \text{ rad} \approx 72^\circ$$

**Comment:** A 1.2° error after 1 minute is acceptable, but a 72° error after 1 hour is catastrophic. This is why gyroscope drift MUST be corrected using accelerometer/magnetometer fusion.

#### Question B — (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working of a MEMS accelerometer. How is it used to compute roll and pitch in a stationary robot? Derive the relevant equations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Complementary Filter for IMU sensor fusion. Why is a weighting factor of $\alpha \approx 0.96$ typically chosen? Show that as $\alpha \rightarrow 1$, the filter behaves like a pure integrator. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** MEMS Accelerometer (7 marks)

A MEMS accelerometer consists of a tiny **proof mass** suspended by compliant **flexure springs** between fixed capacitor plates. When the body accelerates, the proof mass displaces relative to the casing, changing the capacitance. This capacitance change is proportional to the applied force $F = ma$, yielding acceleration $a$.

For a stationary robot, the accelerometer reads the reaction to gravity. Using the gravity vector rotated into the body frame:

$$\text{Roll: } \phi = \arctan\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right), \quad \text{Pitch: } \theta = \arctan\left(\frac{-a_x}{\sqrt{a_y^2 + a_z^2}}\right)$$

(Derivation shown in Section 3.2 of these notes.)

**(b)** Complementary Filter (7 marks)

The complementary filter fuses two estimates:

$$\hat{\theta}_k = \alpha \cdot \hat{\theta}_{gyro,k} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \hat{\theta}_{accel,k}$$

**Limit behavior:**

As $\alpha \rightarrow 1$:

$$\hat{\theta}_k \approx \hat{\theta}_{gyro,k} = \hat{\theta}_{k-1} + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$$

This is exactly the discrete integrator derived in Section 3.1, confirming the filter degenerates into a pure gyroscope integration at $\alpha = 1$.

**Why $\alpha \approx 0.96$?**
- High enough to trust the gyro's short-term accuracy (preserves high-frequency response).
- Low enough that $(1-\alpha) = 0.04$ provides a steady correction from the accelerometer to cancel drift before it accumulates significantly.
- Acts as a first-order low-pass filter on accelerometer noise and a high-pass filter on gyroscope drift — the classic **frequency-domain complementary** property.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing acceleration with specific force.** The accelerometer measures $a - g$, NOT pure $a$. Many students write $a = F/m$ directly and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting units in drift calculations.** Always convert degrees to radians before integration; mixing units gives wrong answers and full mark deduction.
> 3. **Writing $\phi = \arcsin(a_y/g)$ instead of $\arctan(a_y/a_z)$.** Both are valid for small angles but the examiner's key for KTU 2024 uses the arctan form. Memorize both, but default to arctan.
> 4. **Omitting the zero-order hold assumption** in the gyro derivation. The KTU valuation key explicitly awards 1 mark for this assumption statement.
> 5. **Confusing yaw drift compensation with roll/pitch drift.** The accelerometer CANNOT correct yaw (gravity has no horizontal component); only the magnetometer can. Many students incorrectly say "accelerometer corrects all drift."

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save This!)**
> - IMU = **Accelerometer + Gyroscope** (± Magnetometer).
> - Accelerometer measures **specific force** $a - g$, not pure acceleration.
> - Gyroscope measures **angular velocity**; orientation is obtained by **integration**.
> - Gyro **drift grows linearly with time**: $\theta_{error} = \beta \cdot t$.
> - Accelerometer gives **roll & pitch**, magnetometer gives **yaw**.
> - **Complementary filter:** $\hat{\theta} = \alpha \cdot \theta_{gyro} + (1-\alpha)\cdot\theta_{accel}$, with $\alpha \approx 0.96$.
> - **Sample rate:** 100 Hz – 1 kHz for robotics; 1 kHz for quadcopters.
> - **Coordinate frames:** Body frame (sensors), World frame (ENU or NED).
> - **9-DOF** = 3 accel + 3 gyro + 3 mag; **6-DOF** = 3 accel + 3 gyro only.
> - **Use Euler angles** for human-readable orientation; **use quaternions** for computation (avoid gimbal lock).
> - **Kalman filter** is the statistically optimal fusion method; complementary filter is its steady-state approximation.
> - **Mars Rover / Drone / Self-driving car** — all rely on IMU as the **primary** high-frequency motion sensor.
> - KTU 2024 examiner expects derivation of gyro integration equation (Sec 3.1) and tilt equations (Sec 3.2) as **mandatory** for full marks in any 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ground-Based Beacons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ground-Based Beacons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

In the context of Mobile Robotics (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3: Localization & Mapping), **Ground-Based Beacons** are fixed, known-position reference transmitters (or passive reflectors) installed at surveyed coordinates within the robot's operational environment. They provide the robot with absolute measurements (typically **range**, **bearing**, or both) from which the robot's global pose—position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$—can be computed via **trilateration**, **triangulation**, or **multilateration**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Ground-based beacons form the foundation of *absolute (global) localization* methods, contrasting with *relative (dead-reckoning) localization*. They are the practical indoor/outdoor substitute for GPS in GPS-denied environments.

**Standard Physical Metrics (KTU Expected Values):**

| Metric | Value / Symbol |
|---|---|
| Speed of Light (RF beacons) | $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Speed of Sound (Ultrasonic beacons) | $v_s \approx 343$ m/s (at 20 °C) |
| Standard operating frequency (GPS L1) | **1.57542 GHz** |
| Pseudorange resolution (typical civil GPS) | **~3–10 m** without augmentation |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in a large circular playground and three of your friends stand at three fixed corners holding bullhorns. Each friend shouts the *distance* between you and them, calculated from how long their echo takes to return. With these three distances, you can pinpoint your exact location on the playground by drawing three circles—the single intersection point is where you stand.

**Key Mapping to Robotics:**
- **Friends at fixed corners** $\rightarrow$ *Ground-based beacons at surveyed coordinates*
- **Bullhorns and echoes** $\rightarrow$ *Active RF/Ultrasonic/IR transmitters*
- **You** $\rightarrow$ *The mobile robot*
- **Three distances** $\rightarrow$ *Range measurements enabling trilateration*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Ground-Based"?** The term explicitly excludes satellite beacons (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo). While mathematically similar, ground-based beacons are surveyed and installed by the system engineer, removing orbital ephemeris errors and clock-satellite bias.

### Categories of Ground-Based Beacons

1. **Active Beacons** — Self-powered transmitters (RF, ultrasonic, IR LEDs, laser).
2. **Passive Beacons** — Retroreflectors or fiducial markers (barcodes, QR tags, AprilTags) that reflect the robot's emitted signal.
3. **Hybrid / Modulated Beacons** — Emit a coded ID allowing the receiver to identify *which* beacon is being measured (essential when multiple beacons share a channel).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Localization Pipeline

The process of locating a robot using ground-based beacons proceeds in **four structured steps**:

- **Step 1 — Survey (Beacon Placement):** Beacon $i$ is placed at a known, fixed coordinate $\mathbf{p}_i = (x_i, y_i)$ in the global frame $\{W\}$.
- **Step 2 — Measurement (Range / Bearing Acquisition):** The robot's receiver measures either time-of-flight, signal strength, phase shift, or angle-of-arrival relative to beacon $i$.
- **Step 3 — Triangulation / Trilateration:** The set of measurements is fused mathematically to solve for the robot's unknown pose $\mathbf{q} = (x, y, \theta)$.
- **Step 4 — Filtering (Optional):** A Kalman Filter or Particle Filter smooths noisy measurements over time, producing an optimal estimate $\hat{\mathbf{q}}$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why fixed coordinates?** They remove one degree of freedom from the unknown, allowing the geometric equations to be inverted.
- **Why $\geq 3$ beacons in 2-D?** Two circles intersect in two points (ambiguity); a third circle resolves the ambiguity and supplies redundancy for error detection.
- **Why modulation/coding?** So that the robot can distinguish Beacon #4 from Beacon #7 when multiple beacons transmit simultaneously or on the same frequency.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Range from Time-of-Flight (RF) | $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ | $c = 3\times10^8$ m/s, $\Delta t$ in seconds |
| 2 | Range from Time-of-Flight (Ultrasonic) | $d = v_s \cdot \Delta t$ | $v_s \approx 343$ m/s |
| 3 | Pseudorange (with clock bias) | $\rho = c(\Delta t + \delta t_r) + \epsilon$ | $\delta t_r$ = receiver clock bias |
| 4 | Range equation (circle) | $(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2$ | $(x,y)$ robot pose; $(x_i,y_i)$ beacon |
| 5 | Linearized trilateration | $2(x_j - x_i)x + 2(y_j - y_i)y = d_i^2 - d_j^2 + x_j^2 - x_i^2 + y_j^2 - y_i^2$ | After subtracting circle $i$ from circle $j$ |
| 6 | Matrix form | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ | Solved by least squares: $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$ |
| 7 | Bearing from angle-of-arrival | $\theta_i = \text{atan2}(y - y_i, x - x_i)$ | Bearing from beacon $i$ to robot |
| 8 | Triangulation (2 beacons + angles) | $\tan(\alpha) = \frac{y - y_1}{x - x_1}$, $\tan(\beta) = \frac{y - y_2}{x - x_2}$ | Two lines intersect at robot position |
| 9 | Dilution of Precision (2-D) | $\text{DOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}((\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1})}$ | $\mathbf{H}$ = geometry Jacobian |
| 10 | Received Signal Strength (log-normal) | $P_r = P_0 - 10 n \log_{10}(d) + X_\sigma$ | $n$ = path-loss exponent, $X_\sigma$ = shadow fading |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the *subtraction* step that converts two nonlinear circle equations into a linear pair. This is the single most expected derivation in any KTU 2024 question on beacons.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva, Fetch):** RFID and IR beacons taped to floor grids enable centimeter-level localization for inventory robots.
- **Autonomous AGVs in Factories:** Retro-reflective laser beacons read by onboard LIDARs provide sub-millimeter accuracy on assembly lines.
- **Hospital Asset Tracking:** Low-frequency RF beacons locate wheelchairs, infusion pumps, and patients in real time.
- **Indoor GPS-Denied Rescue Robotics:** Ultrasonic beacons deployed in collapsed buildings guide search-and-rescue UGVs to survivors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation: Trilateration from Three Beacons

**Problem Setup:** A robot at unknown position $(x, y)$ measures distances $d_1, d_2, d_3$ to three beacons at known positions $\mathbf{p}_1 = (x_1, y_1)$, $\mathbf{p}_2 = (x_2, y_2)$, $\mathbf{p}_3 = (x_3, y_3)$. Derive the robot's position.

**Step 1 — Write the three Euclidean distance equations.**

$$
(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2 = d_1^2
$$

$$
(x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2 = d_2^2
$$

$$
(x - x_3)^2 + (y - y_3)^2 = d_3^2
$$

> Text: Each equation expresses that the squared distance between the robot and beacon $i$ equals the squared measured range.

**Step 2 — Expand the squares.**

$$
x^2 - 2x x_1 + x_1^2 + y^2 - 2y y_1 + y_1^2 = d_1^2
$$

$$
x^2 - 2x x_2 + x_2^2 + y^2 - 2y y_2 + y_2^2 = d_2^2
$$

$$
x^2 - 2x x_3 + x_3^2 + y^2 - 2y y_3 + y_3^2 = d_3^2
$$

> Text: Standard algebraic expansion of $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$.

**Step 3 — Subtract equation 1 from equations 2 and 3.**

This eliminates the nonlinear $x^2$ and $y^2$ terms.

$$
-2x x_2 + 2x x_1 - 2y y_2 + 2y y_1 + x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 = d_2^2 - d_1^2
$$

$$
-2x x_3 + 2x x_1 - 2y y_3 + 2y y_1 + x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 = d_3^2 - d_1^2
$$

> Text: Rearranging so all $x^2, y^2$ terms cancel since they are equal in every equation.

**Step 4 — Group unknowns on the left side.**

$$
2(x_1 - x_2)x + 2(y_1 - y_2)y = (d_2^2 - d_1^2) - (x_2^2 - x_1^2) - (y_2^2 - y_1^2)
$$

$$
2(x_1 - x_3)x + 2(y_1 - y_3)y = (d_3^2 - d_1^2) - (x_3^2 - x_1^2) - (y_3^2 - y_1^2)
$$

> Text: This produces a pair of **linear** equations in the two unknowns $x$ and $y$.

**Step 5 — Cast into matrix form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
2(x_1 - x_2) & 2(y_1 - y_2) \\
2(x_1 - x_3) & 2(y_1 - y_3)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
x \\ y
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
d_2^2 - d_1^2 - x_2^2 + x_1^2 - y_2^2 + y_1^2 \\
d_3^2 - d_1^2 - x_3^2 + x_1^2 - y_3^2 + y_1^2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> Text: We now have a classic $2 \times 2$ linear system. For more than three beacons, replace the square matrix with a tall matrix and use the pseudo-inverse.

**Step 6 — Solve using Cramer's Rule.**

$$
x = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} b_1 & 2(y_1 - y_2) \\ b_2 & 2(y_1 - y_3) \end{vmatrix}}{\begin{vmatrix} 2(x_1 - x_2) & 2(y_1 - y_2) \\ 2(x_1 - x_3) & 2(y_1 - y_3) \end{vmatrix}}
$$

$$
y = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 2(x_1 - x_2) & b_1 \\ 2(x_1 - x_3) & b_2 \end{vmatrix}}{\begin{vmatrix} 2(x_1 - x_2) & 2(y_1 - y_2) \\ 2(x_1 - x_3) & 2(y_1 - y_3) \end{vmatrix}}
$$

> Text: Determinants provide the explicit closed-form solution. The denominator (determinant of $\mathbf{A}$) being zero indicates collinear beacons—geometrically a *poor* configuration.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** Beacons at $(0,0)$, $(10,0)$, $(0,10)$. Measured ranges: $d_1 = 5.83$ m, $d_2 = 6.32$ m, $d_3 = 7.62$ m. Find the robot position.

> Text: We will use the linearized equations derived above.

**Compute $\mathbf{A}$:**

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 2(0 - 10) & 2(0 - 0) \\ 2(0 - 0) & 2(0 - 10) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -20 & 0 \\ 0 & -20 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Compute $\mathbf{b}$:**

$$
b_1 = 6.32^2 - 5.83^2 - 100 + 0 - 0 + 0 = 39.94 - 33.99 - 100 = -94.05
$$

$$
b_2 = 7.62^2 - 5.83^2 - 0 + 0 - 100 + 0 = 58.06 - 33.99 - 100 = -75.93
$$

> Text: Note the squaring of distances; be careful with units.

**Solve:**

$$
x = \frac{-94.05}{-20} = 4.70 \text{ m}, \quad y = \frac{-75.93}{-20} = 3.80 \text{ m}
$$

> Text: Verification: distance from $(4.70, 3.80)$ to $(0,0)$ is $\sqrt{22.09 + 14.44} = \sqrt{36.53} \approx 6.04$ m. The small error from $5.83$ m is consistent with realistic measurement noise.

### 3.3 Derivation: Triangulation from Two Bearing Measurements

**Setup:** The robot's onboard sensor measures bearings $\alpha$ and $\beta$ from two beacons at $\mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2$.

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{y - y_1}{x - x_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad -x \tan(\alpha) + y = y_1 - x_1 \tan(\alpha)
$$

$$
\tan(\beta) = \frac{y - y_2}{x - x_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad -x \tan(\beta) + y = y_2 - x_2 \tan(\beta)
$$

> Text: The robot is at the intersection of two rays originating from the two beacons.

**Subtraction yields a $2 \times 2$ linear system in $(x,y)$:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} -\tan(\alpha) & 1 \\ -\tan(\beta) & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} y_1 - x_1 \tan(\alpha) \\ y_2 - x_2 \tan(\beta) \end{bmatrix}
$$

> Text: Solve via Cramer's rule. The determinant is $\tan(\beta) - \tan(\alpha)$; degeneracy when the bearings are parallel (robot on the line $p_1 p_2$).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP)

The error in estimated position scales with **Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP)**:

$$
\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}\left((\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1}\right)}
$$

Where for $n$ beacons the geometry matrix is:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix}
\frac{x - x_1}{d_1} & \frac{y - y_1}{d_1} \\
\frac{x - x_2}{d_2} & \frac{y - y_2}{d_2} \\
\vdots & \vdots \\
\frac{x - x_n}{d_n} & \frac{y - y_n}{d_n}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> Text: A *good* beacon geometry (beacons surrounding the robot) gives GDOP near 1. A *poor* geometry (collinear beacons behind the robot) gives GDOP > 5, amplifying range noise dramatically.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Trilateration Geometric Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Robot at unknown pose q] --> A1[Step 1: Survey beacon coordinates p1, p2, p3]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Emit / Receive beacon signals]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Measure ranges d1, d2, d3 via TOF or RSSI]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Write three circle equations]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Subtract eqn 1 from eqn 2 and 3]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Build 2x2 matrix A and vector b]
    A6 --> A7{Step 7: det A not equal zero?}
    A7 -- No --> A8[ERROR: Beacons collinear - Poor GDOP]
    A7 -- Yes --> A9[Step 8: Cramer's Rule / Pseudo-inverse]
    A9 --> A10[Output: Robot position x_hat, y_hat]
    A10 --> A11[Optional: Kalman Filter Smoothing]
    A11 --> A12[Final pose estimate]
```

### 4.2 Beacon-Based Localization System Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SURVEY["Surveyed Environment"]
        B1[Beacon 1 at p1]
        B2[Beacon 2 at p2]
        B3[Beacon 3 at p3]
        BN[Beacon N at pN]
    end
    subgraph ROBOT["Mobile Robot"]
        RX[Receiver / Antenna Array]
        TDC[Time-to-Digital Converter]
        DSP[Signal Processor: ID decode and range calc]
        SOLV[Localization Solver: Trilateration]
        KF[Kalman Filter]
        POSE[Pose Estimator x, y, theta]
    end
    B1 -->|RF / Ultrasonic / IR| RX
    B2 -->|RF / Ultrasonic / IR| RX
    B3 -->|RF / Ultrasonic / IR| RX
    BN -->|RF / Ultrasonic / IR| RX
    RX --> TDC
    TDC --> DSP
    DSP --> SOLV
    SOLV --> KF
    KF --> POSE
```

### 4.3 Comparison Matrix of Beacon Modalities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACT[Active Beacons] --> RF[RF Beacons]
    ACT --> US[Ultrasonic Beacons]
    ACT --> IR[Infrared Beacons]
    ACT --> LAS[Laser Retroreflectors]
    PASS[Passive Beacons] --> BC[Barcode / QR Tags]
    PASS --> APR[AprilTag Fiducials]
    PASS => REF[Retro-reflective Tape]
```

### 4.4 Geometric Configuration vs GDOP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Beacon Geometry] --> G2{Configuration Type}
    G2 -->|Beacons surround robot| G3[GOOD GDOP near 1: High accuracy]
    G2 -->|Beacons on one side| G4[MODERATE GDOP 2 to 3]
    G2 -->|Beacons collinear| G5[POOR GDOP greater than 5: Unstable solution]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a ground-based beacon. Why is a minimum of three beacons required for 2-D localization?*

**Model Answer:**
A ground-based beacon is a transmitter or reflector installed at a known, fixed coordinate in the robot's workspace. It allows the robot to compute range or bearing measurements for absolute localization. In 2-D, two range measurements yield two circle equations whose intersection consists of (in general) **two** mirror-image points. A third circle resolves this ambiguity and additionally provides redundancy, enabling detection of inconsistent or faulty measurements. Hence **$n \geq 3$ beacons** are the minimum for unique planar localization.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between active and passive beacons, giving one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Active Beacon:** Self-powered, actively emits signals (RF, ultrasonic, IR pulses). Example: A wall-mounted ultrasonic pinger modulated with a unique ID.
- **Passive Beacon:** Does not emit; reflects or re-radiates the robot's signal. Example: A printed AprilTag fiducial detected by an onboard camera, or a retro-reflective stripe read by a 2-D LIDAR.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---
