#### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]*

> A robot at unknown position $(x, y)$ measures the following ranges to three surveyed beacons:
> - Beacon 1: $(0, 0)$, range $d_1 = 5$ m
> - Beacon 2: $(8, 0)$, range $d_2 = 5$ m
> - Beacon 3: $(4, 6)$, range $d_3 = 5$ m
>
> **(a)** Derive the linearized trilateration equations. (7 Marks)
> **(b)** Solve the system for the robot's position and compute the GDOP. (7 Marks)

##### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Write the three circle equations.

$$
x^2 + y^2 = 25
$$

$$
(x - 8)^2 + y^2 = 25
$$

$$
(x - 4)^2 + (y - 6)^2 = 25
$$

> Text: Substitute the given coordinates and ranges.

**Step 2:** Subtract equation (1) from equation (2):

$$
(x-8)^2 - x^2 = 0
$$

$$
x^2 - 16x + 64 - x^2 = 0
$$

$$
-16x + 64 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 4
$$

> Text: The $y^2$ terms cancel; the constant terms combine.

**Step 3:** Subtract equation (1) from equation (3):

$$
(x-4)^2 - x^2 + (y-6)^2 - y^2 = 0
$$

$$
-8x + 16 - 12y + 36 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -8x - 12y + 52 = 0
$$

> Text: Expand each square and combine.

**Step 4:** Substitute $x = 4$ into the result of Step 3:

$$
-32 - 12y + 52 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 12y = 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = \tfrac{5}{3} \approx 1.67 \text{ m}
$$

> Text: Solve for the unknown $y$.

##### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Verify by plugging back:

$$
\sqrt{4^2 + (5/3)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 2.78} = \sqrt{18.78} = 4.33 \neq 5 \text{ m}
$$

> Text: Note: the given $d_3 = 5$ is inconsistent with $d_1 = d_2 = 5$ when the beacons form an equilateral triangle of side 6; this reflects measurement noise.

**Step 2:** Form the geometry matrix using the **actual** estimated position $(4, 1.67)$:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix}
4/4.33 & 1.67/4.33 \\
-4/4.33 & 1.67/4.33 \\
0/5 & -4.33/5
\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}
0.924 & 0.386 \\
-0.924 & 0.386 \\
0 & -0.866
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}$:

$$
\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix}
1.708 + 0.854 & 0.357 - 0.357 \\
0.357 - 0.357 & 0.149 + 0.149 + 0.750
\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 2.562 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.048 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Invert:

$$
(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.390 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.954 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** GDOP:

$$
\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{0.390 + 0.954} = \sqrt{1.344} \approx 1.16
$$

> Text: A GDOP near 1 indicates **excellent** geometric spread of the beacons.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]* — Alternative Choice

> **(a)** With the aid of a labeled diagram, explain the working of a beacon-based trilateration system. Compare it with triangulation in terms of (i) number of beacons needed, (ii) type of measurement, and (iii) computational complexity. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** An indoor ultrasonic beacon system operates at $f = 40$ kHz. The robot receives the echo with a measured round-trip time of $\Delta t = 12$ ms. Assuming $v_s = 343$ m/s, compute (i) the one-way distance to the beacon, and (ii) comment on the resolution if the time-measurement has an uncertainty of $\pm 10 \,\mu s$. (7 Marks)

##### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Diagram Reference:** See *Section 4.1* and *Section 4.2*.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Trilateration | Triangulation |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Minimum beacons in 2-D | **3** | **2** |
| (ii) Measurement type | Range / distance | Bearing / angle |
| (iii) Computational complexity | Solve linear system (closed form) | Solve trigonometric intersection (iterative / closed form) |

> Text: Trilateration uses range measurements forming circles; triangulation uses bearings forming rays. Both reduce to a 2$\times$2 linear system after suitable manipulation.

##### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**(i) One-way distance:**

The measured $\Delta t$ is **round-trip** (beacon $\rightarrow$ wall $\rightarrow$ receiver) **or** direct (transmitter on robot $\rightarrow$ beacon $\rightarrow$ receiver). Assuming **one-way** travel $\Delta t$ as stated:

$$
d = v_s \cdot \Delta t = 343 \times 12 \times 10^{-3} = 4.116 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Resolution with $\pm 10 \,\mu s$ uncertainty:**

$$
\delta d = v_s \cdot \delta t = 343 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 3.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 3.43 \text{ mm}
$$

> Text: Ultrasonic beacons therefore offer millimeter-level resolution over short ranges, but suffer from multipath reflections and slow speed-of-sound propagation in air (which is why RF beacons dominate for outdoor or large-indoor applications).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Beacon-Based Localization Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the squaring step** when writing circle equations — most KTU scripts lose 2 marks here.
> 2. **Not subtracting** equation (1) from (2) and (3); students often leave the system nonlinear and fail to proceed.
> 3. **Sign errors** when rearranging $2(x_1-x_2)x + 2(y_1-y_2)y = b_1$. Always expand, then collect.
> 4. **Confusing round-trip vs one-way** time-of-flight in ultrasonic problems. KTU explicitly tests this in Part B.
> 5. **Ignoring GDOP** — KTU 2024 Scheme stresses *configuration quality*, not just the algebraic solution.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Ground-Based Beacons**

- [x] Beacons provide **absolute** localization by giving range / bearing to surveyed points.
- [x] Minimum **3 beacons** for 2-D trilateration, **2 beacons** for 2-D triangulation.
- [x] **Trilateration** = circles intersect; **Triangulation** = rays intersect.
- [x] Always **subtract** the first circle equation from the others to linearize.
- [x] Closed-form solution via Cramer's Rule or matrix pseudo-inverse.
- [x] Active beacons *emit* (RF/Ultrasonic/IR/Laser); passive beacons *reflect* (tags, retroreflectors).
- [x] Time-of-flight: $d = v \cdot \Delta t$ — use $c$ for RF, $v_s \approx 343$ m/s for ultrasonic.
- [x] **GDOP** quantifies geometric strength: surrounding beacons $\Rightarrow$ GDOP near 1; collinear $\Rightarrow$ GDOP large.
- [x] Indoor ultrasonic beacons offer $\sim$mm resolution but limited range and multipath issues.
- [x] Outdoor ground-based RF beacons scale GPS-like trilateration to local environments.
- [x] Kalman or Particle filtering is typically layered on raw trilateration to suppress noise.
- [x] KTU expects **derivation** of the linearized equations as the central skill — practice this repeatedly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active Ranging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Active Ranging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Active Ranging?

**Active Ranging** is a category of sensor-based distance measurement techniques in mobile robotics where the robot itself *actively* emits a signal (acoustic, optical, or electromagnetic) into the environment and measures the response (reflection, echo, or phase shift) to compute the distance to surrounding objects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Active ranging is the process of determining the range (distance) between a robotic platform and objects in its surroundings by actively projecting an energy signal (such as light, sound, or radio waves) toward the target and analyzing the characteristics of the returned signal. It forms the perceptual backbone of obstacle avoidance, mapping, and SLAM in mobile robots.

Unlike **passive ranging** (e.g., stereo vision), active ranging does not depend on ambient illumination or external energy sources — the robot is self-sufficient.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bat in a Dark Cave" 🦇

Imagine you are a **bat flying in a completely dark cave**. You cannot see the walls, but you have a superpower: you can *shout a high-pitched sound* and then *listen for the echo* to know how far the wall is.

| Bat's Behavior | Robotic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Shouting a chirp | Emitting laser pulse / ultrasonic wave / RF wave |
| Waiting for echo | Measuring return time (Time-of-Flight) |
| Loudness of echo | Signal strength / amplitude |
| Pitch change in echo (Doppler) | Frequency shift for velocity measurement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Active vs. Passive:**
> - **Active ranging** = Robot provides the energy (laser, sonar, radar).
> - **Passive ranging** = Robot only receives external energy (cameras, IR receivers).
> Active sensors give **direct, calibrated** distance; passive sensors require complex indirect computation.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Active Ranging

All active ranging techniques — regardless of whether they use light, sound, or radio — measure distance using **one of these three physical principles**:

1. **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** — Measure how long the signal takes to return.
2. **Triangulation** — Measure the angle of the reflected signal at known baseline.
3. **Phase Shift / Frequency Modulation** — Measure the change in phase or beat frequency of the returned wave.

## 1.4 Classification of Active Ranging Sensors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Active Ranging Sensors in Mobile Robotics (KTU 2024):**
> - **Ultrasonic Sensors** (Sonar) — acoustic wave, ToF based
> - **Laser Range Finders (LIDAR)** — optical ToF
> - **Radar (mmWave / Microwave)** — RF ToF / Doppler
> - **Structured Light Sensors** — triangulation based
> - **IR Sensors with PSD** — triangulation based
> - **FMCW Radar / LIDAR** — frequency modulation based

## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants Used

The following constants must be memorized for KTU derivations:

- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- Speed of sound in air (at 20°C): $v_s = 343 \text{ m/s}$
- One-way travel time for light to travel 1 m: $\approx 3.33 \text{ ns}$
- One-way travel time for sound to travel 1 m: $\approx 2.91 \text{ ms}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Active Ranging — Operational Taxonomy

Active ranging sensors can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Categories |
|---|---|
| **Carrier Medium** | Acoustic (sonar) / Optical (LIDAR, IR) / RF (radar) |
| **Measurement Principle** | ToF / Triangulation / Phase / FMCW |
| **Beam Type** | Narrow pencil beam / Wide conical beam / Fan beam / Sheet of light |

## 2.2 Time-of-Flight (ToF) Ranging — Step-by-Step Logic

**ToF ranging** is the most fundamental active ranging technique. Its operational logic is:

1. The robot's emitter generates a **short pulse** (or a continuous modulated wave).
2. The pulse travels through the medium at a known propagation speed ($c$ for light, $v_s$ for sound).
3. The pulse strikes a target object and **reflects** (specular or diffuse).
4. The robot's receiver detects the **returning echo**.
5. The onboard timer measures the **round-trip time** $\Delta t$.
6. Distance is computed as: $d = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ (the factor of 2 is for round-trip).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by 2?**
> The measured time $\Delta t$ corresponds to the signal going **from emitter → target** AND **target → receiver**. Distance is the *one-way* travel, so we take half.

### 2.2.1 Sub-Categories of ToF

- **Pulsed ToF (Direct ToF):** Direct measurement of pulse round-trip time. Used in laser rangefinders and depth cameras.
- **Continuous Wave ToF (Indirect ToF):** Emitter sends a continuously modulated wave; receiver measures phase shift. Used in mmWave radar and PMD sensors.

## 2.3 Triangulation-Based Active Ranging

**Triangulation ranging** uses geometry of a known baseline and angle of reflection:

1. A laser/IR emitter projects a point on the target.
2. A receiver (typically a Position-Sensitive Detector, **PSD**, or linear CCD) detects the reflected spot's position.
3. The **angle** at the receiver is computed from the spot position on the PSD.
4. Knowing the baseline $b$ between emitter and receiver, distance $d$ is computed via simple trigonometry: $d = \dfrac{b}{\tan(\alpha + \beta)}$ where $\alpha$ is the projection angle and $\beta$ is the angle at the PSD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Triangulation sensors are excellent for **short-range, high-precision** measurements (e.g., 5–100 cm) used in obstacle detection arms of indoor mobile robots.

## 2.4 Phase-Shift Ranging

In this technique:

1. A continuous sinusoidal wave of frequency $f$ is emitted.
2. The reflected wave returns with a **phase shift** $\phi$ relative to the emitted wave.
3. The phase shift is related to distance by: $\phi = \dfrac{4\pi f d}{c}$ (round-trip).
4. Solving for $d$: $d = \dfrac{\phi \cdot c}{4\pi f}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use phase-shift?**
> Measuring phase is often more accurate than measuring sub-nanosecond time-of-flight for light. The phase technique converts a difficult *time* measurement into a comparatively easier *phase* measurement.

## 2.5 FMCW (Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave) Ranging

1. The emitted wave's frequency is **swept linearly** over a bandwidth $B$ during a sweep time $T$.
2. The reflected wave is delayed by $\tau = 2d/c$.
3. Mixing the transmitted and received signals produces a **beat frequency** $f_b = \dfrac{2 B d}{c T}$.
4. Range is recovered: $d = \dfrac{f_b \cdot c \cdot T}{2 B}$.
5. **Velocity** is also recovered simultaneously using the Doppler shift on consecutive chirps.

## 2.6 🚀 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table for KTU 2024 ESE — every formula here is examinable:**

| # | Technique | Range Formula | Key Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pulsed ToF (light) | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\Delta t$ = round-trip time | meters |
| 2 | Pulsed ToF (sound) | $d = \dfrac{v_s \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $v_s = 343$ m/s | meters |
| 3 | Continuous-Wave Phase | $d = \dfrac{\phi \cdot c}{4 \pi f}$ | $\phi$ = phase shift (rad), $f$ = modulation freq. | meters |
| 4 | Triangulation | $d = \dfrac{b}{\tan(\alpha + \beta)}$ | $b$ = baseline, $\alpha$ = projection angle, $\beta$ = receiver angle | meters |
| 5 | FMCW (Range) | $d = \dfrac{f_b \cdot c \cdot T}{2 B}$ | $f_b$ = beat freq, $B$ = bandwidth, $T$ = sweep time | meters |
| 6 | FMCW (Velocity) | $v = \dfrac{\Delta f \cdot \lambda}{2}$ | $\Delta f$ = Doppler shift, $\lambda$ = wavelength | m/s |
| 7 | Max Unambiguous Range (FMCW) | $d_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot T}{2}$ | $T$ = sweep period | meters |
| 8 | Range Resolution (FMCW) | $\Delta d = \dfrac{c}{2 B}$ | $B$ = sweep bandwidth | meters |
| 9 | Beam Divergence | $\theta = \dfrac{D_{spot}}{d}$ | $D_{spot}$ = spot diameter at distance $d$ | radians |
| 10 | Max Unambiguous ToF Range | $d_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_{PRF}}$ | $f_{PRF}$ = pulse repetition frequency | meters |

> **⚠️ Markdown Safety Note:** All absolute-value / magnitude notations in equations use the LaTeX `\vert` form where required to avoid breaking table syntax.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (e.g., Waymo, Tesla):** LIDAR + mmWave radar fusion for 360° situational awareness.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva):** 2D LIDAR for SLAM and obstacle avoidance.
- **Drones (DJI):** Ultrasonic + ToF for altitude hold and landing.
- **Industrial AGVs:** Scanning laser range finders for line-following and dock alignment.
- **Domestic Robots (Roomba):** IR triangulation + acoustic ToF cliff sensors.
- **Military / Defense:** FMCW radar for perimeter surveillance and drone detection.

## 2.8 Comparative Performance Summary

| Property | Ultrasonic | IR Triangulation | LIDAR (ToF) | mmWave Radar |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range | 0.1 – 10 m | 0.05 – 1 m | 0.1 – 250 m | 0.1 – 300 m |
| Accuracy | cm-level | sub-mm | mm–cm | cm |
| Cost | Very Low | Low | High | Medium |
| Works in fog/dust? | Poor | Poor | Degraded | Excellent |
| Works in dark? | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Update rate | 10–50 Hz | 100+ Hz | 10–100 Hz | 100+ Hz |
| Beam width | Wide (~30°) | Narrow | Very narrow | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pulsed Time-of-Flight Range Equation

**Problem Setup:** A mobile robot's laser rangefinder emits a short optical pulse. The pulse is reflected from a wall and detected after a round-trip time of $\Delta t = 66.67$ ns. Compute the distance to the wall.

### Step 1 — State the physical principle.

The pulse travels from the robot to the wall and back. The total path length covered by the light is:

$$L_{total} = c \cdot \Delta t$$

where $c$ is the speed of light in air, taken as $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$.

### Step 2 — Relate total path to one-way distance.

The path is a **round trip** — forward and backward. So the one-way distance to the wall is:

$$d = \dfrac{L_{total}}{2} = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.

$$d = \dfrac{(3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) \cdot (66.67 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s})}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the numerator.

$$(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (66.67 \times 10^{-9}) = 3 \cdot 66.67 \cdot 10^{8-9} = 200.01 \times 10^{-1} = 20.001 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5 — Divide by 2 to get the final answer.

$$d = \dfrac{20.001}{2} = 10.0005 \text{ m} \approx 10 \text{ m}$$

> ✅ **Result:** The wall is **10 meters** away from the robot.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Triangulation Ranging Geometry

**Setup:** An active triangulation sensor has an emitter–receiver baseline $b = 8$ cm. The laser is projected at angle $\alpha = 30°$ from the baseline. The reflected spot falls on the PSD at an angle $\beta = 45°$ from the receiver's optical axis. Compute the distance to the target.

### Step 1 — Draw the geometry.

The emitter, the receiver, and the laser spot on the target form a triangle. The emitter and receiver are separated by baseline $b$. The angle at the target (between incoming and reflected ray) is $\gamma = 180° - \alpha - \beta$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Law of Sines.

In the triangle formed by emitter, target, and receiver:

$$\dfrac{b}{\sin(\gamma)} = \dfrac{d}{\sin(\beta)}$$

where $d$ is the distance from the receiver to the target (along the receiver's optical axis).

### Step 3 — Compute the third angle.

$$\gamma = 180° - 30° - 45° = 105°$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange for $d$.

$$d = \dfrac{b \cdot \sin(\beta)}{\sin(\gamma)} = \dfrac{0.08 \cdot \sin(45°)}{\sin(105°)}$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the sines.

$$\sin(45°) = 0.7071$$
$$\sin(105°) = \sin(75°) = 0.9659$$

### Step 6 — Compute $d$.

$$d = \dfrac{0.08 \cdot 0.7071}{0.9659} = \dfrac{0.0566}{0.9659} = 0.0586 \text{ m} \approx 5.86 \text{ cm}$$

> ✅ **Result:** Target is approximately **5.86 cm** from the receiver.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Phase-Shift Ranging

**Setup:** A CW ToF sensor emits at $f = 75$ MHz. The received signal shows a phase shift of $\phi = 60°$ relative to the transmitted signal. Compute the range.

### Step 1 — State the phase-distance relation.

For a round-trip, the total phase accumulated by a wave traveling distance $2d$ is:

$$\phi = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot (2d)}{\lambda} = \dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $\lambda = c/f$.

$$\phi = \dfrac{4 \pi d f}{c}$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange for $d$.

$$d = \dfrac{\phi \cdot c}{4 \pi f}$$

### Step 4 — Convert $\phi$ to radians.

$$\phi = 60° = \dfrac{\pi}{3} \text{ rad} \approx 1.0472 \text{ rad}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.

$$d = \dfrac{1.0472 \cdot (3 \times 10^8)}{4 \pi \cdot (75 \times 10^6)}$$

### Step 6 — Simplify the denominator.

$$4 \pi \cdot 75 \times 10^6 = 300 \pi \times 10^6 \approx 9.4248 \times 10^8$$

### Step 7 — Compute $d$.

$$d = \dfrac{1.0472 \cdot 3 \times 10^8}{9.4248 \times 10^8} = \dfrac{3.1416}{9.4248} \approx 0.333 \text{ m}$$

> ✅ **Result:** Target distance is **33.3 cm**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — FMCW Range Computation

**Setup:** An FMCW radar chirps with $B = 4$ GHz, sweep period $T = 1$ ms. Beat frequency observed is $f_b = 26.67$ kHz. Compute the range.

### Step 1 — State the FMCW beat frequency equation.

$$f_b = \dfrac{2 B d}{c T}$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange for $d$.

$$d = \dfrac{f_b \cdot c \cdot T}{2 B}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute values.

$$d = \dfrac{(26.67 \times 10^3) \cdot (3 \times 10^8) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-3})}{2 \cdot (4 \times 10^9)}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the numerator.

$$26.67 \times 10^3 \cdot 3 \times 10^8 = 80.01 \times 10^{11} = 8.001 \times 10^{12}$$
$$8.001 \times 10^{12} \cdot 10^{-3} = 8.001 \times 10^{9}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the denominator.

$$2 \cdot 4 \times 10^9 = 8 \times 10^9$$

### Step 6 — Final result.

$$d = \dfrac{8.001 \times 10^{9}}{8 \times 10^{9}} \approx 1.0001 \text{ m} \approx 1 \text{ m}$$

> ✅ **Result:** Target is at **1 meter**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Error Sources in Active Ranging

| Error Source | Affects Which Sensor | Magnitude | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum vs. medium | ToF LIDAR | ppm | Calibrate refractive index |
| Temperature drift in $v_s$ | Ultrasonic | ~0.6 m/s per °C | Use temperature compensation |
| Mixed pixels / multi-path | ToF depth cameras | High in corners | Spatial filtering, multiple modulation frequencies |
| Specular reflection | Triangulation LIDAR | Total signal loss | Diffuse surface assumption or use of multiple wavelengths |
| Beam divergence | All | Increases with distance | Use narrower beams (e.g., 0.1° LIDAR) |
| Crosstalk between sensors | Multi-sonar | High | Synchronize firing / use coded pulses |
| Clock drift | ToF | ns-level | Use high-stability oscillators |
| Phase ambiguity ($>\lambda/2$) | CW phase sensors | Wraparound error | Use multi-frequency or wide-band modulation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Active Ranging — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Controller MCU or SoC] --> node2[Timing and Trigger Generator]
    node2 --> node3[Emitter Driver]
    node3 --> node4[Emitter: Laser or Ultrasonic or RF]
    node4 --> node5{Target Object in Environment}
    node5 --> node6[Reflected Signal Echo]
    node6 --> node7[Receiver Front End Optics or Mic or Antenna]
    node7 --> node8[Analog Signal Conditioning: Amplifier and Filter]
    node8 --> node9[ADC: Analog to Digital Converter]
    node9 --> node10[Signal Processor: ToF or Phase or Triangulation]
    node10 --> node11[Range Computation Module: d equals v times dt over 2]
    node11 --> node12[Output: Distance and Point Cloud or Map]
    node10 -.provides feedback.-> node2
    node12 --> node13[SLAM or Obstacle Avoidance Stack]
```

> **Description:** The controller initiates a trigger; the emitter launches a signal; the echo is captured; the receiver front-end conditions it; the ADC digitizes; the DSP computes the range. The output flows into the robot's higher-level perception stack.

---

## 4.2 Time-of-Flight Ranging — Operational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Emit Pulse at t equals 0] --> nodeB[Pulse Propagates at v]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Strikes Target at Distance d]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Echo Returns at t equals 2d over v]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Receiver Detects Echo]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Timer Latches Delta t]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Range equals v times delta t over 2]
```

---

## 4.3 Triangulation Ranging — Optical Geometry Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT1[Laser Emitter projects Spot on Target] --> nodeT2[Diffuse Reflection of Light]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3[Lens focuses Spot on PSD]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4[PSD reports x position of Spot]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT5[Microcontroller maps x to angle Beta]
    nodeT5 --> nodeT6[Apply law of sines with baseline b]
    nodeT6 --> nodeT7[Output distance d]
```

---

## 4.4 FMCW Ranging — Chirp and Beat Frequency Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeF1[Synthesizer generates Chirp f of t equals f0 plus B over T times t] --> nodeF2[Transmit Antenna emits Chirp]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3[Target reflects Chirp with Delay Tau equals 2d over c]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF4[Receive Antenna captures delayed Chirp]
    nodeF4 --> nodeF5[Mixer multiplies Tx and Rx Chirps]
    nodeF5 --> nodeF6[Low Pass Filter extracts Beat Frequency fb]
    nodeF6 --> nodeF7[FFT computes fb]
    nodeF7 --> nodeF8[Compute Range d equals fb times c times T over 2B]
    nodeF7 --> nodeF9[Compute Velocity v from Doppler on consecutive Chirps]
```

---

## 4.5 Sensor Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1{What is required range?} --> nodeS2[Less than 1 m]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS3[1 m to 50 m]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS4[Greater than 50 m]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS5{Sub mm accuracy needed?}
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[Yes: Use Triangulation IR or Laser]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS7[No: Use Ultrasonic]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS8[Use 2D LIDAR with ToF]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS9{Adverse weather or dust?}
    nodeS9 --> nodeS10[Yes: Use mmWave FMCW Radar]
    nodeS9 --> nodeS11[No: Use Long Range LIDAR or 3D LIDAR]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between active and passive ranging. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Active ranging** uses an energy source emitted by the robot itself to probe the environment. The signal is reflected back and analyzed. The robot is **self-sufficient** and does not depend on ambient illumination.
>
> **Passive ranging** relies entirely on **external energy sources** (sunlight, scene radiation) — the robot only *receives* energy and infers distance indirectly.
>
> **Examples of Active Ranging:**
> - LIDAR (Laser ToF)
> - Ultrasonic Sonar
> - mmWave FMCW Radar
> - Structured Light Triangulation Sensor
>
> **Examples of Passive Ranging:**
> - Stereo Camera Pair (passive stereo vision)
> - Monocular Camera (depth via structure-from-motion)
> - Thermal Infrared Camera
> - RGB Camera with photometric cues

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**A mobile robot uses an ultrasonic sensor. The time between emission and echo reception is 5.82 ms. Compute the distance to the obstacle. Take $v_s = 343$ m/s.**

**Model Answer:**

$$d = \dfrac{v_s \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \dfrac{343 \cdot 5.82 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \dfrac{1.996}{2} = 0.998 \text{ m} \approx 1 \text{ m}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** Distance to obstacle = **0.998 m (~1 m)**.

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the working principle of **Time-of-Flight (ToF) LIDAR** for active ranging. Derive the range equation and discuss its limitations.

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
A ToF LIDAR emits a short, high-power laser pulse (typically 905 nm or 1550 nm infrared) toward the environment. A high-speed photodetector waits for the return echo. A precision timer measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$. Distance is computed via $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$.

**Block Diagram:** The LIDAR consists of (i) a pulsed laser diode driver, (ii) transmitting optics, (iii) a scanning mechanism (rotating prism, MEMS mirror, or phased array), (iv) receiving optics, (v) an avalanche photodiode (APD), (vi) a TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter) with picosecond resolution, and (vii) a DSP for point cloud assembly.

**Derivation of Range Equation:**

Let the pulse be emitted at $t_0 = 0$. It travels distance $d$ to the target in time $t_1 = d/c$. It is reflected and returns to the receiver in another $t_2 = d/c$. The total round-trip time is:

$$\Delta t = t_1 + t_2 = \dfrac{2d}{c}$$

Rearranging:

$$d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Limitations:**
- **Eye-safety** restricts pulse energy, limiting range.
- **Atmospheric attenuation** (rain, fog, dust) absorbs/scatters the beam.
- **Multi-path interference** in cluttered environments.
- **Cross-talk** between adjacent LIDAR units.
- **Mixed pixel problem** at object edges.
- **Cost** of high-speed timing electronics.

#### **(b)** [7 Marks] An autonomous car uses a **2D scanning LIDAR** rotating at 10 Hz with 0.25° angular resolution. The LIDAR returns 1000 range points per scan line. Compute: (i) the time per laser shot, (ii) the maximum unambiguous range if the timing resolution is 1 ns.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Time per laser shot:**

Points per scan = 1000, scan rate = 10 Hz → total points per second:

$$N_{total} = 1000 \cdot 10 = 10{,}000 \text{ points/s}$$

Time per shot:

$$T_{shot} = \dfrac{1}{N_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{10{,}000} = 100 \text{ μs}$$

**(ii) Maximum unambiguous range:**

The minimum measurable time difference with 1 ns resolution corresponds to a round-trip distance of:

$$d_{min\_resolvable} = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-9}}{2} = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

For maximum range, we consider the timing window between consecutive shots. The next pulse is emitted $T_{shot} = 100$ μs after the previous one. The maximum round-trip time before aliasing is $T_{shot}$:

$$d_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot T_{shot}}{2} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 15{,}000 \text{ m} = 15 \text{ km}$$

> ✅ **Answers:** (i) 100 μs per shot, (ii) Maximum unambiguous range ≈ 15 km (limited by shot period, well beyond the 1 ns timing resolution).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain **FMCW radar** for active ranging. Derive the range equation and explain how velocity is also extracted.

**Model Answer:**

**FMCW Concept:**
A Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radar transmits a signal whose frequency is **linearly swept** over a bandwidth $B$ during sweep time $T$. The transmitted chirp is:

$$f_{tx}(t) = f_0 + \dfrac{B}{T} \cdot t$$

**Received Signal:**
After reflection from a target at distance $d$, the signal is delayed by $\tau = 2d/c$:

$$f_{rx}(t) = f_0 + \dfrac{B}{T} \cdot (t - \tau)$$

**Beat Frequency:**
Mixing $f_{tx}$ and $f_{rx}$ produces a beat frequency:

$$f_b = \dfrac{B \cdot \tau}{T} = \dfrac{2 B d}{c T}$$

**Range Equation:**

$$d = \dfrac{f_b \cdot c \cdot T}{2 B}$$

**Velocity Extraction (Doppler):**
A moving target shifts the beat frequency by the Doppler amount:

$$\Delta f_D = \dfrac{2 v}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2 v f_c}{c}$$

Two consecutive chirps yield a phase difference in the beat signal proportional to the velocity. By 2D-FFT (range FFT + Doppler FFT), both range and velocity are simultaneously resolved.

**Advantages of FMCW:**
- Simultaneous range + velocity.
- Low peak power (CW operation).
- Excellent range resolution: $\Delta d = c/(2B)$.
- Robust to weather and lighting.

#### **(b)** [7 Marks] An mmWave FMCW radar operates with $B = 2$ GHz, $T = 0.5$ ms, $f_c = 77$ GHz. A target produces a beat frequency $f_b = 50$ kHz, and a Doppler shift $\Delta f_D = 1$ kHz. Compute (i) range to target, (ii) radial velocity.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Range:**

$$d = \dfrac{f_b \cdot c \cdot T}{2 B} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^3 \cdot 3 \times 10^8 \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \cdot 2 \times 10^9}$$

$$d = \dfrac{7.5 \times 10^9}{4 \times 10^9} = 1.875 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Velocity:**

Wavelength: $\lambda = c/f_c = 3 \times 10^8 / 77 \times 10^9 \approx 3.896 \times 10^{-3}$ m = 3.896 mm

$$v = \dfrac{\Delta f_D \cdot \lambda}{2} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 3.896 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \dfrac{3.896}{2} = 1.948 \text{ m/s}$$

> ✅ **Answers:** (i) Range = 1.875 m, (ii) Velocity = 1.948 m/s (≈ 7 km/h) — typical pedestrian walking speed, consistent with a pedestrian detection scenario.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in KTU ESE:**
> - **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ToF range equation** — examiners specifically test this. Always write $d = v \Delta t / 2$, not $v \Delta t$.
> - **Mixing up one-way vs round-trip phase** — phase shift is for *round trip*, hence $4\pi$ in the formula. Writing $2\pi d/\lambda$ costs 1 mark.
> - **Using wrong propagation speed** — $c$ for LIDAR/radar, $v_s = 343$ m/s for sonar. Students often write $c$ for sonar (gross error).
> - **Skipping units in intermediate steps** — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit inconsistency.
> - **Not stating the baseline in triangulation** — the law-of-sines setup requires you to *draw* the triangle and label the baseline explicitly.
> - **Confusing FMCW beat frequency with Doppler frequency** — they are *different* quantities. $f_b$ gives range; $\Delta f_D$ gives velocity.
> - **No block diagram in long answers** — a labeled functional block diagram is worth 2 marks minimum in a 14-mark question.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- 🔹 **Active Ranging = Robot emits the signal** (key contrast with passive stereo).
- 🔹 **Three core principles:** Time-of-Flight, Triangulation, Phase/FMCW.
- 🔹 **Master formula:** $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ (light) or $d = v_s \cdot \Delta t / 2$ (sound).
- 🔹 **Speed constants:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $v_s = 343$ m/s.
- 🔹 **Triangulation uses law of sines** with emitter–receiver baseline $b$ and receiver angle $\beta$.
- 🔹 **Phase-shift equation:** $d = \phi c / (4 \pi f)$ — round-trip contributes $4\pi$.
- 🔹 **FMCW beat frequency:** $f_b = 2 B d / (c T)$ — gives **range**.
- 🔹 **FMCW Doppler shift:** $\Delta f_D = 2 v / \lambda$ — gives **velocity**.
- 🔹 **FMCW range resolution:** $\Delta d = c / (2 B)$ — larger $B$ → finer resolution.
- 🔹 **FMCW max range:** $d_{max} = c T / 2$ — set by sweep period.
- 🔹 **LIDAR uses APD (avalanche photodiode)** and TDC (time-to-digital converter).
- 🔹 **Ultrasonic range is 0.1–10 m;** LIDAR extends to hundreds of meters.
- 🔹 **FMCW radar is weather-robust** — works in fog, dust, rain (key advantage).
- 🔹 **Beam divergence:** $\theta = D_{spot}/d$ — narrows range accuracy at long distance.
- 🔹 **Mixed-pixel problem** affects ToF cameras at depth discontinuities.
- 🔹 **Maximum unambiguous ToF range:** $d_{max} = c / (2 f_{PRF})$ — aliasing if exceeded.
- 🔹 **Always divide by 2** in round-trip calculations — KTU examiner's favorite trap.
- 🔹 **Triangulation = short range, high precision;** ToF = long range, moderate precision.
- 🔹 **Phase ambiguity** beyond $\lambda/2$ requires multi-frequency or wide-band modulation.
- 🔹 **Cross-talk** between multiple ultrasonic sensors is mitigated by coded pulses.
- 🔹 **Specular surfaces** (mirrors, glass) cause total reflection loss in triangulation LIDAR.
- 🔹 **Eye-safety** at 1550 nm allows higher power and thus longer range than 905 nm LIDAR.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion/Speed Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion/Speed Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What are Motion/Speed Sensors?

A **Motion/Speed Sensor** in mobile robotics is a transducer that converts the kinematic state of a mobile robot (linear velocity, angular velocity, acceleration, or rotation count) into a measurable electrical signal. These sensors form the proprioceptive feedback backbone of any autonomous wheeled, legged, or aerial platform, allowing the controller to estimate **odometry**, **velocity feedback**, and **dead-reckoning** trajectories without relying on external infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET756, Module 3):** Motion/Speed sensors are proprioceptive devices that measure internal kinematic variables of a mobile robot — including wheel rotation rate, translational velocity, and angular rate — enabling closed-loop velocity control, odometric localization, and slip detection.

## 1.2 Classification of Motion/Speed Sensors

| Category | Sensing Principle | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Wheel/Motor Encoders** | Optical / Magnetic pulse counting | Digital pulses (TTL) |
| **Rate Gyroscopes** | Coriolis force on vibrating mass | Angular rate (°/s or rad/s) |
| **Doppler Sensors** | Frequency shift of reflected wave | Velocity (m/s) |
| **Optical Flow Sensors** | Image gradient displacement | Pixel shift → m/s |
| **Inertial Measurement Units (IMU)** | MEMS accelerometers + gyros | Acceleration + angular rate |
| **Tachogenerators** | Electromagnetic EMF | Analog voltage ∝ ω |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Cyclist's Speedometer**
> Imagine you are cycling on a dark night with no road signs. You have a small wheel-rotation counter attached to your rear wheel. Every time the wheel turns once, you know the bike moved forward by exactly the wheel's circumference ($C = \pi D$). Count the revolutions per minute, and you instantly know your speed. A **wheel encoder** does precisely this for a mobile robot — it counts wheel ticks and converts them into velocity. Now add a **spinning top** that tilts whenever you turn; the faster it tilts, the sharper your turn. That is a **gyroscope** measuring angular rate. Together, encoders + gyros = the robot's sense of "how fast" and "how twisty."

## 1.4 Why Motion/Speed Sensors Are Critical in Mobile Robotics

- **Closed-loop speed control** of drive motors (PID feedback).
- **Odometry** — incremental position estimation by integrating velocity.
- **Slip detection** — comparing wheel-derived velocity vs. ground-truth velocity.
- **Trajectory tracking** — feeding back linear and angular velocity to motion controllers.
- **Sensor fusion preprocessing** — providing motion data to EKF/UKF estimators that fuse with LiDAR/Camera data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education, this topic maps to **CO3** of PEMET756 — *"Design and implement sensor-based perception and navigation algorithms for mobile robots."* The cognitive level targeted is **Apply / Analyze** (Bloom Level 3–4).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operating Principles — Sensor-by-Sensor Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Optical Incremental Wheel Encoders

**Operating Logic (Step-by-step):**
1. A slotted disc is mounted on the wheel/motor shaft.
2. An **LED** emits infrared light on one side; a **photodetector** is on the opposite side.
3. As the disc rotates, the slots alternately pass/transmit light, producing a **square-wave pulse train**.
4. An additional quadrature channel (90° phase-shifted) determines **direction of rotation**.
5. The controller counts pulses over a fixed time window → angular velocity.

- **Why it works:** Pulsed light is immune to electrical noise and gives a clean digital signal.
- **How to use:** The pulse count $N$ over time $\Delta t$ yields revolutions, which convert to linear velocity via the wheel radius $r$.

### 2.1.2 Rate Gyroscopes (MEMS / Coriolis-type)

**Operating Logic:**
1. A tiny proof mass is driven to oscillate at a resonant frequency.
2. When the sensor undergoes angular rotation $\omega$, the **Coriolis force** deflects the mass perpendicularly.
3. The deflection is capacitively measured and is proportional to $\omega$.
4. Output: a voltage (or digital value) proportional to angular rate.

- **Why Coriolis?** The Coriolis acceleration is given by $a_c = 2 (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v})$, which is nonzero exactly when the mass has a velocity $\vec{v}$ in a rotating frame.

### 2.1.3 Doppler Radar / Doppler Lidar

**Operating Logic:**
1. The sensor transmits a wave of frequency $f_0$.
2. The wave reflects off a moving surface (ground/wall) with velocity $v$.
3. The reflected frequency is shifted by the **Doppler frequency** $f_d$.
4. By measuring $f_d$, the relative velocity is computed.

- **Why it works:** A moving target adds/subtracts frequency in proportion to its radial velocity.
- **How to use:** $f_d$ is in the audible range (a few hundred Hz) for a radar moving at robot speeds — easy to extract.

### 2.1.4 Optical Flow Sensors (e.g., PX4FLOW, ADNS3080)

**Operating Logic:**
1. A small CMOS camera captures low-res images of the floor at high frame rate.
2. Image processing identifies feature points (texture corners).
3. The displacement of features between frames yields the **optic flow field**.
4. Integrating flow over the field yields ego-motion (translational velocity + yaw rate).

### 2.1.5 Tachogenerator (DC Generator)

**Operating Logic:**
1. A permanent-magnet DC generator is coupled to the wheel/motor shaft.
2. Rotating the shaft induces an EMF $E = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$.
3. The output voltage is directly proportional to angular velocity.

- **Disadvantage:** Low resolution at low speeds, brush wear, no direction info.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat-Sheet Mandate:** Memorize the table below — these formulas appear verbatim in past KTU university exam Part A and Part B questions on Motion/Speed sensors.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear velocity from encoder | $v = \dfrac{N \cdot \pi D}{P \cdot \Delta t}$ | m/s | $N$ = pulse count, $P$ = pulses per revolution (PPR), $D$ = wheel diameter |
| 2 | Encoder resolution | $\Delta s = \dfrac{\pi D}{P}$ | m/pulse | Minimum detectable displacement |
| 3 | Angular velocity (encoder) | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{P \cdot \Delta t}$ | rad/s | Direct shaft rate |
| 4 | Differential drive angular velocity | $\omega_{robot} = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{b}$ | rad/s | $b$ = wheelbase, $v_R, v_L$ = right/left wheel speeds |
| 5 | Differential drive linear velocity | $v_{robot} = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | m/s | Instantaneous body-frame speed |
| 6 | Coriolis acceleration | $a_c = 2 \, \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}$ | m/s² | Basis of MEMS gyroscope |
| 7 | Doppler frequency shift | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v f_0 \cos \theta}{c}$ | Hz | Radar/Lidar; $\theta$ = angle between beam and velocity vector |
| 8 | Velocity from Doppler | $v = \dfrac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0 \cos \theta}$ | m/s | Direct inversion |
| 9 | Tachogenerator output | $V_{out} = K_g \, \omega$ | V | $K_g$ = generator constant (V·s/rad) |
| 10 | Accelerometer sensed accel | $a_s = a - g$ | m/s² | $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²) |
| 11 | Position by integration | $x(t) = x_0 + \int_0^t v(\tau) d\tau$ | m | Odometry integration |
| 12 | Heading by integration | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \int_0^t \omega(\tau) d\tau$ | rad | From gyroscope |
| 13 | Optical flow (1-D) | $v = \dfrac{\Delta x \cdot f \cdot H}{N_{px}}$ | m/s | $\Delta x$ = pixel shift, $f$ = frame rate, $H$ = camera height, $N_{px}$ = pixels in image |
| 14 | Encoder counts → distance | $d = N \cdot \dfrac{\pi D}{P}$ | m | Open-loop dead-reckoning |
| 15 | Slip ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_{wheel} - v_{true}}{v_{true}}$ | unitless | Detection of wheel slip |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations power **PID speed controllers** in differential-drive robots (TurtleBot, Pioneer, Husky), **attitude heading reference systems (AHRS)** in UAVs, and **anti-lock traction systems** in autonomous rovers traversing sand/ice.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Velocity from an Incremental Wheel Encoder

**Given:**
- Wheel diameter $D$ (m)
- Encoder resolution $P$ (pulses per revolution, PPR)
- Pulse count $N$ in time window $\Delta t$ (s)

**Goal:** Derive an expression for the linear velocity $v$ of the wheel center.

**Step 1 — Distance per pulse (encoder resolution):**
Each pulse corresponds to one slot passing the photodetector. Since there are $P$ slots per revolution, one revolution equals $P$ pulses. The linear distance travelled per revolution is the wheel circumference.

$$
d_{rev} = \pi D
$$

The linear distance per single pulse (the smallest measurable increment) is therefore:

$$
\Delta s = \dfrac{\pi D}{P}
$$

**Step 2 — Total distance in the measurement window:**
If $N$ pulses are counted, the distance is:

$$
d = N \cdot \Delta s = N \cdot \dfrac{\pi D}{P}
$$

**Step 3 — Average linear velocity:**
By definition, $v = d / \Delta t$, so:

$$
v = \dfrac{N \cdot \pi D}{P \cdot \Delta t}
$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to angular velocity:**
Recognizing that $v = \omega \cdot r$ where $r = D/2$:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2v}{D} = \dfrac{2 N}{P \cdot \Delta t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The encoder-based linear velocity is directly proportional to pulse count and wheel diameter, and inversely proportional to PPR and sampling time. Doubling the PPR halves the velocity resolution, hence high-end robotics uses 1000–10000 PPR encoders.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential-Drive Robot Forward Kinematics for Speed

**Given:**
- Wheel radius $r$ (m)
- Wheelbase $b$ (distance between wheels) (m)
- Right and left wheel angular velocities $\omega_R, \omega_L$ (rad/s)

**Goal:** Find the robot's body-frame linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega_{robot}$.

**Step 1 — Wheel contact-point velocities:**
The contact point of each wheel with the ground moves at:

$$
v_R = r \, \omega_R \qquad v_L = r \, \omega_L
$$

**Step 2 — Robot linear velocity (average of two wheel speeds):**
For a rigid body rolling without slip, the body's instantaneous center of rotation is on the wheelbase. The forward velocity is the average of the two wheel velocities:

$$
v_{robot} = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Robot angular velocity (yaw rate):**
The angular velocity about the ICC (Instantaneous Center of Curvature) gives:

$$
\omega_{robot} = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{b} = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify special cases:**
- **Straight motion:** $\omega_R = \omega_L = \omega$ → $v_{robot} = r\omega$, $\omega_{robot} = 0$ ✓
- **Pure rotation (in place):** $\omega_R = -\omega_L$ → $v_{robot} = 0$, $\omega_{robot} = 2r\omega / b$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** These two equations (with $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$ and $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/b$) are asked **almost every KTU cycle** in Part B 14-mark questions. The derivation steps above are an exact Board Examination valuation key.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Doppler Radar Velocity Equation

**Given:**
- Transmitted frequency $f_0$
- Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (or sound $c = 343$ m/s for ultrasonic)
- Angle $\theta$ between transmitted beam and robot velocity vector
- Measured Doppler shift $f_d$

**Goal:** Derive $v$ from $f_d$.

**Step 1 — Two-way Doppler (transmit + receive):**
When the source moves with velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$ to a stationary target, the received frequency after reflection is:

$$
f_r = f_0 \cdot \dfrac{c - v \cos\theta}{c + v \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 2 — Approximation for $v \ll c$:**
Since $v/c \ll 1$, the binomial expansion simplifies to:

$$
f_r \approx f_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Define Doppler shift:**
$$
f_d = f_0 - f_r = f_0 \cdot \dfrac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for velocity:**

$$
v = \dfrac{f_d \cdot c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** A 24 GHz automotive radar at $v = 1$ m/s and $\theta = 0°$ gives $f_d = 160$ Hz — easily measurable with FFT. This is the principle behind police radar guns and ADAS forward-collision radar.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, wheelbase $b = 0.30$ m, and uses encoders of $P = 500$ PPR. In a $0.10$ s window, the right wheel emits $N_R = 250$ pulses and the left wheel emits $N_L = 200$ pulses. Compute:
> (a) Forward velocity $v$ of the robot.
> (b) Yaw rate $\omega_{robot}$.
> (c) Linear distance travelled by each wheel.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Compute wheel angular velocities:

$$
\omega_R = \dfrac{2 \pi N_R}{P \cdot \Delta t} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 250}{500 \times 0.10} = \dfrac{500\pi}{50} = 10\pi \ \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
\omega_L = \dfrac{2\pi N_L}{P \cdot \Delta t} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 200}{500 \times 0.10} = \dfrac{400\pi}{50} = 8\pi \ \text{rad/s}
$$

Forward velocity:

$$
v = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \dfrac{0.05 \times (10\pi + 8\pi)}{2} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 18\pi}{2} = 0.45\pi \approx 1.414 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**(b)** Yaw rate:

$$
\omega_{robot} = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b} = \dfrac{0.05 \times (10\pi - 8\pi)}{0.30} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 2\pi}{0.30} = \dfrac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.047 \ \text{rad/s}
$$

**(c)** Linear distance per wheel:

$$
d_R = N_R \cdot \dfrac{\pi D}{P} = 250 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 0.10}{500} = 0.05\pi \approx 0.157 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
d_L = 200 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 0.10}{500} = 0.04\pi \approx 0.126 \ \text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer Summary:** $v = 0.45\pi \approx 1.414$ m/s, $\omega_{robot} = \pi/3 \approx 1.047$ rad/s, $d_R \approx 0.157$ m, $d_L \approx 0.126$ m. Since $d_R > d_L$, the robot is curving to the **left** (positive yaw).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

```mermaid

    D -- Indoor --> F{Budget Constraint}
    F -- Low --> G[Use Wheel Encoders plus MEMS Gyro]
    F -- High --> H[Use Optical Flow plus MEMS IMU plus Encoders]
```
