<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEMET756]`
**Define the term "encoder resolution" in the context of a mobile robot's wheel encoder. State the formula.**

**Model Answer:**
Encoder resolution $\Delta s$ is the smallest linear displacement of the wheel that produces a single countable pulse. It is given by:

$$
\Delta s = \dfrac{\pi D}{P}
$$

where $D$ is the wheel diameter and $P$ is the pulses-per-revolution of the encoder. For example, a wheel of $D = 0.2$ m with $P = 1000$ PPR has $\Delta s = 0.628$ mm/pulse.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEMET756]`
**State the Doppler frequency shift equation and explain its significance in mobile robot speed measurement.**

**Model Answer:**
For a radar/lidar beam transmitted at frequency $f_0$ reflecting from a target moving with radial velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$ to the beam, the Doppler shift is:

$$
f_d = \dfrac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}
$$

**Significance:** It allows **non-contact, infrastructure-free measurement of ground-relative velocity**, independent of wheel slip. It is used in autonomous cars (ACC radar), UAV altimetry, and Mars-rover velocity tracking.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, PEMET756]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **optical incremental encoder** used for wheel-speed measurement in mobile robots. State the formula relating pulse count, wheel diameter, and linear velocity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A differential-drive mobile robot has $r = 0.08$ m, $b = 0.40$ m, and PPR = 1000. In a 0.20 s interval, the left wheel gives 800 pulses and the right wheel gives 1200 pulses. Compute the forward velocity, yaw rate, and the direction of turning.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The optical incremental encoder consists of:
1. A **slotted disc** rigidly coupled to the wheel/motor shaft.
2. An **infrared LED** on one side of the disc.
3. A **photodetector** on the other side, aligned with the LED.
4. A **Schmitt-trigger comparator** to clean up the signal into a square wave.
5. A **quadrature decoder** providing two channels (A and B) 90° out of phase, to determine direction.

**Working:** As the disc rotates, the slots alternately allow/block light, generating a square-wave pulse train. Channel A leads or lags channel B by 90° depending on direction. The microcontroller counts rising edges in a time window.

**Velocity formula:**

$$
v = \dfrac{N \cdot \pi D}{P \cdot \Delta t}
$$

> For full marks, the student should also mention quadrature decoding, resolution enhancement via ×4 decoding, and index pulse Z for absolute homing.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Wheel angular velocities:**

$$
\omega_L = \dfrac{2\pi \times 800}{1000 \times 0.20} = \dfrac{1600\pi}{200} = 8\pi \ \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
\omega_R = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1200}{1000 \times 0.20} = \dfrac{2400\pi}{200} = 12\pi \ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Forward velocity:**

$$
v = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \dfrac{0.08 \times (12\pi + 8\pi)}{2} = \dfrac{0.08 \times 20\pi}{2} = 0.8\pi \approx 2.513 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Yaw rate:**

$$
\omega_{robot} = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b} = \dfrac{0.08 \times (12\pi - 8\pi)}{0.40} = \dfrac{0.08 \times 4\pi}{0.40} = 0.8\pi \approx 2.513 \ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Direction:**
Since $\omega_R > \omega_L$ and $\omega_{robot} > 0$ (using the right-hand rule convention), the robot is rotating **counter-clockwise**, i.e., turning **to the left**.

**Final answers:**
- $v \approx 2.513$ m/s
- $\omega_{robot} \approx 2.513$ rad/s
- Direction: left turn

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, PEMET756]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle of operation of a MEMS Coriolis gyroscope** for angular-rate sensing in a mobile robot. Why is the Coriolis force used?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Doppler-shift velocity equation** $v = f_d c / (2 f_0 \cos\theta)$ for a radar/lidar speed sensor. A 24 GHz Doppler radar mounted on a robot emits at $f_0 = 24 \times 10^9$ Hz, observes a Doppler shift $f_d = 500$ Hz at $\theta = 0°$, and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. Compute the robot's ground speed.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

A MEMS Coriolis gyroscope measures angular rate using a vibrating proof mass.

**Principle:**
1. A tiny silicon proof mass is electrostatically driven into **resonant oscillation** along the X-axis at frequency $f_x$.
2. When the entire sensor undergoes an angular rotation $\vec{\omega}$ about a perpendicular axis, the mass experiences the **Coriolis pseudo-force** $F_c = -2m (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v})$.
3. This force deflects the mass along the Y-axis (perpendicular to its drive motion).
4. Capacitive electrodes on either side of the mass sense the Y-displacement, which is **directly proportional to $\omega$**.

**Why Coriolis?**
- The Coriolis effect exists **only when there is motion in a rotating frame** — perfect for sensing rotation via a moving mass.
- It is highly **linear** and proportional to $\omega$ (not $\omega^2$), simplifying calibration.
- It enables **MEMS fabrication** at low cost, which is why modern IMUs (e.g., MPU6050, ADIS16470) use this principle.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Derivation (already detailed in Section 3.3):**
The Doppler shift for a monostatic radar (co-located Tx/Rx) reflecting off a target moving with velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$ to the line of sight is:

$$
f_d = \dfrac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}
$$

Solving for $v$:

$$
v = \dfrac{f_d \cdot c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}
$$

**Numerical substitution:**

$$
v = \dfrac{500 \times 3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 24 \times 10^9 \times \cos 0°} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{11}}{4.8 \times 10^{10}} = 3.125 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**Final answer:** $v = 3.125$ m/s (≈ 11.25 km/h).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong unit for $\omega$:** Some students give $\omega_{robot}$ in revolutions/sec instead of rad/s. Always convert to rad/s in the final answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Doppler:** The radar is **monostatic** (same antenna Tx/Rx), so the round-trip shift doubles. Missing this halves the computed velocity.
> 3. **Sign convention in yaw rate:** State explicitly whether positive $\omega$ is CCW or CW. KTU Board expects CCW positive when the right wheel is faster.
> 4. **PPR vs CPR confusion:** Some encoders quote *cycles* per revolution (CPR) = 4× PPR (since each quadrature cycle has 4 edges). Read the datasheet carefully.
> 5. **No quadrature decoding mentioned:** Students frequently lose 1–2 marks by describing only one channel, not both A and B for direction sensing.
> 6. **In Doppler derivation, students often forget $\cos\theta$:** If the radar beam is tilted, the radial velocity component is $v \cos\theta$, not $v$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Encoder velocity formula:** $v = N \pi D / (P \Delta t)$.
- ✅ **Differential-drive kinematics:** $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$ and $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/b$.
- ✅ **Doppler equation:** $f_d = 2 v f_0 \cos\theta / c$ — remember the factor of **2** for monostatic radar.
- ✅ **Coriolis force:** $F_c = -2m(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v})$ — basis of MEMS gyroscopes.
- ✅ **Accelerometer measures:** $a_s = a - g$ (specific force, not true acceleration).
- ✅ **Gyroscope measures:** Angular rate, not angle — must integrate to get heading.
- ✅ **Encoder resolution = $\pi D / P$** — choose higher PPR for finer odometry.
- ✅ **Tachogenerator:** $V = K_g \omega$ — analog output, no digital direction info.
- ✅ **Optical flow sensor** (e.g., PX4FLOW) gives velocity from pixel-shift of floor texture.
- ✅ **Slip detection:** Compare encoder velocity vs. Doppler/optical-flow ground-truth velocity.
- ✅ **Sensor fusion:** Encoders (high-freq, drift-free short term) + IMU (high-freq, drift long term) + GPS (low-freq, no drift) → EKF.
- ✅ **Units discipline:** Always SI — m, s, rad, m/s, rad/s, Hz. Convert rpm → rad/s by multiplying $2\pi/60$.
- ✅ **Block diagrams:** Examiner awards 1–2 marks purely for a correctly labelled functional block diagram of an encoder or gyroscope.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Motion/Speed Sensors.** Cross-reference with **Module 4 (Range/Proximity Sensors)** for the complementary exteroceptive sensing block. Together, they form the complete **sensor suite** required for SLAM and autonomous navigation — a KTU 2024 Scheme CO3 attainment pillar.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Navigation: Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robot Navigation: Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Robot Localization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Localization** is the process by which a mobile robot determines its pose (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) relative to a **given map** of the environment, using noisy sensor measurements and noisy control actions. Formally, it is the problem of estimating the belief distribution $bel(x_t)$ over the robot's state $x_t$ at time $t$, conditioned on all past observations $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$.

Mathematically, the goal of localization is to compute:

$$
bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

where $x_t$ is the robot state, $z_{1:t}$ is the sequence of measurements up to time $t$, and $u_{1:t}$ is the sequence of control inputs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Localization vs. Mapping**: Localization assumes a **known map** is already given. Mapping is the complementary problem — building the map. Doing both simultaneously is the **SLAM** problem (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), covered in later modules.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Blind Shopper

Imagine you enter a huge unfamiliar shopping mall **blindfolded**. You are told the complete layout of the mall (the *map*). As you walk, you use a cane to tap pillars and hear echoes off walls (your *noisy sensors*), and you feel how many steps you have taken (your *noisy odometry*).

- **Position Tracking** = You roughly know which aisle you started in, and you track your location as you walk.
- **Global Localization** = You are placed in the mall randomly with no idea where you are, and must figure it out from scratch using only your senses.
- **Kidnapped Robot Problem** = While shopping, someone quietly picks you up and drops you in a new aisle. You must realize that you have been moved and re-localize.

This is **exactly** what a mobile robot faces with wheel slippage, GPS dropout, and sensor noise.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Localization Problems

| Localization Type | Initial Belief | Difficulty | Key Challenge |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking** | Unimodal Gaussian near true pose | Low | Maintaining accuracy over time |
| **Global Localization** | Uniform over entire map | High | Disambiguating symmetric places |
| **Kidnapped Robot** | Uniform + sudden jump | Very High | Recovery after global jumps |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Examiners frequently test the difference between these three problem categories. Memorize the **initial belief distribution** shape for each — it is the most tested attribute.

## 1.4 Map Representations Used in Localization

| Representation | Storage | Use Case | Pros / Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Metric (Occupancy Grid)** | 2D grid of cells with $P(occupied)$ | Indoor robots, laser scanners | + Easy to update / − Memory heavy |
| **Feature-Based (Landmarks)** | List of $(x, y, \text{id})$ pairs | Outdoor GPS-like settings | + Compact / − Needs extraction |
| **Topological** | Graph of places + edges | Hallways, road networks | + Human-readable / − Less precise |
| **Semantic** | Objects with labels (door, table) | Human–robot interaction | + Rich / − Computationally expensive |

## 1.5 Why Localization is Hard

> [!WARNING]
> Three fundamental difficulties make localization non-trivial:
> 1. **Sensor noise** — every measurement is corrupted.
> 2. **Actuator noise** — odometry drifts over time (unbounded error growth).
> 3. **Perceptual aliasing** — different places look identical (e.g., long corridors, symmetric rooms).

The solution family for these problems is **probabilistic robotics** — never commit to a single pose estimate, instead maintain a **belief distribution** over all possible poses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Framework — Bayes Filter

The **Bayes Filter** is the general recursive algorithm for estimating a belief $bel(x_t)$ from the previous belief $bel(x_{t-1})$. It has two phases: **Prediction** (apply motion model) and **Correction** (apply sensor model).

### 2.1.1 Prediction Step (Motion Update)

Before seeing measurement $z_t$, the robot predicts its new state distribution using the motion model $P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$:

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Intuition (Why):** The robot *predicts* where it should be based on commanded motion, *blurring* the old belief by the motion-noise distribution.

**How it works (How):**
1. Take the previous belief $bel(x_{t-1})$ — a probability cloud of "where I might have been."
2. For every particle/hypothesis in the cloud, *shift* it according to control $u_t$.
3. Add motion noise (because wheels slip, etc.) — this is the convolution.
4. Result: $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ — predicted belief **before** sensing.

### 2.1.2 Correction Step (Measurement Update)

After taking measurement $z_t$, the prediction is multiplied (Bayes' rule) by the likelihood:

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)
$$

where $\eta$ is the normalizer:

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})} = \left[ \int P(z_t \mid x') \cdot \overline{bel}(x') \, dx' \right]^{-1}
$$

**Intuition:** Sensor measurements that disagree with a hypothesis *suppress* that hypothesis; agreements *amplify* it.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note**: The notation $\overline{bel}$ (bar) versus $bel$ (no bar) is **strictly** used in the textbook by Thrun, Burgard, and Fox. Examiners expect this distinction — $\overline{bel}$ is the *predicted* belief, $bel$ is the *corrected* (posterior) belief.

## 2.2 The Three Major Localization Algorithms

### 2.2.1 Markov Localization (Grid-Based)

- Represents the belief as a **histogram** over a discretized grid of the state space.
- Each grid cell stores the probability the robot is at that cell.
- **Pros**: Can represent *multi-modal* beliefs (handles global localization).
- **Cons**: Computationally expensive; resolution × grid size.

### 2.2.2 Kalman Filter Localization (EKF / UKF)

- Represents the belief as a **single Gaussian**: $bel(x_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$.
- **Pros**: Extremely efficient (closed-form updates).
- **Cons**: Can only represent **uni-modal** beliefs → good for *position tracking*, **fails** for global localization.

### 2.2.3 Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter)

- Represents the belief as a **set of weighted samples** (particles) $\mathcal{X}_t = \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$.
- **Pros**: Handles *any* belief shape; robust global localization.
- **Cons**: Needs many particles for high accuracy.

## 2.3 Sensor Models

| Sensor | Model Form $P(z \mid x)$ | Noise Distribution |
|---|---|---|
| **Range-finder (Lidar)** | $P(z = r \mid x)$ = beam hits expected wall at distance $r$ | Mixture: Gaussian + uniform outliers |
| **Camera (Landmarks)** | $P(z = \text{feat} \mid x)$ via reprojection geometry | Gaussian on pixel coordinates |
| **Odometry** | Acts as $u$, not $z$ — used in motion model | Gaussian on $(Δx, Δy, Δθ)$ |
| **GPS** | Position in global frame | Gaussian, ~**3 m** outdoor, useless indoors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **beam model** of a range-finder is the most tested sensor model. It has four components:
> 1. $p_{\text{hit}}$ — correct measurement (Gaussian centered on true range)
> 2. $p_{\text{short}}$ — unexpected obstacles (exponential)
> 3. $p_{\text{max}}$ — max-range returns (point mass)
> 4. $p_{\text{rand}}$ — random noise (uniform)

The total likelihood is the **mixture**:

$$
P(z_t \mid x_t, m) = 
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha_{\text{hit}} \\
\alpha_{\text{short}} \\
\alpha_{\text{max}} \\
\alpha_{\text{rand}}
\end{pmatrix}^{T}
\begin{pmatrix}
p_{\text{hit}} \\
p_{\text{short}} \\
p_{\text{max}} \\
p_{\text{rand}}
\end{pmatrix}
\quad \text{with} \quad \sum \alpha_i = 1
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Posterior belief | Definition |
| 2 | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$ | Prediction | Bayes filter |
| 3 | $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Correction | Bayes filter |
| 4 | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - \hat{z}_t)$ | Kalman mean update | EKF / UKF |
| 5 | $K_t = \Sigma'_t \, H^T (H \Sigma'_t H^T + Q)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | EKF |
| 6 | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H) \Sigma'_t$ | Covariance update | EKF |
| 7 | $w_t^{[i]} \propto P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \cdot P(x_t^{[i]} \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$ | Particle weight | MCL |
| 8 | $N_{\text{eff}} = \left( \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2 \right)^{-1}$ | Effective sample size | Resampling trigger |
| 9 | $P(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \sum_{k} \alpha_k \cdot p_k(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | Mixture beam model | Lidar |
| 10 | $\bar{\mu}_t = \mu_{t-1} + \Delta_t(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Odometry propagation | Motion model |

> [!TIP]
> Memorize rows **2, 3, 4, 5, 7** — they appear in **every** KTU ESE paper on localization.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla)**: Multi-modal MCL with lidar + camera + GPS.
- **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva)**: EKF-style localization using floor fiducials / 2D barcodes.
- **Indoor Service Robots (Vacuum, Pepper)**: Particle filter SLAM on 2D occupancy grids.
- **Drones (Quadrotors)**: UKF on visual-inertial odometry (VIO).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Bayes Filter

Starting from the definition:

$$
bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

**Step 1 — Condition on the latest measurement $z_t$:**

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$
bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Rule** (swap $x_t$ and $z_t$):

$$
P(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

**Step 3 — Markov Assumption** (the world is "complete" — past is summarized by $x_t$):

$$
P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(z_t \mid x_t)
$$

Therefore:

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

where $\eta = 1 / P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ is the **normalizer**.

**Step 4 — Marginalize over $x_{t-1}$** (law of total probability):

$$
P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Markov again:**

$$
P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)
$$

and note that $P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = bel(x_{t-1})$ (control $u_t$ does not affect $x_{t-1}$).

**Step 6 — Final Bayes Filter Equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{bel}(x_t) &= \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} \\
bel(x_t) &= \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Kalman Filter Localization — Full Update Cycle

Assume the belief at time $t-1$ is Gaussian: $bel(x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$.

### 3.2.1 Prediction Step (State Estimate)

Apply the (non-linear) motion model to the mean:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})
$$

Linearize the motion model around $\mu_{t-1}$ using the **Jacobian** $G_t$:

$$
G_t = \left. \frac{\partial g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})}{\partial x_{t-1}} \right|_{x_{t-1}=\mu_{t-1}}
$$

Propagate the covariance:

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^{T} + R_t
$$

where $R_t$ is the motion-noise covariance.

### 3.2.2 Measurement Prediction

Predict the expected measurement from the predicted pose:

$$
\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t)
$$

Linearize using the observation Jacobian $H_t$:

$$
H_t = \left. \frac{\partial h(\bar{\mu}_t)}{\partial x_t} \right|_{x_t = \bar{\mu}_t}
$$

Compute the **innovation covariance**:

$$
S_t = H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^{T} + Q_t
$$

where $Q_t$ is the sensor-noise covariance.

### 3.2.3 Correction Step (Kalman Gain and Update)

Compute the Kalman gain:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^{T} \, S_t^{-1}
$$

Update the mean using the **innovation** $(z_t - \hat{z}_t)$:

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - \hat{z}_t)
$$

Update the covariance (Joseph form for numerical stability is preferred):

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for $K_t$:** $K_t \in [0, 1]$ balances *trust in the model* vs. *trust in the sensor*. If $Q_t$ is small (sensor very accurate) → $K_t \to 1$ (trust sensor). If $R_t$ is small (model very accurate) → $K_t \to 0$ (trust model).

## 3.3 Monte Carlo Localization — Sample-by-Sample Update

**Initialization:** Draw $M$ particles $\{x_0^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$ from the prior $P(x_0)$. If position-tracking, draw near known start. If global, draw uniformly.

**For each time step $t$:**

**Step 1 — Sampling (Prediction):** For each particle, sample a new pose from the motion model:

$$
x_t^{[i]} \sim P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})
$$

**Step 2 — Importance Weighting (Correction):** Set each weight proportional to the sensor likelihood:

$$
w_t^{[i]} = P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)
$$

Normalize so $\sum_i w_t^{[i]} = 1$:

$$
\tilde{w}_t^{[i]} = \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_j w_t^{[j]}}
$$

**Step 3 — Resampling:** Draw $M$ new particles with replacement, where particle $x_t^{[i]}$ is drawn with probability $\tilde{w}_t^{[i]}$. After resampling all weights become $1/M$.

**Step 4 — (Optional) Compute $N_{\text{eff}}$:**

$$
N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (\tilde{w}_t^{[i]})^2}
$$

If $N_{\text{eff}} < 0.5 \, M$, trigger resampling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why resampling?** Without it, after a few iterations one particle has nearly all the weight and the rest are wasted. Resampling refocuses computation on promising regions.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — 1D Kalman Filter

A robot is at unknown position $x$. Initial belief: $\mu_0 = 0$, $\sigma_0^2 = 1$.  
Motion model: $x_t = x_{t-1} + 0.5 + w_t$ where $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.2)$.  
Measurement: $z_t = x_t + v_t$ where $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.3)$.  
At $t=1$: actual motion added 0.5, sensor reads $z_1 = 0.9$.

**Prediction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\mu}_1 &= 0 + 0.5 = 0.5 \\
\bar{\sigma}_1^2 &= 1 + 0.2 = 1.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Innovation:** $z_1 - \hat{z}_1 = 0.9 - 0.5 = 0.4$

**Innovation covariance:** $S_1 = H \bar{\sigma}_1^2 H + Q = 1 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 1 + 0.3 = 1.5$

**Kalman gain:** $K_1 = 1.2 / 1.5 = 0.8$

**Update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1 &= 0.5 + 0.8 \cdot 0.4 = 0.5 + 0.32 = 0.82 \\
\sigma_1^2 &= (1 - 0.8) \cdot 1.2 = 0.24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Posterior mean **0.82** lies between prediction 0.5 and measurement 0.9, but closer to measurement because $Q = 0.3 < R = 0.2$... wait, $K = 0.8$ is *high* because the model uncertainty (1.2) is much greater than sensor uncertainty (0.3), so we **trust the sensor more**. The posterior variance shrank from 1.2 to 0.24 — a **5×** reduction in uncertainty. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Bayes Filter Localization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Belief bel x0] --> B[Motion Command u t]
    B --> C[Prediction Step: Apply Motion Model]
    C --> D[Predicted Belief bar bel x t]
    D --> E[Sensor Measurement z t]
    E --> F[Correction Step: Apply Sensor Model Pz given x]
    F --> G[Posterior Belief bel x t]
    G --> H{Next Time Step?}
    H -- Yes --> B
    H -- No --> I[Output Pose Estimate]
```

## 4.2 Kalman Filter Localization Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: mu t-1, Sigma t-1] --> P1[Compute Predicted Mean: mu bar = g of u, mu]
    P1 --> P2[Linearize Motion: Jacobian G t]
    P2 --> P3[Propagate Covariance: Sigma bar = G Sigma G T plus R t]
    P3 --> M1[Predict Measurement: z hat = h of mu bar]
    M1 --> M2[Linearize Sensor: Jacobian H t]
    M2 --> M3[Innovation Covariance: S t = H Sigma bar H T plus Q t]
    M3 --> K1[Kalman Gain: K t = Sigma bar H T S t inverse]
    K1 --> U1[Update Mean: mu t = mu bar plus K t times z minus z hat]
    U1 --> U2[Update Covariance: Sigma t = I minus K t H times Sigma bar]
    U2 --> OUT[Output: Pose Estimate and Uncertainty]
```

## 4.3 Monte Carlo Localization — Particle Filter Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I[Initial Particles: M samples from prior] --> S1[Step 1: Sample x t from Motion Model P of x t given u t, x t-1]
    S1 --> W1[Step 2: Compute Importance Weights w t = P of z t given x t, map]
    W1 --> N1[Step 3: Normalize Weights w tilde]
    N1 --> R1{N effective below threshold?}
    R1 -- Yes --> RES[Step 4: Resample M particles with replacement using w tilde]
    R1 -- No --> OUT
    RES --> OUT[Output Pose: Weighted Mean of Particles]
    OUT --> S1
```

## 4.4 Comparison of the Three Localization Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KF[EKF / UKF Localization]
        KF1[Uni-modal Gaussian] --> KF2[Closed form update] --> KF3[Fast and Compact]
    end
    subgraph ML[Markov Grid Localization]
        ML1[Histogram over Grid] --> ML2[Discrete update] --> ML3[Multi-modal but Expensive]
    end
    subgraph PF[Particle Filter MCL]
        PF1[Samples with Weights] --> PF2[Monte Carlo Approximation] --> PF3[Flexible and Robust]
    end
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture of a Complete Localization System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        I1[Wheel Encoders: Odometry u t]
        I2[Lidar / Camera: Range or Feature z t]
        I3[IMU: Angular rate]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Processing Layer]
        P1[Motion Model: P of x t given u t, x t-1]
        P2[Sensor Model: P of z t given x t, map]
        P3[Bayes Filter Core]
        P4[Belief Representation]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Pose Estimate: x, y, theta]
        O2[Uncertainty Ellipse]
        O3[Path for Navigation]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I3 --> P1
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between position tracking, global localization, and the kidnapped robot problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Type | Initial Belief | Complexity | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking** | Localized near true pose (narrow Gaussian) | Low | Robot knows roughly where it is; refines pose as it moves. |
| **Global Localization** | Uniform over entire state space | High | Robot has no idea where it is; must determine pose from scratch. |
| **Kidnapped Robot** | Uniform + sudden teleport | Very High | Robot is moved to a new location without being told; must detect and recover. |

Marking scheme: 1 mark per correct differentiation row, 1 mark for overall description, 1 mark for example. (3/3)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Why is the Kalman Filter unsuitable for global localization?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Kalman Filter represents the belief as a **single uni-modal Gaussian** distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$. Global localization, by contrast, requires a **multi-modal** belief because the robot can initially be in *any* of many equally likely poses (e.g., the four corners of a square room look identical from a single scan). A Gaussian can only have **one peak**, so it cannot express multiple competing hypotheses. Hence, the Kalman Filter is limited to **position tracking** and cannot solve the global localization or kidnapped robot problems. (3 marks — 1 for uni-modality, 1 for multi-modal need, 1 for the conclusion.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Bayes Filter** recursive update equations from first principles, clearly stating all assumptions.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Localization** algorithm. State its limitations.

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Definition of belief (1 mark)**

$$
bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

**Step 2: Condition on latest measurement (1 mark)**

$$
bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

**Step 3: Apply Bayes' rule (1 mark)**

$$
bel(x_t) = \frac{P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

**Step 4: Markov Assumption — sensor depends only on current state (1 mark)**

$$
P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(z_t \mid x_t)
$$

So:

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

**Step 5: Marginalize over $x_{t-1}$ (1 mark)**

$$
P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Step 6: Second Markov — state transition depends only on $u_t$ and $x_{t-1}$ (1 mark)**

$$
P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})
$$

Also $P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = bel(x_{t-1})$.

**Step 7: Final two equations (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{bel}(x_t) &= \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} \\
bel(x_t) &= \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** See Section 4.2 of these notes for the EKF cycle. Required components: Prediction → Measurement prediction → Kalman gain → Update.

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**

1. **Initialize** belief as Gaussian: $(\mu_0, \Sigma_0)$.
2. **Predict mean** using motion: $\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})$.
3. **Linearize motion** via Jacobian $G_t$.
4. **Propagate covariance**: $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^{T} + R_t$.
5. **Predict measurement**: $\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t)$.
6. **Compute Kalman gain**: $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^{T} (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^{T} + Q_t)^{-1}$.
7. **Update mean**: $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - \hat{z}_t)$.
8. **Update covariance**: $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Uni-modal — cannot represent global localization ambiguity.
- Linearization (Jacobian) introduces error for highly non-linear systems.
- Sensitive to data association errors (wrong landmark matches cause divergence).
- Cannot recover from global localization failures.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter)** algorithm with its algorithmic steps. How does it differ from EKF in handling global localization?

**(b) [7 marks]** A robot uses a 1D Kalman Filter. Initial belief: $\mu_0 = 0$, $\sigma_0 = 2$. Motion model: $x_t = x_{t-1} + 1 + w_t$ with $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w = 0.5)$. Measurement model: $z_t = x_t + v_t$ with $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_v = 1)$. At $t=1$, the sensor measures $z_1 = 2.5$. Compute the posterior mean and variance.

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Algorithm (5 marks):**

1. **Initialization (1 mark):** Draw $M$ particles $\{x_0^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$ from the prior. For global localization, sample uniformly over the map; for position tracking, sample around the initial guess.

2. **Sampling / Motion Update (1 mark):** For each particle, propagate using the motion model with noise:
$$x_t^{[i]} \sim P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$$

3. **Importance Weighting / Measurement Update (1 mark):** Compute the weight as the sensor likelihood:
$$w_t^{[i]} = P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$$
Normalize: $\tilde{w}_t^{[i]} = w_t^{[i]} / \sum_j w_t^{[j]}$.

4. **Resampling (1 mark):** Draw $M$ new particles with replacement, where particle $i$ is selected with probability $\tilde{w}_t^{[i]}$. All weights reset to $1/M$.

5. **Pose Estimate (1 mark):** Return the weighted mean $\hat{x}_t = \sum_i \tilde{w}_t^{[i]} x_t^{[i]}$.

**Comparison with EKF for global localization (2 marks):**

| Aspect | EKF | MCL |
|---|---|---|
| Belief shape | Uni-modal Gaussian | Any shape (multi-modal) |
| Global localization | Fails | Works |
| Computation | $O(K^2)$ per step | $O(M)$ per step |
| Recovery from failure | No | Yes (multiple particles) |

MCL uses many particles to represent **multiple hypotheses** simultaneously — the EKF can only track one peak.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $\mu_0 = 0$, $\sigma_0^2 = 4$
- Motion: $\Delta = 1$, $\sigma_w^2 = 0.5$
- Sensor: $z_1 = 2.5$, $\sigma_v^2 = 1$

**Step 1 — Prediction (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\mu}_1 &= \mu_0 + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1 \\
\bar{\sigma}_1^2 &= \sigma_0^2 + \sigma_w^2 = 4 + 0.5 = 4.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation and innovation covariance (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Innovation: } \nu &= z_1 - \bar{\mu}_1 = 2.5 - 1 = 1.5 \\
S_1 &= H \bar{\sigma}_1^2 H + \sigma_v^2 = 1 \cdot 4.5 \cdot 1 + 1 = 5.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Kalman gain (1 mark):**

$$
K_1 = \frac{\bar{\sigma}_1^2}{S_1} = \frac{4.5}{5.5} = 0.818
$$

**Step 4 — Posterior update (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1 &= \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 \cdot \nu = 1 + 0.818 \times 1.5 = 1 + 1.227 = 2.227 \\
\sigma_1^2 &= (1 - K_1) \bar{\sigma}_1^2 = (1 - 0.818) \times 4.5 = 0.182 \times 4.5 = 0.819
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Posterior mean $\mu_1 \approx 2.23$ and posterior variance $\sigma_1^2 \approx 0.82$. The posterior standard deviation shrank from $\sqrt{4.5} \approx 2.12$ to $\sqrt{0.82} \approx 0.91$ — a major uncertainty reduction. (7/7)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing $\overline{bel}$ and $bel$** — many students write only one of the two Bayes filter equations. Both are mandatory; the bar (predicted) and the corrected (posterior) are **separate**.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$** — without it, the posterior is not a valid probability. Examiners deduct **1 mark** if $\eta$ is missing in the correction step.
> 3. **Markov assumption must be stated explicitly** in derivations — a full mark requires naming it as "the Markov assumption" rather than silently applying it.
> 4. **EKF linearization must include both $G_t$ and $H_t$** — students often linearize the motion model but forget the sensor Jacobian.
> 5. **In numerical KF problems**, show units and intermediate values; rounding too early loses marks. Use $K_t = \bar{\sigma}^2 / S$ for the 1D case — do not invert a 2×2 matrix.
> 6. **Particle filter question trap**: students often describe resampling *after* weighting but forget to **normalize** the weights first.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Localization = pose estimation on a known map** using noisy sensors and noisy control.
- **Three problem classes**: position tracking (local, easy), global localization (uniform prior, hard), kidnapped robot (uniform + teleport, hardest).
- **Bayes Filter** = the universal recursive estimator. It has exactly two steps: **prediction** (motion) and **correction** (measurement).
- **Markov assumption** is the foundation: future is conditionally independent of the past given the present state.
- **EKF Localization** = uni-modal Gaussian; fast; for **position tracking** only.
- **MCL / Particle Filter** = sample-based; **multi-modal**; handles global localization and kidnapped robot.
- **Kalman Gain $K$** balances trust: $K \to 1$ trust sensor (low $Q$); $K \to 0$ trust model (low $R$).
- **Beam sensor model** is a four-component mixture: hit, short, max, random.
- **Resampling** is triggered when effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}}$ drops (typically below $0.5 M$).
- **Jacobians $G_t, H_t$** are mandatory for EKF — they linearize the non-linear motion and sensor models.
- **Posterior variance always decreases or stays the same** — sensing reduces uncertainty; it can never increase it.
- **Kidnapped robot recovery** requires either a stochastic step in the particle filter (random injections) or a multi-hypothesis EKF.
- **Exam tip**: always draw the **prediction–correction cycle diagram** in long answers; it scores 1–2 free marks and demonstrates command over the concept.

topics.title: Error Propagation Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Error Propagation Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Error Propagation Model** is a probabilistic mathematical framework used in mobile robotics to characterize how uncertainties (errors) in a robot's pose estimate evolve as a function of robot motion, control inputs, and sensor noise. It provides a quantitative mechanism to predict the **growing confidence ellipse** around the robot's estimated state by propagating the state **covariance matrix** through the **non-linear motion model** using first-order **Taylor series linearization (Jacobians)**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET756 - Mobile Robotics, Module 3)**, this concept is rooted in *Probabilistic Robotics* (Thrun, Burgard, Fox). The error propagation model formally answers the question:

> *"Given that the robot's current pose is uncertain, how does that uncertainty evolve after the robot executes a motion command?"*

Mathematically, the error propagation is governed by:

$$\Sigma_{t+1} = G_t \, \Sigma_t \, G_t^T + V_t \, Q_t \, V_t^T$$

where $\Sigma_t$ is the pose covariance at time $t$, $G_t$ is the Jacobian of the motion model w.r.t. the state, $V_t$ is the Jacobian of the motion model w.r.t. the control input, and $Q_t$ is the control noise covariance.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Walking Blindfolded in a Room"**

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to walk from one corner of a room to another, but you only have a rough pedometer (step counter).

- **Step 1:** You are fairly confident about your starting point — the error is small (a tight bubble around you).
- **Step 5:** Each step has a tiny ±2 cm error. As you walk, these tiny errors **accumulate**.
- **Step 50:** The accumulation of small slips, rotations, and bumps causes a much larger uncertainty — the bubble becomes a huge **oval** around your estimated position.

This "growing oval" is the **Error Propagation Model**. The oval is called the **uncertainty ellipse**, and its mathematical description (orientation, major/minor axes) is derived from the **covariance matrix**.

A second analogy: **Snooker/Billiards**. When you strike the cue ball, you cannot predict the exact final position because of:
- Slight angle error (systematic error),
- Cloth friction variations (non-systematic error),
- Imperfect cue stroke (control noise).

The error propagation model mathematically combines all these disturbances to give a probabilistic region where the ball will likely stop.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard deviation:** $\sigma$ (units: meters, radians)
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2$ (squared units)
- **Covariance:** $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathbb{E}[(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)]$
- **Gaussian distribution notation:** $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$
- **Confidence level multipliers (Mahalanobis distance):**
  - **68.27 %** confidence → $d^2 = 2.30$ (1$\sigma$)
  - **95.45 %** confidence → $d^2 = 6.18$ (2$\sigma$)
  - **99.73 %** confidence → $d^2 = 11.83$ (3$\sigma$)

---

### 1.4 Why is the Error Propagation Model Important in KTU Examinations?

The KTU 2024 Scheme places heavy emphasis on:
- **Probabilistic Robotics** (Module 3 core)
- **Bayesian Filtering** (Kalman / EKF / Particle)
- **SLAM** (Module 4 / 5 prerequisite)

The Error Propagation Model is the **mathematical backbone** of the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Localization** and **EKF SLAM**, which are examinable high-weightage topics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Error in Mobile Robotics

The KTU syllabus identifies **three primary sources of error** that must be modelled:

| # | Source | Type | Typical Magnitude | Examples |
|---|--------|------|-------------------|----------|
| 1 | **Actuator / Control Noise** | Non-systematic | ~1–5 % of commanded motion | Wheel slip, encoder quantization |
| 2 | **Sensor Noise** | Non-systematic | Sensor-dependent | Range finder ±0.01 m, gyro ±0.1° |
| 3 | **Model / Systematic Errors** | Systematic | Bias-like | Wheel diameter mismatch, axis misalignment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU textbook reference (Thrun et al., *Probabilistic Robotics*), the motion model error is split into **two distinct components**: (a) errors in **rotation**, and (b) errors in **translation**. Both are modelled as zero-mean Gaussians added to the deterministic motion output.

---

### 2.2 The Motion Model (Velocity / Odometry Form)

For a differential-drive robot with pose $\mathbf{x}_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$, the velocity motion model is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{pmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ \theta' \end{pmatrix} &= \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} -\frac{v}{\omega}\sin\theta + \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(\theta+\omega\Delta t) \\ \frac{v}{\omega}\cos\theta - \frac{v}{\omega}\cos(\theta+\omega\Delta t) \\ \omega\Delta t \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $u_t = (v, \omega)^T$ is the control input (linear velocity, angular velocity).

For the **odometry motion model**, the robot reports a rotation $\delta_{\text{rot1}}$, translation $\delta_{\text{trans}}$, and rotation $\delta_{\text{rot2}}$ — each corrupted by noise.

---

### 2.3 The Covariance Propagation Theorem (Core Equation)

Let the **non-linear motion model** be:

$$\mathbf{x}_{t+1} = f(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + \varepsilon_t$$

where $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ is the control noise. Linearizing $f$ via the **first-order Taylor expansion** about the mean $(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{u}_t) &\approx f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + \underbrace{\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\bigg|_{\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t}}_{G_t}(\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t) + \underbrace{\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{u}}\bigg|_{\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t}}_{V_t}(\varepsilon_t) \\
&= f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + G_t (\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t) + V_t \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the covariance of both sides yields the **Error Propagation Equation**:

$$\boxed{\Sigma_{t+1} = G_t \, \Sigma_t \, G_t^T + V_t \, Q_t \, V_t^T}$$

This is the **single most important equation** in KTU Module 3.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Form |
|---|--------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $\Sigma_t$ | Pose covariance at time $t$ | $3\times 3$ symmetric positive definite matrix |
| 2 | $G_t$ | Jacobian of $f$ w.r.t. state | $G_t = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\big\rvert_{\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t}$ |
| 3 | $V_t$ | Jacobian of $f$ w.r.t. control | $V_t = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{u}}\big\rvert_{\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t}$ |
| 4 | $Q_t$ | Control noise covariance | Diagonal: $\text{diag}(\sigma_v^2, \sigma_\omega^2)$ |
| 5 | $R_t$ | Measurement noise covariance | Diagonal or full depending on sensor |
| 6 | **Propagation (Prediction)** | State | $\bar{\mu}_{t+1} = f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t)$ |
| 7 | **Propagation (Prediction)** | Covariance | $\bar{\Sigma}_{t+1} = G_t \Sigma_t G_t^T + V_t Q_t V_t^T$ |
| 8 | **Mahalanobis distance** | Statistical distance | $d^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mu)$ |
| 9 | **Uncertainty ellipse axes** | Major / minor | Eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ of $\Sigma_{xy}$ (2D slice) |
| 10 | **Ellipse orientation** | Angle | $\phi = \tfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\tfrac{2\sigma_{xy}}{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_y^2}\right)$ |
| 11 | **Velocity model Jacobian $G_t$** | Top-left $2\times 2$ block | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ (in straight-line limit) |
| 12 | **Gaussian PDF** | Probability density | $p(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^n \vert\Sigma\vert}}\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^T\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)\right)$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $\Sigma$ with $\Sigma^2$. Covariance is **not** the square of standard deviation — it is $\sigma \cdot \sigma^T$ for a vector. Also, never forget the **transposes** in $G \Sigma G^T$ — they are essential for symmetry.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The error propagation model is the **mathematical heart** of:

1. **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Tesla, Waymo, Cruise use error propagation to predict vehicle position uncertainty bounds at every time-step. This determines whether the car can confidently change lanes.
2. **Indoor Cleaning Robots (Roomba):** Uses covariance-propagated Monte Carlo Localization (a particle-filter variant) to estimate room position.
3. **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva):** Tracks growing pose uncertainty and triggers relocalization when covariance exceeds a threshold.
4. **Mars Rovers (NASA Curiosity / Perseverance):** The entire Visual Odometry + EKF SLAM stack uses Jacobians to bound position error to < 1 % of distance travelled.
5. **Drone Swarms:** Each drone propagates its uncertainty to safely avoid collision with neighbours using chance-constrained trajectories.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Taylor Linearization of the Motion Model

**Goal:** Linearize a non-linear motion function $f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})$ around the operating point $(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t)$.

**Step 1 — State the non-linear function.**

Let $f: \mathbb{R}^n \times \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}^n$ be the motion function, written as:

$$\mathbf{x}_{t+1} = f(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 2 — Expand as a multivariate Taylor series to first order.**

For small perturbations $\Delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t$ and noise $\varepsilon_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{u}_t) &= f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + \underbrace{\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\bigg|_{\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t}}_{G_t}(\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t) + \underbrace{\frac{\partial f}{\partial \varepsilon}\bigg|_{0}}_{I}\varepsilon_t + \text{H.O.T.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Explanation: The first term is the deterministic prediction. The second term captures how a small state perturbation propagates. The third term is the direct injection of control noise.

**Step 3 — Drop higher-order terms (H.O.T.).**

Since the noise and state error are small, second-order and higher derivatives are negligible, giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{u}_t) &\approx f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + G_t (\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t) + V_t \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the state equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_{t+1} &\approx f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + G_t (\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t) + V_t \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the mean of $\mathbf{x}_{t+1}$.**

Taking expectation $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ on both sides and noting $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}_t] = \mu_t$, $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_t] = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}_{t+1}] &= f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + G_t (\mu_t - \mu_t) + V_t \cdot 0 \\
\bar{\mu}_{t+1} &= f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the covariance of $\mathbf{x}_{t+1}$.**

Covariance is invariant to constants, so $\text{Cov}[f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t)] = 0$. Using the linearity-of-covariance identity $\text{Cov}[A\mathbf{z} + B\mathbf{w}] = A\,\text{Cov}[\mathbf{z}]\,A^T + B\,\text{Cov}[\mathbf{w}]\,B^T$ (when $\mathbf{z}, \mathbf{w}$ are independent):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_{t+1} &= \text{Cov}[G_t(\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t) + V_t \varepsilon_t] \\
&= G_t \,\text{Cov}[\mathbf{x}_t]\, G_t^T + V_t \,\text{Cov}[\varepsilon_t]\, V_t^T \\
\boxed{\Sigma_{t+1} &= G_t \, \Sigma_t \, G_t^T + V_t \, Q_t \, V_t^T}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Jacobians of the Velocity Motion Model

**Given the velocity motion model:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x - \frac{v}{\omega}\sin\theta + \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(\theta + \omega\Delta t) \\
y' &= y + \frac{v}{\omega}\cos\theta - \frac{v}{\omega}\cos(\theta + \omega\Delta t) \\
\theta' &= \theta + \omega\Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $G_t = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial (x,y,\theta)}$.**

Take partial derivatives row by row:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial x'}{\partial x} &= 1, \quad \frac{\partial x'}{\partial y} = 0 \\
\frac{\partial x'}{\partial \theta} &= -\frac{v}{\omega}\cos\theta + \frac{v}{\omega}\cos(\theta+\omega\Delta t)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial y'}{\partial x} &= 0, \quad \frac{\partial y'}{\partial y} = 1 \\
\frac{\partial y'}{\partial \theta} &= -\frac{v}{\omega}\sin\theta + \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(\theta+\omega\Delta t)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \theta'}{\partial x} &= 0, \quad \frac{\partial \theta'}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial \theta'}{\partial \theta} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

So in matrix form:

$$
G_t = \begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0 & -\frac{v}{\omega}\cos\theta + \frac{v}{\omega}\cos(\theta+\omega\Delta t) \\
0 & 1 & -\frac{v}{\omega}\sin\theta + \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(\theta+\omega\Delta t) \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $V_t = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial (v,\omega)}$** (non-trivial, requires quotient rule, omitted for brevity but follows identical procedure).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Uncertainty Ellipse Geometry from a 2×2 Covariance

Given the $2\times 2$ covariance $\Sigma_{xy} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_x^2 & \sigma_{xy} \\ \sigma_{xy} & \sigma_y^2 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 1 — Eigenvalues give the squared semi-axes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(\Sigma_{xy} - \lambda I) &= 0 \\
(\sigma_x^2 - \lambda)(\sigma_y^2 - \lambda) - \sigma_{xy}^2 &= 0 \\
\lambda^2 - (\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2)\lambda + (\sigma_x^2 \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_{xy}^2) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the quadratic.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_y^2}{2}\right)^2 + \sigma_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Ellipse parameters.**

- **Major semi-axis:** $a = \sqrt{\lambda_{\max}}$
- **Minor semi-axis:** $b = \sqrt{\lambda_{\min}}$
- **Orientation:** $\phi = \tfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\tfrac{2\sigma_{xy}}{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_y^2}\right)$

This derivation is essential for the KTU question on **"visualizing robot pose uncertainty as an ellipse."**

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Style Problem]**
> A differential-drive robot has pose $\mu_t = (1.0, 2.0, 0.5\,\text{rad})^T$ and covariance:
> $$\Sigma_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.04 & 0.01 & 0 \\ 0.01 & 0.09 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.0025 \end{pmatrix}$$
> It executes $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega = 0.2$ rad/s for $\Delta t = 1$ s.
> Compute the new covariance assuming $Q = \text{diag}(0.01, 0.0004)$.

**Step 1 — Compute the predicted pose.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta + \omega\Delta t &= 0.5 + 0.2 = 0.7 \text{ rad} \\
x' &= 1.0 - \frac{0.5}{0.2}\sin(0.5) + \frac{0.5}{0.2}\sin(0.7) \\
   &= 1.0 - 2.5 \times 0.4794 + 2.5 \times 0.6442 \\
   &= 1.0 - 1.1985 + 1.6105 = 1.4120 \\
y' &= 2.0 + 2.5\cos(0.5) - 2.5\cos(0.7) \\
   &= 2.0 + 2.5 \times 0.8776 - 2.5 \times 0.7648 \\
   &= 2.0 + 2.1940 - 1.9120 = 2.2820 \\
\theta' &= 0.7 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $G_t$ numerically.**

Using $\sin(0.5) \approx 0.4794$, $\cos(0.5) \approx 0.8776$, $\sin(0.7) \approx 0.6442$, $\cos(0.7) \approx 0.7648$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_{13} &= -2.5 \times 0.8776 + 2.5 \times 0.7648 = -2.1940 + 1.9120 = -0.2820 \\
g_{23} &= -2.5 \times 0.4794 + 2.5 \times 0.6442 = -1.1985 + 1.6105 = 0.4120
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
G_t = \begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0 & -0.2820 \\
0 & 1 & 0.4120 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $G_t \Sigma_t G_t^T$.**

First, $G_t \Sigma_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t \Sigma_t &= \begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0 & -0.2820 \\
0 & 1 & 0.4120 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix}
0.04 & 0.01 & 0 \\
0.01 & 0.09 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0.0025
\end{pmatrix} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix}
0.04 & 0.01 & -0.2820 \times 0.0025 \\
0.01 & 0.09 & 0.4120 \times 0.0025 \\
0 & 0 & 0.0025
\end{pmatrix} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix}
0.04 & 0.01 & -0.000705 \\
0.01 & 0.09 & 0.001030 \\
0 & 0 & 0.0025
\end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then $(G_t \Sigma_t) G_t^T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(G_t \Sigma_t) G_t^T &= \begin{pmatrix}
0.04 & 0.01 & -0.000705 \\
0.01 & 0.09 & 0.001030 \\
0 & 0 & 0.0025
\end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 \\
-0.2820 & 0.4120 & 1
\end{pmatrix} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix}
0.04 + 0.000199 & 0.01 - 0.000291 & -0.000705 \\
0.01 - 0.000291 & 0.09 + 0.000424 & 0.001030 \\
-0.000705 & 0.001030 & 0.0025
\end{pmatrix} \\
&\approx \begin{pmatrix}
0.0402 & 0.00971 & -0.000705 \\
0.00971 & 0.0904 & 0.001030 \\
-0.000705 & 0.001030 & 0.0025
\end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add control noise term $V_t Q_t V_t^T$ (omitted detailed sub-calculation).**

The final propagated covariance shows:
- $\sigma_x^2$ grew from $0.04$ to $\approx 0.0402$
- $\sigma_y^2$ grew from $0.09$ to $\approx 0.0904$
- Cross-covariance $\sigma_{xy}$ changed from $0.01$ to $\approx 0.0097$ — **the ellipse orientation shifts!**

This is a key KTU insight: **as the robot moves, the uncertainty ellipse elongates and rotates.**

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Error Propagation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Pose Mean mu_t at time t"] --> B["Motion Model f of x_t and u_t"]
    C["Control Input u_t of v and omega"] --> B
    D["Control Noise Covariance Q_t"] --> E["Jacobian V_t of f wrt u"]
    B --> F["Jacobian G_t of f wrt x"]
    B --> G["Predicted Mean mu_t+1 = f mu_t u_t"]
    F --> H["Propagated State Covariance G_t Sigma_t G_t transpose"]
    A2["Pose Covariance Sigma_t at time t"] --> H
    E --> I["Control Noise Term V_t Q_t V_t transpose"]
    D --> I
    H --> J["Total Predicted Covariance Sigma_t+1"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["Uncertainty Ellipse Extraction Eigen decomposition"]
    K --> L["2D Pose Estimate with Confidence Ellipse for visualization"]
    G --> L
    J --> M["Gate for next EKF iteration t+1"]
```

---

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Robot Pose Uncertainty at time t"] --> B["Sources of Error"]
    B --> C["Actuator Noise"]
    B --> D["Sensor Noise"]
    B --> E["Model Mismatch"]
    C --> C1["Wheel slip and skid"]
    C --> C2["Encoder quantization"]
    C --> C3["Motor torque ripple"]
    D --> D1["Range finder noise"]
    D --> D2["IMU bias drift"]
    D --> D3["Camera photometric noise"]
    E --> E1["Wheel diameter mismatch"]
    E --> E2["Axle misalignment"]
    E --> E3["Floor unevenness"]
    C1 --> F["Propagated Covariance Sigma_t+1"]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G["Growing Confidence Ellipse over time"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Uncertainty Ellipse Evolution Over Time

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Step 0: Small circular uncertainty Sigma 0 equal to identity scaled"] --> S1["Step 1: Slight elongation along motion direction"]
    S1 --> S2["Step 2: Ellipse grows and rotates due to Jacobian coupling"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Major axis aligned with travelled path"]
    S3 --> S4["Step N: Highly elongated ellipse parallel to path"]
    S4 --> S5["Trigger relocalization when ellipse exceeds threshold"]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture (Textual Schematic)

```
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|        INPUTS                                               |
|   - Pose mean  mu_t  (3x1)                                  |
|   - Pose covariance  Sigma_t  (3x3)                        |
|   - Control  u_t = (v, omega)                               |
|   - Control noise covariance  Q_t  (2x2)                    |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
                          |
                          v
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|        MOTION MODEL  f(x_t, u_t)                            |
|   x' = x - (v/w) sin(theta) + (v/w) sin(theta + w dt)       |
|   y' = y + (v/w) cos(theta) - (v/w) cos(theta + w dt)       |
|   theta' = theta + w dt                                     |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
                          |
              +-----------+-----------+
              v                       v
   +-------------------+   +-------------------+
   |  G_t = df/dx      |   |  V_t = df/du      |
   |  3x3 Jacobian     |   |  3x2 Jacobian     |
   +-------------------+   +-------------------+
              |                       |
              v                       v
   +-------------------+   +-------------------+
   | G_t Sigma_t G_t^T |   | V_t Q_t V_t^T     |
   | (state error term)|   | (control noise)   |
   +-------------------+   +-------------------+
              |                       |
              +-----------+-----------+
                          v
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|   OUTPUT:  Sigma_t+1 = G_t Sigma_t G_t^T + V_t Q_t V_t^T  |
|   - 3x3 symmetric positive-definite matrix                  |
|   - 2D slice Sigma_xy used to draw confidence ellipse       |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
                          |
                          v
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|   APPLICATIONS: EKF Localization, EKF SLAM, ICP,           |
|   Monte Carlo Localization, Autonomous navigation           |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
```
