## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the error propagation model in mobile robotics. Why is it essential for robot localization?**

**Model Answer:**
> The error propagation model is a probabilistic framework that quantifies how the **uncertainty** (covariance) in a robot's pose evolves when it executes a motion command. It uses the **Jacobian** of the non-linear motion model to first-order linearly propagate the covariance matrix forward in time:
> $$\Sigma_{t+1} = G_t \Sigma_t G_t^T + V_t Q_t V_t^T$$
> It is essential because (i) sensors and actuators are noisy, (ii) uncertainty grows unboundedly without correction, (iii) it underpins **EKF localization and SLAM**, and (iv) it enables **chance-constrained motion planning** by quantifying confidence ellipses around the pose estimate. **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is the role of the Jacobian matrix $G_t$ in the error propagation equation? Explain with reference to the velocity motion model.**

**Model Answer:**
> The Jacobian $G_t = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\big\rvert_{\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t}$ captures the **sensitivity** of the predicted pose to small perturbations in the current pose. It is a $3 \times 3$ matrix for a 2D planar robot. For the velocity motion model with $v = 0.5, \omega = 0.2, \theta = 0.5, \Delta t = 1$:
> $$G_t = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -0.282 \\ 0 & 1 & 0.412 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$
> The off-diagonal elements ($-0.282$, $0.412$) represent the **cross-coupling** between angular and translational uncertainty — a tiny heading error introduces a growing position error. **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **one full question of 14 marks** from a choice of two. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the **error propagation equation** $\Sigma_{t+1} = G_t \Sigma_t G_t^T + V_t Q_t V_t^T$ starting from the non-linear motion model $x_{t+1} = f(x_t, u_t) + \varepsilon_t$ using first-order Taylor expansion. Clearly state the assumptions. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** For a differential-drive robot executing $v = 0.3$ m/s and $\omega = 0.1$ rad/s for $\Delta t = 2$ s starting at pose $(0, 0, 0)$, compute the Jacobian $G_t$ of the velocity motion model. Given initial covariance $\Sigma_t = \text{diag}(0.02, 0.02, 0.005)$ and $Q = \text{diag}(0.008, 0.0002)$, compute the propagated covariance $\Sigma_{t+1}$. **[(7 Marks)]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — State the non-linear motion model.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_{t+1} &= f(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Assumptions:
1. Motion function $f$ is twice continuously differentiable.
2. Noise $\varepsilon_t$ is zero-mean, additive, and Gaussian.
3. State perturbation $(\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t)$ is small.
4. $\mathbf{x}_t$ and $\varepsilon_t$ are statistically independent.

**Step 2 — First-order Taylor expansion about $(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{u}_t) &\approx f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + G_t (\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t) + V_t \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the state equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_{t+1} &\approx f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + G_t (\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t) + V_t \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mean.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\mu}_{t+1} = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}_{t+1}] &= f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + G_t \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t] + V_t \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_t] \\
&= f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t) + 0 + 0 \\
\bar{\mu}_{t+1} &= f(\mu_t, \mathbf{u}_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the covariance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_{t+1} &= \mathbb{E}[(\mathbf{x}_{t+1} - \bar{\mu}_{t+1})(\mathbf{x}_{t+1} - \bar{\mu}_{t+1})^T] \\
&= \mathbb{E}[(G_t(\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t) + V_t \varepsilon_t)(G_t(\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t) + V_t \varepsilon_t)^T] \\
&= G_t \mathbb{E}[(\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t)(\mathbf{x}_t - \mu_t)^T] G_t^T + V_t \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_t \varepsilon_t^T] V_t^T \\
\boxed{\Sigma_{t+1} &= G_t \Sigma_t G_t^T + V_t Q_t V_t^T}
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross-terms vanish because $\mathbf{x}_t$ and $\varepsilon_t$ are independent. **[(7 Marks)]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the new heading.**

$$\theta + \omega\Delta t = 0 + 0.1 \times 2 = 0.2 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Build the Jacobian $G_t$.**

Using $\sin(0) = 0$, $\cos(0) = 1$, $\sin(0.2) \approx 0.1987$, $\cos(0.2) \approx 0.9801$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_{13} &= -\frac{v}{\omega}\cos(0) + \frac{v}{\omega}\cos(0.2) \\
       &= -\frac{0.3}{0.1}(1) + \frac{0.3}{0.1}(0.9801) \\
       &= -3 + 2.9403 = -0.0597 \\
g_{23} &= -\frac{v}{\omega}\sin(0) + \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(0.2) \\
       &= -3(0) + 3(0.1987) = 0.5961
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
G_t = \begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0 & -0.0597 \\
0 & 1 & 0.5961 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $G_t \Sigma_t G_t^T$.**

Since $\Sigma_t$ is diagonal:

$$
G_t \Sigma_t = \begin{pmatrix}
0.02 & 0 & -0.0597 \times 0.005 \\
0 & 0.02 & 0.5961 \times 0.005 \\
0 & 0 & 0.005
\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}
0.02 & 0 & -0.000298 \\
0 & 0.02 & 0.002981 \\
0 & 0 & 0.005
\end{pmatrix}
$$

Now multiply by $G_t^T$:

$$
G_t \Sigma_t G_t^T = \begin{pmatrix}
0.02 & 0 & -0.000298 \\
0 & 0.02 & 0.002981 \\
0 & 0 & 0.005
\end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 \\
-0.0597 & 0.5961 & 1
\end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{pmatrix}
0.02 + 1.78 \times 10^{-5} & -1.78 \times 10^{-4} & -0.000298 \\
-1.78 \times 10^{-4} & 0.02 + 1.78 \times 10^{-3} & 0.002981 \\
-0.000298 & 0.002981 & 0.005
\end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\approx \begin{pmatrix}
0.02002 & -0.000178 & -0.000298 \\
-0.000178 & 0.02178 & 0.002981 \\
-0.000298 & 0.002981 & 0.005
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the control noise term (assuming $V_t Q_t V_t^T$ gives a small contribution; in KTU-style answers, it is often added separately):**

$$
\Sigma_{t+1} \approx \begin{pmatrix}
0.02002 & -0.000178 & -0.000298 \\
-0.000178 & 0.02178 & 0.002981 \\
-0.000298 & 0.002981 & 0.005
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Interpretation:** The $\sigma_{xy}$ covariance is now negative ($-0.000178$), meaning the uncertainty ellipse is **rotated** in the counter-clockwise direction — the robot's heading has introduced an angular coupling into positional uncertainty. **[(7 Marks)]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the **three primary sources of error** in mobile robot localization. Describe how systematic and non-systematic errors differ in their treatment by the error propagation model. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** Given a 2D pose covariance $\Sigma_{xy} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.05 & 0.02 \\ 0.02 & 0.08 \end{pmatrix}$, compute (i) the eigenvalues, (ii) the major and minor semi-axes, and (iii) the orientation of the 95 % confidence ellipse. **[(7 Marks)]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Three Sources of Error:**

1. **Actuator / Control Noise (Non-systematic):** Wheel slip, uneven floor, motor torque ripple, encoder quantization. Modelled as **zero-mean Gaussian noise** added to the control input: $u_t^{\text{actual}} = u_t + \varepsilon_t$, with $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$. This enters the error propagation via $V_t Q_t V_t^T$.

2. **Sensor Noise (Non-systematic):** Range finders, cameras, IMUs have measurement noise. Modelled as $z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t$, with $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$. This is handled in the **measurement update** step of the EKF, not the propagation step.

3. **Model / Systematic Errors:** Wheel diameter mismatch, axle misalignment, biased calibration. These cause **consistent drift** and are typically handled by:
   - Re-calibration
   - Online parameter estimation (EM algorithm)
   - Adding bias terms to the state vector

**Systematic vs Non-systematic:** Non-systematic errors are random, zero-mean, and **propagated via covariance** matrices. Systematic errors are deterministic biases and require **explicit state augmentation** or calibration. The error propagation model **only** handles non-systematic noise; systematic errors must be eliminated beforehand. **[(7 Marks)]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $\sigma_x^2 = 0.05$, $\sigma_y^2 = 0.08$, $\sigma_{xy} = 0.02$.

**Step 1 — Compute eigenvalues.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{1,2} &= \frac{0.05 + 0.08}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{0.05 - 0.08}{2}\right)^2 + 0.02^2} \\
&= 0.065 \pm \sqrt{0.000225 + 0.0004} \\
&= 0.065 \pm \sqrt{0.000625} \\
&= 0.065 \pm 0.025
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\lambda_1 = 0.090$ and $\lambda_2 = 0.040$.

**Step 2 — Compute semi-axes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \sqrt{\lambda_{\max}} = \sqrt{0.090} \approx 0.300 \text{ m (major semi-axis)} \\
b &= \sqrt{\lambda_{\min}} = \sqrt{0.040} \approx 0.200 \text{ m (minor semi-axis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute orientation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \frac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\frac{2 \times 0.02}{0.05 - 0.08}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\frac{0.04}{-0.03}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\arctan(-1.333) \\
&= \frac{1}{2}(-53.13°) \\
&= -26.57°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Scale to 95 % confidence (multiplier = $\sqrt{5.991} \approx 2.448$ for 2D):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{95\%} &= 2.448 \times 0.300 \approx 0.734 \text{ m} \\
b_{95\%} &= 2.448 \times 0.200 \approx 0.490 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The 95 % confidence ellipse is **elongated along a direction tilted $-26.57°$ from the x-axis**, with semi-major axis 0.734 m and semi-minor axis 0.490 m. This means the robot's true position is likely within a tilted oval of these dimensions centered at the estimated mean. **[(7 Marks)]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the transpose in $G_t \Sigma_t G_t^T$.** The transpose is mandatory to maintain **symmetric positive-definite** structure. Losing it loses 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up $G_t$ and $V_t$.** $G_t$ is the Jacobian w.r.t. **state** $(x, y, \theta)$, $V_t$ is the Jacobian w.r.t. **control** $(v, \omega)$. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Sign errors in $g_{13}$ and $g_{23}$** of the velocity motion model Jacobian. Remember the **quotient rule** when $v/\omega$ is present.
> 4. **Failing to draw the uncertainty ellipse** in graphical questions — KTU awards 1–2 marks for the visual representation.
> 5. **Confusing eigenvalue formula** for $2 \times 2$ covariance — write the determinant expansion explicitly.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (covariance must be in $\text{m}^2$ or $\text{rad}^2$).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Error Propagation Model**

- ✅ The **error propagation equation** is: $\Sigma_{t+1} = G_t \Sigma_t G_t^T + V_t Q_t V_t^T$
- ✅ It is derived via **first-order Taylor linearization** of the non-linear motion model.
- ✅ **$G_t$ = Jacobian of motion model w.r.t. state** ($3\times 3$ for 2D).
- ✅ **$V_t$ = Jacobian of motion model w.r.t. control** ($3\times 2$ for differential drive).
- ✅ **$Q_t$ = control noise covariance**, typically diagonal: $\text{diag}(\sigma_v^2, \sigma_\omega^2)$.
- ✅ The covariance matrix is always **symmetric and positive semi-definite**.
- ✅ The **uncertainty ellipse** is drawn from the $2\times 2$ positional slice $\Sigma_{xy}$.
- ✅ **Semi-axes** of the ellipse: $\sqrt{\lambda_1}, \sqrt{\lambda_2}$ where $\lambda_{1,2}$ are eigenvalues.
- ✅ **Ellipse orientation:** $\phi = \tfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\tfrac{2\sigma_{xy}}{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_y^2}\right)$.
- ✅ **Confidence scaling:** 95 % uses multiplier $\sqrt{5.991}$ in 2D.
- ✅ **Three error sources:** actuator noise, sensor noise, model/systematic error.
- ✅ **Systematic errors** cannot be removed by averaging — they require calibration.
- ✅ The model underpins **EKF Localization**, **EKF SLAM**, and **uncertainty-aware planning**.
- ✅ For a **straight-line motion** at heading $\theta$, $G_t$ simplifies: $g_{13} = -v\Delta t \sin\theta$, $g_{23} = v\Delta t \cos\theta$.
- ✅ **Whitening transformation:** $y = L^{-1}(x - \mu)$ where $L L^T = \Sigma$ turns an ellipse into a unit circle (used in data association gating).
- ✅ The propagated covariance is used as the **prior** in the next Bayesian filter iteration.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Error Propagation Model**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (PEMET756 — Mobile Robotics)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Map-Based Localisation: Kalman Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Map-Based Localisation: Kalman Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Probabilistic Map-Based Localisation** is the process of estimating a robot's pose (position and orientation) within a known environment map, while explicitly modelling the uncertainties associated with robot motion, sensor measurements, and the environment itself. The **Kalman Filter (KF)** method is an optimal, recursive Bayesian estimator that solves this localisation problem under two stringent assumptions: (i) the system dynamics and measurement models are **linear**, and (ii) all noise sources (process and measurement) are **zero-mean Gaussian** with known covariances.

When these assumptions are violated (which is the rule in real robotics), the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** is used, which linearises non-linear models around the current state estimate using **Jacobian matrices**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET756 – Module 3):**
> Probabilistic map-based localisation is one of the foundational paradigms of mobile robotics. The Kalman Filter family (KF/EKF) forms the mathematical backbone for the "Tracking" problem, where the robot's pose is treated as a Gaussian random variable that gets updated recursively as new control inputs and sensor readings arrive.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy: "The Foggy Mountain Hiker"**
> Imagine you are a hiker lost on a mountain in heavy fog. You cannot see the full map, but you have a **rough paper map** of the terrain. You take one step, and because your legs are slightly unreliable, you are **not 100% sure exactly where you ended up** — your belief about your position has now become a *cloud* (an ellipse) of probable locations.
> 
> Then, a few seconds later, your phone's GPS gives a reading of your location. The GPS is also imperfect, so it has its own cloud of uncertainty — but a *different shaped* one.
> 
> Your brain does two things:
> 1. **Predicts** your new location based on the step you just took (and is uncertain about it).
> 2. **Corrects** this prediction using the GPS reading.
> 
> The **Kalman Filter** is the mathematical formalisation of this exact "predict-then-correct" loop. The final belief about your location is a *blend* of your prediction and the measurement, weighted by how much you trust each. The **Kalman Gain** is essentially the "trust factor" assigned to the sensor reading.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **State Vector ($x_k$)** | The robot's pose $[x, y, \theta]^T$ at time step $k$ |
| **Control Input ($u_k$)** | Odometry / wheel encoder readings / commanded velocities |
| **Measurement ($z_k$)** | Sensor observation (e.g., range to a landmark) |
| **Process Noise ($w_k$)** | Uncertainty in motion, modelled as $\mathcal{N}(0, Q_k)$ |
| **Measurement Noise ($v_k$)** | Uncertainty in sensors, modelled as $\mathcal{N}(0, R_k)$ |
| **Covariance ($P_k$)** | Matrix representing the spread/uncertainty of the belief |
| **Kalman Gain ($K_k$)** | Optimal weighting factor balancing prediction vs. measurement |
| **Jacobian ($F, H$)** | Matrix of first-order partial derivatives used in EKF linearisation |

### 1.4 Problem Classification in Localisation

| Position Tracking | Global Localisation | Kidnapped Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Robot's initial pose is known. Tracks the pose over time using KF/EKF. | Robot does not know its initial pose. Uses techniques like Markov Localisation. | Robot is teleported arbitrarily. Tests global localisation robustness. |

**The Kalman Filter specifically addresses the "Position Tracking" sub-problem** because it requires an initial belief (mean and covariance) as its starting point and is excellent at refining this belief over time.

### 1.5 Why Gaussian Models?

The Kalman Filter restricts beliefs to **unimodal Gaussian distributions** (ellipsoidal uncertainty regions). This is computationally efficient because a Gaussian is fully described by just two parameters: a **mean vector** ($\mu$) and a **covariance matrix** ($P$). Multiplication, addition, and integration of Gaussians remain Gaussians, making recursive updates closed-form and tractable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Three" Drawbacks of KF:**
> 1. Cannot represent **multi-modal** beliefs (e.g., "I am either in the kitchen OR the living room").
> 2. Requires **linear** models (EKF only approximates non-linearity).
> 3. Gaussian assumption is often violated by real-world sensor noise (e.g., walls causing false negatives in sonar).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Probabilistic Localisation Problem — Formal Statement

Given:
- A known map $m$ of the environment.
- A sequence of control inputs $u_{1:k} = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_k\}$.
- A sequence of observations $z_{1:k} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_k\}$.

We wish to estimate the posterior probability distribution of the robot's pose $x_k$ at time $k$:

$$bel(x_k) = p(x_k \mid z_{1:k}, u_{1:k})$$

By the **Bayes Filter** recursion, this can be computed in two steps:
- **Prediction** (motion update): $\overline{bel}(x_k) = \int p(x_k \mid x_{k-1}, u_k) \, bel(x_{k-1}) \, dx_{k-1}$
- **Correction** (measurement update): $bel(x_k) = \eta \, p(z_k \mid x_k) \, \overline{bel}(x_k)$

The Kalman Filter is the **closed-form analytical solution** to this recursion when all densities are Gaussian and all models are linear.

### 2.2 The Kalman Filter Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

#### **STEP 1: Prediction (Motion Update)**

The robot applies a control $u_k$. The motion model is:

$$x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_k + w_k$$

where $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_k)$ is the process noise with covariance $Q_k$.

The predicted state mean and covariance are:

$$\hat{x}_k^- = A \hat{x}_{k-1} + B u_k$$

$$P_k^- = A P_{k-1} A^T + Q_k$$

**Why this works:** Adding independent Gaussians gives a Gaussian. The covariance grows because the system is uncertain about the control outcome.

#### **STEP 2: Measurement Prediction (Innovation)**

The predicted measurement is computed using the observation model:

$$z_k^{pred} = H \hat{x}_k^-$$

The actual sensor reading $z_k$ is obtained. The **innovation** (or **measurement residual**) is:

$$\nu_k = z_k - H \hat{x}_k^-$$

The innovation covariance (the expected spread of the residual) is:

$$S_k = H P_k^- H^T + R_k$$

#### **STEP 3: Compute Kalman Gain**

$$K_k = P_k^- H^T S_k^{-1} = P_k^- H^T (H P_k^- H^T + R_k)^{-1}$$

**Interpretation:** 
- If $R_k \to 0$ (sensor is extremely accurate), then $K_k \to H^{-1}$ — we trust the measurement completely.
- If $P_k^- \to 0$ (prediction is very confident), then $K_k \to 0$ — we ignore the measurement.

#### **STEP 4: Update (Measurement Correction)**

The state estimate is updated by adding a weighted version of the innovation:

$$\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_k^-)$$

$$\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K_k \nu_k$$

The covariance is reduced to reflect the gained information:

$$P_k = (I - K_k H) P_k^-$$

A numerically more stable alternative (the **Joseph form**) is:

$$P_k = (I - K_k H) P_k^- (I - K_k H)^T + K_k R_k K_k^T$$

This formulation guarantees that $P_k$ remains symmetric and positive semi-definite, even with rounding errors.

### 2.3 The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for Non-Linear Systems

Real robot systems have **non-linear** motion and observation models:

$$x_k = f(x_{k-1}, u_k) + w_k$$

$$z_k = h(x_k) + v_k$$

EKF linearises these models using a **first-order Taylor series expansion** around the current best estimate:

- **State transition Jacobian**: $F_k = \left. \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} \right|_{x = \hat{x}_{k-1}}$
- **Observation Jacobian**: $H_k = \left. \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x} \right|_{x = \hat{x}_k^-}$

The EKF equations become:

$$\hat{x}_k^- = f(\hat{x}_{k-1}, u_k)$$

$$P_k^- = F_k P_{k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$$

$$K_k = P_k^- H_k^T (H_k P_k^- H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$$

$$\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K_k \left( z_k - h(\hat{x}_k^-) \right)$$

$$P_k = (I - K_k H_k) P_k^-$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — Every formula here has been asked in KTU exams in some form.**

| # | Equation | Name | Used In | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{x}_k^- = A\hat{x}_{k-1} + Bu_k$ | State Prediction (KF) | KF Prediction | Linear motion model |
| 2 | $P_k^- = AP_{k-1}A^T + Q_k$ | Covariance Prediction (KF) | KF Prediction | Uncertainty **grows** by $Q_k$ |
| 3 | $K_k = P_k^- H^T (H P_k^- H^T + R_k)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | KF/EKF Update | Optimal blend weight |
| 4 | $\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K_k (z_k - H\hat{x}_k^-)$ | State Update (KF) | KF Update | Adds innovation |
| 5 | $P_k = (I - K_k H) P_k^-$ | Covariance Update (KF) | KF Update | Uncertainty **shrinks** |
| 6 | $\hat{x}_k^- = f(\hat{x}_{k-1}, u_k)$ | State Prediction (EKF) | EKF Prediction | Non-linear motion |
| 7 | $P_k^- = F_k P_{k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$ | Covariance Prediction (EKF) | EKF Prediction | Uses Jacobian $F_k$ |
| 8 | $\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K_k (z_k - h(\hat{x}_k^-))$ | State Update (EKF) | EKF Update | Uses non-linear $h$ |
| 9 | $F_k = \partial f / \partial x \big\vert_{\hat{x}_{k-1}}$ | State Transition Jacobian | EKF Linearisation | Matrix of partial derivatives |
| 10 | $H_k = \partial h / \partial x \big\vert_{\hat{x}_k^-}$ | Observation Jacobian | EKF Linearisation | Matrix of partial derivatives |
| 11 | $P_k = (I - K_k H_k) P_k^- (I - K_k H_k)^T + K_k R_k K_k^T$ | Joseph Form | Stable Covariance Update | Numerically stable |
| 12 | $S_k = H P_k^- H^T + R_k$ | Innovation Covariance | Intermediate | Spread of residual |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How Kalman Filter is Used |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars)** | EKF fuses GPS, IMU, LiDAR, and wheel odometry to localise the car on a HD map (e.g., Tesla FSD, Waymo). |
| **Aerial Drones (UAVs)** | EKF in the **PX4 / Ardupilot** flight stacks fuses accelerometer, gyroscope, GPS, and barometer for real-time pose estimation. |
| **Indoor Mobile Robots (AGVs/AMRs)** | EKF combines wheel odometry with 2D LiDAR scan-matching against a known map (e.g., ROS `robot_localization` package). |
| **Spacecraft Navigation** | NASA's Deep Space Network uses Kalman filters to track spacecraft state vectors in deep space. |
| **Submarine / AUV Localisation** | EKF fuses Doppler Velocity Log (DVL), IMU, and acoustic beacons for underwater navigation. |

### 2.6 Computational Complexity

- **Standard KF:** $O(n^3)$ per cycle (dominated by the matrix inversion in $K_k$), where $n$ is the state dimension.
- **Typical Mobile Robot:** State = $[x, y, \theta, \dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$, so $n = 6$. This is extremely fast on modern CPUs.
- **Limitation:** The KF cannot be directly applied to **observability issues** (when a sensor cannot distinguish between two distinct states) or to **multi-modal** belief states.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Kalman Filter as the Optimal Bayesian Estimator

We will derive the Kalman Filter update equations from first principles using the principle of **minimum mean squared error (MMSE)**.

#### **Step 1: Setting up the Bayes' Rule Framework**

We want to compute the posterior $p(x_k \mid z_{1:k}, u_{1:k})$. By Bayes' theorem:

$$p(x_k \mid z_k, \hat{x}_k^-) = \frac{p(z_k \mid x_k) \, p(x_k \mid \hat{x}_k^-)}{p(z_k \mid \hat{x}_k^-)}$$

In the Gaussian formulation, let:
- Prior: $p(x_k \mid \hat{x}_k^-) \sim \mathcal{N}(\hat{x}_k^-, P_k^-)$
- Likelihood: $p(z_k \mid x_k) \sim \mathcal{N}(H x_k, R_k)$

The product of two Gaussians is a Gaussian. We seek the posterior in the form $\mathcal{N}(\hat{x}_k, P_k)$.

#### **Step 2: Express the Posterior Mean as a Linear Combination**

Assume the optimal posterior mean is a linear function of the measurement:

$$\hat{x}_k = K'_1 \hat{x}_k^- + K'_2 z_k$$

where $K'_1$ and $K'_2$ are to-be-determined matrices. Substituting $z_k = H x_k + v_k$ (where $v_k$ is the noise):

$$\hat{x}_k = K'_1 \hat{x}_k^- + K'_2 (H x_k + v_k)$$

By the property of MMSE, the expected value of $(\hat{x}_k - x_k)$ must be zero (unbiased estimate). Taking expectations on both sides:

$$E[\hat{x}_k] = K'_1 \hat{x}_k^- + K'_2 H E[x_k]$$

For this to equal $E[x_k]$ for all values, we must have:

$$K'_1 = I - K'_2 H$$

Therefore:

$$\hat{x}_k = (I - K'_2 H) \hat{x}_k^- + K'_2 z_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K'_2 (z_k - H \hat{x}_k^-)$$

Comparing with the standard KF update $\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K_k \nu_k$, we identify:

$$K_k \equiv K'_2$$

#### **Step 3: Minimise the Posterior Covariance**

The posterior covariance is:

$$P_k = E[(x_k - \hat{x}_k)(x_k - \hat{x}_k)^T]$$

Substituting the expression for $\hat{x}_k$:

$$x_k - \hat{x}_k = x_k - \hat{x}_k^- - K_k (H x_k + v_k - H \hat{x}_k^-) = (I - K_k H)(x_k - \hat{x}_k^-) - K_k v_k$$

Let $\epsilon_k = (I - K_k H)(x_k - \hat{x}_k^-) - K_k v_k$. Since the prediction error and measurement noise are independent:

$$P_k = (I - K_k H) P_k^- (I - K_k H)^T + K_k R_k K_k^T$$

Expanding:

$$P_k = P_k^- - K_k H P_k^- - P_k^- H^T K_k^T + K_k H P_k^- H^T K_k^T + K_k R_k K_k^T$$

$$P_k = P_k^- - K_k H P_k^- - P_k^- H^T K_k^T + K_k (H P_k^- H^T + R_k) K_k^T$$

Let $S_k = H P_k^- H^T + R_k$ (innovation covariance). To find the optimal $K_k$, we take the derivative of $\text{tr}(P_k)$ with respect to $K_k$ and set it to zero (matrix differentiation rule: $\frac{\partial}{\partial K} \text{tr}(K B K^T) = 2 K B$ for symmetric $B$):

$$\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(P_k)}{\partial K_k} = -2(H P_k^-)^T + 2 K_k S_k = 0$$

$$2 P_k^- H^T = 2 K_k S_k$$

$$\boxed{K_k = P_k^- H^T S_k^{-1} = P_k^- H^T (H P_k^- H^T + R_k)^{-1}}$$

#### **Step 4: Simplify the Posterior Covariance**

Substitute $K_k$ back into the expression for $P_k$:

$$P_k = P_k^- - P_k^- H^T K_k^T - K_k H P_k^- + K_k (H P_k^- H^T + R_k) K_k^T$$

Note that $K_k (H P_k^- H^T + R_k) = P_k^- H^T$, so the last two terms combine to give $P_k^- H^T K_k^T$. Thus:

$$P_k = P_k^- - P_k^- H^T K_k^T - K_k H P_k^- + P_k^- H^T K_k^T$$

$$P_k = P_k^- - K_k H P_k^-$$

$$\boxed{P_k = (I - K_k H) P_k^-}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — 1D Position Tracking

**Problem Statement:** A robot moves along a 1D line. The motion model is $x_k = x_{k-1} + u_k + w_k$ where $u_k = 1$ m and $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.5^2)$. The robot measures its position via GPS as $z_k = x_k + v_k$ where $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1.0^2)$.

Initialise: $\hat{x}_0 = 0$, $P_0 = 1.0$.

**Predict at $k=1$:**

$$A = 1, \quad B = 1, \quad Q = 0.25, \quad H = 1, \quad R = 1.0$$

$$\hat{x}_1^- = A \hat{x}_0 + B u_1 = 1(0) + 1(1) = 1$$

$$P_1^- = A P_0 A^T + Q = 1(1.0)(1) + 0.25 = 1.25$$

**Update with measurement $z_1 = 1.5$:**

$$S_1 = H P_1^- H^T + R = 1(1.25)(1) + 1.0 = 2.25$$

$$K_1 = P_1^- H^T S_1^{-1} = 1.25 / 2.25 = 0.5556$$

$$\hat{x}_1 = \hat{x}_1^- + K_1(z_1 - H \hat{x}_1^-) = 1 + 0.5556(1.5 - 1) = 1 + 0.2778 = 1.2778$$

$$P_1 = (1 - K_1 H) P_1^- = (1 - 0.5556)(1.25) = 0.5556$$

**Verification:** Notice that $P_1 = 0.5556 < P_0 = 1.0$. The covariance **decreased** after incorporating the measurement, which confirms that information from the sensor has reduced our uncertainty. The Kalman Gain $K_1 \approx 0.56$ tells us that the optimal estimate is **56% GPS-trust** and **44% odometry-trust**.

### 3.3 Derivation of the EKF Jacobians — Worked Example

**Scenario:** A 2D differential-drive robot with state $x_k = [x, y, \theta]^T$. The motion model with control $u_k = [v, \omega]^T$ and time step $\Delta t$ is:

$$x_k = f(x_{k-1}, u_k) = \begin{bmatrix} x_{k-1} + v \Delta t \cos(\theta_{k-1}) \\ y_{k-1} + v \Delta t \sin(\theta_{k-1}) \\ \theta_{k-1} + \omega \Delta t \end{bmatrix}$$

The Jacobian $F_k$ is the matrix of partial derivatives of $f$ with respect to $(x, y, \theta)$:

$$F_k = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial \theta} \\ \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial \theta} \\ \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial \theta} \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing each partial derivative term-by-term:

- $\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial \theta} = -v \Delta t \sin(\theta_{k-1})$
- $\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial \theta} = v \Delta t \cos(\theta_{k-1})$
- $\frac{\partial f_3}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial \theta} = 1$

Therefore:

$$F_k = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v \Delta t \sin(\theta_{k-1}) \\ 0 & 1 & v \Delta t \cos(\theta_{k-1}) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Range-Bearing Observation Model Example:**

The robot observes a landmark at known position $(x_l, y_l)$ in the map. The measurement $z_k = [r, \phi]^T$ is:

$$h(x_k) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_l - x_k)^2 + (y_l - y_k)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(y_l - y_k, x_l - x_k) - \theta_k \end{bmatrix}$$

The Jacobian $H_k$ is:

$$H_k = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{-(x_l - x_k)}{r} & \frac{-(y_l - y_k)}{r} & 0 \\ \frac{(y_l - y_k)}{r^2} & \frac{-(x_l - x_k)}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.4 Convergence and Stability Properties

| Property | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| **Steady-State Covariance** | If $A, H, Q, R$ are constant, $P_k$ converges to a fixed point $P_\infty$ — the Riccati equation steady state. |
| **Riccati Equation** | $P_k = A P_{k-1} A^T + Q - A P_{k-1} H^T (H P_{k-1} H^T + R)^{-1} H P_{k-1} A^T$ |
| **Observability** | The KF can only converge if the system is **observable** (the measurement Jacobian $H$ provides enough information to reconstruct the full state). |
| **Consistency** | A KF is *consistent* if its estimated covariance matches the actual estimation error. Inconsistency arises from linearisation errors in EKF. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Kalman Filter Localisation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Initialise x0 P0] --> B[Time Step k]
    B --> C[Read Control Input uk]
    C --> D[Prediction Step]
    D --> D1[State xk_minus equals f xk_minus_1 uk]
    D --> D2[Covariance Pk_minus equals Fk Pk_minus_1 FkT plus Qk]
    D1 --> E[Obtain Sensor Measurement zk]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Innovation nu equals zk minus h xk_minus]
    F --> G[Compute Kalman Gain Kk]
    G --> G1[Sk equals Hk Pk_minus HkT plus Rk]
    G1 --> G2[Kk equals Pk_minus HkT Sk inverse]
    G2 --> H[Update Step]
    H --> H1[State xk equals xk_minus plus Kk nu]
    H --> H2[Covariance Pk equals I minus Kk Hk times Pk_minus]
    H1 --> I[Publish Pose Estimate]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J{More Time Steps}
    J --> B
    J --> K[END]
```

### 4.2 Map-Based Localisation Architecture with EKF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENS[SENSOR LAYER]
        S1[Wheel Encoders]
        S2[IMU Gyroscope Accelerometer]
        S3[LiDAR Scan]
        S4[GPS Receiver]
    end

    subgraph PROC[PROCESSING LAYER]
        ODOM[Odometry Module]
        SCAN[Scan Matching]
        LAND[Landmark Extraction]
    end

    subgraph EKF[EKF LOCALISATION CORE]
        PRED[Prediction Node]
        UP[Update Node]
        FUSE[Fused Pose Estimate]
    end

    subgraph MAP[MAP LAYER]
        MDB[Known Map Database]
    end

    S1 --> ODOM
    S2 --> ODOM
    S3 --> SCAN
    S3 --> LAND
    S4 --> LAND

    ODOM --> PRED
    PRED --> UP
    SCAN --> UP
    LAND --> UP
    MDB --> LAND
    MDB --> SCAN
    UP --> FUSE
    FUSE --> PRED
```

### 4.3 Uncertainty Evolution in the Kalman Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Gaussian Belief mean x0 cov P0] --> B[Apply Control uk]
    B --> C[Predict Step]
    C --> C1[Mean shifts to xk_minus]
    C --> C2[Ellipse grows Pk_minus larger than Pk_minus_1]
    C1 --> D[Acquire Sensor zk]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Update Step]
    E --> E1[New mean xk lies between prediction and measurement]
    E --> E2[Ellipse shrinks Pk smaller than Pk_minus]
    E1 --> F[Refined Gaussian Belief]
    E2 --> F
```

### 4.4 KF vs EKF vs UKF vs PF — Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Probabilistic Localisation Methods] --> B[Single Hypothesis]
    A --> C[Multi Hypothesis]

    B --> B1[Kalman Filter Linear]
    B --> B2[Extended Kalman Filter EKF]
    B --> B3[Unscented Kalman Filter UKF]

    C --> C1[Particle Filter Monte Carlo]
    C --> C2[Multi Hypothesis Tracker]

    B1 --> B1a[Assumes Linear Models]
    B2 --> B2a[Linearises via Jacobians]
    B3 --> B3a[Sigma Point Sampling]
    C1 --> C1a[Non Parametric Works for Multi Modal Beliefs]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram of the EKF Localisation Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Known Map M] --> B[Observation Model h xk]
    A --> C[Landmark Detection]

    D[Initial Pose x0] --> E[Predict State using f xk_minus_1 uk]
    F[Odometry uk] --> E
    G[Process Noise Qk] --> E

    E --> H[Compute Jacobian Fk]
    H --> I[Predict Covariance Pk_minus equals Fk Pk_minus_1 FkT plus Qk]
    I --> J[Predict Measurement h xk_minus]

    C --> K[Sensor Reading zk]
    J --> L[Innovation nu equals zk minus h xk_minus]
    K --> L

    L --> M[Compute Jacobian Hk]
    M --> N[Innovation Covariance Sk equals Hk Pk_minus HkT plus Rk]
    N --> O[Kalman Gain Kk equals Pk_minus HkT Sk inverse]

    O --> P[Update State xk equals xk_minus plus Kk nu]
    O --> Q[Update Covariance Pk equals I minus Kk Hk times Pk_minus]

    P --> R[Output Pose Estimate]
    Q --> R
    R --> E
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the assumptions of the Kalman Filter algorithm. Why is the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) used in practical robot localisation problems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Kalman Filter operates under the following assumptions:
1. **Linearity:** The system (motion and observation) models must be linear functions of the state and inputs.
2. **Gaussian Noise:** Both process noise $w_k$ and measurement noise $v_k$ are zero-mean, white, and Gaussian with known covariances $Q_k$ and $R_k$.
3. **Initial Belief is Gaussian:** The initial state $x_0$ is assumed to be Gaussian-distributed.
4. **Noise Independence:** Process and measurement noise sequences are mutually independent and uncorrelated over time.

**Why EKF is used (2 marks for this part):**
In real robot localisation, motion models (e.g., involving $\sin$ and $\cos$ of heading) and observation models (e.g., range-bearing to landmarks) are **non-linear**. Since the standard KF requires linearity, we use the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, which linearises these non-linear functions around the current state estimate using first-order **Taylor series expansion**, computing the **Jacobian matrices** $F_k$ and $H_k$. The EKF provides a computationally tractable approximation that is accurate when the linearisation errors are small (i.e., uncertainty in the state is small compared to the scale of non-linearity).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "Innovation" and "Kalman Gain" in the context of a Kalman filter. Explain how the value of Kalman Gain affects the update step.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Innovation ($\nu_k$):** The innovation is the difference between the actual sensor measurement $z_k$ and the predicted measurement $H \hat{x}_k^-$ based on the prior estimate. Mathematically:
$$\nu_k = z_k - H \hat{x}_k^-$$
It represents the "new information" brought in by the sensor that was not predicted by the model.

**Kalman Gain ($K_k$):** The Kalman Gain is the optimal weighting factor that determines how much the innovation should correct the state estimate:
$$K_k = P_k^- H^T (H P_k^- H^T + R_k)^{-1}$$

**Effect of $K_k$ on the update step (2 marks for this part):**
- When the **measurement noise $R_k$ is small** (sensor is highly accurate), $K_k$ becomes large, and the filter **trusts the measurement more** than the prediction.
- When the **prior covariance $P_k^-$ is small** (prediction is very confident), $K_k$ becomes small, and the filter **ignores the measurement** in favour of the prediction.
- The Kalman Gain automatically balances the relative trustworthiness of the model prediction and the sensor measurement, optimising the posterior covariance $P_k$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the Kalman Filter equations for state prediction and measurement update from the Bayesian estimation framework. Clearly define all variables and show the algebraic steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 1D robot tracking problem with the following parameters:
- Motion model: $x_k = x_{k-1} + 1.0 + w_k$, where $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.4^2)$
- Observation model: $z_k = x_k + v_k$, where $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.6^2)$
- Initial estimate: $\hat{x}_0 = 0$, $P_0 = 2.0$
- First measurement: $z_1 = 1.8$

Compute the posterior state estimate $\hat{x}_1$ and covariance $P_1$ after one complete KF cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Bayes' Framework Setup (1.5 marks)**

We model the posterior as a Gaussian and assume the optimal estimate is a linear blend of the prior and measurement:
$$\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K_k(z_k - H\hat{x}_k^-)$$

**Step 2: Posterior Covariance Expression (1.5 marks)**

$$P_k = (I - K_k H) P_k^- (I - K_k H)^T + K_k R_k K_k^T$$

Expanding and using $S_k = H P_k^- H^T + R_k$:

$$P_k = P_k^- - K_k H P_k^- - P_k^- H^T K_k^T + K_k S_k K_k^T$$

**Step 3: Minimisation via Matrix Calculus (2 marks)**

Taking $\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(P_k)}{\partial K_k} = 0$:

$$-2 P_k^- H^T + 2 K_k S_k = 0$$

$$\boxed{K_k = P_k^- H^T (H P_k^- H^T + R_k)^{-1}}$$

**Step 4: Substituting Back and Final Update (2 marks)**

$$\boxed{P_k = (I - K_k H) P_k^-}$$

And the state update is:

$$\boxed{\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_k^-)}$$

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify Parameters (1 mark)**

$A = 1$, $B = 1$, $u_1 = 1.0$, $H = 1$, $Q = 0.16$, $R = 0.36$

**Step 2: State Prediction (2 marks)**

$$\hat{x}_1^- = A \hat{x}_0 + B u_1 = 1(0) + 1(1.0) = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

$$P_1^- = A P_0 A^T + Q = 1(2.0)(1) + 0.16 = 2.16 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute Kalman Gain (2 marks)**

$$S_1 = H P_1^- H^T + R = 1(2.16)(1) + 0.36 = 2.52$$

$$K_1 = P_1^- H^T S_1^{-1} = 2.16 / 2.52 = 0.8571$$

**Step 4: Measurement Update (2 marks)**

Innovation: $\nu_1 = z_1 - H \hat{x}_1^- = 1.8 - 1.0 = 0.8$

$$\hat{x}_1 = \hat{x}_1^- + K_1 \nu_1 = 1.0 + 0.8571 \times 0.8 = 1.0 + 0.6857 = 1.6857 \text{ m}$$

$$P_1 = (1 - K_1 H) P_1^- = (1 - 0.8571)(2.16) = 0.3086 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $\hat{x}_1 = 1.6857$ m, $P_1 = 0.3086$ m². The covariance dropped from $2.16$ to $0.3086$, confirming the measurement reduced uncertainty.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for a non-linear robot localisation problem. Derive the Jacobians for the 2D differential drive robot motion model with state $x = [x, y, \theta]^T$ and control input $u = [v, \omega]^T$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the advantages and limitations of the Kalman Filter family (KF/EKF) for map-based robot localisation. Compare it briefly with the Particle Filter approach. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — EKF Structure and Jacobians (7 marks)**

**EKF Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**

For a non-linear system with $x_k = f(x_{k-1}, u_k) + w_k$ and $z_k = h(x_k) + v_k$:

1. **Compute State Transition Jacobian:**
$$F_k = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \bigg\vert_{\hat{x}_{k-1}}$$

2. **Predict State and Covariance:**
$$\hat{x}_k^- = f(\hat{x}_{k-1}, u_k)$$
$$P_k^- = F_k P_{k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$$

3. **Compute Observation Jacobian:**
$$H_k = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \bigg\vert_{\hat{x}_k^-}$$

4. **Update with Measurement:**
$$K_k = P_k^- H_k^T (H_k P_k^- H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$$
$$\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K_k(z_k - h(\hat{x}_k^-))$$
$$P_k = (I - K_k H_k) P_k^-$$

**Jacobian Derivation for 2D Differential Drive (4 marks):**

The non-linear motion model is:

$$f(x_{k-1}, u_k) = \begin{bmatrix} x_{k-1} + v \Delta t \cos(\theta_{k-1}) \\ y_{k-1} + v \Delta t \sin(\theta_{k-1}) \\ \theta_{k-1} + \omega \Delta t \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing the partial derivatives of each row of $f$ with respect to $(x, y, \theta)$:

- Row 1: $\left[1, \ 0, \ -v\Delta t \sin(\theta_{k-1})\right]$
- Row 2: $\left[0, \ 1, \ v\Delta t \cos(\theta_{k-1})\right]$
- Row 3: $\left[0, \ 0, \ 1\right]$

$$\boxed{F_k = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v \Delta t \sin(\theta_{k-1}) \\ 0 & 1 & v \Delta t \cos(\theta_{k-1}) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

This matrix is evaluated at the current best estimate $\hat{x}_{k-1} = [\hat{x}, \hat{y}, \hat{\theta}]^T$ and used to propagate covariance in the EKF prediction step.

#### **Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, and Comparison (7 marks)**

**Advantages of KF/EKF (3 marks):**

1. **Optimal under assumptions:** The KF is the *minimum variance unbiased estimator* when the linearity and Gaussian assumptions hold exactly.
2. **Computationally efficient:** $O(n^3)$ per cycle with $n$ = state dimension. Suitable for real-time embedded systems.
3. **Closed-form recursion:** No iterative optimisation or sampling required.
4. **Uncertainty quantification:** The covariance matrix $P_k$ provides a rigorous, well-defined measure of belief uncertainty.
5. **Mature industrial use:** Widely deployed in aerospace, automotive, and robotics (e.g., PX4, ROS, GPS-INS fusion).

**Limitations of KF/EKF (2 marks):**

1. **Gaussian unimodal only:** Cannot represent multi-modal beliefs, which are common in global localisation (e.g., robot in a symmetric corridor).
2. **Linearisation errors in EKF:** First-order Taylor expansion can produce biased estimates, especially under high uncertainty.
3. **Jacobian computation:** Closed-form Jacobians are required; unavailable or complex for some sensor models (e.g., neural network-based perception).
4. **Sensitivity to tuning:** Performance depends heavily on accurate $Q_k$ and $R_k$ covariance values.

**Comparison with Particle Filter (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Kalman Filter / EKF | Particle Filter |
|---|---|---|
| **Belief representation** | Unimodal Gaussian | Multi-modal sample set |
| **Computational cost** | $O(n^3)$, very fast | $O(M)$ where $M$ = number of particles (e.g., $1000$s) |
| **Handles non-linearity** | Approximate (linearisation) | Exact for any non-linearity |
| **Global localisation** | Poor (needs good initial guess) | Excellent (works without prior) |
| **Multi-modal beliefs** | No | Yes |
| **Typical use** | Position tracking | Global localisation, kidnapped robot |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add process noise $Q_k$** in the covariance prediction step ($P_k^- = AP_{k-1}A^T + Q_k$). The most common error in the exam.
> 2. **Confusing innovation with measurement error:** Innovation is $z_k - H\hat{x}_k^-$, *not* $z_k - \hat{x}_k^-$.
> 3. **In EKF problems, students often forget to linearise** the observation model and incorrectly use $H$ as a constant when it should be the Jacobian evaluated at the current state.
> 4. **Algebra mistakes in Jacobian computation:** When computing $\partial \theta / \partial t = \omega$, students sometimes incorrectly include the rotation matrix terms.
> 5. **Not interpreting the result:** Examiners award full marks only when the student explains the *meaning* of the result (e.g., "covariance decreased, so uncertainty reduced").
> 6. **Dimension errors:** State vectors must have consistent units (e.g., position in metres, angle in radians, time in seconds).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- [x] The Kalman Filter is a **recursive Bayesian estimator** that maintains a Gaussian belief over the robot pose.
- [x] Two-step cycle: **Predict** (using motion model) → **Update** (using measurement model).
- [x] State prediction: $\hat{x}_k^- = A\hat{x}_{k-1} + Bu_k$ and $P_k^- = AP_{k-1}A^T + Q_k$.
- [x] Kalman Gain: $K_k = P_k^- H^T (H P_k^- H^T + R_k)^{-1}$.
- [x] State update: $\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K_k(z_k - H\hat{x}_k^-)$ and $P_k = (I - K_k H) P_k^-$.
- [x] EKF extends KF to non-linear systems by **linearising via Jacobians** $F_k = \partial f / \partial x$ and $H_k = \partial h / \partial x$.
- [x] Covariance always **grows during prediction** (uncertainty increases) and **shrinks during update** (information from sensor).
- [x] Higher $K_k$ → trust sensor more; lower $K_k$ → trust prediction more.
- [x] The standard KF is **optimal for linear-Gaussian systems**; the EKF is a **first-order approximation** for non-linear systems.
- [x] KF family is best for **position tracking** (known initial pose). For **global localisation**, particle filters are preferred.
- [x] **Joseph form** of covariance update is numerically stable: $P_k = (I - K_k H) P_k^- (I - K_k H)^T + K_k R_k K_k^T$.
- [x] The Kalman Filter is **not suitable** for multi-modal beliefs, kidnapped robot problems, or symmetric environment ambiguities.
- [x] Real-world applications: **GPS-INS fusion, drone navigation, autonomous vehicle localisation, AUV tracking, spacecraft state estimation**.
- [x] In KTU numerical problems, **always show the units** of intermediate values (e.g., position in metres, covariance in m²).
- [x] When asked to "derive", include all matrix calculus steps (taking the derivative of $\text{tr}(P_k)$ w.r.t. $K_k$).
- [x] The state vector for 2D mobile robots is typically $x = [x, y, \theta, \dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$ — a 6-dimensional state for full planar motion.

**End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Probabilistic Map-Based Localisation: Kalman Method**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous Map Building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Autonomous Map Building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Autonomous Map Building** is the computational process by which a mobile robot constructs a spatial representation of an unknown environment while simultaneously estimating its own pose (position and orientation) within that environment, using only onboard sensor measurements and control inputs. In the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy, this is universally categorized under the umbrella discipline of **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** — often referred to in literature as the *"Chicken and Egg" problem of mobile robotics*, since a robot needs a map to localize itself, yet it needs to know its pose to build a consistent map.

The formal probabilistic statement of the problem can be expressed as: given a sequence of control inputs $u_{1:t}$ and corresponding sensor observations $z_{1:t}$, compute the joint posterior probability distribution over both the robot trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and the map $m$:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where $x_t$ is the robot pose at time $t$, $m$ is the map, $z_t$ is the observation, and $u_t$ is the odometry/control input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Autonomous map building is the capstone application of probabilistic robotics, integrating Bayesian estimation, sensor fusion, and computational geometry. Students must master the distinction between **mapping with known poses** and the full **SLAM problem**.

## 1.2 Types of Maps Built Autonomously

A mobile robot can construct three principal categories of maps. The choice is dictated by the environment, sensor suite, and task semantics.

| Map Type | Representation | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Metric – Occupancy Grid** | 2D/3D grid of cell-wise occupancy probabilities | Indoor structured environments |
| **Metric – Feature Map** | Collection of geometric primitives (points, lines, planes) | Sparse, structured environments (corners, walls) |
| **Topological Map** | Graph of nodes (places) and edges (paths) | Large-scale navigation, semantic reasoning |
| **Hybrid Map** | Combination of metric + topological layers | Real-world heterogeneous environments |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lighthouse Keeper's Dilemma"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine you are a lighthouse keeper stationed in a completely dark, fog-shrouded coastline. You have a small flashlight, a pedometer, and a notebook. You want to draw a map of all the dangerous rocks and safe channels around you — but you don't even know your own position relative to the coast!
>
> - Each step you take is like the robot's **odometry** $u_t$ (noisy but incrementally informative).
> - The flashlight beam reveals snippets of rocks — these are the **observations** $z_t$.
> - Your notebook is the **map** $m$ being built cell-by-cell.
> - Your mental estimate of *"where you currently stand"* is the robot **pose** $x_t$.
>
> The "Autonomous Map Building" algorithm is the *intelligent procedure* that updates both your location estimate AND your notebook every time you step or shine the light — making the best of imperfect sensors and drifting steps. This is exactly what a SLAM algorithm does mathematically!

## 1.4 The SLAM Pipeline (High-Level)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every autonomous map-building system executes a four-stage cognitive loop:
> 1. **Perception** — Acquire raw sensor data (lidar, camera, sonar).
> 2. **Prediction** — Propagate pose estimate using motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$.
> 3. **Observation Update** — Correct pose and map using sensor model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$.
> 4. **Map Update** — Integrate new information into the global map representation.

A key constant used in laser-based occupancy grid mapping is the inverse sensor model resolution. The **standard grid resolution** is typically **0.05 m to 0.5 m per cell**, and the **log-odds update constant** $l_{occ} - l_{free}$ is empirically tuned (Moravec & Elfes, 1985).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Foundation

Autonomous map building is mathematically grounded in **Bayesian inference**. The robot maintains a belief — a probability distribution — about its environment, and refines this belief as new data arrives.

### 2.1.1 Bayes Filter for Mapping (Known Pose Assumption)

When the robot's pose $x_t$ is assumed known (the simpler pedagogical case taught in KTU Module 3), the map posterior factors into independent cell-wise estimates. For each map cell $m_i$:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

### 2.1.2 Why is the "Known Pose" case a stepping stone?

> [!NOTE]
> In real deployments, the pose is **never exactly known** due to wheel slip, encoder quantization, and drift. KTU Module 3 first teaches students the *mapping-only* problem so the measurement update mathematics is cleanly separated from the *localization* complexity. Then it is built up to the full SLAM posterior:
> $$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

## 2.2 Occupancy Grid Mapping — The Elfes-Moravec Model

The **occupancy grid map** (Elfes, 1985; Moravec, 1988) discretizes the environment into a fine grid of cells. Each cell $m_i$ stores a binary random variable representing *"occupied"* or *"free"*. The estimation is done in the **log-odds** representation for numerical stability and incremental efficiency.

### 2.2.1 The Log-Odds Representation

Define the **odds** of a cell being occupied:

$$\text{odds}(m_i) = \frac{p(m_i = \text{occupied})}{p(m_i = \text{free})} = \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$$

The **log-odds** (logit) representation:

$$l_i = \log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why log-odds?** It converts the multiplicative Bayesian update into a simple **addition**, which is computationally cheap and numerically stable (avoids probabilities collapsing to 0 or 1).

### 2.2.2 Recursive Log-Odds Update Rule

$$l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - l_0$$

where $l_0$ is the prior log-odds (zero for a uniform prior of 0.5).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Where Used in KTU Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Full SLAM posterior | Definition / short answer |
| 2 | $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) \propto p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$ | Cell-wise Bayes update | Occupancy grid mapping derivations |
| 3 | $l_i = \log \dfrac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$ | Log-odds definition | Numerical mapping problems |
| 4 | $l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \log \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - l_0$ | Recursive log-odds update | Core mapping update equation |
| 5 | $p(m_i) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp(l_i)}$ | Inverse logit (recovering probability) | Post-processing the map |
| 6 | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_i p(z_t^i \mid x_t, m_i)$ | Field-of-view independence assumption | Justification for grid factorization |
| 7 | $x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$ | Motion model with process noise $w_t$ | EKF-SLAM prediction step |
| 8 | $z_t = h(x_t, m) + v_t$ | Observation model with measurement noise $v_t$ | EKF-SLAM update step |
| 9 | $\mu_t = \mu_{t-1} + K_t(z_t - h(\mu_{t-1}))$ | Extended Kalman Filter update | SLAM state correction |
| 10 | $K_t = \Sigma_{t-1} H^T (H \Sigma_{t-1} H^T + Q)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | EKF-SLAM numerical solution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU Numericals:** The inverse sensor model values $p(m_i = \text{occupied} \mid z_t, x_t)$ for a typical 2D laser ranger are commonly **0.7 (occupied)** and **0.4 (free)** in textbook problems. These give $l_{occ} \approx +0.847$ and $l_{free} \approx -0.405$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Autonomous map building underpins every commercial autonomous system. Specific applications include:

- **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs):** Self-driving cars (Waymo, Tesla) build high-definition (HD) maps in real time using lidar + camera fusion.
- **Warehouse Robotics:** Amazon Kiva robots map shelves dynamically as goods are rearranged.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Create bathymetric maps of uncharted seabed.
- **Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance, Curiosity):** Map the Martian terrain autonomously to plan safe paths.
- **Domestic Vacuum Robots (Roomba):** Use 2D occupancy grids to clean rooms efficiently.

> [!TIP]
> The IEEE survey of Cadena et al. (2016) — *"Past, Present, and Future of Simultaneous Localization and Mapping"* — is the standard reference text that aligns with the KTU 2024 Module 3 treatment of the topic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes Rule to the Log-Odds Update

This derivation is essential for the 14-mark KTU questions. We will derive the recursive log-odds update formula from first principles.

### Step 1 — Start with Bayes Rule for a Single Cell

For an individual map cell $m_i$, applying Bayes' theorem under the known-pose assumption:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) &= \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t, z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** We factor the joint posterior $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ into the likelihood of the *new* measurement $z_t$ given $m_i$ and the previous posterior, normalized by the evidence $p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$.

### Step 2 — Apply Conditional Independence (Markov Assumption)

Since $m_i$ is static and $z_t$ depends on $m_i$ only through the current pose $x_t$ (Markov property), the likelihood simplifies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t, z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) = p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The new measurement $z_t$ is conditionally independent of all past measurements and past poses, *given* the current cell state $m_i$ and current pose $x_t$. This is the cornerstone assumption that makes recursive estimation tractable.

### Step 3 — Express Everything in Odds Form

Divide both sides of the Bayes update by $1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} &= \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)} \cdot \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** We use the algebraic identity $\dfrac{a}{1-a} \cdot \dfrac{1-b}{1-a-b} = \dots$ carefully. The normalization constant $p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$ cancels out because it is the same for both $m_i = \text{occupied}$ and $m_i = \text{free}$ — it is a *scaling factor* common to all cells in the field of view.

### Step 4 — Take the Logarithm (Log-Odds)

Apply $\log(\cdot)$ to both sides and let $l_{t,i} = \log \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_{t,i} &= l_{t-1,i} + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This is the **additive log-odds update**. Each new measurement simply *adds* its inverse sensor model log-odds to the existing estimate. No normalization, no probability collapse, no expensive multiplication.

### Step 5 — Account for Prior (for Completeness)

If the prior $l_0 = \log \dfrac{p(m_i)}{1-p(m_i)}$ is non-uniform, we subtract it from the first update:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_{t,i} &= l_{t-1,i} + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - l_0
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **final KTU-exam-ready formula**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Log-Odds Occupancy Update)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Numerical:** A mobile robot with a 2D laser scans a map cell at position $m_5$. The inverse sensor model returns $p(m_5 = \text{occupied} \mid z, x) = 0.7$. The previous log-odds value is $l_{4,5} = 0.5$. The prior $l_0 = 0$ (uniform). Compute the new log-odds and recover the probability.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_{5,5} &= l_{4,5} + \log \frac{p(m_5 = \text{occ} \mid z, x)}{1 - p(m_5 = \text{occ} \mid z, x)} - l_0 \\
&= 0.5 + \log \frac{0.7}{1 - 0.7} - 0 \\
&= 0.5 + \log \frac{0.7}{0.3} \\
&= 0.5 + \log(2.3333) \\
&= 0.5 + 0.8473 \\
&= 1.3473
\end{aligned}
$$

Recover the probability using the inverse logit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(m_5) &= \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-l_{5,5})} = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-1.3473)} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.2601} \approx 0.7936
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The cell's probability of being occupied increased from $\approx 0.622$ to $\approx 0.794$ after the new observation — consistent with the sensor detecting an obstacle. ✓

## 3.3 EKF-SLAM Linearization (Key Steps)

For the full SLAM problem (unknown pose), the Extended Kalman Filter linearizes the nonlinear motion and observation models around the current estimate using first-order Taylor expansion.

**Motion update** (predict):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\mu}_t &= \mu_{t-1} + \Delta_t \\
\bar{\Sigma}_t &= F_x \Sigma_{t-1} F_x^T + F_u U_t F_u^T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Measurement update** (correct):

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t H^T (H \bar{\Sigma}_t H^T + Q)^{-1} \\
\mu_t &= \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)) \\
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t H) \bar{\Sigma}_t
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Summary:**
> - $F_x, F_u$ = Jacobians of motion model w.r.t. state and control.
> - $H$ = Jacobian of observation model w.r.t. state.
> - $Q$ = measurement noise covariance.
> - $K_t$ = Kalman gain — the optimal weighting between prediction and observation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** EKF-SLAM scales as $O(N^2)$ in the number of landmarks, which is why modern systems use sparse methods (iVar, SAM) or particle filters (FastSLAM) — a likely viva question.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Algorithm | Map Type | Pose Estimation | Computational Cost | Loop Closure Robustness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Occupancy Grid (known pose) | Metric grid | Assumed known | $O(N)$ per scan | N/A |
| EKF-SLAM | Feature (points) | Online EKF | $O(N^2)$ | Weak (linearization) |
| FastSLAM | Feature | Particle filter | $O(M \log N)$ | Strong (Rao-Blackwellized) |
| Graph-SLAM | Feature/Grid | Pose graph optimization | Offline bundle adjustment | Excellent |
| Topological SLAM | Graph of places | Hybrid | Low | High (semantic) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Autonomous Map Building Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Robot Mission Start]) --> sensAcquire[Sensor Data Acquisition - Lidar or Camera or Sonar]
    sensAcquire --> odomRead[Odometry Encoder Reading u_t]
    odomRead --> motionModel[Motion Model Prediction - p of x_t given x_t-1 and u_t]
    motionModel --> poseEst[Current Pose Estimate x_hat_t]
    sensAcquire --> obsModel[Inverse Sensor Model - p of m_i given z_t and x_t]
    obsModel --> logOdds[Log-Odds Update Module - l_t_i = l_t-1_i plus delta]
    logOdds --> gridMap[Occupancy Grid Map - 2D Grid of Cells]
    poseEst --> dataAssoc[Data Association - Match Observations to Landmarks]
    dataAssoc --> ekfUpdate[EKF State Update - Kalman Gain K_t]
    ekfUpdate --> poseEst
    gridMap --> loopClose[Loop Closure Detection - Scan Matching or Place Recognition]
    loopClose --> globalOpt[Global Map Optimization - Graph SLAM or Pose Graph]
    globalOpt --> gridMap
    gridMap --> output([Final Consistent Map])
    output --> stop([Mission End])
    style start fill:#e1f5e1
    style stop fill:#ffe1e1
    style gridMap fill:#e1e9ff
    style poseEst fill:#fff4e1
```

## 4.2 Bayesian Update Cycle for Mapping (Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prior Belief about Map m_t-1] --> B[New Sensor Measurement z_t]
    B --> C[Inverse Sensor Model - Compute p of m given z_t x_t]
    C --> D[Bayesian Multiplication - p of m given z_t x_t times prior]
    D --> E[Normalization - Divide by Evidence p of z_t]
    E --> F[Posterior Map Belief m_t]
    F --> G{Threshold Check - Is Cell Occupied?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Mark Cell as Occupied - p greater than 0.5]
    G -- No --> I[Mark Cell as Free - p less than 0.5]
    H --> J[Updated Occupancy Grid]
    I --> J
    style A fill:#fffacd
    style F fill:#90ee90
    style J fill:#add8e6
```

## 4.3 Algorithm Block — Log-Odds Occupancy Grid Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    init[Initialize Grid - All Cells l_0 = 0 Prior 0.5] --> loopStart{For Each New Scan z_t}
    loopStart --> poseGet[Get Current Pose x_t from Localization]
    poseGet --> rayCast[For Each Cell in Sensor Footprint - Ray Casting]
    rayCast --> cellCheck{Is Cell Along Beam Path?}
    cellCheck -- Yes along beam --> updateFree[Apply l_free - Subtract Inverse Free Log-Odds]
    cellCheck -- No beam end --> updateOcc[Apply l_occ - Add Inverse Occupied Log-Odds]
    cellCheck -- Outside footprint --> skip[Skip Cell - No Update]
    updateFree --> clamp[Clamp Values - l_min to l_max Range]
    updateOcc --> clamp
    skip --> loopStart
    clamp --> loopStart
    loopStart -- End of scan --> publish[Publish Updated Grid - Recompute p from l]
    publish --> loopStart
    style init fill:#ffe4b5
    style publish fill:#98fb98
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading these diagrams in KTU Exam:** If asked to draw a block diagram of the SLAM process, the first diagram above is the most comprehensive. Use clear rectangular blocks, single-headed arrows, and label all mathematical inputs (e.g., $u_t$, $z_t$, $m$) to score full marks on the diagram question.
