# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the autonomous map building problem. What is meant by the "Chicken and Egg" dilemma in SLAM?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Autonomous map building is the process by which a mobile robot creates a spatial representation of an unknown environment using onboard sensors. The *"Chicken and Egg"* dilemma refers to the circular dependency: **a robot needs a map to know where it is (localize)**, but it **needs to know its location to build a consistent map**. This chicken-and-egg situation is resolved probabilistically through the **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** framework, which jointly estimates both the map $m$ and the robot trajectory $x_{1:t}$ as a single posterior $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Compare metric occupancy grid maps and topological maps. List one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Occupancy Grid Map:** Discretizes the environment into a 2D/3D grid of cells, each storing a probability of occupancy. *Advantage:* Preserves fine geometric detail, ideal for collision avoidance and path planning in local regions.
- **Topological Map:** Represents the environment as a graph of distinct places (nodes) connected by navigable paths (edges). *Advantage:* Highly compact and scalable, suitable for large-scale navigation and symbolic reasoning.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice 1]`

**(a) Derive the recursive log-odds update rule for occupancy grid mapping starting from Bayes' theorem. Assume known robot poses. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bayes' Rule Application** (2 marks):
For a single cell $m_i$, applying Bayes' theorem with the known-pose assumption $x_{1:t}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Odds Form Transformation** (2 marks):
Dividing numerator and denominator to obtain the odds ratio, the evidence $p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$ cancels because it is identical for $m_i = \text{occupied}$ and $m_i = \text{free}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)} \cdot \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Log-Odds Definition** (1 mark):
Define $l_{t,i} = \log \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}$ and apply $\log$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Prior Subtraction** (2 marks):
To account for non-uniform prior $l_0$, we subtract it from the first update, yielding the final KTU formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - l_0
\end{aligned}
$$

> Each new measurement **additively** updates the log-odds, giving numerical stability and $O(1)$ computational cost per cell.

---

**(b) A robot scans a cell three times. The inverse sensor model returns occupancy probabilities of 0.7, 0.6, and 0.7. Prior log-odds is zero. Compute the final occupancy probability. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Log-Odds of Each Measurement** (3 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_1 &= \log \frac{0.7}{0.3} = \log(2.333) = 0.8473 \\
l_2 &= \log \frac{0.6}{0.4} = \log(1.500) = 0.4055 \\
l_3 &= \log \frac{0.7}{0.3} = \log(2.333) = 0.8473
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum Updates (Prior is 0)** (1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_{final} = 0 + 0.8473 + 0.4055 + 0.8473 = 2.1001
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Logit** (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(m_i) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-2.1001)} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1225} = \frac{1}{1.1225} \approx 0.8909
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation** (1 mark):
After three consistent "occupied" detections, the cell's probability of being occupied is $\approx 89.09\%$, strongly indicating a real obstacle. The log-odds accumulation efficiently reinforces repeated evidence.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice 2]`

**(a) Explain the Extended Kalman Filter SLAM algorithm. State the prediction and update equations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Problem Statement** (1 mark):
EKF-SLAM represents the map as a collection of point landmarks and estimates the joint state vector $\mu_t = [x_t, l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_N]^T$ with covariance $\Sigma_t$.

**Step 2 — Prediction Step** (2 marks):
Using the nonlinear motion model $x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$, linearize via Jacobians $F_x = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$, $F_u = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial u}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\mu}_t &= \mu_{t-1} + f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) \\
\bar{\Sigma}_t &= F_x \Sigma_{t-1} F_x^T + F_u U_t F_u^T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Update Step** (3 marks):
Using observation model $z_t = h(x_t, m) + v_t$ with Jacobian $H = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ and noise $Q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t H^T (H \bar{\Sigma}_t H^T + Q)^{-1} \\
\mu_t &= \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)) \\
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t H) \bar{\Sigma}_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Limitations** (1 mark):
EKF-SLAM scales as $O(N^2)$ and is sensitive to data association errors. Modern systems use FastSLAM or Graph-SLAM.

---

**(b) Describe the three main types of maps in mobile robotics with suitable applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Metric Feature Maps** (2 marks):
Store geometric primitives (points, lines). Best for environments with distinguishable features (corners, edges). Used in **landmark-based navigation** in structured indoor spaces.

**2. Occupancy Grid Maps** (2.5 marks):
Divide the environment into a 2D grid where each cell holds an occupancy probability $p(m_i) \in [0, 1]$. Best for **dense sensor data** (laser, depth cameras) and **collision avoidance**. Widely used in **autonomous vacuum robots** and **warehouse AGVs**.

**3. Topological Maps** (2.5 marks):
Represent the environment as a graph: nodes are distinctive places, edges are connectivity relations. Best for **large-scale semantic navigation** where geometric detail is secondary. Used in **multi-floor building navigation** and **route planning in GPS-denied environments**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the log-odds inversion step** when computing final probabilities from accumulated $l$ values — worth 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not stating the Markov/conditional independence assumption** explicitly in derivations — KTU examiners *will* deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing the inverse sensor model** $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ with the forward sensor model $p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)$ — these are different.
> 4. **Skipping the prior term** $l_0$ when the question says "non-uniform prior" — full marks lost.
> 5. **Mixing up $x_{1:t}$ and $x_t$** in the posterior notation — examiners are strict on notation.
> 6. **Ignoring normalization constants** in Bayes' derivation — explicitly showing the cancellation earns the full 2 marks in part (a).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Autonomous Map Building:**
> - ✓ Autonomous map building = constructing a spatial model of an unknown environment from sensor data, with or without pose knowledge.
> - ✓ The full SLAM posterior is $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
> - ✓ Three map types: **Occupancy Grid (metric)**, **Feature Map (metric)**, **Topological Map (graph)**.
> - ✓ Bayes Rule is the engine of all recursive map updates.
> - ✓ Log-odds representation: $l_i = \log \dfrac{p(m_i)}{1-p(m_i)}$ — convert multiplication into addition.
> - ✓ Recursive update: $l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \log \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1-p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - l_0$.
> - ✓ Inverse logit: $p(m_i) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp(-l_i)}$.
> - ✓ EKF-SLAM: predict with motion model, update with measurement model using Kalman gain $K_t$.
> - ✓ FastSLAM scales as $O(M \log N)$ — uses particle filters + EKF.
> - ✓ Graph-SLAM optimizes the full pose graph offline for loop closure.
> - ✓ Real-world systems: AVs, Roomba, warehouse robots, Mars rovers, AUVs.
> - ✓ Standard inverse sensor model values: $p_{occ} = 0.7$, $p_{free} = 0.4$ (for typical 2D laser rangers).
> - ✓ Standard grid resolution: **0.05 m to 0.5 m per cell**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is the computational problem faced by an autonomous mobile robot (or agent) of constructing or incrementally updating a spatial map of an unknown environment while simultaneously estimating its own pose (position and orientation) within that very same map, using only onboard sensor measurements and control inputs.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, SLAM is formally defined as the recursive estimation of the **joint posterior probability density** over the robot's trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and the map $m$, conditioned on the entire history of sensor observations $z_{1:t}$ and control actions $u_{1:t}$. This is mathematically abstracted as:

$$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

Where:
- $x_{1:t} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_t\}$ denotes the sequence of robot poses up to time $t$
- $m$ denotes the map of the environment (collection of landmark locations)
- $z_{1:t}$ denotes the sequence of sensor observations
- $u_{1:t}$ denotes the sequence of control commands (odometry)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Blind Explorer" Analogy:** Imagine being blindfolded and dropped into the basement of an unknown building. You are given a walking stick (sensor) and a pedometer (odometry). As you walk, you tap the stick against walls, feel corners, and slowly sketch a mental map on a notepad. However, every step you take introduces uncertainty about how far you've actually walked. Without an accurate map, you cannot know if a turn you just made was 90° or 75°. Without knowing your location, you cannot place the wall you just felt on the correct coordinate of your sketch. **This chicken-and-egg loop is exactly the SLAM problem.**

- **Localization alone** = Knowing the map but figuring out where you are.
- **Mapping alone** = Knowing where you are but trying to draw the map.
- **SLAM** = Doing both at the same time, where each task helps the other.

### 1.3 The "SLAM Problem" – Why is it Hard?

The fundamental difficulty stems from three intertwined uncertainties:

| Source of Uncertainty | Description |
|---|---|
| **Sensor Noise** | Imperfections in LiDAR, cameras, IMUs producing erroneous readings |
| **Actuator Noise** | Wheel slippage and quantization in odometry leading to dead-reckoning drift |
| **Data Association Ambiguity** | The robot cannot reliably tell whether the corner it sees now is the *same* corner it saw 10 seconds ago |

The environment typically imposes a **0.05 m – 0.20 m** landmark observation noise for indoor LiDARs and roughly **1°–3°** angular noise, which when integrated over hundreds of meters causes a robot to be meters off without SLAM's correction loop.

### 1.4 SLAM in the KTU Syllabus Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 Focus:** Under the Outcome-Based Education framework, SLAM is mapped to **CO3 (Apply perception and navigation algorithms for autonomous mobile platforms)**. Questions typically test the student's ability to formulate the SLAM posterior, identify the role of Extended Kalman Filters (EKF) or Particle Filters, and explain data association.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Probabilistic Foundation of SLAM

The SLAM problem is rooted in **Bayesian inference**. The robot must maintain a belief over the state of the world given all available evidence. The complete belief is factorized into three core sub-problems:

#### 2.1.1 The Motion Model (Prediction Step)

Given the previous pose $x_{t-1}$ and the current control input $u_t$, the robot predicts its new pose. This is governed by a probabilistic motion model:

$$P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

For a differential drive robot moving with linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ over time step $\Delta t$:

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + \begin{bmatrix} v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega \Delta t}{2}) \\ v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega \Delta t}{2}) \\ \omega \cdot \Delta t \end{bmatrix} + \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

The additive Gaussian noise $\mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ models process uncertainty.

#### 2.1.2 The Observation Model (Measurement Update Step)

When the robot observes a landmark $m_i$ from pose $x_t$, it receives a measurement $z_t$ governed by:

$$P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

A standard 2D range-bearing sensor model is:

$$z_t = \begin{bmatrix} r_t \\ \phi_t \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{i,x} - x_t)^2 + (m_{i,y} - y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{i,y} - y_t, m_{i,x} - x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix} + \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$

#### 2.1.3 The Map Model

The map $m = \{m_1, m_2, \dots, m_N\}$ is treated as a collection of stationary point landmarks. For EKF-SLAM, the map state is augmented into the robot's state vector.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of SLAM Algorithms

```
SLAM
├── Filter-Based (Online)
│   ├── EKF-SLAM (Extended Kalman Filter)
│   ├── UKF-SLAM (Unscented Kalman Filter)
│   └── PF-SLAM / FastSLAM (Particle Filter / Rao-Blackwellized)
├── Graph-Based (Optimization-Based / Full SLAM)
│   ├── Pose Graph Optimization
│   └── Bundle Adjustment (for Visual SLAM)
└── Sensor-Modality Variants
    ├── LiDAR SLAM (e.g., GMapping, Cartographer)
    ├── Visual SLAM (vSLAM) — Mono / Stereo / RGB-D
    └── Visual-Inertial SLAM (VIO) — ORB-SLAM3, VINS-Mono
```

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU Board Examination**

| # | Concept | Equation / Identity | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SLAM Joint Posterior | $P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Probability density, dimensionless |
| 2 | Bayes Filter Prediction | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Belief update |
| 3 | Bayes Filter Update | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | $\eta$ is normalization constant |
| 4 | EKF State Vector | $\mathbf{y}_t = [x_t, m_1^T, m_2^T, \dots, m_N^T]^T$ | Dimension grows with $N$ landmarks |
| 5 | EKF Covariance | $\Sigma_{yy}$ of size $(3+2N) \times (3+2N)$ | Quadratic complexity $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ |
| 6 | Jacobian (Observation) | $H = \frac{\partial h(x_t, m_i)}{\partial (x_t, m_i)}$ | Partial derivative matrix |
| 7 | Kalman Gain | $K_t = \Sigma_{t \vert t-1} H^T (H \Sigma_{t \vert t-1} H^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Updates state covariance |
| 8 | Mahalanobis Distance | $D_M^2 = v^T S^{-1} v$, with $S = H \Sigma H^T + Q$ | Used for data association gating |
| 9 | ICP Objective | $\min_{R,t} \sum_i \vert\vert p_i - (R q_i + t) \vert\vert^2$ | Iterative Closest Point for scan matching |
| 10 | Pose Graph Error | $\min_{x} \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \vert\vert e_{ij}(x_i, x_j, z_{ij}) \vert\vert^2_{\Omega_{ij}}$ | Non-linear least squares |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Google/Waymo's self-driving stack uses LiDAR SLAM for centimeter-accurate localization in pre-mapped HD maps.
- **Warehouse Robotics:** Amazon's Kiva robots use 2D SLAM to navigate without floor markings or QR codes.
- **Search and Rescue:** DARPA Subterranean Challenge required SLAM in GPS-denied environments (caves, tunnels).
- **AR/VR Headsets:** Apple Vision Pro and Meta Quest use Visual-Inertial SLAM (VINS) for 6-DoF tracking of the user's head pose in real time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Recursive Bayes Filter for Online SLAM

The "Online SLAM" posterior $P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ is obtained by integrating out the past trajectory from the full SLAM posterior. We start with:

$$P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, P(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Rule to the measurement likelihood.**
The most recent observation $z_t$ depends only on the current pose $x_t$ and the map $m$. This allows us to factor the posterior:

$$P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov assumption to the control input.**
The current pose $x_t$ is conditionally independent of past observations given $x_{t-1}$ and $u_t$:

$$P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, P(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 3 — Combine Steps 1 and 2 into the recursive update.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) &= \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \\
&\quad \times \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, P(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Recursive Bayes Filter for Online SLAM**. It is a two-stage loop:
- **Prediction (Integral term):** Propagates the previous belief through the motion model.
- **Correction ($P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$):** Multiplies by the latest sensor likelihood and normalizes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Unlike pure localization, the state space here includes $m$, making the integral intractable for continuous maps. This is precisely why EKF (Gaussian approximation) or Particle Filters (Monte Carlo sampling) are required.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the EKF-SLAM Update Equations

EKF-SLAM linearizes the nonlinear observation and motion models using **first-order Taylor expansion** (Jacobians). Let the augmented state be $\mathbf{y}_t = [x_t^T, m^T]^T$ with covariance $\Sigma_t$.

**Step 1 — Define the observation prediction.**
The predicted observation $\hat{z}_t$ is computed by passing the predicted state through the nonlinear model $h(\cdot)$:

$$\hat{z}_t = h(x_t, m_i) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{i,x} - x_t)^2 + (m_{i,y} - y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{i,y} - y_t, m_{i,x} - x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the measurement Jacobian $H$.**
The Jacobian is computed with respect to the augmented state $\mathbf{y}_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{y}_t} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial h}{\partial x_t} & 0 & \cdots & \frac{\partial h}{\partial m_i} & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The non-zero blocks for a range-bearing observation are:

$$\frac{\partial h}{\partial x_t} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{\Delta x}{\sqrt{q}} & -\frac{\Delta y}{\sqrt{q}} & 0 \\ \frac{\Delta y}{q} & -\frac{\Delta x}{q} & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \frac{\partial h}{\partial m_i} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\Delta x}{\sqrt{q}} & \frac{\Delta y}{\sqrt{q}} \\ -\frac{\Delta y}{q} & \frac{\Delta x}{q} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\Delta x = m_{i,x} - x_t$, $\Delta y = m_{i,y} - y_t$, and $q = \Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2$.

**Step 3 — Compute the innovation (measurement residual).**
The innovation vector $v_t$ quantifies the discrepancy between actual and predicted measurements:

$$v_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$$

The innovation covariance $S_t$ measures the spread of the innovation:

$$S_t = H \Sigma_{t \vert t-1} H^T + Q_t$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Kalman Gain.**
The Kalman Gain $K_t$ determines how much weight to assign to the innovation:

$$K_t = \Sigma_{t \vert t-1} H^T S_t^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Update the state estimate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{y}_t &= \mathbf{y}_{t \vert t-1} + K_t v_t \\
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t H) \, \Sigma_{t \vert t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Joseph form for numerical stability** (preferred in practice):

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H) \, \Sigma_{t \vert t-1} \, (I - K_t H)^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common derivation error is forgetting that the Jacobian is taken with respect to the **augmented** state (robot + landmarks), not just the robot pose. Marks are awarded specifically for correctly identifying the $\frac{\partial h}{\partial m_i}$ block.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of FastSLAM Particle Filter Update

FastSLAM factorizes the SLAM posterior using **Rao-Blackwellization**:

$$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = P(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \, \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(m_i \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})$$

**Step 1 — Sample trajectory hypotheses (particles).**
Each particle $k$ maintains a complete trajectory hypothesis $x_{1:t}^{(k)}$. New poses are sampled from the proposal distribution:

$$x_t^{(k)} \sim P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(k)}, u_t)$$

**Step 2 — Compute importance weights.**
The weight $w_t^{(k)}$ corrects for the difference between the proposal and the true posterior:

$$w_t^{(k)} = w_{t-1}^{(k)} \cdot P(z_t \mid x_{1:t}^{(k)}, z_{1:t-1})$$

**Step 3 — Update per-landmark EKFs.**
For each particle, the $i$-th landmark's position is maintained by a **local 2×2 EKF** (independent of all others):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{i,t}^{(k)} &= \mu_{i,t-1}^{(k)} + K_t^{(k)} (z_t - \hat{z}_t^{(k)}) \\
\sigma_{i,t}^{(k)} &= (I - K_t^{(k)} H_i^{(k)}) \, \sigma_{i,t-1}^{(k)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Resample particles based on weights.**
Particles with low weight $w_t^{(k)}$ are discarded; those with high weight are duplicated. The effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}}$ triggers resampling when:

$$N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{k=1}^{M} (w_t^{(k)})^2} < \frac{M}{2}$$

**Complexity Comparison:**

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Map Size Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| EKF-SLAM | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ | Few hundred landmarks |
| FastSLAM (PF) | $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$ | $\mathcal{O}(M N)$ | Scalable to millions |
| Graph-SLAM | $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ (sparse) | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ | Best for offline mapping |

---

### 3.4 Data Association: Mahalanobis Distance Test

A critical sub-problem in SLAM is determining whether the currently observed feature corresponds to a previously mapped landmark. The **Mahalanobis distance** provides a statistically grounded gating test:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_M^2 &= v_t^T S_t^{-1} v_t \\
\text{Association Hypothesis:} \quad &D_M^2 < \chi^2_{d, 1-\alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 2D range-bearing sensor ($d = 2$ dimensions), at confidence level $\alpha = 0.05$, the gating threshold from the chi-squared table is **$\chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.991$**. If $D_M^2 < 5.991$, the observation is accepted as belonging to that landmark; otherwise, it is treated as a new landmark or rejected as outlier.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level SLAM Functional Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the closed-loop interaction between prediction and correction that defines every SLAM algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Pose Estimate x0] --> B[Receive Control Input ut]
    B --> C[Motion Model P xt xt-1 ut]
    C --> D[Predicted State xt-predicted]
    D --> E[Receive Sensor Observation zt]
    E --> F[Observation Model P zt xt m]
    F --> G[Compute Innovation vt]
    G --> H{Data Association}
    H -->|Known Landmark| I[Update Existing Landmark EKF]
    H -->|Unknown Feature| J[Add New Landmark to Map]
    I --> K[Compute Kalman Gain Kt]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Updated State xt and Map m]
    L --> M[Store Pose in Trajectory]
    M --> N{Loop Closure Detected?}
    N -->|No| B
    N -->|Yes| O[Pose Graph Optimization]
    O --> P[Globally Consistent Map]
```

### 4.2 EKF-SLAM State and Covariance Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Pose x y theta] -->|Augmented State Vector| D[Full State y]
    B[Landmark 1 mx1 my1] --> D
    C[Landmark N mxN myN] --> D
    D --> E[State Vector Size 3 + 2N]
    E --> F[Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    F --> G[Robot-Robot Block 3x3]
    F --> H[Robot-Landmark Block 3x2N]
    F --> I[Landmark-Landmark Block 2Nx2N]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Blocks:**
> - The **Robot-Robot block** captures pose uncertainty.
> - The **Robot-Landmark block** captures cross-correlations — this is the unique feature of EKF-SLAM that enforces landmark correlations.
> - The **Landmark-Landmark block** grows quadratically, leading to the famous $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ computational bottleneck.

### 4.3 FastSLAM Particle Filter Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Sensor Data zt and ut] --> B[Particle Cloud M Particles]
    B --> P1[Particle 1 Trajectory + Map EKF per landmark]
    B --> P2[Particle 2 Trajectory + Map EKF per landmark]
    B --> P3[Particle 3 Trajectory + Map EKF per landmark]
    B --> Pk[Particle M Trajectory + Map EKF per landmark]
    P1 --> Q[Compute Importance Weights w]
    P2 --> Q
    P3 --> Q
    Pk --> Q
    Q --> R{Resample if Neff lt M/2}
    R -->|Yes| S[Discard Low Weight Particles]
    S --> T[Duplicate High Weight Particles]
    R -->|No| U[Output Best Particle Map]
    T --> U
```

### 4.4 Graph-Based SLAM Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Trajectory x0 x1 xT] --> B[Pose Nodes]
    B --> C[Odometry Edges between consecutive poses]
    B --> D[Measurement Edges between pose and landmark]
    D --> E[Loop Closure Edges non-consecutive poses]
    C --> F[Construct Sparse Information Matrix]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Nonlinear Least Squares Optimization]
    G --> H[Optimized Trajectory and Map]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the SLAM problem mathematically. Why is it considered a "chicken-and-egg" problem?

**Model Answer:**

The SLAM problem is mathematically formulated as estimating the joint posterior probability density:

$$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where $x_{1:t}$ is the robot's trajectory, $m$ is the map, $z_{1:t}$ are observations, and $u_{1:t}$ are control inputs.

It is called a "chicken-and-egg" problem because:
- **Localization** requires an accurate map to determine the robot's pose.
- **Mapping** requires an accurate pose to place observed landmarks correctly.
- Neither the map nor the pose is known beforehand, so each must be estimated simultaneously while aiding the other.

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for explanation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List and briefly explain any three sources of uncertainty in the SLAM problem.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sensor Noise:** Real sensors such as LiDARs, cameras, and IMUs produce measurements corrupted by Gaussian or non-Gaussian noise, typically **±0.05 m** in range and **±1°–3°** in bearing for indoor units.

2. **Actuator / Odometry Noise:** Wheel slippage and encoder quantization cause the robot's predicted motion to drift over time. Without correction, dead-reckoning error grows unboundedly with distance traveled.

3. **Data Association Ambiguity:** The robot cannot always reliably determine whether a currently observed feature is the *same* feature it observed previously, leading to false associations and map corruption.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per source with explanation)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) failing to write the *measurement Jacobian* explicitly, (2) omitting the **normalization constant** $\eta$ in the Bayes update, or (3) confusing *Online SLAM* ($x_t, m$) with *Full SLAM* ($x_{1:t}, m$). Marks are awarded for explicit distinction.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the recursive Bayes filter update for Online SLAM. Clearly distinguish between the prediction and correction steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an EKF-SLAM system. Write the state vector, observation model, and the Kalman gain update equations. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

The Online SLAM posterior is given by $P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Rule to factor out the most recent measurement $z_t$:**

$$P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the total probability theorem to integrate over the previous pose $x_{t-1}$:**

$$P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, P(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two steps to obtain the recursive update equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot P(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the two components:**

- The **integral term** (Prediction) propagates the prior belief through the motion model $P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$.
- The **likelihood term** $P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ (Correction) updates the predicted belief using the latest sensor measurement.

**Step 5 — Note on intractability:**
The continuous integral over $x_{t-1}$ is analytically intractable for general distributions, motivating approximations like the **Extended Kalman Filter** (Gaussian assumption) or **Particle Filters** (Monte Carlo sampling).

*(7 marks: 2 for Bayes' rule application, 2 for integration step, 2 for final recursive equation, 1 for identifying intractability)*

##### Model Solution for (b):

**EKF-SLAM System Architecture:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Control Input ut] --> B[Predict Pose using Motion Model]
    B --> C[Predicted State xt-pred]
    C --> D[Predicted Covariance Sigma t-pred]
    E[Sensor Observation zt] --> F[Predict Measurement hat-zt]
    F --> G[Compute Innovation vt]
    D --> H[Compute Jacobian H]
    G --> I[Compute Innovation Covariance S]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute Kalman Gain Kt]
    J --> K[Update State xt]
    J --> L[Update Covariance Sigma t]
    K --> M[Output Corrected Pose and Map]
    L --> M
```

**State Vector:**
The augmented state vector concatenates the robot pose with all landmark positions:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = [x_t, y_t, \theta_t, m_{1,x}, m_{1,y}, m_{2,x}, m_{2,y}, \dots, m_{N,x}, m_{N,y}]^T$$

**Observation Model:**
For observing landmark $m_i$ from pose $x_t$:

$$z_t = h(\mathbf{y}_t) + \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{i,x} - x_t)^2 + (m_{i,y} - y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{i,y} - y_t, m_{i,x} - x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix} + \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$

**Kalman Gain Update:**

$$K_t = \Sigma_{t \vert t-1} H^T (H \Sigma_{t \vert t-1} H^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

**State and Covariance Update:**

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{y}_{t \vert t-1} + K_t (z_t - h(\mathbf{y}_{t \vert t-1}))$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H) \, \Sigma_{t \vert t-1}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for state vector and observation model, 3 for Kalman gain and update equations)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the FastSLAM algorithm based on Rao-Blackwellized particle filters. How does it factorize the SLAM posterior? What is the computational advantage over EKF-SLAM? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the data association problem in SLAM. Explain the Mahalanobis distance test with its mathematical formulation. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Rao-Blackwellization Factorization:**
FastSLAM decomposes the SLAM posterior into a product of a trajectory estimator and $N$ independent landmark estimators:

$$P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = P(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \, \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(m_i \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})$$

**Interpretation:**
- The trajectory is estimated using a **particle filter** with $M$ particles.
- Each particle maintains a complete hypothesis of the trajectory $x_{1:t}^{(k)}$ and an independent low-dimensional EKF for *each* landmark.

**Algorithmic Steps:**
1. Sample a new pose for each particle from the motion model: $x_t^{(k)} \sim P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(k)}, u_t)$.
2. Compute importance weight: $w_t^{(k)} = w_{t-1}^{(k)} \cdot P(z_t \mid x_{1:t}^{(k)}, z_{1:t-1})$.
3. Update each landmark's local EKF using the standard Kalman update with innovation.
4. Resample particles when effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}} < M/2$.

**Computational Advantage over EKF-SLAM:**

| Aspect | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM |
|---|---|---|
| State Dimension | $3 + 2N$ (monolithic) | $3 + 2M$ (distributed per particle) |
| Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$ |
| Scalability | Few hundred landmarks | Millions of landmarks |
| Map Representation | Single global estimate | $M$ particle-specific maps |

The key insight is that landmark estimates are **conditionally independent** given the trajectory, so they can be updated independently rather than jointly.

*(7 marks: 2 for factorization, 2 for algorithm steps, 3 for complexity comparison)*

##### Model Solution for (b):

**The Data Association Problem:**
In SLAM, the robot must determine *which* previously mapped landmark (if any) corresponds to a *new* observation. Incorrect data association is one of the most catastrophic failure modes, as it can corrupt the entire map.

**Types of Data Association:**
1. **Known Correspondence:** The mapping is given (e.g., fiducial markers with IDs).
2. **Unknown Correspondence:** The robot must infer the association from feature descriptors or geometry.

**Mahalanobis Distance Test:**
Given a candidate landmark $m_i$ with state $\mu_i$ and covariance $\sigma_i$, and a predicted observation $\hat{z}_t$ with innovation covariance $S_t$, the Mahalanobis distance is:

$$D_M^2 = v_t^T S_t^{-1} v_t$$

where $v_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ is the innovation vector.

**Decision Rule:**

$$\text{Accept association if } D_M^2 < \chi^2_{d, 1-\alpha}$$

For a 2D range-bearing sensor ($d = 2$) at confidence $\alpha = 0.05$, the threshold is **$\chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.991$**.

**Validation Gate Implementation:**
1. Compute predicted observation $\hat{z}_t$ and innovation $v_t$ for each candidate landmark.
2. Compute $S_t = H_i \sigma_i H_i^T + Q_t$.
3. Compute $D_M^2 = v_t^T S_t^{-1} v_t$.
4. Select the landmark that minimizes $D_M^2$ subject to $D_M^2 < 5.991$.
5. If no landmark passes the gate, initialize a new landmark at the observed location.

**Multiple Hypothesis Tracking (MHT):**
For robust association in cluttered environments, multiple competing hypotheses are maintained until sufficient evidence disambiguates them.

*(7 marks: 2 for problem definition, 3 for Mahalanobis formula, 2 for decision rule and gating threshold)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- ✅ **SLAM Joint Posterior:** $P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — the defining equation.
- ✅ **Online vs Full SLAM:** Online marginalizes past trajectory ($x_t, m$); Full retains it ($x_{1:t}, m$).
- ✅ **Two SLAM Equations:** Motion model $P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ + Observation model $P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$.
- ✅ **EKF-SLAM Bottleneck:** $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ complexity due to the monolithic $(3+2N) \times (3+2N)$ covariance matrix.
- ✅ **FastSLAM Trick:** Rao-Blackwellization factorizes the posterior, decoupling landmark updates.
- ✅ **Mahalanobis Threshold:** $\chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.991$ for 2D range-bearing gating at 95% confidence.
- ✅ **Jacobian $H$** must be taken with respect to the **augmented** state, not just the robot pose.
- ✅ **Joseph Form** of covariance update is numerically stable: $\Sigma_t = (I-KH)\Sigma_{t\vert t-1}(I-KH)^T + KQK^T$.
- ✅ **Loop Closure Detection** is what differentiates Graph-SLAM from filter-based methods.
- ✅ **KTU Marks Pattern:** 14-mark questions always have two 7-mark sub-parts (typically theory + derivation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Path Planning: Local vs Global Path Planning, Graph Search, Potential Field-Based Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Path Planning: Local vs Global Path Planning, Graph Search, Potential Field-Based Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Path Planning — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Path Planning** is the computational process of determining a **collision-free, kinematically feasible trajectory** for a mobile robot from a **start configuration** to a **goal configuration** while optimizing a chosen cost function (e.g., shortest distance, minimum time, minimum energy, or maximum clearance from obstacles) within the robot's **Configuration Space (C-space)**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 syllabus (PEMET756)**, path planning is treated as a *deliberative* layer of the mobile robot navigation stack, sitting directly above perception/localization and directly below the motion control loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Configuration Space (C-space):** The complete set of all possible configurations a robot can attain. For a planar mobile robot (e.g., a differential drive), C-space is typically $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ (x, y, θ). For a point robot it reduces to $\mathbb{R}^2$.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blindfolded Walker in a Room"

Imagine you are **blindfolded inside a furnished room** holding a GPS that tells you the room layout (global map) and the chair positions (obstacles).

* **Global Path Planning** = You are given the *full floor plan* in advance. You study it, compute the *shortest collision-free route to the door* on paper, then start walking along that pre-planned line. This is the "look-before-you-leap" philosophy.
* **Local Path Planning (Reactive)** = You are given *only a flashlight beam* (limited sensor range). At every step you *react* to whatever the beam reveals, nudging around the chair you just spotted. This is the "feel-your-way" philosophy.

Modern mobile robots (e.g., autonomous cars, warehouse AMRs) typically **fuse both**: the global planner proposes a coarse route, and the local planner performs real-time obstacle avoidance along it.

---

### 1.3 Local vs. Global Path Planning — Comparative Overview

| Attribute | Global Path Planning | Local Path Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Map Knowledge** | Complete a-priori map required | Partial / sensor-based / unknown |
| **Computational Horizon** | Entire environment (offline or slow online) | Immediate neighbourhood (real-time, > 50 Hz) |
| **Optimality** | Near-optimal (under chosen metric) | Locally optimal / satisficing |
| **Re-planning Frequency** | Infrequent (topological changes) | Every control cycle (10–100 ms) |
| **Typical Algorithms** | A*, Dijkstra, D*, Visibility Graph, Cell Decomposition | Artificial Potential Field (APF), Dynamic Window Approach (DWA), VFH, Bug algorithms |
| **Failure Mode** | Trapped in local minima *offline*; outdated map | Local minima, oscillations, dead-ends |
| **Sensor Dependency** | None (map-based) | High (LiDAR, sonar, cameras) |
| **Output** | Geometric / topological path (waypoints) | Velocity commands (v, ω) |
| **KTU Exam Keyword** | "Deliberative / Off-line planner" | "Reactive / On-line planner" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly treats these as **complementary**, not competing. A classical architecture (e.g., Moravec & Elfes 1985) uses a global grid-based planner feeding a local potential-field executor.

---

### 1.4 Graph Search — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Graph Search** is a class of path planning algorithms in which the **continuous free C-space is discretized** into a finite graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is a set of nodes (configurations / waypoints) and $E$ is a set of edges (collision-free connections). The planner then searches this discrete graph for a minimum-cost path from $s \in V$ to $g \in V$.

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — "Treasure Hunt on a Subway Map"

Think of the **Kochi Metro map**. The stations are the **nodes**, and the tracks between adjacent stations are the **edges** (each with a ticket cost = edge weight). A graph search algorithm is the **Google Maps subway routing engine**: it tries every reasonable combination of stations until it finds the *cheapest* route from *Aluva* to *Thykoodam*. **Dijkstra's** explores in expanding rings of equal ticket cost; **A\*** uses the *air-distance* from each station to the goal as a heuristic "compass" to skip useless branches.

---

### 1.6 Potential Field-Based Path Planning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Artificial Potential Field (APF) Method** (Khatib, 1986): A reactive local path planning technique in which the robot is treated as a **charged particle** moving under the influence of a synthesized **artificial potential function** $U(q): \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$. The goal exerts an **attractive potential** pulling the robot toward it, while obstacles exert **repulsive potentials** pushing the robot away. The negative gradient $-\nabla U(q)$ yields the artificial force that drives the robot.

Mathematically, the total potential is the linear superposition:

$$U(q) = U_{\text{att}}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} U_{\text{rep},i}(q)$$

where $m$ is the number of obstacles sensed.

### 1.7 Intuitive Analogy — "The Magnet and the Hills"

Imagine a steel ball rolling on a **rubber sheet landscape**. The goal is a *valley* the ball is pulled into (attractive well). Each obstacle is a *bump* the ball must roll around (repulsive hill). The ball naturally rolls downhill along the *negative gradient* of the terrain. Path planning = watching the ball's trajectory.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The ball can get **stuck in a local minimum** — a "saddle dip" between two obstacle bumps that exactly cancels the pull of the goal. The robot stops in open space, believing it has reached the goal. This is the **#1 examiner trap** in potential field questions.

---

### 1.8 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics used in KTU board exam problems:**
> * **Path Length** $L = \int_0^1 \lvert \dot{\gamma}(s) \rvert \, ds$ (in meters, **m**)
> * **Path Smoothness** (curvature) $\kappa = \dfrac{\vert \dot{x}\ddot{y} - \dot{y}\ddot{x} \vert}{(\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2)^{3/2}}$ (in m⁻¹)
> * **Search Nodes Expanded** (dimensionless count)
> * **Time Complexity** (Big-O notation, e.g., $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ for Dijkstra with a heap)
> * **Clearance** (minimum distance to nearest obstacle, **m**)

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of Path Planning into Operational Stages

The complete path planning pipeline (in KTU 2024 framework) can be decomposed into the following structured logic steps:

1. **World Modelling / Map Building** — Construct a representation of the environment.
   * *Metric maps*: Occupancy grid, point cloud.
   * *Topological maps*: Graph of places and corridors.
   * *Semantic maps*: Labelled entities (door, wall, chair).
2. **C-space Construction** — Shrink the robot to a point and inflate obstacles by the robot's radius $r_{\text{robot}}$ (for a circular robot).
   * $C_{\text{obs}} = \{ q \in C \mid A(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \}$
   * $C_{\text{free}} = C \setminus C_{\text{obs}}$
3. **Discretization (if Graph Search)** — Sample $C_{\text{free}}$ into nodes and edges.
   * *Roadmap methods*: Visibility graph, Voronoi diagram, Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM).
   * *Cell decomposition*: Exact (trapezoidal, Boustrophedon) or approximate (quadtree, grid).
4. **Search / Optimization** — Find minimum-cost path through the discrete structure.
   * *Uninformed*: BFS, UCS, Dijkstra.
   * *Informed*: A*, D*, ARA\*, AD*.
   * *Sampling-based*: RRT, RRT*.
   * *Reactive*: APF, DWA, VFH.
5. **Smoothing & Validation** — Shortcutting, spline fitting, kinematic feasibility check.
6. **Execution / Re-planning** — Stream waypoints to the controller; trigger re-plan on divergence.

---

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why & How")

* **C-space inflation** turns the *kinematically complex* "moving body" problem into a *point-mass* problem — a massive simplification that is **valid only for disc-shaped** or pre-decomposed robots.
* **Discretization** trades *optimality* for *computability*; finer grids give better paths but explode the search space.
* **Heuristics** (in A*) prune the search tree; an *admissible* heuristic guarantees optimality, while a *consistent* heuristic guarantees efficiency.
* **APF gradients** give instantaneous velocity/force commands, bypassing the search entirely — hence real-time.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Module 4 high-yield. Memorize the table; the derivation logic follows in Section 3.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Potential | $U(q) = U_{\text{att}}(q) + \sum U_{\text{rep},i}(q)$ | $U$ in **Joules (J)** | Linear superposition holds |
| 2 | Attractive Potential (conic) | $U_{\text{att}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \zeta \, \rho_{\text{goal}}^2$ | $\zeta$ gain (N/m), $\rho_{\text{goal}} = \lvert q - q_{\text{goal}} \rvert$ (m) | Quadratic well, smooth at goal |
| 3 | Attractive Force | $F_{\text{att}} = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} = -\zeta (q - q_{\text{goal}})$ | $F$ in **Newtons (N)** | Points toward goal |
| 4 | Attractive Potential (conic, far-field) | $U_{\text{att}} = \zeta \, \rho_{\text{goal}}$ | — | Used when $\rho_{\text{goal}} > d^*$ (linear, constant force) |
| 5 | Repulsive Potential (Khatib) | $U_{\text{rep}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2$ for $\rho \le \rho_0$ | $\eta$ gain (N·m²), $\rho$ distance to obstacle (m), $\rho_0$ influence radius (m) | Zero outside $\rho_0$ |
| 6 | Repulsive Force | $F_{\text{rep}} = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = \eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \tfrac{1}{\rho^2} \hat{n}$ | $\hat{n}$ unit vector from obstacle to robot | Points away from obstacle |
| 7 | Artificial Force | $F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{att}} + \sum F_{\text{rep}}$ | $F_{\text{total}}$ in **N** | Drives robot along $-\nabla U$ |
| 8 | Graph Cost (edge) | $w(e) = \int_e c(x) \, dx$ | $c(x)$ local cost density | For shortest path |
| 9 | Dijkstra Optimal Cost | $d[v] = \min_{(u,v)\in E} \{ d[u] + w(u,v) \}$ | $d[\cdot]$ in **m** (if cost = distance) | Dynamic programming recursion |
| 10 | A* Evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $f$ estimated total cost, $g$ cost-so-far, $h$ heuristic (m) | Optimal if $h$ is admissible |
| 11 | Admissibility | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ | $h^*$ true cost-to-go | $h$ never overestimates |
| 12 | Consistency (Monotonicity) | $h(n) \le w(n,n') + h(n')$ | for every edge $n \to n'$ | Stronger than admissibility |
| 13 | Euclidean Heuristic | $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$ | m | Admissible in $\mathbb{R}^2$ |
| 14 | Manhattan Heuristic | $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_g \vert + \vert y_n - y_g \vert$ | m | Admissible in 4-connected grid |
| 15 | BFS Time Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | — | For unweighted graph |
| 16 | Dijkstra Time Complexity | $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ | with binary heap | Without heap: $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ |
| 17 | A* Time Complexity (worst) | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | exponential in depth if $h=0$ | A* dominates Dijkstra when $h > 0$ |
| 18 | C-space Inflation (disc) | $C_{\text{obs}}' = C_{\text{obs}} \oplus B(0, r_{\text{robot}})$ | Minkowski sum, $r$ in m | Valid for circular robot |
| 19 | Path Length | $L = \int_0^1 \lVert \dot{\gamma}(s) \rVert \, ds$ | m | Functional definition |
| 20 | Local Minima Condition | $\nabla U(q^*) = 0$ and $q^* \neq q_{\text{goal}}$ | — | APF failure mode |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Planner Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Autonomous Cars (Highway)** | Local (DWA) + Global (A*/RRT) | High-speed reactive needs + static HD map |
| **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva)** | Topological + Grid A* | Structured environment, time-optimal |
| **Planetary Rovers (Mars Perseverance)** | D* Lite | Unknown terrain, dynamic re-planning |
| **Indoor Service Robots** | APF + SLAM | Real-time obstacle avoidance in cluttered homes |
| **Drone Swarms** | RRT* + APF | 3-D C-space, decentralized reactive layer |
| **Underwater AUVs** | Visibility Graph + APF | Sparse obstacles, current-aware |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Attractive Potential (Quadratic Form)

**Goal:** Derive $U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \zeta \rho_{\text{goal}}^2$ starting from the design requirement that the resulting force must be proportional to the distance to the goal.

**Step 1 — Define the distance function.**
Let $q = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ be the robot's configuration and $q_g = (x_g, y_g)$ the goal configuration. The Euclidean distance is:

$$
\rho_{\text{goal}}(q) = \lVert q - q_g \rVert_2 = \sqrt{(x - x_g)^2 + (y - y_g)^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Design the attractive force.**
We require the force to be a **restoring (linear spring) force** that pulls the robot toward $q_g$ with magnitude proportional to the displacement. By Hooke's law analogy:

$$
F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\zeta \cdot (q - q_g)
$$

where $\zeta > 0$ is the **attractive gain** with units of **N/m**.

**Step 3 — Recover the potential from the force.**
The potential is the negative integral of the force along any path from $q_g$ to $q$:

$$
U_{\text{att}}(q) = - \int_{q_g}^{q} F_{\text{att}}(q') \cdot dq'
$$

Substituting the force expression:

$$
U_{\text{att}}(q) = - \int_{q_g}^{q} \left[ -\zeta (q' - q_g) \right] \cdot dq' = \zeta \int_{q_g}^{q} (q' - q_g) \cdot dq'
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the line integral.**

$$
U_{\text{att}}(q) = \zeta \int_{q_g}^{q} (q' - q_g) \, dq' = \zeta \left[ \tfrac{1}{2}(q' - q_g)^2 \right]_{q_g}^{q}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the limits.**

$$
U_{\text{att}}(q) = \zeta \left( \tfrac{1}{2}(q - q_g)^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}(q_g - q_g)^2 \right) = \tfrac{1}{2} \zeta \, \rho_{\text{goal}}^2
$$

Therefore, the **attractive potential** is:

$$
\boxed{U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \zeta \, \lVert q - q_g \rVert^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** $\nabla U_{\text{att}} = \zeta (q - q_g)$, so $F_{\text{att}} = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} = -\zeta(q - q_g)$, which is exactly the Hooke force. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Repulsive Potential (Khatib 1986)

**Goal:** Derive $U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2$ such that the robot is repelled from obstacle $O_i$ when within influence radius $\rho_0$ and unaffected beyond it.

**Step 1 — Define obstacle distance.**
Let $\rho(q)$ be the minimum Euclidean distance from the robot to obstacle $O_i$:

$$
\rho(q) = \min_{p \in O_i} \lVert q - p \rVert
$$

**Step 2 — Design requirements.**
(a) $U_{\text{rep}}(\rho) \to \infty$ as $\rho \to 0$ (infinite barrier at obstacle surface).
(b) $U_{\text{rep}}(\rho) = 0$ for $\rho \ge \rho_0$ (no influence beyond $\rho_0$).
(c) $U_{\text{rep}}$ is $C^1$-continuous at $\rho = \rho_0$ (smooth force transitions).

**Step 3 — Propose a candidate function of the form $U_{\text{rep}} = f(\rho)$ that diverges at $\rho = 0$.** A natural choice is:

$$
U_{\text{rep}}(\rho) = \tfrac{1}{2} \eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 \quad \text{for } 0 < \rho \le \rho_0
$$

**Step 4 — Verify requirement (a).**
As $\rho \to 0^+$, $\tfrac{1}{\rho} \to \infty$, so $U_{\text{rep}} \to \infty$. ✓

**Step 5 — Verify requirement (b).**
At $\rho = \rho_0$: $U_{\text{rep}}(\rho_0) = \tfrac{1}{2} \eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 = 0$. ✓

**Step 6 — Verify requirement (c).** Compute the derivative (force magnitude):

$$
\frac{dU_{\text{rep}}}{d\rho} = \eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \left( -\tfrac{1}{\rho^2} \right) = -\eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \tfrac{1}{\rho^2}
$$

At $\rho = \rho_0$: $\tfrac{dU_{\text{rep}}}{d\rho} \big\vert_{\rho_0} = -\eta \cdot 0 \cdot \tfrac{1}{\rho_0^2} = 0$. ✓ (Force is zero at the boundary, ensuring smooth transition.)

**Step 7 — Convert to spatial gradient.**
The gradient with respect to $q$ requires the chain rule and the unit normal $\hat{n} = \dfrac{q - q_{\text{closest}}}{\rho}$:

$$
\nabla_q U_{\text{rep}} = \frac{dU_{\text{rep}}}{d\rho} \cdot \nabla_q \rho = -\eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \tfrac{1}{\rho^2} \hat{n}
$$

**Step 8 — The repulsive force.**

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{rep}} = -\nabla_q U_{\text{rep}} = \eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \tfrac{1}{\rho^2} \hat{n} \quad \text{for } \rho \le \rho_0, \text{ else } 0}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the A* Optimality Condition

**Goal:** Prove that A* returns an optimal path if and only if the heuristic $h$ is **admissible** ($h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$).

**Step 1 — Define A* evaluation.**
At every node $n$, A* uses:

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$

where $g(n)$ is the *actual* cost from start to $n$ (computed by the algorithm) and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate of cost from $n$ to goal.

**Step 2 — Suppose for contradiction that A* returns a suboptimal goal $g_{\text{sub}}$ with cost $C_{\text{sub}} > C^*$.** Let $n^*$ be a node on an **optimal** path that A* never expanded.

**Step 3 — On the optimal path, the true cost-to-go is $h^*(n^*) = C^* - g(n^*)$.** Admissibility gives:

$$
h(n^*) \le h^*(n^*) = C^* - g(n^*)
$$

**Step 4 — Therefore the A* evaluation at $n^*$ is:**

$$
f(n^*) = g(n^*) + h(n^*) \le g(n^*) + C^* - g(n^*) = C^*
$$

**Step 5 — Since A* expands nodes in non-decreasing order of $f$, and $f(n^*) \le C^* < C_{\text{sub}}$,** the optimal node $n^*$ would have been expanded **before** the suboptimal goal $g_{\text{sub}}$. Contradiction. ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corollary (Examiner's Favourite):** If $h$ is **consistent** (monotone), the first time a node is expanded by A* its $g$-value is optimal, and re-expansions are unnecessary. This is why A* with a consistent heuristic is "fast".

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A mobile robot at $q = (1, 2)$ must reach goal $q_g = (8, 6)$ in a 2-D plane. Compute the attractive potential and force using $\zeta = 1.5$ N/m.

**Step 1 — Compute the displacement vector.**

$$
q - q_g = (1 - 8, \, 2 - 6) = (-7, -4)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\rho_{\text{goal}}$.**

$$
\rho_{\text{goal}} = \sqrt{(-7)^2 + (-4)^2} = \sqrt{49 + 16} = \sqrt{65} \approx 8.062 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the attractive potential.**

$$
U_{\text{att}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \zeta \rho_{\text{goal}}^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.5 \cdot 65 = 48.75 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the attractive force vector.**

$$
F_{\text{att}} = -\zeta (q - q_g) = -1.5 \cdot (-7, -4) = (10.5, 6.0) \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the force magnitude.**

$$
\lVert F_{\text{att}} \rVert = 1.5 \cdot \sqrt{65} \approx 12.09 \text{ N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The force vector $(10.5, 6.0)$ N points from the robot toward the goal in the direction of $(7, 4)$ (since $F = -\zeta (q - q_g)$), with magnitude proportional to the displacement (Hookean spring).

---

### 3.5 Worked APF Example with a Single Obstacle

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Robot at $q = (3, 3)$, goal at $q_g = (10, 3)$, single circular obstacle centred at $O = (6, 3)$ with radius $r_O = 1$ m. Use $\zeta = 1.0$, $\eta = 5.0$, $\rho_0 = 3.0$ m.

**Step 1 — Distance to obstacle.**

$$
\rho = \lVert q - O \rVert = \sqrt{(3-6)^2 + (3-3)^2} = 3.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Check influence range.** $\rho = 3.0 = \rho_0$ → boundary case, $U_{\text{rep}} = 0$, $F_{\text{rep}} = 0$. Robot is on the influence boundary.

**Step 3 — Attractive potential and force.**

$$
U_{\text{att}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.0 \cdot 49 = 24.5 \text{ J}
$$

$$
F_{\text{att}} = -1.0 \cdot ((3-10), (3-3)) = (7, 0) \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Total force at this instant.**

$$
F_{\text{total}} = (7, 0) + (0, 0) = (7, 0) \text{ N}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The robot is on the cusp of obstacle influence. As it advances toward $(6, 3)$, $\rho$ will drop below 3.0 and a repulsive force will emerge in the $-\hat{x}$ direction, deflecting the robot *above* or *below* the obstacle.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (The KTU "Why" Table)

| Criterion | A* (Graph Search) | APF (Potential Field) | D* (Dynamic A*) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Optimality** | Yes (admissible h) | Local minima possible | Yes (re-plans optimally) |
| **Completeness** | Yes (finite graph) | No (can get stuck) | Yes (incremental) |
| **Real-time Re-planning** | Expensive | Very fast | Efficient (propagates changes) |
| **Handling Unknown Env.** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Memory** | Stores full graph | Stores only local geometry | Stores full graph + RHS |
| **KTU Module Coverage** | ✓ Core | ✓ Core | ✓ Re-planning extension |
| **Typical Use** | Warehouse AMR | Indoor service robot | Mars rover |
