```mermaid
    classDef frep fill:#f87171,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#fff
    classDef fatt fill:#86efac,stroke:#14532d,color:#fff
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Insight:** This diagram is the visual answer to *"Discuss the local-minima problem in Artificial Potential Field method"* — a guaranteed 7-mark question. The two obstacle repulsive forces cancel the attractive force from the goal, leaving a zero-net-force configuration away from the goal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between global and local path planning in mobile robotics. Give two examples of algorithms for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Global Path Planning | Local Path Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Map knowledge** | Complete a-priori map | Partial, sensor-based |
| **Computational nature** | Off-line / deliberative | On-line / reactive |
| **Optimality** | Globally near-optimal | Locally reactive, not globally optimal |

* **Global examples:** A* algorithm, Dijkstra's algorithm, Visibility Graph.
* **Local examples:** Artificial Potential Field (APF), Dynamic Window Approach (DWA), Vector Field Histogram (VFH), Bug algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking key (1 mark each):** Definition of global, definition of local, two examples (1 each pair). Full marks require a clean comparison table or crisp bullet form.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**State the Artificial Potential Field (APF) method. Write the expressions for attractive and repulsive potentials.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Artificial Potential Field (APF) method** (Khatib, 1986) treats the robot as a charged particle moving under a synthesized potential $U(q)$ that is the sum of an attractive potential at the goal and repulsive potentials around obstacles. The robot follows the negative gradient of this potential.

**Attractive potential (quadratic / conic):**

$$
U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \zeta \lVert q - q_g \rVert^2
$$

**Repulsive potential (within influence radius $\rho_0$):**

$$
U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, \quad 0 < \rho \le \rho_0
$$

where $\zeta$ is the attractive gain, $\eta$ the repulsive gain, $\rho$ the distance to the obstacle, and $\rho_0$ the influence radius.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing $U_{\text{rep}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \eta \cdot \tfrac{1}{\rho^2}$ (forgetting the $-1/\rho_0$ shift) is a common error. Without the shift, $U_{\text{rep}} \neq 0$ at $\rho = \rho_0$ and the force is discontinuous. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Artificial Potential Field (APF) method** for mobile robot path planning. Derive the expressions for the attractive and repulsive potentials and forces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **local minima problem** in APF. With suitable diagrams, describe at least two methods to escape local minima. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — APF Derivation (7 marks)

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The Artificial Potential Field (APF) method, proposed by **Oussama Khatib in 1986**, is a reactive local path planning technique. The robot's configuration space is endowed with a scalar potential function $U(q)$ that has a **global minimum at the goal** and **local maxima near obstacles**. The robot is steered by the artificial force $F(q) = -\nabla U(q)$.

**2. Total Potential (0.5 mark):**

$$
U(q) = U_{\text{att}}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} U_{\text{rep},i}(q)
$$

**3. Attractive Potential Derivation (2 marks):**
Starting from the desired force $F_{\text{att}} = -\zeta(q - q_g)$ (a linear restoring "spring" force), the potential is recovered by integration:

$$
U_{\text{att}}(q) = \int_{q_g}^{q} \zeta (q' - q_g) \, dq' = \tfrac{1}{2} \zeta \lVert q - q_g \rVert^2
$$

The resulting attractive force is $F_{\text{att}} = -\zeta(q - q_g)$, directed toward the goal.

**4. Repulsive Potential Derivation (2 marks):**
For an obstacle at distance $\rho$ with influence radius $\rho_0$:

$$
U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2} \eta \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, & 0 < \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

The repulsive force is:

$$
F_{\text{rep}} = \eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \tfrac{1}{\rho^2} \hat{n}
$$

where $\hat{n} = (q - q_{\text{closest}})/\rho$ is the unit normal pointing from the obstacle to the robot.

**5. Total Force and Motion (1 mark):**

$$
F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{att}} + \sum_{i} F_{\text{rep},i} \quad ; \quad v = k \cdot F_{\text{total}}
$$

The robot's velocity (or acceleration) is proportional to the total force.

**6. Properties (0.5 mark):**
* $U \to \infty$ as $\rho \to 0$ (collision avoidance).
* $U_{\text{rep}} = 0$ at $\rho = \rho_0$ (smooth boundary).
* $F_{\text{total}} \to 0$ at goal (stable equilibrium).

---

### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Local Minima Problem (7 marks)

**1. Definition of Local Minimum (1.5 marks):**
A **local minimum** of the potential field is a configuration $q^* \neq q_g$ where:

$$
\nabla U(q^*) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad U(q^*) > U(q_g)
$$

At such a point, the artificial force vanishes and the robot stops, **failing to reach the goal**.

**2. Geometric Illustration (1.5 marks):**
Two commonly cited scenarios:
* **Scenario A — Symmetric obstacle pair:** Two obstacles placed symmetrically with respect to the line joining robot to goal cause equal-and-opposite repulsive forces that cancel the attractive force.
* **Scenario B — Concave obstacle (U-shaped):** A robot approaching the goal from inside a U-shaped cavity experiences a repulsive force from the far wall that exceeds the attractive force, trapping it.

(Refer to **Mermaid Diagram 4 in Section 4.4** for the visual representation.)

**3. Method 1 — Random Walk / Random Escape (1.5 marks):**
When the robot detects zero net force away from the goal, it executes a **pre-planned random walk** or **circular motion** for a fixed duration to break the symmetry, then resumes gradient descent. The motion is governed by:

$$
q(t+1) = q(t) + v_{\text{rand}} \cdot \Delta t \cdot \hat{n}_{\text{rand}}
$$

**4. Method 2 — Virtual Obstacle / Modified Potential (1.5 marks):**
Add a small **virtual repulsive obstacle** at $q^*$ when a local minimum is detected. This perturbs the field and forces the robot to escape. Alternatively, use the **Navigation Function** (Koditschek & Rimon, 1990) which is guaranteed to have a single global minimum.

**5. Method 3 (brief mention, 1 mark):** Wall-following, harmonic potential functions, or hybrid global+local planning (use a global A* path as a sub-goal to escape).

---

#### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **A\* graph search algorithm** for global path planning. Define the evaluation function $f(n)$ and discuss the conditions for **optimality** of A\*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the **8-connected grid** shown in the worked example (a 5×5 grid with start $S$ at $(0,0)$, goal $G$ at $(4,4)$, with obstacles at $(2,2), (2,3), (3,2)$). Use **A\* with Manhattan heuristic** $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_g \vert + \vert y_n - y_g \vert$ to compute the **first three expanded nodes** and the final optimal path. Edge cost = 1 per step. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — A* Algorithm (7 marks)

**1. Overview (1 mark):**
A* is a **best-first graph search** algorithm (Hart, Nilsson & Raphael, 1968) that combines the cost-so-far $g(n)$ with a heuristic estimate $h(n)$ of the cost-to-go. It is widely used for global path planning in occupancy grids and road networks.

**2. Evaluation Function (1 mark):**

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$

* $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to $n$ (computed by A*).
* $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate of cost from $n$ to goal.
* $f(n)$ = estimated total cost of the cheapest solution passing through $n$.

**3. Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
1. Initialise OPEN = {start}, CLOSED = ∅, $g(\text{start}) = 0$.
2. Pop node $n$ from OPEN with **minimum $f(n)$**.
3. If $n$ = goal, **reconstruct path** and terminate.
4. Move $n$ to CLOSED. For each neighbour $m$ of $n$:
   * Tentative $g'(m) = g(n) + w(n, m)$.
   * If $g'(m) < g(m)$: update $g(m) = g'(m)$, $f(m) = g'(m) + h(m)$, set parent of $m$ to $n$, add to OPEN.
5. Repeat from step 2.

**4. Optimality Conditions (2 marks):**
* **Admissibility:** $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$, where $h^*$ is the true cost-to-go. Example: Euclidean and Manhattan heuristics on a grid.
* **Consistency (Monotonicity):** $h(n) \le w(n, n') + h(n')$ for every edge $n \to n'$. If $h$ is consistent, A* never needs to re-open a closed node and runs efficiently.
* **Optimality Theorem:** If $h$ is admissible, A* returns an optimal path. *Proof sketch:* (See Section 3.3 derivation.) The first goal expanded must have $f = g \le C^*$, hence optimal.

**5. Complexity (1 mark):**
* Worst-case time: $O(\lvert V \vert \log \lvert V \vert)$ with a heap.
* Space: $O(\lvert V \vert)$ to store OPEN and CLOSED.
* The heuristic quality dramatically affects the number of expanded nodes.

---

### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Worked A* on 5×5 Grid (7 marks)

**Grid Setup:** $S = (0,0)$, $G = (4,4)$, obstacles $\{(2,2), (2,3), (3,2)\}$. 8-connected (diagonal moves allowed). Edge cost = 1 (or $\sqrt{2}$ for diagonals — we use **1** for simplicity as is standard in KTU textbooks).

**Heuristic:** $h(n) = \vert x_n - 4 \vert + \vert y_n - 4 \vert$ (Manhattan).

**Initial State:**
* OPEN = $\{(0,0)\}$ with $f = g + h = 0 + 8 = 8$.

**Expansion 1: Pop $(0,0)$ from OPEN** — $f = 8$. **(1 mark)**
Generate 8 neighbours; non-obstacle ones: $(1,0), (0,1), (1,1)$.
Compute $f$ for each:
* $(1,0)$: $g = 1$, $h = 6$, $f = 7$.
* $(0,1)$: $g = 1$, $h = 6$, $f = 7$.
* $(1,1)$: $g = 1$, $h = 5$, $f = 6$.

**OPEN = $\{(1,1), (1,0), (0,1)\}$ with $f = \{6, 7, 7\}$**.

**Expansion 2: Pop $(1,1)$ from OPEN** — $f = 6$. **(1 mark)**
Neighbours (non-obstacle): $(0,0)$[closed], $(1,0)$, $(0,1)$, $(2,1)$, $(1,2)$, $(2,2)$[obstacle], $(2,0)$, $(0,2)$.
Re-evaluate:
* $(1,0)$: existing $g=1$, new $g = 1 + 1 = 2$ → no update.
* $(0,1)$: existing $g=1$, new $g = 2$ → no update.
* $(2,1)$: $g = 2$, $h = 5$, $f = 7$. **Add.**
* $(1,2)$: $g = 2$, $h = 5$, $f = 7$. **Add.**
* $(2,0)$: $g = 2$, $h = 6$, $f = 8$. **Add.**
* $(0,2)$: $g = 2$, $h = 6$, $f = 8$. **Add.**

**OPEN = $\{(1,0), (0,1), (2,1), (1,2), (2,0), (0,2)\}$ with $f = \{7,7,7,7,8,8\}$**. **(1 mark)**

**Expansion 3: Pop $(1,0)$ (or $(0,1)$, tie)** — $f = 7$. **(1 mark)**
By symmetry, the algorithm continues to expand nodes with $f = 7$ (cost-so-far 2, Manhattan 5) before any with $f = 8$. The path progresses through $(2,1), (3,1), (4,2), (4,3), (4,4)$ (avoiding the obstacles).

**Final Optimal Path (one of several):** **(2 marks)**

$$
S(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,1) \to (4,2) \to (4,3) \to (4,4) = G
$$

* **Path length:** 6 steps.
* **Optimality check:** No shorter path exists without crossing obstacles. The Manhattan lower bound is 8; with diagonal moves the optimal is 4 diagonal steps = 4 (if diagonal cost = 1). With edge cost 1 uniformly, the optimal is 6 steps.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Q.B(b)):** Students often forget to **update the parent pointer** when re-discovering a node via a shorter path. They also confuse the OPEN-list ordering. **The third expanded node is critical** — write down its $f$, $g$, $h$ explicitly. Missing the parent-pointer update loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Universal Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps (all 14-mark questions):**
> 1. **Forgetting boundary condition** $U_{\text{rep}} = 0$ for $\rho > \rho_0$ → **−1 mark**.
> 2. **Writing $F = \nabla U$ instead of $-\nabla U$** (sign error in force direction) → **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing admissibility with consistency** in A* — they are not the same; consistency implies admissibility but not vice versa → **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the units** in numerical problems (Joules, Newtons, meters) → **−0.5 mark each**.
> 5. **Drawing graph diagrams without node IDs or with reserved keywords** (`end`, `graph`) → **−0.5 mark** in diagrams.
> 6. **Not labelling axes** on potential-field plots → **−1 mark** in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 7. **Failing to mention local-minima escape strategies** in any APF answer → **−2 marks** because it is a *mandatory* syllabus topic.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Path Planning: Master Recall List (Read this 5 minutes before the exam)**

* ✅ **Two planner classes:** *Deliberative (Global)* uses full map → A*, Dijkstra, Visibility Graph. *Reactive (Local)* uses sensors → APF, DWA, Bug, VFH.
* ✅ **Hybrid architecture** = global proposes + local refines; standard in modern autonomous vehicles.
* ✅ **C-space inflation** by robot radius converts a rigid body into a point (valid for disc robots).
* ✅ **Attractive potential:** $U_{\text{att}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \zeta \rho^2$ (conic, far field uses $\zeta \rho$).
* ✅ **Repulsive potential (Khatib):** $U_{\text{rep}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2$ **only for** $\rho \le \rho_0$, else $0$.
* ✅ **Force** is always $F = -\nabla U$ (negative gradient).
* ✅ **APF failure modes:** (1) Local minima trap, (2) Oscillations in narrow corridors, (3) No passage between closely spaced obstacles.
* ✅ **Local minima escape methods:** Random walk, virtual obstacle, navigation functions, wall-following, hybrid planning.
* ✅ **A\* evaluation:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.
* ✅ **Admissibility:** $h(n) \le h^*(n)$; **Consistency:** $h(n) \le w(n, n') + h(n')$.
* ✅ **Common admissible heuristics:** Euclidean ($\sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$), Manhattan ($\vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$).
* ✅ **Dijkstra** is A* with $h(n) = 0$. **BFS** is Dijkstra with uniform edge weight 1.
* ✅ **D\*** is dynamic A* for environments where edge costs change (used in Mars rovers).
* ✅ **Complexity cheat codes:** BFS $O(V+E)$, Dijkstra $O((V+E)\log V)$, A* worst-case exponential, often polynomial with good heuristic.
* ✅ **Real-world mapping:** Amazon AMRs → A*; Service robots → APF; Mars rovers → D* Lite; Highway AVs → DWA + Lane A*.
* ✅ **Examiner's favourite 7-marker:** *"Discuss local minima in APF with remedies"* — must include diagram + 2 remedies.
* ✅ **Examiner's favourite 14-marker:** *A* + APF comparison*, or *Derive both potentials and discuss hybrid planning*.
* ✅ **Units are mandatory:** J for potential, N for force, m for distance, s for time, N/m for $\zeta$, N·m² for $\eta$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" two algorithms, **always use a table** with at least 4 criteria (optimality, completeness, complexity, real-time performance). Examiners allocate marks row-by-row.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Map-Based Path Planning: Dijkstra's Algorithm, A*, D* Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Map-Based Path Planning: Dijkstra's Algorithm, A*, D* Algorithms # 🗺️ Module 4 — Map-Based Path Planning: Dijkstra's, A* and D* Algorithms ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Map-Based Path Planning (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > Map-based (or **global / deliberative**) path planning is the process of computing an **optimal collision-free trajectory** from a *start configuration* to a *goal configuration* using a **priori knowledge of the environment** represented as an occupancy grid, topological graph, or weighted road-map. The configuration space $C_{free}$ is searched using graph-search algorithms such as **Dijkstra, A*, and D***, where each cell or node $n$ is evaluated through a cost function that combines *path cost-so-far* and an *estimate of remaining cost-to-goal*. ### 1.2 The Three Algorithmic Pillars of Global Planning | Algorithm | Search Direction | Uses Heuristic? | Best Suited For | |---|---|---|---| | **Dijkstra** | Forward from start | ❌ No | Uniform-cost networks, dense graphs | | **A\*** | Forward from start | ✅ Yes (admissible) | Static known maps with grid / graph | | **D\*** | Backward from goal (dynamic) | ✅ Yes (admissible) | Partially known / changing environments | > [!NOTE] > **Key Metric — Optimality & Completeness:** > All three algorithms are **complete** (they find a path if one exists) and, under correct conditions, **optimal** (least-cost path). The trade-off is **computational complexity** — heuristic guidance drastically reduces node expansions. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-world analogy — Google Maps navigation:** > - **Dijkstra's** is like exploring *every* road within a 50 km radius of your house until you find the destination — slow but exhaustive. > - **A\*** is like checking the *straight-line distance* to the destination at every junction so you steer toward it — much faster because the GPS estimates "as the crow flies" distance. > - **D\*** is like having your phone **re-route on the fly** when a road becomes blocked by an accident — instead of recomputing from scratch, it surgically repairs only the affected portion of the route. > > The straight-line estimate is the **heuristic function $h(n)$**, and the sum of driven distance + estimate is the **evaluation function $f(n)$**. ### 1.4 Core Symbol Glossary | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | $g(n)$ | Actual (known) cost from start node to node $n$ | | $h(n)$ | **Heuristic** — estimated cost from $n$ to goal | | $f(n)$ | Total evaluation function: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | | $c(n, n')$ | Edge cost between $n$ and $n'$ | | $\text{rhs}(n)$ | (D*) One-step lookahead cost; update of $g$ | | $\text{OBSTACLE}, \text{FREE}$ | Map cell classifications | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Uniform-Cost Search ### Logic Steps - Initialize a **priority queue (OPEN list)** ordered by the current shortest distance $g(n)$. - Set $g(\text{start}) = 0$ and $g(n) = \infty$ for all others. - Repeatedly **pop the node with the smallest $g(n)$** (Lazy deletion handles revisited nodes). - For each successor $n'$ of the popped node $n$, compute a tentative distance $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n')$. - If the tentative distance is **smaller** than the recorded $g(n')$, update $g(n')$ and push into OPEN. - Terminate when **goal node is popped** (guaranteed shortest path). > [!NOTE] > **Why it works:** It is a discrete implementation of the **Bellman-Ford relaxation** on non-negative weights. Once a node is popped (its $g$ is finalized), no shorter path can ever be discovered, because all remaining open nodes have $g$-values $\geq$ the popped node. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}\bigl(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert\bigr)$ with a binary heap, where $\vert V \vert$ = number of cells/nodes, $\vert E \vert$ = number of edges. For an 8-connected grid of size $N \times N$, this becomes $\mathcal{O}(N^2 \log N)$. ### Why / How Analysis - **Why "Dijkstra" before A*:** Dijkstra is the **parent** of A*. Setting $h(n) = 0$ reduces A* to Dijkstra. - **How it differs from BFS:** BFS assumes unit costs; Dijkstra accepts **heterogeneous edge weights** (e.g., terrain slope, surface friction). - **Why slow:** Expands in *all directions equally* — wasteful when goal is in one corner. --- ## 2.2 A\* Algorithm — Heuristic-Guided Best-First Search ### Logic Steps - Maintain an **OPEN list (priority queue)** sorted by $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. - Maintain a **CLOSED list** of already expanded nodes to avoid re-expansion. - Pop node $n$ with the smallest $f(n)$. - If $n = \text{goal}$, **reconstruct path** by backtracking parents → DONE. - For each successor $n'$, compute tentative $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n')$ and $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$. - If $n'$ is already in CLOSED with a smaller $g$, **skip** (re-opening is suboptimal under a consistent heuristic). - Otherwise, update its $g, f, \text{parent}$ and push into OPEN. - Loop until goal popped or OPEN becomes empty (no path). ### Admissibility & Consistency > [!IMPORTANT] > **Admissible heuristic:** $h(n) \leq$ *true shortest distance* from $n$ to goal. Never over-estimates. > **Consistent (monotone) heuristic:** $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ for every successor $n'$ of $n$. Guarantees $f$ is non-decreasing along any path, so A* never needs to re-open closed nodes. ### Common Admissible Heuristics | Grid Type | Heuristic $h(n)$ | Optimal? | |---|---|---| | 4-connected | Manhattan: $\vert x_n - x_G \vert + \vert y_n - y_G \vert$ | Yes | | 8-connected | Diagonal (Chebyshev): $\max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ | Yes | | Any grid | Euclidean: $\sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | Yes | | Octile grid | $\max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert) + (\sqrt{2} - 1) \min(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ | Yes | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Euclidean is always admissible but may be *less informed* on 4-connected grids (allows diagonal moves in thought but robot cannot execute them). ### Why / How Analysis - **Why $f(n)$ works:** Among all nodes in OPEN with $f(n) \leq f^*$, A* is guaranteed to expand them before expanding any node with $f > f^*$, where $f^*$ = optimal path cost. This is the **optimality proof**. - **How tie-breaking matters:** When two nodes have identical $f$, breaking ties by *smaller $h$* (or larger $g$) expands fewer nodes — the **tie-breaking bias**. --- ## 2.3 D\* Algorithm — Dynamic Replanning ### Core Idea D\* (Dynamic A\*) runs A* **backwards** from the goal to the start. When the robot discovers a new obstacle mid-traversal, **only the affected portion of the cost map is repaired**, instead of restarting the search. ### Two Cost Functions for Every Node $$ \begin{aligned} g(n) &= \text{current best estimate of distance from } n \text{ to goal} \\ \text{rhs}(n) &= \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } n = \text{goal} \\ \min_{n' \in \text{Succ}(n)} \bigl( c(n, n') + g(n') \bigr) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{aligned} $$ - **Node consistency states:** - **Consistent:** $g(n) = \text{rhs}(n)$ - **Under-consistent (raise):** $g(n) < \text{rhs}(n)$ — the path got cheaper - **Over-consistent (lower):** $g(n) > \text{rhs}(n)$ — the path got costlier (obstacle) ### Replanning Trigger > [!IMPORTANT] > When an edge cost $c(n, n')$ **increases** (obstacle discovered), the algorithm: > 1. Sets $g(n) = \infty$ for the affected predecessor $n$. > 2. Recomputes $\text{rhs}(n)$ using the (now known) increased costs. > 3. Pushes $n$ into OPEN with key $k = [\min(g(n), \text{rhs}(n)) + h(n, \text{start}), \; \min(g(n), \text{rhs}(n))]$. > 4. Propagates changes *only downstream* until the start node is consistent again. > > This is called **focused dynamic repair** — and is the central innovation of D*. ### State-Space Reduction (Why D\* is Fast on Repairs) > [!TIP] > The cost repair in D* propagates **outward** from the change site. In contrast, re-running A* would scan the **entire reachable map**. Thus D* is preferred for large maps where environmental changes are localized (typical in mobile-robot exploration). ### Comparison with A* (Operational View) | Aspect | A* | D* | |---|---|---| | Initial search direction | Start → Goal | **Goal → Start** | | Path representation | Successor pointers | **Predecessor pointers** | | Replanning cost | Full re-search | **Local repair** | | Edge cost changes | Not handled | Designed for it | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — every KTU question pivots on them.** | # | Equation | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | A\* evaluation function | | 2 | $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n')$ | Edge relaxation (cost-to-come update) | | 3 | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ | Admissibility condition ($h^*$ = true cost) | | 4 | $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ | Consistency / monotonicity | | 5 | $\text{rhs}(n) = \min_{n' \in \text{Succ}(n)} \bigl[ c(n, n') + g(n') \bigr]$ | D\* one-step lookahead | | 6 | $h_{Manhattan}(n) = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ | Manhattan heuristic | | 7 | $h_{Euclid}(n) = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | Euclidean heuristic | | 8 | $k = [\min(g,\text{rhs}) + h, \; \min(g,\text{rhs})]$ | D\* OPEN-list priority key | | 9 | $\mathcal{O}\bigl(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert\bigr)$ | Time complexity (binary-heap Dijkstra / A*) | | 10 | $f^* = g(\text{goal})$ | Optimal path cost | > **KTU Mnemonic:** *Admissible + Consistent ⇒ Optimal + No Reopens.* ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics) use A\* on a 2-D occupancy grid. - **Autonomous cars** (Waymo, Cruise) use A\* / hybrid A\* on road-network graphs. - **Mars rovers** (Curiosity, Perseverance) and **Deep Space missions** use **D\*-Lite** for traversability-based replanning. - **Drone swarms** combine A\* with RRT\* for kinodynamic feasibility. - **Disaster-response robots** use D\* to handle collapsed-rubble dynamic maps. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Worked Example & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solved example for all three algorithms — same graph, same start (S), same goal (G).** ## 3.1 Reference Graph Weighted undirected graph with **5 nodes** and **admissible Euclidean-like heuristics**. | Edge | Cost | Edge | Cost | |---|---|---|---| | $S \leftrightarrow A$ | 1 | $A \leftrightarrow C$ | 3 | | $S \leftrightarrow B$ | 5 | $B \leftrightarrow C$ | 1 | | $A \leftrightarrow B$ | 2 | $B \leftrightarrow G$ | 9 | | | | $C \leftrightarrow G$ | 4 | | Node | $h(n)$ | |---|---| | $S$ | 5 | | $A$ | 4 | | $B$ | 4 | | $C$ | 2 | | $G$ | 0 | All $h$ values are **admissible** (verified: $h \leq$ true shortest distance to $G$). --- ## 3.2 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Manual Trace **Initialization:** $g(S) = 0$, $g(\text{all else}) = \infty$. OPEN priority queue = $\{S:0\}$. ### Iteration 1 — Pop $S$ (g = 0) $$ \begin{aligned} g(A) &= g(S) + c(S, A) = 0 + 1 = 1 \\ g(B) &= g(S) + c(S, B) = 0 + 5 = 5 \end{aligned} $$ OPEN = $\{A:1,\; B:5\}$ ### Iteration 2 — Pop $A$ (g = 1) $$ \begin{aligned} g(B) &= \min(5,\; g(A) + c(A, B)) = \min(5,\; 1+2) = 3 \quad \text{(improved)} \\ g(C) &= g(A) + c(A, C) = 1 + 3 = 4 \end{aligned} $$ OPEN = $\{B:3,\; C:4\}$ *(B's old entry of 5 still present but ignored when popped)* ### Iteration 3 — Pop $B$ (g = 3) $$ \begin{aligned} g(C) &= \min(4,\; g(B) + c(B, C)) = \min(4,\; 3+1) = 4 \quad \text{(no change)} \\ g(G) &= g(B) + c(B, G) = 3 + 9 = 12 \end{aligned} $$ OPEN = $\{C:4,\; G:12\}$ ### Iteration 4 — Pop $C$ (g = 4) $$ g(G) = \min(12,\; g(C) + c(C, G)) = \min(12,\; 4+4) = 8 \quad \text{(improved)} $$ OPEN = $\{G:8\}$ ### Iteration 5 — Pop $G$ (g = 8) → **GOAL REACHED** $$ \boxed{\text{Optimal path: } S \to A \to C \to G, \quad g(G) = 8} $$ --- ## 3.3 A\* Algorithm — Manual Trace on the Same Graph **Evaluation function:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. **Initialization:** $g(S) = 0$, $f(S) = 0 + 5 = 5$. OPEN = $\{S: f=5\}$. ### Iteration 1 — Pop $S$ (f = 5) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Successor } A:&\quad g = 0 + 1 = 1,\; f = 1 + 4 = 5 \\ \text{Successor } B:&\quad g = 0 + 5 = 5,\; f = 5 + 4 = 9 \end{aligned} $$ OPEN = $\{A: f=5,\; B: f=9\}$ ### Iteration 2 — Pop $A$ (f = 5) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Successor } B:&\quad g_{tent} = 1 + 2 = 3 < 5 \;\Rightarrow\; g(B) = 3,\; f(B) = 3 + 4 = 7 \\ \text{Successor } C:&\quad g = 1 + 3 = 4,\; f = 4 + 2 = 6 \end{aligned} $$ OPEN = $\{C: f=6,\; B: f=7\}$ *(B's stale f=9 entry discarded)* ### Iteration 3 — Pop $C$ (f = 6) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Successor } B:&\quad g_{tent} = 4 + 1 = 5 > 3 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{skip} \\ \text{Successor } G:&\quad g = 4 + 4 = 8,\; f = 8 + 0 = 8 \end{aligned} $$ OPEN = $\{B: f=7,\; G: f=8\}$ ### Iteration 4 — Pop $B$ (f = 7) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Successor } C:&\quad \text{already expanded (CLOSED)} \Rightarrow \text{skip} \\ \text{Successor } G:&\quad g_{tent} = 3 + 9 = 12 > 8 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{skip} \end{aligned} $$ ### Iteration 5 — Pop $G$ (f = 8) → **GOAL REACHED** $$ \boxed{\text{Optimal path: } S \to A \to C \to G, \quad g(G) = 8} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison insight:** Dijkstra expanded 4 nodes before reaching G. A\* also expanded 4 nodes here, but the **expansion was goal-directed** — note $C$ was popped *before* $B$ even though $g(B) < g(C)$, because the heuristic pulled $C$ toward the goal. In larger graphs with stronger heuristic dominance, A* can expand up to **10–100× fewer nodes**. --- ## 3.4 D\* — Dynamic Replanning Demonstration ### Initial State (Map as Above) D\* runs A\* **backwards** from $G$. The resulting predecessor pointers give the path: $$ G \leftarrow C \leftarrow A \leftarrow S $$ Cost: $g(G) = 8$, $g(C) = 4$, $g(A) = 1$, $g(S) = 0$. ### Robot Moves to Node $A$, then Discovers Edge $C \to G$ is Blocked (Obstacle) **Step 1 — Edge cost update:** $$ c(C, G) : 4 \;\longrightarrow\; \infty $$ **Step 2 — Affected predecessor of $G$ is $C$; set $g(G) = \text{rhs}(G) = 0$ unchanged but recompute $\text{rhs}(C)$:** $$ \text{rhs}(C) = \min\bigl[ c(C,A) + g(A),\; c(C,B) + g(B),\; c(C,G) + g(G) \bigr] $$ $$ \text{rhs}(C) = \min[3 + 1,\; 1 + 3,\; \infty + 8] = \min[4,\; 4,\; \infty] = 4 $$ So $\text{rhs}(C) = 4 = g(C)$ → $C$ remains consistent. **BUT** propagate upward: $$ \text{rhs}(A) = \min[1 + 0,\; 2 + 3,\; 3 + 4] = \min[1,\; 5,\; 7] = 1 $$ Wait — $A$ is still consistent. Let's instead **raise** $g(A)$ because the alternative path through $B$ is the new best: **Step 3 — Replan affected ancestor: $A$'s cost must now go through $B$.** $$ \text{rhs}(A) = \min\bigl[ c(A,S) + g(S),\; c(A,B) + g(B),\; c(A,C) + g(C) \bigr] = \min[1, 5, 7] = 1 $$ Since $\text{rhs}(A) = 1 = g(A)$, $A$ is **still consistent**. Now check $S$: $$ \text{rhs}(S) = \min\bigl[ c(S,A) + g(A),\; c(S,B) + g(B) \bigr] = \min[1, 8] = 1 $$ But $g(S) = 0$ originally — wait, with reverse costs, the **current optimal** from $S$ to $G$ now is: $$ S \to A \to C \to B \to \cdots? $$ Let me recompute properly: with edge $C \to G$ blocked, the new shortest path is: $$ S \xrightarrow{1} A \xrightarrow{2} B \xrightarrow{9} G = 12 $$ **D\* only repairs nodes on the affected frontier** ($C$ and $G$ get re-inserted into OPEN with raised keys; $A$ and $S$ are rechecked only via $\text{rhs}$ recomputation). The algorithm terminates when the start node is consistent: $$ g(S) = 12, \quad \text{New path: } S \to A \to B \to G $$ > [!TIP] > **Time saved:** A* restart would re-expand all 5 nodes. D* reused the previous tree and modified only $C$, $A$, and $B$ — saving ~40 % computation. --- ## 3.5 Comparative Performance Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Favorite Comparison Table — Memorize this.** | Property | Dijkstra | A\* | D\* | |---|---|---|---| | Uses heuristic $h(n)$ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Search direction | Start → Goal | Start → Goal | **Goal → Start** | | Optimality guarantee | ✅ Always | ✅ If $h$ admissible | ✅ If $h$ admissible | | Completeness | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Replanning | Not supported | Not supported | **Yes, dynamic** | | Node expansions | Most | Fewer (heuristic-guided) | Fewest on replanning | | Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(V \log V + E)$ | $\mathcal{O}(V \log V + E)$ | Same, but incremental | | Best application | Road networks | Static maps | Dynamic / unknown terrain | --- ## 3.6 Worked Grid Example (Occupancy Grid — 8-connected) Consider a $5 \times 5$ grid where shaded cells are obstacles. Start $S = (0,0)$, Goal $G = (4,4)$. Movement cost = 1 for 4-neighbors, $\sqrt{2}$ for diagonals. Heuristic: Euclidean. For a board problem, list the **trace table**: | Step | OPEN (sorted by $f$) | Pop | $g$ updates | $f$ updates | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\{S: f = 5.66\}$ | S | $g(A) = 1, g(B) = 1$ | $f(A) = 4.83, f(B) = 4.83$ | | 2 | $\{A:4.83,\; B:4.83\}$ | A | $g(C) = 2.41, g(D) = 3$ | $f(C)=4.24, f(D)=4.83$ | | 3 | $\{C:4.24,\; B:4.83,\; D:4.83\}$ | C | $g(E) = 3.83, g(F)=4.24$ | $f(E)=4.30, f(F)=4.65$ | | … | … | … | … | … | The student should continue this trace until $G$ is popped. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 A\* Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start]) --> initA[Initialize g of start to 0
Set f of start to h of start] initA --> openA[Push start into OPEN list] openA --> loopA{OPEN list empty?} loopA -- Yes --> failA[Return FAILURE no path] loopA -- No --> popA[Pop node n with minimum f of n] popA --> isGoalA{Is n the goal?} isGoalA -- Yes --> pathA[Reconstruct path by
backtracking parents] pathA --> doneA([Goal reached]) isGoalA -- No --> markA[Move n to CLOSED list] markA --> succA[For each successor s of n] succA --> calcA[Compute g of s equals g of n plus c of n and s
Compute f of s equals g of s plus h of s] calcA --> checkA{Is s in CLOSED
with smaller g?} checkA -- Yes --> skipA[Skip s] checkA -- No --> updateA[Update parent and g of s
Update f of s
Push or update s in OPEN] updateA --> loopA skipA --> loopA ``` ## 4.2 D\* Replanning Logic (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD initD([Initial Planning]) --> backD[Run A star backwards
from goal to start
Compute g and rhs for all nodes] backD --> moveD[Robot moves along path] moveD --> senseD{Sensor detects
edge cost change?} senseD -- No --> moveD senseD -- Yes --> updD[Update edge cost c of affected edge] updD --> rhsD[Recompute rhs of predecessor nodes] rhsD --> checkD{Any node has
g not equal rhs?} checkD -- No --> moveD checkD -- Yes --> insD[Insert affected nodes
into OPEN with raised keys] insD --> propD[Propagate changes
via Expand State process] propD --> startD{Start node
consistent?} startD -- No --> propD startD -- Yes --> pathD[Output updated path] pathD --> moveD ``` ## 4.3 Algorithm Comparison Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DIJK["DIJKSTRA"] d1[g equals f] d2[Uniform expansion] d3[No heuristic] end subgraph ASTAR["A STAR"] a1[f equals g plus h] a2[Goal directed expansion] a3[Admissible heuristic required] end subgraph DSTAR["D STAR"] s1[Backward search goal to start] s2[Dynamic cost repair] s3[Uses rhs and g inconsistency] end MAP[Known Map Representation] --> DIJK MAP --> ASTAR MAP --> DSTAR DIJK -.->|Adds heuristic h| ASTAR ASTAR -.->|Adds dynamic repair| DSTAR ``` ## 4.4 Map Representation & Cost Propagation (Grid View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph GRID["OCCUPANCY GRID 5 by 5"] c00[Start S g equals 0] c01[Cell A g equals 1] c02[Cell B g equals 2] c03[Cell C g equals 3] c04[Cell D g equals 4] c10[Cell E] c11[Cell F] c12[Cell G] c13[Cell H] c14[Cell I] c20[Cell J] c21[Cell K] c22[Cell L] c23[Cell M] c24[Cell N] c30[Cell O] c31[Cell P] c32[Cell Q] c33[Cell R] c34[Cell S2] c40[Cell T] c41[Cell U] c42[Cell V] c43[Cell W] c44[Goal G g equals 8] end c00 ==> c01 ==> c02 ==> c03 ==> c04 c04 ==> c14 ==> c24 ==> c34 ==> c44 ``` ## 4.5 State Consistency Diagram for D\* ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Consistent Consistent --> OverConsistent: rhs increased
edge cost up OverConsistent --> Consistent: Recompute rhs
and propagate Consistent --> UnderConsistent: rhs decreased
edge cost down UnderConsistent --> Consistent: Recompute rhs
and propagate OverConsistent --> OverConsistent: Cascade raise UnderConsistent --> UnderConsistent: Cascade lower ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > Part A carries **3 marks** each (short answer). Part B carries **14 marks** each with internal choice — two 7-mark sub-parts per question. Total per module-end exam: usually one full Part B question (14 marks) on this topic. --- ## 📘 Part A — 3-Mark Questions ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the A\* evaluation function $f(n)$. State the conditions under which A\* guarantees an optimal path.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The A\* evaluation function is defined as: $$ f(n) = g(n) + h(n) $$ where $g(n)$ is the actual cost from start to $n$, and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate from $n$ to the goal. **Optimality conditions (3 marks total, distributed as):** 1. The heuristic $h(n)$ must be **admissible**, i.e., $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$, where $h^*(n)$ is the true shortest cost to the goal. 2. (For stronger guarantees) The heuristic should be **consistent / monotone**: $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ for every successor $n'$. Consistency guarantees no re-expansion of closed nodes. 3. Edge costs must be **non-negative**. When these hold, A\* expands nodes in order of increasing $f$ and returns the optimal path. **(Full 3 marks)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between Dijkstra's algorithm and A\* algorithm in terms of heuristic usage and computational efficiency.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Dijkstra | A\* | |---|---|---| | Heuristic | Not used ($h(n)=0$ for all) | Uses heuristic $h(n)$ | | Node expansion | Uniform in all directions | Biased toward goal | | Optimality | Always optimal | Optimal if $h$ is admissible | | Efficiency | Slow on large graphs | Faster when $h$ is informed | **Key sentence for full marks:** A\* is Dijkstra with a non-zero heuristic; it expands fewer nodes because the heuristic guides the search toward the goal. **(Full 3 marks)** --- ## 📕 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` #### **(a)** Explain Dijkstra's algorithm with a suitable example graph of at least 6 nodes. Show the trace table and compute the shortest path from start $S$ to goal $G$. **(7 marks)** #### **(b)** Explain the A\* algorithm. Discuss admissibility and consistency of the heuristic. Give an example showing how A\* differs from Dijkstra in node expansion. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question A:** #### **(a) Dijkstra's Algorithm** **Definition (1 mark):** Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a single source to all other nodes in a graph with **non-negative edge weights**, using a priority queue ordered by cumulative cost $g(n)$. **Graph (2 marks):** Consider the graph with nodes $\{S, A, B, C, D, G\}$ and edges: - $S\!-\!A:1$, $S\!-\!B:4$, $A\!-\!B:2$, $A\!-\!C:5$, $A\!-\!D:12$, $B\!-\!C:2$, $C\!-\!D:3$, $C\!-\!G:7$, $D\!-\!G:3$. **Trace Table (3 marks):** | Step | Popped Node | $g$ (pop) | Updated $g$ for neighbors | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | S | 0 | $g(A)=1, g(B)=4$ | | 2 | A | 1 | $g(B)=\min(4,1+2)=3, g(C)=1+5=6, g(D)=13$ | | 3 | B | 3 | $g(C)=\min(6,3+2)=5$ | | 4 | C | 5 | $g(D)=\min(13,5+3)=8, g(G)=5+7=12$ | | 5 | D | 8 | $g(G)=\min(12,8+3)=11$ | | 6 | G | 11 | Goal reached | **Shortest path (1 mark):** $S \to A \to B \to C \to D \to G$ with cost $1 + 2 + 2 + 3 + 3 = 11$. #### **(b) A\* Algorithm** **Definition (2 marks):** A\* is a best-first search that uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ as its priority. It combines the cost-so-far with a heuristic estimate of the cost-to-go. **Admissibility & Consistency (3 marks):** - **Admissible:** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ — never over-estimates. - **Consistent:** $h(n) \leq c(n,n') + h(n')$ for all successors — guarantees monotonic $f$ along any path. **Example (2 marks):** On the same graph with $h(S)=11, h(A)=9, h(B)=7, h(C)=4, h(D)=3, h(G)=0$: | Step | Pop | $f$ | Updates | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | S | 11 | A: $f=1+9=10$, B: $f=4+7=11$ | | 2 | A | 10 | B: $f=3+7=10$, C: $f=6+4=10$, D: $f=13+3=16$ | | 3 | B | 10 | C: $f=5+4=9$ (improved) | | 4 | C | 9 | D: $f=8+3=11$, G: $f=12+0=12$ | | 5 | D | 11 | G: $f=11+0=11$ (improved) | | 6 | G | 11 | Goal | **Difference vs Dijkstra (2 marks):** Dijkstra popped strictly in $g$-order (S, A, B, C, D). A\* reordered C before D because its lower $h$ made $f(C)$ smaller, demonstrating goal-directed expansion. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` #### **(a)** Describe the D\* algorithm. How does it differ from A\* in handling dynamic environments? Show with a small example how replanning occurs when a newly detected obstacle blocks an edge. **(7 marks)** #### **(b)** Compare Dijkstra, A\*, and D\* algorithms in terms of (i) search direction, (ii) heuristic usage, (iii) replanning capability, and (iv) computational complexity. When would you choose each algorithm in a real mobile-robot application? **(7 marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question B:** #### **(a) D\* Algorithm** **Definition (2 marks):** D\* (Dynamic A\*) is a path-planning algorithm designed for **partially known or changing environments**. It performs an initial A\* search **backwards from the goal** to the start, storing predecessor pointers and the cost-to-go $g(n)$ at each node. When the robot discovers new obstacles mid-traversal, D\* **surgically repairs** only the affected portion of the cost map using the one-step lookahead value $\text{rhs}(n)$. **Key equations (2 marks):** $$ g(n) = \text{current best estimate of cost from } n \text{ to goal} $$ $$ \text{rhs}(n) = \min_{n' \in \text{Succ}(n)} \bigl[ c(n, n') + g(n') \bigr] $$ A node is *consistent* when $g(n) = \text{rhs}(n)$. An *over-consistent* node ($g > \text{rhs}$) signals an obstacle-induced cost increase. **Difference from A\* (2 marks):** | Aspect | A\* | D\* | |---|---|---| | Direction | Forward (start → goal) | Backward (goal → start) | | Replanning | Full re-search required | **Local repair** | | Replanning trigger | Not supported | Edge-cost change | **Example (1 mark):** Suppose the robot traversed $S \to A$ and now detects that edge $C \to G$ is blocked ($c(C,G):4 \to \infty$). D\* raises $g(C)$ from 4 to $\text{rhs}(C)=\min(3+1,1+3)=4$ — wait, $g(C)$ needs to be reconsidered through $B \to G$. The repair propagates and the new path becomes $S \to A \to B \to G$ with cost 12. #### **(b) Comparative Analysis** **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Property | Dijkstra | A\* | D\* | |---|---|---|---| | (i) Search direction | Start → Goal | Start → Goal | **Goal → Start** | | (ii) Heuristic | None | Admissible $h$ | Admissible $h$ | | (iii) Replanning | Not supported | Not supported | **Yes, dynamic** | | (iv) Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(V\log V + E)$ | $\mathcal{O}(V\log V + E)$ | Same, incremental | **Application Choice (3 marks):** - **Dijkstra:** Offline road-network routing (Google Maps without traffic). - **A\*:** Indoor warehouse AMR on a fixed occupancy grid. - **D\*:** Mars rover / disaster-response robot in partially unknown terrain. --- ## 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks on this topic (based on KTU 2018–2024 past trends):** > 1. **Confusing $g$ and $f$:** Writing $f = $ cost-to-go instead of $f = g + h$. (–2 marks) > 2. **Non-admissible heuristic claims:** Stating Euclidean is *always* best — it is admissible but **not** most-informed on 4-connected grids. (–1 mark) > 3. **D\* treated as forward A\*:** D\* is **backward**. Examiners explicitly penalize wrong search direction. (–3 marks) > 4. **Skipping the trace table:** A 7-mark sub-part on Dijkstra/A\* requires the **OPEN list trace** — describing it in prose without iteration steps loses 2–3 marks. > 5. **Forgetting consistency condition:** Many students write only admissibility. Consistency is **separately** required for "no reopens" guarantee. (–1 mark) > 6. **Mixing up D\* with D\*-Lite:** The original D\* (Stentz 1994) is rarely asked; if asked, students confuse it with the simplified D*-Lite (Koenig & Likhachev 2002). > 7. **No mention of $c(n,n') \geq 0$:** Dijkstra requires non-negative weights. Forgetting this is a common 1-mark deduction. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-density rapid-revision checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4:** - ⭐ **Map-based planning** = global path planning using a priori maps (grids, roadmaps, topological graphs). - ⭐ **Three algorithms:** Dijkstra (uniform), A\* (heuristic-guided), D\* (dynamic replanning). - ⭐ **Core equation:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — memorize this verbatim. - ⭐ **Admissibility:** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ — never over-estimate. - ⭐ **Consistency:** $h(n) \leq c(n,n') + h(n')$ — guarantees monotonic $f$ and no re-expansions. - ⭐ **Dijkstra = A\* with $h(n) = 0$.** - ⭐ **D\* searches backwards** (goal → start) and uses **two cost values**: $g(n)$ and $\text{rhs}(n)$. - ⭐ **Replanning in D\*:** When edge cost changes, set $g$ of affected predecessor to $\infty$, recompute $\text{rhs}$, propagate via OPEN list. - ⭐ **Heuristics for grids:** Manhattan (4-connected), Chebyshev/Diagonal (8-connected), Euclidean (any). - ⭐ **Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(V \log V + E)$ for Dijkstra/A\* with binary heap. - ⭐ **Real-world usage:** Warehouses → A\*, Mars rovers → D\*, Road networks → Dijkstra. - ⭐ **Key paper to cite (KTU viva):** Hart, Nilsson & Raphael, *"A Formal Basis for the Heuristic Determination of Minimum Cost Paths"* (1968) — origin of A\*. - ⭐ **D\* inventor:** Anthony Stentz, 1994 ("Optimal and Efficient Path Planning for Partially Known Environments"). - ⭐ **CLOSED list** in A\* prevents redundant expansion; **OPEN list** is the priority queue. - ⭐ **Tie-breaking bias:** On equal $f$, prefer larger $g$ (closer to goal) to expand fewer nodes. - ⭐ **A\* is optimally efficient** — no other algorithm using the same heuristic expands fewer nodes. ---

topics.title: Obstacle Avoidance: Bug Algorithm, Vector Field Histogram, Dynamic Window Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET756 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Obstacle Avoidance: Bug Algorithm, Vector Field Histogram, Dynamic Window Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Obstacle Avoidance — Formal Definition

**Obstacle Avoidance** in mobile robotics is the local decision-making capability that enables an autonomous agent to traverse a partially or fully unknown workspace by continuously modulating its motion commands (linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$) in response to perceived obstacles sensed through onboard range finders (ultrasonic, LiDAR, depth cameras) or proximity sensors. The objective is to guarantee **collision-free navigation** while making **bounded progress** toward a goal configuration $(x_g, y_g, \theta_g)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Obstacle avoidance is classified as a *reactive* or *local planning* layer that sits beneath the global planner. The three algorithms mandated in Module 4 — **Bug**, **VFH**, and **DWA** — span the design spectrum from purely **sensor-driven reactive** (Bug) to **geometric-statistical** (VFH) to **kinodynamically constrained** (DWA) approaches.

### 1.1.1 The Bug Family (Bug 0, Bug 1, Bug 2)

The **Bug algorithms** are a family of simple, sensor-based reactive planners introduced by Lumelsky and Stepanov (1987). The robot is modeled as a **point** with a **contact sensor** (or finite-range range sensor), operating in a 2D planar workspace with **static** obstacles of arbitrary shape.

| Variant | Behaviour | Optimality |
|---|---|---|
| **Bug 0** | Move toward goal; if blocked, follow obstacle boundary; resume motion to goal | Non-optimal |
| **Bug 1** | Complete the entire obstacle boundary; pick the leave-point closest to goal | Locally optimal leave-point |
| **Bug 2** | Use a virtual $m$-line from start to goal; circumnavigate until the line is recrossed closer to the goal | Tighter bound than Bug 1 |

### 1.1.2 Vector Field Histogram (VFH)

The **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)**, developed by Borenstein and Koren (1991), is a *real-time* obstacle avoidance method for fast mobile robots. It builds a **2D Cartesian occupancy grid** from ultrasonic sensor readings, compresses it into a **1D polar histogram** $H$ of angular sector densities, applies a **smoothing filter**, and then selects a **candidate valley** below a threshold $H_{th}$ that best aligns with the goal direction.

### 1.1.3 Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)

The **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)**, proposed by Fox, Burgard and Thrun (1997), directly searches the **velocity space** $(v, \omega)$ reachable within one control cycle $\Delta t$, subject to:
- Admissible velocities (collision-free within the next cycle).
- Dynamic window (reachable given acceleration limits).
- The optimal $(v^*, \omega^*)$ maximizes a weighted multi-objective function of *heading*, *clearance*, and *velocity*.

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are blindfolded, walking through a cluttered room toward a door, holding a cane (touch sensor):**

- **Bug Algorithm** — You tap the wall with your cane, then *hug* it with your hand until your other hand (compass) says "the door is now straight ahead" or "this is the shortest detour", and you let go and walk straight again. Pure **touch-and-go instinct**, no map.
- **VFH** — You sweep a fan of radar beams (like a rotating flashlight) around you, count how many echoes come from each direction, build a *mental angular bar chart*, ignore the noisy/risky directions, then pick the *clearest gap* that points roughly toward the door. **Think radar operator on a ship.**
- **DWA** — Instead of choosing a direction, you choose a *speed and a turn rate* that the wheelchair can physically achieve in the next second without hitting anything, and you re-evaluate 20 times per second. **Think Formula 1 driver choosing a steering + throttle combo every 100 ms.**

### 1.3 Core Algorithmic Metrics (Highlighted)

The following performance metrics recur across all three algorithms and are **favourite KTU exam keywords**:

- **Path length** $L_{path} = \sum_{k} \sqrt{\Delta x_k^2 + \Delta y_k^2}$
- **Clearance** $c(q)$: distance from robot centre to the nearest obstacle in configuration $q$.
- **Heading** $\theta_{goal} - \theta_{robot}$: alignment with goal direction.
- **Velocity** $v, \omega$: linear and angular control inputs (units: **m/s** and **rad/s**).
- **Sensor range** $r_{max}$ (typical LiDAR: **30 m**; ultrasonic: **0.2 – 4 m**).
- **Control cycle** $\Delta t$ (DWA typical: **0.1 s**).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bug Algorithm — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Bug 0 (Greedy Wall Follower)

1. Compute direction to goal. Drive straight toward goal.
2. If a hit-point $q_{hit}$ is detected (contact sensor triggers), transition to **boundary-following** mode.
3. Follow the obstacle boundary in a fixed handedness (say, clockwise) until a leave-point $q_{leave}$ is reached such that the straight line to goal is clear.
4. Resume straight motion.

> [!NOTE]
> Bug 0 may **loop infinitely** on certain concave obstacles; it is presented mainly for didactic clarity.

### 2.1.2 Bug 1 (Complete Boundary with Optimal Leave)

- At each $q_{hit}$, the robot traces the **entire closed boundary** of the obstacle, recording the point $q_{leave}$ on the boundary that minimises the Euclidean distance to the goal:

$$q_{leave}^{*} = \arg\min_{q \,\in\, \partial O} \; \Vert q - q_{goal}\Vert_2$$

- Worst-case performance bound (Lumelsky):

$$L_{bug1} \;\le\; n \cdot P_{hi} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

where $n$ is the number of obstacle hits, $P_{hi}$ is the perimeter of the $i^{th}$ obstacle, and $p_i$ is the straight-line path from $q_{leave,i-1}$ to $q_{hit,i}$.

### 2.1.3 Bug 2 (m-line Strategy)

- The **$m$-line** is the virtual line segment from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$.
- The robot moves along the $m$-line. On hitting an obstacle, it follows the boundary until it **re-crosses** the $m$-line at a point **closer to the goal** than $q_{hit}$. It then leaves the boundary and resumes $m$-line motion.
- Performance bound:

$$L_{bug2} \;\le\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} d_i$$

where $d_i$ is the distance between consecutive leave-points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Bug 2 usually shorter than Bug 1?** Because Bug 1 always traces the *full* perimeter, while Bug 2 leaves at the *first favourable crossing* of the $m$-line. In the worst case they are equal, but in practice Bug 2 wins on most non-convex obstacles.

---

## 2.2 Vector Field Histogram (VFH) — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 The Three-Stage Pipeline

**Stage 1 — Cartesian Occupancy Grid Update**
Each range reading $d_i$ from sensor $i$ at angular position $\beta_i$ is mapped onto a 2D grid of cells of size $C$ (typically $C = 100$ mm). A cell is marked occupied with certainty $c$ depending on the reading and robot radius $R_r$.

**Stage 2 — Polar Histogram Construction**
The grid is divided into $n_s$ angular sectors of width $\alpha = 360^\circ / n_s$ (typical $n_s = 72 \Rightarrow \alpha = 5^\circ$). The angular sector $k$ accumulates a *certainty value*:

$$H_k \;=\; \sum_{i \,:\, \beta_i \,\in\, \text{sector } k} \; \left( c_{i,j} - c_{\text{thresh}} \right)^2$$

where the sum runs over all occupied cells in sector $k$, $c_{i,j}$ is the certainty of cell $(i,j)$, and $c_{\text{thresh}}$ is a lower bound (typically **0.2 to 0.5**).

**Stage 3 — Smoothing and Valley Selection**
A weighted moving-average filter of window $l$ smooths $H_k$. The threshold $H_{th}$ is then applied, and the **candidate valleys** (contiguous angular runs of sectors with $H_k < H_{th}$) are extracted. The valley whose **centroid direction** $k_{c,t}$ is closest to the goal direction $k_{goal}$ is selected:

$$k_{c,t} \;=\; \arg\min_{k \,\in\, \text{valleys}} \;\vert k - k_{goal} \vert$$

The robot's steering command is then:

$$\theta_{steer} \;=\; \frac{(k_{c,t} - n_s / 2) \cdot 360^\circ}{n_s}$$

### 2.2.2 Variants: VFH+ and VFH*

- **VFH+** accounts for the robot's **physical dimensions** and trajectory curvature by considering not only the current cell but the **swept volume** over a prediction horizon.
- **VFH*** uses a **two-level threshold** ($H_{free}$, $H_{blocked}$) and a binary passability map, improving performance in narrow corridors.

---

## 2.3 Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) — Operational Theory

### 2.3.1 Velocity Search Space

The robot's dynamic model is the unicycle:

$$\dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

The admissible velocity space $V_a$ is the set of $(v, \omega)$ that can be commanded *and* permit a **collision-free arc** within one prediction horizon. The **dynamic window** $V_d$ is the set reachable in $\Delta t$ given acceleration limits $(\dot v_{max}, \dot\omega_{max})$:

$$V_d \;=\; \left\{ (v,\omega) \,\Big\vert\, v \,\in\, [v_a - \dot v_{max}\Delta t,\; v_a + \dot v_{max}\Delta t] \;\wedge\; \omega \,\in\, [\omega_a - \dot\omega_{max}\Delta t,\; \omega_a + \dot\omega_{max}\Delta t] \right\}$$

The final search space is $V_s = V_a \cap V_d$.

### 2.3.2 Objective Function

For each candidate $(v, \omega) \in V_s$, the trajectory is **simulated** for a short horizon. Three sub-goals are evaluated:

$$G(v,\omega) \;=\; \sigma\!\left[ \alpha \cdot heading(v,\omega) \;+\; \beta \cdot dist(v,\omega) \;+\; \gamma \cdot velocity(v,\omega) \right]$$

where:
- $heading(v,\omega) = \pi - \angle(\text{goal} - \text{robot head at end of arc})$
- $dist(v,\omega)$ = distance to closest obstacle on the arc
- $velocity(v,\omega) = v$ (rewards faster forward motion)
- $\sigma$ is a normalisation operator; $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are positive weights.

The optimal control is $(v^*, \omega^*) = \arg\max_{(v,\omega) \in V_s} G(v,\omega)$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_{hit}$ | Point where robot first contacts obstacle | metres (m) |
| 2 | $q_{leave}^{*} = \arg\min_{q} \Vert q - q_{goal}\Vert$ | Bug 1 optimal leave-point | m |
| 3 | $L_{bug1} \le n P_{hi} + \sum p_i$ | Bug 1 worst-case path length | m |
| 4 | $L_{bug2} \le \sum p_i + \sum d_i$ | Bug 2 worst-case path length | m |
| 5 | $H_k = \sum (c_{i,j} - c_{thresh})^2$ | VFH polar histogram density for sector $k$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\alpha = 360^\circ / n_s$ | VFH sector width | degrees |
| 7 | $H_{th}$ | VFH valley threshold (tuneable) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $V_a$ | Admissible (collision-free) velocity set | pairs of (m/s, rad/s) |
| 9 | $V_d = [v_a \pm \dot v_{max}\Delta t] \times [\omega_a \pm \dot\omega_{max}\Delta t]$ | Dynamic window | (m/s, rad/s) |
| 10 | $V_s = V_a \cap V_d$ | DWA total search space | (m/s, rad/s) |
| 11 | $G = \sigma(\alpha h + \beta d + \gamma v)$ | DWA objective function | dimensionless |
| 12 | $heading = \pi - \angle(\vec{goal} - \vec{pose}_{end})$ | DWA alignment term | radians |
| 13 | $\Delta t$ | DWA control cycle | seconds (typical **0.1 s**) |
| 14 | $r_{max}$ | Sensor maximum range | m (LiDAR ≈ **30 m**) |
| 15 | $C$ | Grid cell size in VFH | m (typical **0.1 m**) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bug algorithms**: Deployed in **low-cost cleaning robots** (Roomba 500/600 series) and **search-and-rescue** micro-UGVs with minimal sensing.
- **VFH**: Used in **wheelchair guidance systems**, **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle)** warehouses (Kiva/Amazon Robotics derivatives), and historically the **Nomad 200** outdoor robot.
- **DWA**: The de-facto local planner in the **ROS Navigation Stack** (`base_local_planner` / `dwa_local_planner`); used in **TurtleBot**, **Husarion ROSbot**, **Service robots in hospitals** (like HSR from Toyota), and **autonomous forklifts**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are written for board examination. Each algebraic transition is justified by a one-line physical or geometric explanation. Equations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` for full marks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bug 2 Path Length Upper Bound

**Statement to prove:** For a Bug 2 robot navigating a workspace with $n$ disjoint convex obstacles of perimeters $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$, the total path length is bounded by

$$L_{bug2} \;\le\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} d_i$$

where $d_i$ is the Euclidean distance between the leave-point of obstacle $i$ and the hit-point of obstacle $i+1$.

**Step 1 — Decompose the path into alternating segments.**

The Bug 2 path is a concatenation of two alternating types of motion segments:

- **Boundary-following segments** of lengths $b_1, b_2, \dots, b_n$ (one per obstacle).
- **$m$-line traversal segments** of lengths $m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n$ (one before each hit, plus one final to goal).

So:

$$L_{bug2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$$

**Step 2 — Bound each boundary segment by the full perimeter.**

For a **convex** obstacle, the boundary arc traced between hit and leave is at most the full perimeter:

$$b_i \;\le\; P_i$$

Summing over all obstacles:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \;\le\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$

**Step 3 — Bound $m$-line segments by inter-hit distances.**

The straight-line distance from the leave-point of obstacle $i$ to the hit-point of obstacle $i+1$ is, by triangle inequality, **less than or equal to** the actual $m$-line segment traversed (since the leave-point lies on the $m$-line closer to goal than the previous hit):

$$m_i \;\le\; d_i$$

Adding the final $m$-line segment $m_n \le d_n = $ straight line to goal:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \;\le\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} d_i + d_n$$

**Step 4 — Combine both bounds.**

$$L_{bug2} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \;\le\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} d_i + d_n$$

Dropping the final $d_n$ for a tighter **worst-case** bound used in the KTU textbook:

$$\boxed{L_{bug2} \;\le\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} d_i}$$

**Geometric interpretation:** The bound says the robot traverses, in the worst case, *every perimeter once* and *the gap between obstacles once* — i.e., it never has to double back over a perimeter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — VFH Polar Histogram Density

**Statement:** Given a Cartesian grid of cell size $C$ and an ultrasonic sensor reading $d_i$ at bearing $\beta_i$, derive the contribution of cell $(i,j)$ to the polar histogram sector $k$.

**Step 1 — Compute cell coordinates from the sensor reading.**

The sensor is mounted at the robot centre. The reading $d_i$ falls at world coordinates:

$$x_i = x_r + d_i \cos\beta_i, \quad y_i = y_r + d_i \sin\beta_i$$

**Step 2 — Convert to grid indices.**

$$r_i = \left\lfloor \frac{x_i - x_{origin}}{C} \right\rfloor, \quad c_i = \left\lfloor \frac{y_i - y_{origin}}{C} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 3 — Assign to angular sector.**

The sector index $k$ containing this cell is:

$$k \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{1}{\alpha}\left(\text{atan2}(y_i - y_r,\; x_i - x_r)\right) \right\rfloor \mod n_s$$

**Step 4 — Accumulate the squared excess certainty.**

For every occupied cell $(r, c)$ whose distance from the robot is less than $r_{max}$, we increment:

$$H_k \;\leftarrow\; H_k + (c_{r,c} - c_{thresh})^2$$

which after summation gives:

$$H_k \;=\; \sum_{(r,c) \,\in\, \text{sector } k} (c_{r,c} - c_{thresh})^2$$

**Step 5 — Apply smoothing filter (l-point moving average).**

$$\hat H_k \;=\; \frac{1}{2l+1} \sum_{j=-l}^{+l} H_{k+j}$$

Smoothing eliminates spurious peaks caused by specular reflections from ultrasonic sensors — this is a key KTU point.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — DWA Objective Function Decomposition

**Statement:** Show how the DWA objective function $G(v,\omega)$ rewards heading alignment, obstacle clearance, and forward velocity, then derive the optimal $(v^*, \omega^*)$.

**Step 1 — Heading term.**

The robot's final pose on the simulated arc $(v, \omega)$ over horizon $T$ is:

$$x_{end} = x + v \cos\theta \cdot T - \frac{v}{\omega}\sin\theta \cdot (\omega T) \cdot \dots$$

More compactly, the angle to the goal at the end of the arc is $\phi = \text{atan2}(y_g - y_{end},\; x_g - x_{end})$. The heading term is:

$$h(v,\omega) \;=\; \pi - \vert \theta_{end} - \phi \vert$$

Normalised as:

$$\hat h(v,\omega) \;=\; \frac{h(v,\omega)}{\pi} \in [0, 1]$$

**Step 2 — Distance (clearance) term.**

The minimum distance to any obstacle along the simulated arc is:

$$d(v,\omega) \;=\; \min_{t \,\in\, [0, T]} \; \text{dist}(\text{pose}(t), \;\mathcal{O})$$

Normalised by sensor range:

$$\hat d(v,\omega) \;=\; \frac{d(v,\omega)}{r_{max}}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity term.**

$$v_{term}(v,\omega) \;=\; \frac{v}{v_{max}} \in [0, 1]$$

**Step 4 — Combine into weighted sum.**

$$G(v,\omega) \;=\; \alpha \cdot \hat h(v,\omega) \;+\; \beta \cdot \hat d(v,\omega) \;+\; \gamma \cdot v_{term}(v,\omega)$$

**Step 5 — Optimise over the search space $V_s$.**

The continuous optimum is:

$$(v^*, \omega^*) \;=\; \arg\max_{(v,\omega) \,\in\, V_s} \; G(v,\omega)$$

In practice, $V_s$ is **discretised** into a grid of $N_v \times N_\omega$ cells (e.g., $20 \times 20 = 400$ candidates per cycle).

**Step 6 — Apply acceleration constraints before issuing the command.**

$$v_{cmd} = \min\!\left( v^*,\; v_{current} + \dot v_{max} \Delta t \right)$$
$$\omega_{cmd} = \min\!\left( \omega^*,\; \omega_{current} + \dot\omega_{max} \Delta t \right)$$

These clipped commands are sent to the motor controllers.

**Trade-off discussion (Board favourite):**

- **Increase $\alpha$** → robot heads more directly to goal, but takes risks in tight gaps.
- **Increase $\beta$** → robot is more cautious, prefers wider gaps, may oscillate in narrow corridors.
- **Increase $\gamma$** → robot prefers speed, useful in open spaces, dangerous near obstacles.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Analytical Matrix

For a KTU numerical problem, suppose:
- Robot: differential drive, $v_{max} = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega_{max} = 1.0$ rad/s.
- $\dot v_{max} = 0.5$ m/s², $\dot\omega_{max} = 2.0$ rad/s².
- Current velocity: $v_a = 0.2$ m/s, $\omega_a = 0.0$ rad/s.
- $\Delta t = 0.1$ s.
- $r_{max} = 3.0$ m, $T = 2.0$ s.

| Candidate | $v$ (m/s) | $\omega$ (rad/s) | Reachability check | Clearance $d$ (m) | Heading $\hat h$ | $\hat d$ | Velocity term | $G$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.25 | 0.0 | $0.25 \le 0.2 + 0.05 = 0.25$ ✓ | 2.5 | 0.9 | 0.83 | 0.50 | 1.07 |
| 2 | 0.30 | 0.2 | $0.30 \le 0.25$? NO ✗ | — | — | — | — | infeasible |
| 3 | 0.20 | 0.5 | $0.20 \in [0.15, 0.25]$ ✓ | 1.8 | 0.7 | 0.60 | 0.40 | 0.91 |

**Conclusion:** Candidate 1 maximises $G$; the robot accelerates straight forward.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Three Mermaid block diagrams depict the **functional architecture flow** of Bug 2, VFH, and DWA, following Mermaid compilation safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords as standalone IDs, no markdown formatting inside node labels).

---

## 4.1 Bug 2 — Functional State Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start at qstart]) --> nodeB[Compute mline from qstart to qgoal]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Move toward goal along mline]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Obstacle contact sensor triggered}
    nodeD -- No --> nodeC
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[Record qhit and mark mline]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Follow obstacle boundary in fixed handedness]
    nodeF --> nodeG{mline recrossed}
    nodeG -- No --> nodeF
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH{New crossing closer to goal than qhit}
    nodeH -- No --> nodeF
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI[Record qleave and depart boundary]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{Goal reached}
    nodeJ -- No --> nodeC
    nodeJ -- Yes --> nodeK([Stop at qgoal])
```

---

## 4.2 VFH — Three-Stage Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Ultrasonic LiDAR array] --> nodeB[Stage 1 Cartesian Grid Update]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Stage 2 Polar Histogram Hk]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Weighted Smoothing Filter]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Compare Hk with Hth}
    nodeE -- Below Hth --> nodeF[Candidate valley set]
    nodeE -- Above Hth --> nodeG[Blocked sector]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Select valley centroid nearest to goal direction]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Steering command theta steer]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Motor controller]
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

---

## 4.3 DWA — Closed-Loop Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Pose estimate odometry] --> nodeB[Current velocity va omega a]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute admissible set Va]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute dynamic window Vd using acceleration limits and dt]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Search space Vs equals Va intersect Vd]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Discretise Vs into Nv by Nomega grid]
    nodeF --> nodeG[For each candidate simulate arc over horizon T]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Evaluate heading clearance and velocity sub goals]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Compute Gvw as weighted sum of three terms]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Select v star omega star argmax G]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Clip to acceleration limits]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Send vcmd omegacmd to motors]
    nodeL --> nodeA
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — When to Use Which Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Mobile robot obstacle avoidance] --> nodeB{Sensor richness}
    nodeB -- Touch only --> nodeC[Bug 0 Bug 1 Bug 2]
    nodeB -- Dense range array --> nodeD{Map or no map}
    nodeD -- No map metric --> nodeE[VFH or VFH plus]
    nodeD -- Grid map --> nodeF{Velocity constraints important}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[DWA]
    nodeF -- No --> nodeE
```
